stats: (35 min, plus at least 8 hrs’ resting time)

comments.commentBody: The butter amount (141g) listed in the ingredients is incorrect and should be closer to 197g (4 Tbs = 56g in the dough; 10 Tbs = 141g for the butter block).
Definitely need to correct the prep and cook times, plus chilling!
In what way should the prep and cook times be corrected?

@Boris isn’t rising and resting part of the prep?

This is a recipe that is screaming out for a video.
@Greg Creedon Yes!  A video would be very helpful!
I just watched the YouTube video of this recipe. I think it’s a must if you want to really understand how to make this beautiful dough.

For this, or any other baking for which you need to keep a dough cold, get a marble slab and put it in the refrigerator a few hours before starting. You can get a purpose-made rolling slab for a hefty price, or you can look for:
*A marble chessboard at yard and garage sales
*A scrap of marble from a company that makes marble countertops - a sink cut-out is an ideal size
*A single 18"X18" marble floor tile from a flooring store - they may have some that are leftovers, or slightly chipped.
Just watched episode of Julia Child and guest baker make this dough on PBS. It is a great tutorial on how to make it.
Guest baker was Beatrice Ojakangas. If you want to bake anything from the Scandinavian countries, she's the best source!

A video would be fantastic! 
Until then, I’ll let the entire recipe  rest.
how did the Danish make this before electricity and mixers?
Not the recipe to attempt during a heat wave....
Could puff pastry be used for this?
Just asking ...could the frozen Phyllo Dough somehow be utilized as a substitute for the homemade dough?
I do not have a dough hook or standing mixer, how do I mix the dough?
Aren't all good dough recipes "wrapped around a sheet of butter, rolled out and folded several times to create layer upon layer of flaky pastry"?
Just watched your video for this dessert and it looks delicious. BTW, what is your red spatula called with the white markings; I’d love to get my own. Thanks in advance.
@Donna - the Danish dough recipe is linked in the ingredient list. Happy baking! This looks like a fun and delicious way to practice making laminated pastry.

@Donna here it is (I love mine): https://www.thermoworks.com/products/hi-temp-silicone-reduction-spatula?variant=45991967719586&gad_source=1&gad_campaignid=20562571647&gbraid=0AAAAAD_RHEe7TYP3v-mNY8wpT96GliAC4&gclid=CjwKCAjw6f3RBhApEiwAMaCqWTdzjeGPs14Yzev5A_DHPSXqCKiLVutyXTkShAMG-IipEPQP9S9oQhoCW1cQAvD_BwE
Donna, check out Hedley & Bennett.

I don’t know what Danish dough is.  Do you buy it?  Is it in the freezer section?  I don’t think I have ever seen it at my store.  A link or a little more information would be helpful.  Instead it is relegated to looks yummy but will not be made.
@Beth The recipe for the Danish dough is linked in the tart recipe
@Beth there is a link to the danish dough recipe in the ingredient list. It is also a Claire Saffitz recipe published previously in NYT Cooking.
@Beth there is a link to a recipe for the dough but according to the comments it’s problematic. I think I’m going to pass on this recipe unless I could buy store bought dough.
@Beth There's a link to the Classic Danish Dough recipe in the ingredients listing.
They link to a recipe for it in the ingredient list! @Beth
@Donna there's a link to the Danish dough recipe.
@Beth The recipe for the dough is linked in the ingredients list.
@Beth - click on the link in the ingredients, it will take you to the dough recipe
@Beth Danish dough recipe is linked
@Beth there's a link to the Danish dough recipe in this recipe
I watched your video and will definitely give this a try.  It’s such a gorgeous dessert. As per commenter Donna, I would also love to purchase “the red spatula”. Please give details.
Is the correct amount of butter 14 Tablespoons or 10 Tablespoons?  HELP!!
Made this today. It’s absolutely delicious! Well worth the time on the danish dough. I’ll be making over and over again!

This lovely sounding recipe confuses me because I don’t know how much Danish Dough to use?
Am I just to work with what I imagine it should be. 
Just am amateur baker who loves pastry.

Absolutely divine. You need to plan ahead but fairly simple to execute. Very subtle sweetness. 
I left it filled proofing in fridge overnight before baking so that we could have it for breakfast and it turned out perfect.

Of course anything Claire Saffitz does is delectable perfection, but this one is honestly beyond. From her precise but not fussy danish dough recipe that sets you up for success to the quick and easy fillings, the author of the recipe makes it easy for you to look like a dessert hero. Brava!

I baked the danish today. The presentation looks the business and the flavor is nice. The crust tastes like a croissant with all that butter. However, I undercooked it a bit.  Top was nicely browned but bottom was slightly underdone at 30 minutes.  I’ll definitely make it again.  

Next time I’ll put it in a 10” tart pan to improve the bake and thin out the cream cheese filling some.  

I used fresh blueberries since they are in season.
Any suggestions for baking if you don’t have a tart pan? I have a springform pan, a glass pie pan, and a metal cake pan. Not sure which is better.

these versions of Basque cheesecake always have vanilla in them when it is not needed and is not in the original recipe created at La Vina in San Sebastian Spain. The burning causes a decent caramelisation of sugar that has the correct flavour without going over the top with all the vanilla.
@Brian it’s almost like recipes have been modified from their original versions for millennia or something

@Hunter I think what the commenter is saying is that the “modification” called for here is unnecessary and that people should maybe try the original recipe, created by a Spanish chef, and widely available, which has a much better flavor profile. Do so, and perhaps one will understand why the original recipe is still the best.
@Darnell Hillman Trying the original is a wonderful choice for many bakers, but so is experimenting with new takes. If I’m not mistaken, that’s how the original San Sebastián cheese cake came to be.
Made exactly as written, not much effort needed and absolutely delicious. I didn’t need to use the broiler.

Excellent cheesecake, excellent recipe! I made this in my 13 cup food processor and even though it was above the fill line, it still all came together just fine. Using my thermometer was really helpful - it was fully cooked even though my gut would have said to go longer. I needed a quick 30-second broil to get the right amount of browning in my oven. Served this with a little rhubarb compote as it's in season, and I think it will pair beautifully with different seasonal fresh fruits all summer!
@Gillian what size pan did you use?
@GolfTan I used a 9” springform with a 3” side!
This seems like a formidable recipe but I bet it’s absolutely wonderful and worth the time and effort
I don't have a food processor.  Can this be made with just a hand mixer?
@Betty Baker Yes! Claire made a video in her YouTube channel for this recipe and she talks about it!
@Betty Baker: Yes. There are instructions in the Tips section on how to make this with a smaller processor.
@Betty Baker Absolutely. Might not be as smooth, but will still be delicious.
Is 150° degrees meant to be Farenheit or Celsius?
@Myriam  Fahrenheit.  If you are using the phone app, you can choose US or Metric measure by hitting the little ruler at the beginning of the ingredient list.   That includes temperature.
Farenheit.
I make a version with fresh goat cheese, Greek yogurt, and honey!
A this be frozen?
Really delicious. Mine had a perfectly brown top even before the set baking time. The center was deliciously creamy. Thank you Vlaire for a fantastic recipe.
Delicious
Delicious. I bravely halved the recipe, using a 7" springform pan and reducing the cook time.It turned out well.
Would love to make this. Does anyone know if I can I sub GF flour or cornstarch? Thanks so much!
Yes. A gluten-free flour substitute such as Bob's Red Mill will work fine.

Watched Dessert Person episode last week and immediately made this over the weekend! Using the thermometer to know when to take it out is key. I had to split the batter between two pans, one I cooked to 150° accidentally and the other was around 140° and much better. I can't wait to make this again!
Can you substitute the flour some a gf flour or other binding agent to make it gluten free?
@Scott- You could use Bob’s Red Mill 1:1 gf flour or just brown rice flour that lives with the rest of the flours, or often found in the bulk section of many stores now.
Has anyone tried making it gluten-free with GF flour?
Has anyone made this gluten free? Also, has anyone made it with all cream cheese, i.e., no mascarpone?
@Jill Baily Yes to GF because I accidentally forgot the flour haha. It was still great! It just sunk slightly in the centre while cooking.
*while cooling, not while cooking

Followed recipe precisely, except reduced lemon to 1.5 lemons zested and 3 oz lemon in cake. Mixed in a Vitamix blender, in this order: 
eggs, whipping cream, flour ,cheeses (chopped into small pieces, sugar
Added 1/2 teaspoon sea salt
The blender was full, but handled it fine, made sure everything room temperature before adding. This recipe. made one 7” and four 4” cakes; Adjusted baking times 4” cake about 20 minutes, 7” cake about 30. Left in refrigerator overnight, even better 2nd day!
I think this version is overly complicated.  There's another MUCH simpler Basque Cheesecake recipe on this site (Recipe from Marti Buckley Adapted by Tanya Sichynsky) which is wonderful

Unless you’re not eating it until the next day, keep it at room temperature. Refrigerating it wrecks the custardy consistency that is essential for this to be Basque cheesecake. There’s a reason these things are stacked on open shelves all day at La Viña and it’s not just that they sell so fast that refrigerating them is pointless.
Absolutely divine! Serve it with Martha Rose Shulman’s Blueberry Compote and you will weep for joy.

I couldn't procure marscapone easily so I used 32oz/4 bricks of cream cheese and it came out brilliantly. 

I used the recipe tip about lower capacity food processor use (mine was about 8-9 cup working capacity - a 40 year old, made in America Hamilton Beach that will never die), and that also turned out without any drama. the recipe was maybe even a little conservative to require a 14 cup processor. I could see 10+ working. 

I tried the probe thermometer trick but probably due to the airiness inside the cheesecake I was getting inaccurate readings of 170f at 30 min! Visual indications told me it was more like 150f - center jiggled but outside was set. Light browning in spots on top. after broiling and glazing it was perfection. It didn't really even crack, nor deflate at all. 

I'll definitely make this one for parties frequently. Quite easy despite the large volume of ingredient.
Can this be done in a mixer instead of a 14cup food processor?
@Nathalie In Claire’s YouTube video, she says yes, stand mixer with paddle will work!
Has anyone halved this recipe? If so, what size pan did you use?

@Janet the 9” pan has an area of 63.5 sq inches , a 6” pan has  an area of 28.2 sq inches. A 7” pan has an area of 38 sq inches. I would use the 6” pan and possibly anticipate baking a little bit longer.
I'm new to gluten-free baking since a new girlfriend addition to our family.  Could this be made GF with Bob's 1:1 without adverse consequence to the result?
@Shelley Bob’s GF 1:1 should work just fine.
Yes, absolutely!

I am sure the recipe is fine but the cheeses are not correct.  No cream cheese and Mascarpone is Italian.  The San Sebastian version is lighter and not as dense.  The chef in our restaurant came as close as she could but I still have never encountered it the same in the US.

The trick of using a food processor and also crumbling parchment paper for the springform pan is exactly the same as the recipe developed over a year ago by King Arthur baking company. The technique is utterly simple and amazingly delicious. It is part of my routine now and frequently requested by friends and guests. BTW, I’ve used a food processor for my New York style cheesecake for decades now with perfect results every  time.  I’m looking forward to baking Claire’s lemon version of the Basque style cheesecake. The addition of mascarpone promises to elevate this already wonderful cheesecake to new heights!

It appears you are using a commercial cream cheese that would have gum and stabilizers....like Phila....   I try never to use that stuff in something like a cheese cake.  can you confirm the type of cream cheese?

Delicious. I quartered the recipe and the batter fit perfectly in two crème brûlée ramekins. I also forgot the flour by accident and it was fine. They did sink slightly in their centres but not a big deal IMO. It only took ~15 minutes in the oven to reach 145F and I only had to chill them about 5 hours. Win!
Made with my stand mixer & paddle attachment, per Claire’s instructions in her YouTube video and it came out flawlessly!

we made this in the wrong sized pan, subbed a mix of honey and maple syrup for sugar, forgot the flour, and used strawberry jam with lemon juice for the glaze. it still turned out perfect. (we think that the instant read thermometer was the key to our success).
Can I substitute the flour for almond flour for a gluten free version?
@Camille you can use cornstarch
Has anyone tried this without the flour to make it gluten free?
@Emma I left out the flour accidentally (see comment below) and it was fine.
@Emma you can use cornstarch - it will turn out great
Made as written. FABULOUS flavor and texture. At 35 min my oven took it to 141 degrees so gave it 6 or 7 more minutes. Beautiful color.

I made this for a family “bake-off” and it was a huge hit.  I didn’t have to put it in the oven a second time to get the beautiful, burnt top, and served it with both a blueberry and a rhubarb compote, and anise shortbread cookies for a bit of crunch.  I’ll be working on adapting it  to individual cheesecakes for a tea party next month.  (Made for a delightful breakfast the next day!)
@Myriam this would be fahrenheit be because 60 Celsius is 140 F
Do you think this recipe can be halved?  (Smaller Springform, smaller food processor, fewer servings)
I’m asking the same thing! I don’t have a single tin that’s big enough for all the batter.
@Chris B. I would do 2/3 in a 7 or 8".
Awesome. Best cheesecake I have ever had. Dont be afraid to under bake it.

My cheesecake came out looking just like the picture! And absolutely delicious! One thing with the recipe seemed to be incorrect the measuring of the lemon juice and sugar for the glaze when it listed them as ingredients:
sugar, said leave out 1 tablespoon for the glaze 
lemon juice did not specify the exact measurement to leave out for the glaze. It did, however, say 1 tablespoon in the directions at the end of the recipe.
Can you make these in an air fryer instead of deep frying?
I'm sorry to be the bearer of bad news, but no.
@Justin I’ve tried, and no. The air fryer is awesome for reheating, though.
@Justin like churros, these must be deep fried. There are recipes for baking churros, which are very similar in texture to these, but those recipes are completely different from this
I have not made this recipe, but I have made Mexican churros, which are almost exactly the same thing. The only different seems to be the shape. A churro is a straight stick instead of a ring.

I thought these  were just okay.  Rather labor intense and time consuming for the end result I got.  I love donuts, but will keep searching for a better recipe.  
I expected it to be lighter, similar to a French donut at Dunkin'.  The interior was not as light.

I've made crullers many times, but discovered that piping thick dough onto small pieces of parchment difficult and fussy. The parchment moves as you move the pastry bag and hangs on when you try to break it off. 
Instead, I line a baking sheet with parchment to fit, the dab a bit of dough under each corner to stick the parchment to the pan, then pipe the crullers on to it. After resting, I just cut the parchment around each cruller, then lift and add it to the hot oil.
Use chia seeds ONLY after soaking a few hours or they are grainy and stomach upsetting. Please give accurate times.
@Linda I don’t see chia seeds in this recipe….

@Linda
May I ask what you encountered, as you mentioned the timing of some steps? I’m a chef and may try this recipe for clients and I’d love some info!
What are the chia seeds for?
Has anyone tried this with gluten free flour substitute?

Does anyone have a recipe for an old fashioned cruller? A culler that is like the ones Auntie Em gave to Dorothy and the hired hands. They were amazing. Crazy crisp outside with a dense dough inside and a twisted square shape. They have been lost to me for many years, and I miss them terribly.

I just finished eating this, it was delicious but I have some notes :)
I left the walnuts out of the batter, STP should not be crunchy. I wanted STP with banana not banana cake with dates. Otherwise it was made as directed. Noticed there were none of the typical spices you would find in STP so I added it to the caramel sauce after I removed it from the heat. Cinnamon, ginger, clove and nutmeg all go great with banana as well. I would make it again, and again.... with my small alterations.
It says the prep time for this is only 5 minutes.  Has to be a mistake.
@JKR it says 2 hrs 5 minutes
@Lisa C that’s the total time.  The prep time is listed as 5 minutes

The prep time issue comes up over and over. My understanding is that we have to get each ingredient into the form or amount stated in the ingredient list on our own time. In other words, we coarsely chop the dates, chop the nuts, and measure all of the ingredients as per the ingredient list, all on our own time. Only then does the prep time clock start ticking.

@Linda yes, you’re spot on. So many complain about prep time not being long enough, knife skills needing to be pro etc. You’re correct, gathering ingredients as listed is not the prep. Prep starts at step 1, and assumes you’ve got all ingredients chopped, weighed, measured…
I cut the sugar down to 120g for the cake and 120g for the toffee; the bananas and dates already add enough sweetness.
Would it work to use frozen bananas (like you can in banana bread)?

I don't think frozen bananas would work for the caramelized layer, but I used two previously frozen, super-ripe bananas for the date/banana mixture. I used fresh bananas to create the caramelized banana layer.
What about very ripe bananas that haven't yet been frozen?

Very delicious! I used two super-ripe, frozen bananas in the date/banana mixture, and fresh bananas for the caramelized layer. I think it would be just as good without the caramelized bananas if you are looking to take one of the several steps out. The cake was very moist and not overly sweet. I took it to a friend's house, tightly wrapped in foil, and held it in her warm oven until it was time to serve. It was gobbled up and enjoyed by all!

I loved this recipe! I've made it twice now and gotten rave reviews from friends.  It is absolutely best eaten day of, but I brought it into work on day 3 to no complaints and greatly enjoyed leftovers myself.  I also just poured the rest of the toffee sauce over the cake to serve–highly recommend.
Not feelin' the  dates. OK without?
@Charlie   I believe dates are the sine qua non of sticky toffee pudding
@Charlie Dates are integral to sticky toffee pudding. Without them you’d have a delicious banana cake, but not banana sticky toffee pudding.
@Charlie Imho, they’re essential to the consistency, but not an overtly strong flavor. I imagine the addition of bananas here would subdue their flavor even more.
6-8 dates do not a sticky toffee pudding make. Banana bread WITH dates is like banana bread with raisins or dried cranberries. Just renaming something doesn't make it so.

Had some dates in the freezer and the perfect number of ripe bananas, so I thought I’d give it a go. It’s a bit of a process, and I’ve never made a super traditional STP recipe, so I can’t compare, but it seems worth it to me! So delicious. I like the added texture of the walnuts (I happened to have them, so I added them), but it would be delicious without.

Tweaked the recipe scale for my airfryer (as I don't have an oven in my tiny apartment :( ), cooked on roast setting at 130'C for 30 mins and then 120'C for 10 mins and it turned out lovely! I didn't include the caramelised banana layer, omit the walnuts and added an extra date.
I love this recipe. It’s absolutely incredible! It is quite rich though and I wouldn’t recommend serving it with ice cream. I bit of crème fraîche is nice for some acidity

The batter was absolutely delicious.  But I found this more of a tasty banana cake than a super rich pudding.  The broiled bananas looked terribly unappetizing, almost a purple hue by the next morning.  You need to char them almost completely to make it look decent.  Make it and see if it's worth all of the steps, or just make a tasty banana cake?
If I make it the night before, should I wait to pour over the toffee sauce until I warm it up tomorrow? Or add the toffee sauce but reserve the extra that I will pour over after warming? Thanks!
My egg + sugar + butter mix looked curdled. any tips on how to prevent or fix?
@Katie yes I would keep beating the mix at a high speed for a few minutes.  It will smooth out.

Just made this - did everything to the letter, added the walnuts.  It came out as it should and it tasted, well, like banana bread with a sauce.  Jury’s out in our house whether it was worth the effort (which really wasn’t that bad, but I coulda just made regular old banana bread and gotten more or less the same result).

Such a good project - I ended up using an 8in pan which meant I had to be clever with how to arrange the broiled bananas on the bottom. I did a grid like pattern which I think came out beautifully. This pudding was rich and incredible, I recommend serving with extra cream on top!

I have an old fashioned crank style apple peeler/corer/slicer.  Put an apple on the metal spit, crank and it’s all done in the blink of an eye.  Then cut the mound of peeled and cored apples in half.  Would this contraption work for this recipe?
I was thinking the same thing. That device has changed apple pie into an easy bake from its previous status as an ordeal, for me!

In step 5, "Prepare the Apples", are the cored, sliced apple halves placed on a clean jelly roll pan, or are they being placed in the caramel on the the jelly roll pan that was covered in caramel in step 3?

@Anne

Caramel goes onto the jelly roll pan in step 3.  I’m thinking peaches later in the year.

THe cored, halved apples pre-cook on top of the caramel in a slow (300) oven. When they come out and cool, then you slice them. Meanwhile the caramel has gone into a fast (400) overn to reduce the apples' juices. Watch the video.

A bit of a project, but spectacular. Really benefitted from whipped cream on top because the apples, after caramelizing all the way through, are thoroughly bittersweet.

I had two apples (four halves) left over and wish I had packed them in. The apples continue to shrink in the second bake, and my final tarte had small gaps between some halves.
This one needs video folks!  A Claire video on your channel is always a great baking day!!
@Ellen you can find a great video on Claire’s Dessert x Person YouTube site.

I made this today for Mother’s Day and it was fabulous. I wasn’t sure if it would come out at various stages but it was perfect. The flavor was so complex and it wasn’t oversweet … definitely more of an adult dessert. The dollop of whipped cream really set it off. I didn’t have a jelly roll pan so I used a 9x13 cake pan and it worked great! I used one less apple than the recipe called for, and definitely packed them in. Watching the full video first was incredibly helpful. It’s what inspired me to try it! Also… in response to another comment asking for temps for the caramel, I appreciate the description of what to look for in the caramel instead of temps because when I see a recipe that needs a candy thermometer I normally just get too intimidated and don’t try it. This was a stretch recipe for me, and it was fun and turned out great!

Why would this recipe not give temperatures for cooking the syrup (steps 1 and 2)? Great to give other ways of determining when it's ready as well, but I suspect an awful lot of people who would attempt this have a candy or instant thermometer, and the thermometer gives a much better idea of when you're getting close.

I feel sad to be leaving my first review on here on one of Claire’s recipes but I think I might be one of the first ones to make this so I feel a duty! I purchased a jelly roll pan just for this, followed the instructions and watched the video but after four hours i flipped it and the apples are complete mush, basically pureed. Tasty mush, but really disappointed after all that effort.
What about a smaller version?
What  is a jellyroll pan (as compared to a normal baking pan)?
I keep trying this but for some reason I cant get the caramel right.  it comes out looking beautiful but tasting a bit burnt...

This was a fun challenge and certainly a labor of love - don’t underestimate how long it takes to peel and prep the apples. After refrigerating the prepared apples and reduced caramel overnight, I still only needed to bake for around 50 minutes before the pastry turned golden. It took some nerve to flip the cooled pan decisively, but was well worth all the prep for such a beautiful final product. Tons of positive feedback from the group on this one!

stats.Total Time: 1 hour 10 minutes, plus at least 2 ½ hours’ freezing and cooling
stats.Cook Time: 1 hour, plus at least 2 ½ hours freezing

comments.commentBody: I am reading this recipe on a packed train riding through the snowy Polish countryside, where every   second traveller unpacks a garlicky sausage sandwich at some point of the journey. Would have loved, if some of those were cookies instead. Looking forward to bake them after arrival!
@Kristin please let us know. Looks so interesting but I’m not a sweets person. I want something that isn’t dark or milk chocolate

Please everyone note that the Chocolate Kolbasa recipe that is cited in the intro to this recipe is from 7 years ago and that that recipe has many comments noting that many many other countries also have traditional holiday cokes that look like sausage slices. This is an original take on an old, widespread, international tradition. And it is original! Strawberries, macadamias, and pistachios! I don’t see those in any of the other sausage-cookie recipes. So just cool it please.
These make me want a whole charcuterie cookie collection for gifting!

Kanoa, my exact thought when I saw this. I immediately remembered a previous recipe for coconut twists that look like cheese twists. Will try to find a recipe for chocolate salami that’s mentioned here. Any more suggestions?Thanks.
Maybe add some sort of chocolate ball/truffle that looks like an olive?!
@Susan dip another pinkish cookie in white chocolate to resemble the fat on prosciutto! Maybe another log cookie that you shape more like an oblong rectangle to emulate the slices.

Nobody seems to be mentioning the flavor of these cookies which is EXCELLENT. It has a gourmet “strawberry pop-tart” flavor that I can’t get enough of. These won’t last 24 hours in my house and I live alone.
@Will think these would taste as nice using freeze dried tart cherries? Clearly everyone is baking these cause I can’t find freeze dried strawberries anywhere!!
@erock j I made them this morning using a mix of dried cherries and dried raspberries (both from Trader Joe’s). The cherries are certainly tart, but they accentuate the almond flavor!
@erock my Target carries both freeze dried raspberries and strawberries.

As with most log-to-cookie recipes, it is easier instead to roll the dough to the desired thickness and cut out cookies with a sharp cookie cutter.  Rolling the edges of the cookies over plates with egg and Demerara sugar is simple.  Check out  NYT salted chocolate-chunk shortbread recipe.  If you are still into logs, cut with wire cheese cutter rather than a knife.
@Mr. Poultry Going to disagree with Mr Poultry on this one! Logs are much easier and I suggest a meat slicing knife (very thin blade) if you have one. I’ve sliced *a lot* of cookies!

@Mr. Poultry the wire cheese cutter is a good idea. I also disagree with you about rolling these out and cutting them out with a cookie cutter. That would completely ruin the intended effect of them looking like sliced mortadella.
@Mr. Poultry I 100% agree with this! I’ve made the Salted Butter Chocolate Chunk cookies hundreds of times, and used the same technique for these.
I learned soooo much from Claire's instructions(and video) here!!!! Great techniques and I am definitely going to make these.  Bravo on your amazing creativity. You slayed it lol

I’d like to try making these with a few changes.  Freeze dried raspberries instead of strawberries.  Raspberry and almond are a better combo.  Leave out the macadamia and pistachios and add slivered almonds and chopped dark chocolate.
@Options If you leave the pistachios out, or replace it with dark chocolate, then the cookies no longer looks like mortadella.
@Joyce Exactly! Those might be great for April 1st, I was looking to make them more holiday cookies.
Claire is kind of a baking legend, so you do you. But calling your preference "better" is subjective. I'm deeply tickled by the inventiveness of Claire's recipe.

The look of these cracked me up. 
Alas, my holiday cookie swap host has tree nut issues. These will have to wait for another swap. Is there an easy list of log-to-slice cookie recipes here? I'm not sure what search term will get me just cookies. The "similar recipes" do not seem to be hitting that mark either.

@junction
Check out the gochujang cookies and read the comments about baking them in a pinwheel log. I make them that way all the time. Delicious beautiful and unique. They would be great!

Just about any butter-based recipe can be rolled into a log.  It just needs enough butter that it will get really firm in the refrigerator.  Also, there are biscotti.  (If you're looking for quick cookie recipes that make a lot, check out recipes that use a cookie press.  The dough doesn't need to be chilled, and they press out in seconds.)
I love Dorie Greenspan's World Peace cookies - they are very delicious and shaped into a log first.

Not a big fan of the gimmick but I was curious what a strawberry-flavored cookie would taste like. Is it good? After making them with minor adjustments (no macadamias, rolled out the dough), for me the flavor wasn’t great. Just personal preference but they weren’t delicious- the most important test for a cookie.

Just made these with lightly toasted hazelnuts (the macadamias were exorbitant at the supermarket-shoulda picked some up at Costco) and regular pistachios, not raw. Very delicious strawberry flavor, enhanced by the almond paste.  And they are very pretty as well. Mortadella would not come to mind necessarily, if I  didnt know. Also made the chocolate kolbasa, now chilling. And plan on making Melissa Clark's coconut twists that look like cheese straws for a cookie charcuterie platter.

I’m baking every cookie week cookie as it’s released this week so baked this as written and it’s very fun aesthetically. These are very much strawberry cookies, I don’t get much almond flavor from them but the nuts balance it out. I had no issues with slicing the log cookies! Don’t do what I did and dump all the nuts in the middle of the dough and fold the dough over. I ended up with a lot of the nuts concentrated in the center instead of the speckled appearance

@Lindsey Trader Joe’s has all of these specialty ingredients in smaller amounts for a fairly reasonable price if you want to try the recipe. You could use the extra pistachios to make Claire’s Pistachio Pinwheels, which are absolutely beautiful and delicious.
Macadamia nuts, pistachios, almond paste, and freeze-dried strawberries? This is a great recipe if you don't intend to buy anyone Christmas gifts and instead want to blow your budget on one recipe.
@adumbbaker this is true. A treat recipe in my kitchen. I kept saying to my husband that they better be good because of the expense.

These turned out great! I watched the video a few times and it was enjoyable and helpful. I don't have a food processor large enough for the dough, so I processed the strawberries and did the rest with a handheld mixer. My dough felt a little dry when I rolled it into a log but after sitting overnight in the fridge it was fine. I had a lot of strawberry pieces left in my sieve and I didn't want to waste them, so I just added them in and nothing bad happened. They really do look like mortadella!
I get the use of pistachios and macadamia for color, but the flavor suffers when raw.  I'm going to try again with roasted macadamia nuts and see if I like that better.
@KB I used unsalted peanuts in lieu of the mac nuts. Worked fine and the aesthetic was the same. Also used roasted pistachios not raw.
Any suggestions if you don’t have a food processor?  These look so fun!
You could probably do the strawberries in a blender and then the rest in a mixer/by hand. good luck!
@Holly A I have a small processor so I used is to grind the strawberries. Everything else was done in my stand mixer

Followed the recipe exactly and here is my feedback:
Would appreciate if the units were consistent (spoons, ounces, grams, please choose one)
Even after sifting (in a very fine mesh sieve) lots of strawberry seeds still snuck through 
Claire’s directions along with the video are spot on
Watch cookies closely when baking 
Final cookie is a bit “one note” for us agree that maybe freeze-dried cherry might lend complexity 
Raw macadamias were hard to find and we decided toasted and salted would have tasted better in the final cookie 
Less macadamia nuts and more pistachios please
Fun cookie, but not quite there yet

Look fab, taste fab, recipe is like a first draft. The ingredients jump between grams, ounces, and volume, with some items having no weight equivalents at all. The (not cheap) freeze-dried strawbs are especially confusing, that 1.5 oz yields only 1 cup (half of what the recipe lists), and 0.6 oz of that is left behind in the sieve, which you're told to discard. Also, there’s nooo way the prep time is only 10 mins - mine was triple. Overall happy, but at the cost of my sanity for the day!‌
Excuse my ignorance, but what is sanding sugar? In my defence, I live in Australia so it probably has a different name here.
@MandyM It’s a slightly more coarse sugar sold in baking supply stores. It comes in different colours. In a pinch you could use ordinary granulated sugar.
@MandyM sanding sugar is a type of decorative sugar the granules are larger than reg sugar it gives a sparkly look

Question:  could small chunks of white chocolate work in place of macadamia (or even with)..  Love the recipe as written, just wondering about possible variations.  Full disclosure, I still haven’t been able to find raw macadamia nuts.  Picked up dry roasted from Trader Joe’s.  Recommendations on where to find raw?
@Marilouise dry toasted from TJs worked just fine!

I’m about to be one of those annoying people who makes modifications to the recipe to the point where they don’t fully resemble the original, but I wanted to make a few adjustments to make them a little more holiday-ish in taste and more attuned to the flavors I enjoy. 

I did freeze dried cherries instead of strawberries, almonds and pistachios instead of macadamias, and entirely skipped the step of rolling in sugar to make the process easier, as I’m planning to make these for a cookie party. Also added about 1/4 tsp of vanilla extract. Me and my husband can’t stop eating them! 

Don’t skip the freezing step before slicing—I did at first and could not get them to slice properly.
Would the recipe still work with gluten free flour instead, and subsituting all the sugars with allulose or monkfruit?

@Anne I made mine with the King Arthur measure for measure gluten free flour and they came out fine. Most log/refrigerator cookies/shortbreads work well with the rice flours  - a lovely crisp and it’s much harder to over work the dough. However, I have always found that you can do one substitution for a type of ingredients, so if you take out the flour, and the sugar, and the butter and the egg, it probably won’t work. So if gluten is your issue, I’d stick with just subbing that out.

I made them today. The idea that these are not a lot of work is SOOOO wrong.  They are a TON of work!  Very tasty, but for about 24 cookies, too much work.  They suffer from the same failing as all refrigerator cookies that have any hard pieces inside ( same with a great chocolate cookie with bittersweet pieces inside)—they start falling apart when you cut them and you have to push the pieces back in.  These are very pretty, though, which may make it worth it but I’d omit the nuts next time
Just wondering if someone had a nut-free alternative to the pistachio and macadamia nuts that will still yield the same mortadella look. Would white chocolate chips and pumpkin seeds be good options?
@Justin Currants & white chocolate chips.  Not adding sweetness is what makes it with nuts.
These are delicious and look exactly like mortadella. I found the look sort of unsettling and will leave them off my holiday cookie tray. I’ll save them for another time!

Because it's Christmas and because it's the NYTimes Cooking comments section, I will confess I made these with freeze dried cherries. 

Is that what the recipe called for? No. 
Did Trader Joe's have freeze-dried strawberries? No. 
Would I survive life as a NYTimes recipe developer, knowing my hard and careful work would be abandoned and desecrated by the whims and hubris of smug NYTimes subscribers? 100% no. 

But--should you find yourself in the same position--dried cherries are also nice.
I'd like to use freeze-dried powder (strawberry or raspberry) but would I still use 1.5 ounces? Thanks.

The powder often has many additives including citric acid in it, which completely changes the ingredient's taste and ph and performance in recipes. Beware. You would use the same weight, yes, but you might get a very different result from using whole freeze-dried strawberries.

@David

Yes
@David I just used one 1.2 oz bag of freeze dried berries from Trader Joe’s and it was fine. I wasn’t going to worry about the slight difference.

Out of the oven this morning.
12” dough roll, sliced into 1/4”  discs yields 24 cookies?  I got 40, with some wobbly cutting.
Upped the strawberry to 3 ounces.  I also needed more egg white (50/50 with aqua faba) in the dough to get it to come together (probably my local flour).  
LOOK GREAT & TASTE GREAT!

Thank you for the recipe!
@Seattle Ray may i ask why did you up the strawberries? is it for the colour?

It's far more likely that doubling the freeze-dried strawberries (and not your local flour) is what led to you needing more egg white. More dry ingredient(s) = more wet ingredient(s). ;)

I agree that the yield math isn't math-ing. I'd expect to get at least 36 cookies, and as many as 48, if made exactly as written. Based on the video demo, Claire gets 32 cookies. They're also clearly cut thicker than 1/4 inch (more like 1/3 inch), so that makes sense.

I made them and they are delicious. Very dense.. almond amd strawberry turn out to be a yummy mix, and they look just like the picture. I was ably to cut them with a sharp cleaver no problem I'd like to add a picture but I don't know how.

By the way, though I sifted the pulverized strawberries, there was so much stuff leftover that I decided to add it back into the dough. The resulting strong strawberry flavor was really good, and cookie texture was fine.
Took these to our Italian family reunion / Christmas party and they were a big hit! I was really tempted to place them on the antipasto platter next to the salami...

This recipe is awesome and unique, kudos to Claire for developing this! The flavor is fantastic and nuanced. 

I brought these to a bake-off and paired them with Melissa Clark’s coconut twists for a charcuterie theme. I won people’s choice at the bake-off and people raved about the cookies and theme!

This cookie deserves a place on your holiday table and your charcuterie board! It’s unique and delicious - give it a chance!

I'm in Canada and had a lot of trouble sourcing the ingredients for these. For fellow Ontarians: you can get freeze-dried strawberries at Dollarama and the nuts at Bulk Barn. TBD on almond paste – I'm pretty sure I was sold marzipan. We'll see how these turn out...

HINT: as you're laying out cookies onto the baking sheet, check to make sure the "pretty" sides are up, i.e., the up-side of each cookie should be the one that has the most interesting mix of nuts.  I noticed this too late, looking at the undersides of my baked cookies, and realizing I should've turned them over.  That said, they were still beautiful!  And, BTW: on second making of this recipe, I used dried raspberry powder, which enhanced both the color and the flavor.
This recipe takes forever, yeesh. Had fun though.

Made this for a cookie contest party. Did not break top 10, which I thought was fair. They looked great. We actually had them on a charcuterie board, and people thought they were hilarious, but these cookies are just too sour. Maybe they would be better with freeze dried cherries, but I did not bring any home and will not make again. Ingenious, but pure novelty.
Mine turned brown in the oven. Tried baking for less time but they still turned brown and lost the lovely pink hue! I wonder what happened…?
I think the ingredients sound yummy but this does not look at all appetizing. Is there a way to make it look less flesh-like?
The point of the recipe is for the cookies to look like sliced mortadella. That said, you could omit the freeze-dried strawberries for a less flesh-colored cookie.
It helps to press, not saw, log cookies. I use a Shun utility knife.
Is there a substitute for almond paste? It's so hard for me to find.
Almond paste can be made at home using almond flour, powdered sugar, egg white, salt, and almond extract; a food processor helps. I think this recipe needs the almond paste.
@Sandy Solo or Odense brand almond paste in boxes is usually available at Safeway and other big grocery stores in the cookie/baking department — I never noticed it before I started using it!
@Sandy I found it at Trader Joe’s for less than half the price at Whole Foods, they also have the nuts and strawberries

I love Claire, but this recipe was a disaster. I’ve never had a recipe fail me so completely, out of nowhere. The dough that was meant to form in the food processor was a messy paste, completely impossible to form into any sort of cylinder, and broke apart at every turn. It’s a mess, it’s not even worth eating

This cookie gets a 10/10 for looks! And I am full on planning a “charcuterie board” for the holidays with the addition of the Chocolate Kolbasa, Orange/Cardamom Twists, and a Cannoli Dip. Didn’t have any issues baking these cookies or following the recipe. 
As far as taste of the cookie, jury is still out. For me, it’s missing flavor (almond isn’t coming through) and it mostly tastes of freeze dried strawberry. However, I didn’t have the sanding sugar. So will make more with that finishing touch.

Didn't have the intense flavor I'd hoped for, especially considering the expense of the ingredients. VERY pretty, but to me, only average-tasting. Probably will appeal most to those who don't like very-sweet cookies.
I love these! They are so pretty. I added a 1/4tsp of strawberry extract and they taste wonderful
This was a big disappointment. As much effort as I put into compacting it, the dough was crumbly and hard to slice.
I don’t have a food processor so I made these in a stand mixer and they turned out great! I did crush the strawberries using a hand blender

It would be nice to set up this recipe to not waste ingredients. The almond paste comes in a 7 ounce package so you have to use more than half. Also the freeze dried strawberries come in a 1.2 ounce package not 1.5 ounces. I found that I had to process the flour and strawberries several times to get it fine enough to go through the sieve without losing all of the strawberry flavor I ended up using a Japanese mortar and pestle to really grind up the rest of them so that all of the strawberry would end up in the cookie.

@Diane I used cherries (wife hates strawberries) from T Joes, understand your frustration on the package size there.  I had a ton of pieces that didn’t make it through the sieve on the first pass.  I threw them back in the processor to grind them down a bit more.  About half of the went through the second time is sifted. I tossed the in rest.
Teaspoons, cups, grams, ounces, make up your mind. Can't we all agree that the metric system is much better for baking. It's all over the place...

We're tickled pink by this recipe! It took a little time to integrate all the nuts gradually into the "meatloaf." Thank you for the tip to use the parchment paper to aid kneading. We omitted the sanding sugar step. The dough stayed in the fridge for a few days inside a paper towel roll to keep its shape. No problem slicing. Some of our roll was more square than round That was OK: square lunch meat is also a thing. We loved the delicate flavor of strawberries, almonds, and nuts.

These just came out of the oven, and they are beautiful! I sent a Pic to my son who is the deli manager in a large grocery chain, and he just laughed and said they look great. Flavor is delightful! Almond and berry may be my new favorite combo. One thing I found is that when they are chilling you really need to turn the log or alas, you’ll end up with a flat side like I did. But overall these are the bomb!

Mmm, these are a LOT of work AND rather expensive to make for a cookie I found just so-so. The strawberry flavor was pleasant but there was no big win with this recipe. The cookie was rather dry and unpleasantly hard/tough even when I reduced the baking time. Fyi, I weighed ingredients and followed the recipe exactly as written.

I understand this is a big ask and maybe this recipe is just not for me, but does anyone have any ideas for nut-free mix-ins that will still give the mortadella effect? Maybe white chocolate chips and… something green?

@Eden, as a mix-in, I’m thinking some sort of hard candy, crushed/broken into pieces, like green starlight mints or green life savers (though it’s not the right shade of green, and it would be a weird texture/flavor in a cookie). Maybe go to a candy store and see what’s there for inspiration? Another option would be to use broken bits of Graham cracker or Candy-Quik, hand-painted with a little yellow-green food dye, and instead of baking them into the cookies, rapidly press them into the cookies immediately after pulling the cookie sheet out of the oven while they’re still soft enough to take the addition.
@Eden could you use chopped up pretzels? They would give you the white color and the bit of salt. For green maybe candied lime?
If seeds are ok, perhaps pepitas and sunflower seeds?
The Granny's Spice Cookie in the NY Times can be prepared as a sliced log cookie. Excellent spice cookie.

I'm VERY excited to make these! I have a very small food processor that would only really work to process the strawberries (and maybe blend in a bit of flour + salt and baking powder). Do you think I could combine the rest in a bowl with a spatula or spoon (and some elbow grease) and achieve the same consistency? Alternatively I could figure out a way to combine things in batches...

A bit of a failure but would have been better if: nuts were chopped a little more, the dough needs to be mixed well after the nuts are add.  I would use a stand mixer next time.  I would also cut down on the macadamia nits and add more pistachios.

These are unreal. It’s a great balance of flavors and not overly sweet. These took a decent amount of work and time between prepping the nuts, sifting & freezing, but throwing everything into the food processor was easy. It’s just sad that you can’t double the recipe because of the use of the food processor (I have a 14cup food processor). I’ll make these for myself/family, but it wouldn’t work well for a party or a situation where you’d need many, unless you have a lot of time. They really look like mortadella! Definitely an interesting recipe! Will keep this one on my list.
Blending up the freeze dried strawberries in a spice grinder was more effective than the food processor. We also added a small amount of white chocolate to great effect.
Utilized this recipe to recreate a beloved Vietnamese deli meat called nem chua.

These are great! They look amazing, just like slices of mortadella, and are very tasty. It's fun to find recipes that use freeze dried fruit in such a fashion and that don't rely on food coloring (my other favorite trick is to powderize freeze dried fruit in a blender and then add heavy cream to make a fridge-stable fruity whipped cream).

I made these exactly as specified in the recipe and they turned out great!  Looked like mortadella and tasted delicious. Thank goodness for Trader Joe’s - I was able to find almond paste, all the nuts, and freeze dried strawberries too.

Followed the recipe to a T (except for subbing granulated sugar for sanding sugar on the outside), and they turned out GREAT. I added a touch of food colouring and they turned out the exact colour of Mortadella.

These turned out great with outstanding strawberry-almond flavor, but I found the recipe to be a little overly fussy at times. Sifting the strawberry flour mixture was fine without, and keeping the seeds in gave it some darker specks that make it look more like meat. Roasted macadamias and pistachios were perfectly fine, and regular granulated sugar was also perfectly fine instead of sanding sugar. These small changes made it much easier and more affordable to source in my area

I made this recipe today. I swapped white chocolate for the macadamia nuts just bc I don't like macadamias. I also multiplied the recipe by 1.75 to use up all 7oz of my almond paste. It rolled up into a nice log and I got 30 fat cookies out of it. They look JUST like meat but smell like strawberry jam and almond. I will probably make these again before Christmas
This was such a fun cookie to make! The results were exactly what I wanted and they tasted AMAZING; nutty and fruity. Yummmmmm.

Loved these cookies - flavor and look were both 5 out of 5 for me. The flavor was a delicate combo of almond and strawberry and I think it will add the perfect variation to a "charcuterie cookie board" that I'll be gifting my friends and family.  

I thought the video accompaniment of this recipe was worth watching - particularly the part about how to get the logs tightly packed.

This is the third recipe I’ve made from cookie week and my fave so far. They come together easily and taste so delicious. 
One note is that the recipe states it make 24 cookies. If you cut the 12” log into 1/4” pieces, that would make 48 cookies. Did anyone else notice this? Just because the thickness of cookies will affect the baking time.
Cackled at the color while in log form — it really looked like slimy meat product — and marveled at the final flavor. These are, in fact, truly delicious!

The recipe is well written and easy to follow, and these cookies are a hoot. Very fun to make and serve. The texture is great but the flavor is maybe not the best, in my opinion. They are worth it for how funny and cute they look though.

I made these yesterday and today! For the bit, they're great. They look so much like mortadella. Hilarious.

I think the flavor is a little off though. I wish there was some lemon zest or something else in the dough to perk it up a bit more.

These are fun , tasty and they do end up looking exactly like Mortadella!  Freaked my husband out.  I live at 6000 ft so I needed to add more egg whites than stated in the recipe.  I used the King Arthur Flour cookie molds and they worked fantastically.  The cookies keep well and I cannot wait to give them out on my cookies trays to our neighbors.

UP here in the wilds of Vermont with a 7 mile trip to the IGA so I used a few hacks.  Used dried cherries in lieu of strawberries, unsalted peanuts instead of  mac nuts and turbinado sugar on the outside.  Probably darker than a mortadella but really looks like a sausage for sure.  Mine didn’t roll out in a perfect cylinder but few good sausages look that way anyway.  This was a really fun recipe.  Tastes great and impressive presentation.  Thanks.
A fail in our opinion. Very time-consuming and expensive. They look like mortadella, but the taste is just okay. Will not be part of next year's Christmas cookie bake.

Claire, I took your suggestion & made a Cookie Charcuterie Board! With your delicious Mortadella Cookies as a starting point, I have a bowl of  Samantha's Coconut Snowballs as the mini mozzarellas, the base of Eric's Chocolate Mint Shortbread for cheese, Julia Moskin's Chocolate Kolbasa and added a striped molasses cookie for bacon.  A dipping dish of chocolate covered almonds for olives & some candy fruit slices from a local shop complete the look.  Thanks to you and The Cookie Week Team!!

I really had a fun time making these sausage cookies. However fun the effect, the drawbacks of tasting bad and being very very expensive to make overshadowed the delight. they were fun to make, and I also laughed a lot about how bad they tasted. A cookie for rich children.
Made them exactly as written and they were a HUGE hit. The video was super helpful.

I have made these twice. Excellent! The first was with freeze dried strawberries, and the second with cherries. Only changes were unsalted roasted pistachios (couldn’t find raw) and Turbinado sugar (didn’t want to go back to the store for sanding sugar.). The first batch was given away in gift cookie boxes featuring other Cookie Week cookies, and I have good intentions for another round of gift boxes…but I may end up eating them all myself. 

This was an easy recipe to put together. I used my Vitamix to really pulverize the fruit so sifting didn’t yield much bits to weed out.

Fantastic cookies!! The video was very helpful. Think it’s time for to replace the blade in my food processor, it left more pieces of strawberries than I’d like. My dough also was a bit drier than it should have been. Maybe I’ll weigh the flour and sugar next time. 
Love these cookies!! Delicious and really fun to make.

I did not care for the flavor of these. I love the appearance and the idea, but the flavor was just really strange to me. I don't think I like almond flavor as much as I thought I would. I am thinking of adjusting the idea to a regular slice-n-bake shortbread recipe. Great inspiration!

This get the Maida Heatter Award for the recipe with the most finicky directions…but when you follow them to a tee, the result looks just like the picture and cannot easily be distinguished from a piece of charcuterie. I was honestly amazed as were those I fed them to. The flavor is, as one other commenter aptly mentioned, “a gourmet pop tart” - very strawberry, very buttery, a little vanilla, a little nutty, but not too much contributed by the almond paste except for chewiness. Great recipe.

if you're on the fence, make these cookies! they're fun to make (especially if you watch Claire's video for tips), fun to serve, and fun to eat! the taste is very almond-forward, but the strawberry is noticeable and very nice. the texture is very toothsome. be bold, make a mortadella cookie today!

I’m excited to make these cookies! I only have a small food processor at home. It will work to process the freeze dried strawberries (I’ve done this before!) but I’m not sure it’s big enough and powerful enough for the whole dough to come together. Could the dough be made in a stand mixer? If so, any advice on speed and timing? I’m thinking the standard cookie technique - cream butter etc. for a few minutes on medium until light and fluffy, then mix on low when adding flour, but I’m not sure if almond paste will affect this.

Hi! I wouldn't put the almond paste in the mixer cause then it will get "smushed" in.  You may want to use your food processor to cut up the almond paste into small chunks and then add them to the dough, like a mix-in, but just very carefully, so you don't lose it's chunky texture that makes it appear correctly in the sliced cookies

I think Harvey is mistaken below -- Step 3 says the almond paste should indeed be mixed in thoroughly.  IMO you chould be fine to do it in a stand mixer, but using a standard cookie/creaming technique might result in incorporating too much air.  Rather, be sure you're starting with room temp butter and almond paste, and blend together on a lower speed?   I feel like this should work fine in the absence of a full-sized food processor.
Anyone try making these cookies gluten free?
@Tessa I’m making them GF tonight. Using Bobs 1-1 flour and GF almond paste. I’ll report back to the group after baking!

@Tessa I made this with Bobs 1-1 flour and GF almond paste. I chilled the dough overnight (at least 12 hrs), sliced and baked for 19 minutes and they turned out great. Key is to let them cool and harden a bit on cookie sheet before transferring to wire rack. I skipped the freezer chill given they had been in fridge for so long - no issue with firmness during slicing.

Since these are a dry, shortbread-esque cookie, do folks think they would last long enough to be a part of a cookie tin that gets shipped across the country? Or would the strawberries and almond paste change that? I am always looking for new additions to my holiday cookie gifting!
Is the almond paste sweetened or unsweetened?
@modifications : almond paste is traditionally sweetened, DIY or store bought.
How far in advance can you make these for ultimate taste/texture? How thick are these sliced?
The recipe says to slice 1/4 inch, but if you watch the video, Claire seems to slice them around 1/3 inch thick (she gets 32 cookies from the batch).

I would LOVE to make these for our cookie exchange - it would be so fun. But, are there replacements for the almond paste? We have one person in our group who loathes almond paste (but not actual almonds). Thanks!

If the person who loathes almond paste doesn't mind almonds, it seems likely that their dislike is probably a textural issue, rather than a flavor issue. Since the almond paste is fully incorporated into this dough, not left in chunks, I wouldn't expect any issues. It would be akin to using a recipe that includes almond flour. You could ask them, though, and see what they say!
Has anyone tried subbing the macadamias with almonds? Wondering how.over powering that might be.

How much regular table salt? The kind our mother's successfully used for decades. The kind recommended by King Arthur (the flour people) for baking because it is more consistent and is incorporated into baked goods more readily.

@PRodger
Half the amount of Diamond Crystal kosher salt.
I'm in the UK - is almond paste the same as marzipan?

@HanRachGorms
Yes, the same and you can make it yourself which takes about 5 minutes. Many recipes online. I used a recipe from Daringgourmet minus the rose water which I could not find. Much cheaper and you’ll have marzipan leftovers !!
For those who are gluten free, store-bought almond paste (marzipan) often contains gluten. Has anyone tried these cookies with a GF flour substitute?

@Jennifer, almond paste is actually very easy to make yourself, and a lot cheaper than buying it premade. It's just ground almonds, powdered sugar, and egg white whizzed up in a food processor, so if you have the tools and ingredients for this cookie recipe, you're already 90% of the way there. I use King Arthur's recipe. Made it in the food processor before proceeding with the rest of the cookie recipe, and didn't even have any extra dishes to wash.

There are a lot of brands of dehydrated berries, some available in powder form, some a bit on the gummy side and impossible to pulverize in a blender or food processor.  Some are made in China and therefore, sadly, suspect.  Can you please recommend some safe brands to use?
@Susanj what's wrong with products from china?? and btw 'freeze dried' ones shouldn't be gummy at all so maybe you're looking at the wrong stuff, Susan
@Susanj you need freeze dried, not dehydrated strawberries. And these are always available at Trader Joe’s if you’re close to one. Costco often has them too.

@Susanj to add to the other comment, I used the freeze dried strawberries and they turned out great, making a fine powder without issue. One tip - try measuring by weight instead of volume for these, since the cup volume and oz measurement don’t really match with the gram weight provided on the ingredient list.  It took just over a bag of the Target ones for me

I'm in the middle of making these, but the recipe calls for chilled butter. I think this isn't right. But I don't bake much. In the food processor the sugar and almond paste don't really blend with the cold butter. I'm waiting for everything to get to room temperature so I can try again.

I made my own almond paste using 1/3 of  a King Arthur’s Almond flour recipe.  The fruity flavor is a great contrast to sugary sweet treats or chocolate.  Gorgeous stacked as a gift and colorful on a plate. I sliced my macadamia nuts and pistachios thin so cutting the log was slow but simple to keep the shape. Creative and delicious.

I made these and took them to my bookclub last night. One woman took a bite expecting mortadella! They were a hit. I subbed raspberries for strawberries (couldn’t find the strawberries) and it all seemed fine. I found them generally too sweet - might make again without the fruit, add more flour and red food coloring instead. I also used regular sugar on the outside. They are a fun party trick!

These look novel and are super fun. They are a bit fiddly to make shaping, chilling, coating in sugar, freezing etc. I love Claire Saffitz, but I think this recipe is style over substance. The strawberry flavour was faint, while the almond extract was overpowering. Would not make again.
A bit too strawberry for me, but overall delicious - and yes it does look like a stack of mortadella slices!!

I really liked the tart strawberry flavor that cut through the sweet almond paste and they looked ridiculous which was fun but I found them annoying to make so I don’t think the effort was worth it. Plus its a fairly expensive cookie once you source all the specialty ingredients.
@Tom S.update. These were a big hit at our Xmas party so maybe they’re worth the effort after all

I was excited to make these since seeing the recipe pop up! My final thoughts are that they are delicious and were fun to make. The flavor reminds me of my mom’s almond thumbprint cookies with jam, but honestly these are easier to make than those! Excited to bring these to work and show my coworkers lol

Conceptually these are fun, taste great. The dough resulting from the recipe as written is incredibly sandy and doesn’t come together without additional moisture. Usually Claire’s recipes work to spec but this is a rare miss.
@Ro I'm not sure the recipe was the problem.
@Holmestead Using spoons for some things is perfectly in the norm, especially for small amounts. My scale does not register 1/8 tsp baking powder, so a weight measure would be a non-starter.
Tastes delicious - almonds with a fun strawberry kick. Mine did not look nearly as bright as the photo though after baking!

I made these, and the dough crumbled. I ended up adding two eggs and the dough came together,  chilled, added nuts, did the cylinder,  froze them, cut them, and they look like mortadella, taste great,  and are not too dry. Where do I go wrong?

Oops-- totally forgot the powdered sugar and it's even more tart and almondy. I'll make it next time with it but these were a total hit! Told my kids we were having sausage for dessert and they fell for it until they took a bite. Really fun. Thank you!

I made these for our holiday party and they were a hit! The look and taste were crowd pleasers. 

I would recommend adding some red food coloring - the cookies in the cooking app photo look so cute and bright. Without food coloring these come out looking very “fleshy” and honestly looking more like mortadella. But also a little disgusting looking. 

But hey we didn’t have any left after the party so they were still delicious!!! Also totally great without the sugar on the outside if you need to cut down.

I think the sandy/crumbly dough effect (see other comments) may happen if you use the two cups of freeze- dried strawberries (as I did) vs weighing it in ounces. Once ground, was definitely too much flour mix relative to the liquid, but I added a single egg and it came together. Next time I’ll weigh it by ounces. The cookies were delicious and pretty in the end.

Beautiful, delicious cookies- perfect amount of sweetness. The directions are quite long. One short cut: after rolling the dough into a log, wrap as directed and place in freezer for an hour or two. Let it sit at room temp until  it can be sliced without struggle. Skip additional step of placing on cutting board and freezing (step 7).

Made these using somewhat old dehydrated strawberries so they didn't pulverize and become powder, so needed to sift. Although looking like mortadella turns out to be a bit of a turnoff to the friends who ate them, we all agreed that they're really tasty. I made my logs thinner so wouldn't stand up in a fridge, but still came out pretty round.
The dough crumbled on cutting … either I did something wrong or the instructions are incomplete.

Made exactly as written and they were lovely.  A welcome new flavor on my cookie tray.  they were a little crumbly when cutting with a regular knife, but then I switched to a ceramic knife and the cuts were much cleaner. I only had roasted unsalted pistachios so I used those, but I think a little bit brighter green color would’ve been achieved if I could have used raw pistachios as written
Great cookies the taste reminds of the strawberry filling in a pop tart.

Hmm. I followed the recipe exactly, weighing ingredients. The dough is very dry, hardly forming. Even after overnight in the fridge. It cut easy enough (I used a serrated knife) but after baking 16 minutes in 300f oven they are starting to brown on the edges but are squishy in the center. I might try again with a whole egg. Because they look great. Am I the only one? Did I miss something?
Mustve missed something...my dough was very moist..i would not use a whole egg here, the yolk will make your dough browner, less pink.add more white, it anything.

My cookies are cooling as we speak, ready for cookie exchange at work tomorrow. The strawberry flavor really POPS and adds a delicious tart element to an otherwise creamy and buttery shortbread. The nuts are perfect addition and the look of mortadella is just the icing on the cake. So excited to show these off tomorrow!!

made this the other day and the log is currently sitting in my freezer until wednesday, which is when i'll bake all of my christmas cookies. i'm from new zealand and almond paste isn't available here so i had to make it from scratch, using king arthur's recipe. 

the dough turned out a bit crumbly but after kneading it for a while it finally came together. 
i also cut out a couple pieces to try. they surprisingly taste really good. not too sweet or too tart but i found it really difficult to slice the dough without it crumbling apart. after baking the colour became a little bit dull but i don't really mind

very anxious to slice the rest of it up though. the ingredients are quite pricey here in new zealand and i dont want them to go to waste after all that effort. i hope they make it into my cookie boxes🤞🏼
Dear Claire - these were delicious and fun!

I've made this twice with the same outcome, the dough did not form a ball. The dough is more like sand. It can be compressed into a log but it truly is a pain to do. There is not enough liquid with one Tablespoon of egg white. The end result was a hit with the illusion of something more like salami than mortadella.
My log is very greasy.  Not sure if I did something wrong. Haven’t baked them yet.  I’m keeping them in fridge overnight.

I don't understand how she used the bench scraper and didn't rip the parchment. Anywho.... These were ok.  Interesting to put a strawberry cookie in the mix but I don't think I'd making these again unless I was gonna do a whole charcuterie board of cookies.
They so look just like mortadella! The strawberry aftertaste makes it great with champagne. Cheers to Claire! 🥂

Followed the instructions to a T (except my log wasn't a perfect cylinder) and the came out pretty perfect. Huge hit at a holiday party (I'm a vegetarian, added to the joke). The flavor is not excellent. I think the fact that the nuts aren't roasted doesn't really help, but also it's just lacking something extra. Looks amazing. Tastes OK.

I really love these! They are so fun and not too difficult to make! I love that they really do look like mortadella.

I accidentally made these with rancid macadamia nuts the first time so remade them using slivered almonds, which I actually think worked better. They are more to scale. And the second time I upped the almond extract to 1/2 tsp just to amp up the almond a tiny bit.

LOVE!

Mine do not look as pretty as these ones, mine look like week dry old deli meat. I think I should have blitzed the strawberries in the blender or a spice grinder to get more of them finer to make it through the fine mesh sieve. This is an expensive cookie to make, I wonder if blanched peanuts would work as well as macadamia nuts? 
The cookie does taste good but I don't think I'd made them again, and if I did, I'd be adding some pink food colouring.
I just made these with gluten free flour and they kneaded up and baked beautifully! They are delicious. I did bake them a bit over but the nuts toasted up and they are amazing with a cup of tea!
Did anyone end up with “wet bottoms”?  I ended up with the correct yield, so not too thick, but bottoms were soft and dark - not golden - after recommended baking time.
@MMwe baked ours for maybe 10ish extra minutes. I can’t remember the number ex.

I would really love a version written for a stand mixer instead of a food processor, and with the measurements for strawberry dust already pulverized. I don’t own a scale, so I was trying to eyeball what 1.5 oz of strawberries would be, and my dough came out pretty dry. I’m guessing I have too much strawberry flour.

Dear aduggan: I am a home baker and really recommend that you to invest in a baking scale.  Some cost as little as $8.00.  It saves finding the correct, clean measuring cup every time. American recipes are finally using weights as well as volumes (European recipes have been so for decades).  Truly, you'll be glad you did.  Happy Baking!
cute and delicious. The strawberry adds a nice flavor

I made this recipe as written, but the fragrance gave me a play-doh sense memory.  Maybe my almond paste was off?  Anyway, this time I subbed cream cheese and I found that the strawberry taste became more dominant.  Next time I will reduce the quantity of cream cheese since the additional moisture made it harder to roll beautifully.  I love this recipe and I hope to make it every year.  Btw, Trader Joe’s had affordable strawberries, pistachios, and macas.

This recipe was fun and timely as I was eager to clear out the freeze-dried strawberries and german almond paste randomly sitting in our pantry all year. I subbed dried coconut strips for the macadamias due to an allergy and the finished cookies look great. Bright strawberry flavor even though I used 20% less than the amount called for (1.2oz bag of freeze-dried fruit; didn’t sift out the seeds). The directions and log-forming tips were very helpful and I will be repeating the process in a few days  after picking up more dried strawberries and almond paste.
These are absolutely delicious!

I'm a form follows function type person. I followed the directions to the letter and they came out looking great, they just did not taste very good, I found them to be gritty and kind of muddy tasting, I don't think the strawberry/almond paste combination works. Perhaps another kind of paste, coconut?

They were a hit though on looks alone.

A fun surprise on the cookie plate this year! Followed the recipe exactly and turned out spectacularly. Using a glass to have the cookie log stand upright in the fridge was a brilliant tip for keeping the cylindrical shape. Flavor was unexpected and well balanced. Getting ingredients from Trader Joe's kept things affordable for this fun delight!

I’ve made these twice now and I think they’re a new classic. Everyone commented that they did look quite a bit like meat, but the flavor is fantastic. Nutty, rich, sweet but not overwhelmingly so. I had an issue in the first batch with some of them cracking in half, but the second time came out much better!

Fun to make, came out as pictured, good flavor and texture. I also used gluten free flour, which worked well, essentially no difference. Color was great with only the strawberries, no food color needed. Unfortunately, I think the illusion was too affective, because they went untouched on every cookie plate, even after it was explained that there was no mortadella in them, just strawberry and nuts. Very successful recipe I will probably not make again.

This were a bit of a miss for us, especially for all the steps involved. Far too complicated for what we ended up with. I followed the recipe exactly. The nuts feel incongruous with the strawberry flavor. We all agreed they'd be better with no nuts, and not as a Christmas cookie. The flavor was fine, just not worth the effort.

Had a blast making these! For my vegans, I substituted the butter for miyoko’s unsalted butter and the egg white for aquafaba (the liquid from canned chickpeas). Otherwise followed the directions exactly. Freeze dried strawberries added enough color, no need for food coloring. Fun illusion and add variety of color and flavor to any cookie board.

This recipe made me cackle with delight. I served these cookies to one of my besties, who is vegetarian. :-)

Be warned, the strawberry flavor is POWERFUL, and it makes the cookies fairly tart. So if you don’t love a tart strawberry cookie, these might not be for you. I guess you could serve them with a side of “Mayo” (vanilla icing). Heh. 

Note: My sis made them first and said hers didn’t hold together well when cutting, so I put my dough roll in the freezer for a few days, then let it thaw just a bit in the fridge. Seemed to work well. Use a sharp knife to get a clean cut through those nuts for full effect.
TJs has the strawberries which can be spendy. Not everyone thought the cookie was appetizing but they got a kick and it tasted great
Silly and fun and delicious! Terrific pink color and strawberry flavor. At Christmas,I brought some to our Italian deli and Sicilian hairdressers. Such a hit!
Weirdly, I think these cookies tasted the best on the third day. I had the most fun baking them though. Maybe the funnest baking project ive ever done.

Turned out great! Made these for a Christmas/New Year party, plated them separately from the rest of the holiday cookies. Folks thought they were sliced deli meat and kind of ignored them. My sister-in-law asked what kind of charcuterie it was and was totally surprised when I told her they were "illusion" cookies.  Once guests found that out they disappeared pretty quick, even though my sister commented "I'm not gonna lie, as cookies they look nasty."  What can I say, she's not a mortadella fan.

These were so much fun & yummy. They are going to be an annual addition to my Christmas cookie gift basket routine. I had been in Trader Joe’s and saw dried strawberries, right before I read the recipe, so I had to rush back and purchase some. This recipe doesn’t make a lot of cookies, so purchase twice as many ingredients & make 2 batches.
It was a tasty cookie and they certainly got a lot of attention but given the expense of the ingredients I don't think I'll be making it again.
The illusion gag is fun but the texture of these cookies is tough and leathery. I would not make them again, not even for the gag, especially considering the expensive special ingredients.

I made these into heart shaped cookies for Valentines Day by dividing the dough into three parts and rolling two into logs and the final section into sort of a square and squishing them together a bit. Made a few minor changes: had marzipan instead of almond paste so use less powdered sugar; and used a mixture of strawberries and raspberries because they were sold in 1 oz packages. Very tasty, fun, and cute!

These were delicious and not as difficult as one would be led to believe, reading the comments. They came out beautifully, though I used the whole egg white and a T more butter because it was initially too dry. My friends devoured them and thought they were so interesting. My favorite part was the crunch from the macadamias, I wish the pistachios were equally crunchy and less chewy. Lots of fun to make. Watch the video!

It made my heart sing just to look at them. However, I was not a fan of the strawberry-almond flavor; I like strawberries but not in cookies. Texturally they are of the soft and chewy variety—again not my favorite. I did find storing them in the refrigerator gave them a crunchier texture I liked better. The sanding sugar coating is critical to the success of the cookie so do not skip that step.

stats.Cook Time: About 2 hours, plus 1 ½ hours’ chilling and cooling

comments.commentBody: These cookies were rather underwhelming considering the effort it took to make them.
Mine looked good until I baked them. Unfortunately they spread out during bake and lost all of their shape 😕
@Coleman yes, mine were very stumpy! I also had problems with the dough cracking—might be the dryness of my house right now.

Discard the scraps?
No. No. NO.
Bake them and devour.
could I use just regular cocoa podwer instead of cocoa nibs?or chocolate?
@me it’s as much for texture as anything.  Powder wouldn’t be textural and chocolate would just melt when baked.  Check out the video
@me you could probably sub finely chopped nuts more than either of those.
@me I subbed 1/4 Dutch cocoa powder for the nibs, and I thought it tasted great. The powder also colored the Demerara sugar a mottled brown so it looked like tree bark.
Claire, can the baked cookies be frozen? Seems like that should work

@Catherine - at the end of Claire’s video of her making these cookies she says they can be frozen. Do it after you cut the cookies and before baking. Freeze them on a cookie sheet, then you can carefully transfer them to some other container. Bake on demand!
