@Arika I have never frozen pre-made cookies before and baked them later, but I feel that for this recipe it would be useful (lots of steps!). Should I then use the standard baking time as indicated in the recipe for the frozen cookies? Thanks in advance for any tips!

These cookies taste wonderful. The dark chocolate comes through without being too sweet. The dough, filling, and topping come together quickly and easily. Steps 3/5/6 took the most time. It was a fiddly project to roll out the dough two times to precise measurements. As stated in the recipe, the cookies did not spread and deflated a little. My layers were thinner and less puffy than pictured. Perhaps I over-rolled the dough in trying to get to 15x6. Nice cookie, but a lot of work.
@Ann how did you achieve that they don’t spread? Mine spread a lot 🫣 should I bake them frozen, bake shorter?

These cookies were very pretty and tasted good, but not great. I felt the chocolate cookie filling and dough just blended together so it tasted like a perfectly fine chocolate cookie, but it was a lot of work for a perfectly fine chocolate cookie.

As others have commented, these cookies are a lot of work, but they look nice on the cookie plate. If I made them again, I would skip the double rolling to precise measurements and the other fiddly steps. I'd imprecisely roll the dough out once, use a biscuit cutter for simple rounds, spread on the topping, sprinkle with the coating, and bake.
I agree. This is a good solution.  Recipe too complicated for us home cooks.
If you don't have a food processor can you use a mini chopper for the cacao nibs step and your regular mixer for the dough?

@NG I used a mortar & pestle to grind the nibs with the sugar and salt and it worked well. I think the point is to get them to more manageable sized pieces, so I don’t see why a mini chopper wouldn’t work.

For the rest of the dough, I used my mixer and it turned out great :) for the butter part, I rubbed the butter as small as I could into the dough with my fingers, then transferred to a mixer and mixed at medium with a paddle attachment for about a minute.
LOL. That sounds like more than 5 minutes of prep time!
One quibble with recipe instructions: discard cut-off dough scraps? Sacrilege! Don't you enjoy raw dough as a treat for the baker? Can't wait to make these.
@Barbara B. There are several serious health risks to eating raw flour!  Bake the loosely piled scraps and eat while no one is looking :)

The only problem I had with the recipe was rolling out the logs to the 6” x 15” rectangles.  The dough warmed up very quickly, even with the window open to try to keep the temperature of my kitchen down.  I wound up putting each rectangle on a separate sheet tray on my porch.  Also, the filling was way too liquid, so I put it in the refrigerator until it firmed up.  Last, I have a side-by-side freezer, too narrow for a sheet tray, so cut each log in half and put three halves on a quarter sheet tray in my freezer.

This is a nice idea but for me, the flavor of the finished cookie doesn’t justify the amount of time they take. I used high quality cocoa powder, semisweet chocolate and cocoa nibs but felt the depth of flavor was still lacking. I also had a hard time getting the powdered sugar on just the top part of the cookie so the Yule log effect wasn’t as obvious. For this many steps/time investment I think I’d remake the Neapolitan cookies from last year’s cookie week. But I loved the cocoa nibs on top.

I don’t own a food processor, so for step 2, I blitzed my dry ingredients in a blender. Chopped up the butter and added that to the blender for a few pulses. Then i transferred that mixture to my stand mixer bowl & broke up the larger chunks of butter with a potato masher. Then added the sour cream mixture & used a dough hook until it all came together! A couple more steps if you’re without a food processor, but honestly worked just fine!

Genuinely irritated about these. They take a long, long time. They’re fussy. Now I am too. The video says the cocoa powder doesn’t matter. But it very much does if you want any hope of possibly seeing the spirals - the very POINT of the cookies. God these took so long. Waiting for the filling to get to the right texture took so long for the first log, it completely warmed up. Good luck rolling then. Everything is waiting. Do a thing, wait. Roll them perfectly btw and make sure you place them EXACTLY as in the video. If not, those ones WILL spread and completely wreck your tedious treat. Awesome concept. Wish these landed. Trying hard to recover from this.
This cookies are beautiful a real showstopper for the cookie plate, and super delicious I think Claire just nailed it with this cookies

I made these and they spread so much and didn’t keep their shape at all (they are basically flat round cookies now). I made sure that the dough didn’t get too warm while handling/shaping/cutting. Any ideas what i could try to prevent the spreading?
@Sarah mine also spread when baked from frozen. I was so disappointed

@Sarah
Same here. A little confused how they’re supposed to rise, I checked the recipe several times for baking soda to make sure I wasn’t going crazy.

If I were still competing in the baking competition at County Fair, I might consider making these.  Otherwise, at 76 years old, it would take me to the end of my lifespan to make these.  Too much potchkying!
I really wanted to love these but the result was mediocre for the effort involved.  Not sure I’ll invest the time to make these again.

Don't be fooled out of the oven, these are super delicious the longer they sit in your cookie jar, the more delicious they become. Please either freeze the dough solid before rolling out to 15x6 in or just roll out to 12x4, because, per other users' advice, when you roll into a spiral, it stretches way too much. Also, I had some filling leakage during the bake. Still, super yummy and impressive looking

Rugelah.  That’s all these are in a slightly different iteration.  They have nothing in common with a classic Buche de Noel other than the name.  They are neither innovative nor exceptional.  C’mon - you can find better recipes than this for this special cookie section.

@Toddie So she says IN THE INTRODUCTION that they are inspired by rugelah and sliced to resemble the Yule log. What’s the point of complaining about the name and the recipe when you’ve not even made the recipe?

I made this in spite of the unenthusiastic reviews vis a vis the flavor. I added cinnamon to the filling and a pinch of esplette pepper (cuz I'm fancy) to give the filling a teeny tiny punch. I loved spending the day making this cookie.
At what point can the dough be “chilled” for 2 days? Frozen? Refrigerated? In what? This is a long, timely recipe, and I would like to prep most of it, then bake them with the other NYT recipes!

@Ann from Sonoma
I made the doughs for these, the Lemon-Turmeric Crinkles, the Ginger Cheesecake, and the Old-Fashioned Shortbread yesterday, then baked them all today. No problem holding the dough in the refrigerator overnight, and I doubt another day would have hurt. Nice bunch of cookies!

These take a while to make. About 4 hours total. For that investment I don’t think the flavor is punchy enough. Maybe almond extract or cherry is needed. Also don’t keep well. Get dried out right away. Kids love them though

omg. Three hours of my life I won’t get back. Not sure what happened. Followed the instructions and watched the video, not once but twice, and they looked great going into the oven but immediately deflated into chocolate puddles. I’m so sad.
I did not like these cookies. They were not attractive,   not very tasty and a lot of work. I've made a real Buche de Noel and I think it less work and much more satisfying.

I always feel badly saying these things. I did not like these at all. They took a very long time to make. I was excited because I knew they would not be sweet, but they also had very little flavor. I think they look fine, but not amazing. You can tell a lot of work goes into them, but the payoff is not remotely worth the work.
Anyone else have the cookies spread?   A lot?

Clever & delicious. Instructions a little off. Try this: roll to 12x4 or so right after making dough. Wrap flat & chill. 2-step thing was nuts; 2nd roll was a nightmare. Pinch log seam tightly after filling/rolling. Place cut logs flat side down on cookie sheet with the diagonal cut facing out like a little slide. Or just cut into rounds. And (at least for me & my oven) check for doneness at 16 mins & bake from frozen. Skip powdered sugar. Cute enough! Make sure everything's cold at every step.

@ClaireSaffitz - I have yet to find demerara sugar in the grocery store and I live in a large urban area.  What should I use instead? Turbinado?  I'm asking for all your recipes...because you always call for that particular type of sugar.
@JoJoNYC easily ordered on Amazon.  I got it there, and was worth having.
@JoJoNYC sugar in the raw has large granules

@JoJoNYC I get turbinado cane sugar! Fair Trade from Trader Joe’s. If you don’t have a Trader Joe’s then Florida Crystals from the regular grocery store. Turbinado technically has 1% more natural molasses than demerara but I can’t tell the difference.

If I picked this cookie off of a dessert buffet at a holiday party, after taking one bite I would wrap the rest in a napkin and discretely discard it. They were bland and not worth the calories or the effort.

I have tried this recipe 2 times with the same result. I would rate myself as a good to excellent baker and was a head/lead baker and food photography stylist. The centers are runny. The cookie does not retain its shape and melts, (did she miss the baking powder or soda)?  I recognize that a test kitchen and photography enhances everything however this was a real miss for such EXPENSIVE ingredients and time consuming assembly.

I had the same result--if I made them again I might try baking from frozen to see if they hold their shape, but I think in future I will go back to me trusty double chocolate rugelach. Might use this filling, though.
Same as everyone else here. The cookies flattened out a LOT when baked. They tasted fun, but aren't worth the effort.
I am dying to bake these, but don't want to seek out cocoa nips; what is the best substitute?
@tREXgirl Trader Joe’s has cacao nibs! I have also found them in the “organic” aisle at my regular grocery store.
I found cocoa nips at Target and at Whole Foods .  Target was much less expensive!
@tREXgirl I might try hazelnuts?  I think the flavor would be as good or better.
I am a decent baker, I did not like how these turned out and I had been hoping to use them for a cookie swap. Additionally they don’t taste that nice - on to another recipe.

Those cookies were a little underwhelming for how labour intensive they were to make. The demerara sugar and cocoa nibs do add a nice crunch though, and give the cookies a bark-like look--but the overall flavour wasn't chocolatey enough for me. They would certainly stand out in a cookie box, because they do look great.

These were a lot of work. However, despite my humid home, the dough rolled out easily which is half the battle.  They baked perfectly. Very pretty,  I cut the logs to yield 48 cookies instead of 36.  If you cut to yield 36, the cookies are large.  Delicious!  I used dark chocolate in the filling. Next time may go for semisweet as the chocolate flavor is intense!
Not sure, what I did wrong:  they were not supposed to spread but mine did and they look more like a cow dung than Buche de Noel.   I'm so bummed

These were... fine. A little on the dry side. A ton of work to make. Very aesthetic but nothing special in terms of flavor. I will not be making them next year as the effort-to-reward ratio is too low.
I tried, I really tried, to make these exactly as the video and recipe instructed, but they were a failure, sad to say. They didn't look good, and they didn't taste very good either.

Sure, there are lots of steps however you can easily spread out the workload by making the dough on the first day and the filling/assembly on the second. Great flavor, texture and look—will definitely make again!

@Alex I am curious if yours looked like her photos? Mine didn’t keep the log shape when baked but flattened somewhat. I thought perhaps the dough needed to be cooler before baking so froze somewhat of the sliced dough roles and tried again baking this morning and they still flattened somewhat. I also think her coco was lighter in colour than mine. Once baked there was no distinction between the dough colour and that of the filling. They do taste nice so we will enjoy them.  I usually do well at having my baked items turn out like the chef’s photos, but sadly for me these didn’t this time. I’m determined to make them again to get them right, so I’m just wondering what things you might have done to get yours to look right. Thanks!

CC - mine didn't flatten too much (in my opinion), though I do agree the photo does show a more round shape than what I achieved. I also baked some straight through as well as some from frozen and got the same results. For me, the 'log' effect was still achieved. Regarding the distinction between filling and dough - I was able to achieve a nice distinction. Possibly your issue was in the use of a darker cocoa in the dough or lighter chocolate in the filling?

Mine spread - a lot, as others have mentioned. I even increased the chilling time to try to reduce the chance of that happening. But they taste wonderful! They may not get a starring role in my cookie line-up, but they were worth the effort since i feel like I upped my skills in creating spiral cookies.

Rolling out (step 5) tip #2:
Roll it a little smaller than required: I found it started stretching while I was moving it to baking sheets. Or chill it before handling. 
Because my logs got a little too long and wide while rolling out, I had barely enough filling to go around. I'd suggest making a little more, just to make sure; maybe 1 1/2 times the amount?
Everything else went according to plan. They look a treat.  (2/2)

I made this recipe using the weight measures provided. It makes a tender cookie with a brownie-like chocolate flavor. I had to use my small chopper for the sugar/nibs and my larger, 13-cup food processor for the dough, so I ended up with more dishes. (The sugar/nibs were just spinning without chopping in the larger food processor) All in all, I wouldn’t go through the trouble of making these again. There are easier ways to get a similar chocolate flavor on a cookie tray.
I agree with Liz, either I messed up completely ( I didn't), or these are an absolute pile. Geez Louise, it took me 6 hours and caused me gray hairs (I'm 17), and was it worth it, tbh not really.
can this be done using gluten free flour?

@Sharon yes! I followed recipe and just used Bob’s Red Mill 1-to-1 and they turned out great! They do depress a bit when baking but they still look cute and taste so good. Might be able to reshape a bit when they’re fresh out of oven, but I didn’t try.

Great cookies. I should have chilled more so they slumped. I also didn’t read the instructions closely and I put the egg white in the mixture instead of the yolk. I don’t think you can tell. 

Note to myself to read more carefully.

Delicious and showstopping cookies, and such fun to make. In order to achieve a tender cookie texture while still baking them through, I found that 15 minutes in the oven was more than enough (and that was from frozen)! I pulled them when they were puffed but not totally firm, and they finished baking as they cooled on the baking sheet. I also dusted half of the cookies with powdered sugar (like the photo), and they look fabulous displayed all together. I plan to make these again and again!

I really wanted to love these cookies but they are a disappointment. The visual is the best part but the flavour just isn’t there. It’s rather bland and the texture isn’t great. I wish the insides were softer compared to the hard sugary outside. They are a lot of work for the final result. By comparison Christmas crack takes 30 minutes and tastes x10 better. Rolling out the dimensions is too fussy, I let the dough warm up before rolling which helped. Trying to roll it cold was a nightmare.
I love these! Super tasty and beautiful. They are definitely a project, but very fun :)
I'd make these again. They did slump (and a few cracked on top as they slumped). Next time, I'd put them in the freezer after cutting for 10-20 minutes to help with this.
Absolute fail. Followed directions to the letter-- and I'm not an inexperienced baker. But these were a disaster.

I really enjoyed making these cookies partly because I've never made rolled cookies before and they came out looking beautiful. The instructions were great and I loved the video in particular. 

The texture of the cookies was a lovely. We used mini semisweet chocolate chips and turbinado sugar for the coating that worked great.

But they were not quite sweet enough for me. I'm going to sweeten them up - maybe milk chocolate in the filling?

The texture and flavor of these cookies didn’t “wow” me when they came out of the oven. But, after two days of being stored in an air-tight container, the “rested” result has made my opinion do a 180. They became softer and the flavor deepened to something like a cake-y brownie. For a special occasion, the time and work invested paid off in the end.

Found cacao nibs in the produce section at Target, and substituted Sugar in the Raw for the Demerara sugar. My food processor is too little for this recipe and my electric blender did nothing. So, I used a pastry blender to manually start breaking down the butter, and transferred the mixture in small batches to finish in my little processor to make sure the butter was finely worked. Transferred all a second time to my mixer to bring the dough together.

Mixed results; will try again. 1) Don't make in single day. Dough needs overnight rest in fridge. Otherwise too soft & broke when rolled. 2) Filling was same color as dough and needed extra t flour at least--hard to spread without softening dough despite being cool. 3) Heed advice to chill or freeze once rolled & after cutting. 4) Coating hardly perceptible. Turbinado sugar instead? 5) Bake shorter time.
Yes to turbinado sugar, it's what we used and the coating was a plus.
@Eliz. I also used turbinado sugar and agree on shorter baking time.  So hard to tell when a chocolate cookie is done!

This did not do it for us. Followed the recipe as written, but found the overall flavor underwhelming for what was quite a lot of work. If you closed your eyes you’d think you were eating a mediocre brownie.

I disagree wholeheartedly with the reviewer who said these were underwhelming. I thought they were absolutely delicious! The only issue I had is that the filling was way too loose even when it had cooled down to room temperature. I had to double the flour and powdered sugar in it and chill it a bit and then it was perfect.

I am a baker and wanted to try something challenging.  And I found it. While I had fun making these, because of the challange, amd they were delish - I will not make these again.  It literally took way to long, the logs no matter how long I chilled them, they were still too soft to roll into the topping, I had so much filling left over that I didnt use the egg wash, I used the filling as the 'glue' for the topping amd sprinkled the topping over each roll.  
These are best eaten with tea or coffee as they were drier than anticipated.  
great to make on a snowy weekend.
Why discard the scraps?  Can’t they be baked as their own sort of cookie?  Just because they aren’t a pretty pinwheel it doesn’t mean they can’t be enjoyed, right/
My fav Buche de Noel recipe has a coffee filling and frosting on a hazelnut flavored cake so I will be trying to sub at least the coffee filling into this recipe.
If I wanted to flavor the filling with extract, rind or freeze dried fruit powder, how would you go about it and any ideas for quantity of addition?

Agree with others — these were good, but given the time and effort I expected a better flavor and look. Based on the description as an easier alternative to the fussy Yule log cake, I thought this recipe would be easier. This is an ambitious recipe.   Is this really easier than a Yule cake? I had trouble knowing when they were done (how firm is firm?) and overbaked them. Happily I had frozen the other 2/3 of the recipe so I hope those come out better.

these were nice, but not worth the effort and didn’t even end up looking as nice as the example (think my cocoa powder was too dark, so didn’t get good definition between the dough and filling). bit of a disappointment overall
@Leo mine too! I used a special dark cocoa from King Arthur.

I think I followed the recipe precisely, but mine flattened a bit, unlike Claire’s that sit fairly tall. I chilled/froze as instructed. They took more time, in-and-out of the fridge, than I like to spend on one batch of cookies. Very deep chocolate flavor, but I likely won’t make them again.

I ended up with fairly flat cookies. The swirl isn’t terribly defined but I assume that varies with your chocolate/cocoa powder so it is what it is. The texture was nice (crisp outside, tender inside) but the flavor is lacking for the effort required. I’d normally chalk it up to my execution/ingredients/equipment but it seems like a lot of commenters had similar experiences…a rare miss from Claire maybe.

Beautiful cookies and a very meh taste… venturing so far as to say cardboard flavor. 
With the NON discarded scraps we made an experiment using rum soaked cherries and pulverized vanilla wafers… those didn’t look amazing but tasted great! I would absolutely make this recipe again but would create a filling that would make the fairly basic dough sing while still capturing the beauty of the swirl.

I’m sort of blown away by the negative comments. Compared to flooding sugar cookies with royal icing these were a breeze. I used good butter and chocolate and got a very chocolaty pleasant and dramatic looking cookie. I think the next time I do this I might try white chocolate for the filling with some dehydrated fruit dust stirred in. Or maybe I’ll skip rolling the log in nibs and frost the baked cookies with buttercream and THEN sprinkle on the nibs. Now that would be decadent. This is why I love Claire’s baking—she always gets me creating.

I agree with another review, These cookies were rather underwhelming considering the effort it took to make them, and they were OK not amazing, I loved the filling, but the dough was a little dry, maybe next time I will add more filling.
These are a delicious cookie and a fun project bake. Took them for an office party & they were a hit

The 15 inch logs broke (a bit difficult to handle)-I would make  12 inch max. I also made mine bite size. I had a lot of filling left over (I should have measured out the amount she mentioned) and a lot of topping left over too. They were good (reminds me of the flavor of a family recipe we usually make) but not sure worth the time. Just a note on the video-the way she cuts them at 45 degree angle-I just turned them on their side and sliced diagonally-felt safer way to cut them.

I agree with what others have said: 
- a lot of fuss for a tasty but unexceptional cooking
- my dough was darker than the one in the photo, which meant minimal contrast for the spiral
They were fun to make once, but won't become a regular Christmas cookie.

The title of the recipe is great! The cookie, not so much. The pastry falls short of tasting anything like chocolate with only 2 tablespoons of cocoa powder, I added 3 and still not much chocolate taste. The filling also was flat tasting even using very good quality chocolate. I had to add a bit more confectioners sugar for this confection to come together. It also needed a pinch of salt. All in all, it was not worth the effort. Nutella filling would have had more taste.

Very time consuming.  I had trouble with the filling as written so I added chopped toasted hazelnuts which tasted great.  On the day the cookies were baked they tasted good.  Two days later they tasted delicious.  They really taste better with age.  Nice to try for something different.

Yes, these are very fiddly. I made the dough and chilled overnight. I also had to refrigerate the filling to get it thick enough. After the last freeze, I cut them and then froze them. I’ve been baking them off, a dozen at time. Are they ridiculously chocolate-y? No. Are they delicate with a fun crunch? Yes. I thought they might be too bland, so I’ve just gobbled up three to make sure they are not.

I was jazzed to make these for a party. Started the steps a few days ahead and did everything to the letter. They look nothing like the photo so will buy something for tonight's party. Since I can't eat chocolate and am single, the mishapen chocolate cookies will go to the neighbors. For all the effort, I wish I had just made cookies for the neighbor kids to decorate instead.

This recipe was very complicated and very mediocre in the end. It did not have a very complex flavor profile. Filling leaked out as it was cooking and the cookies slumped to basically flat. I followed the recipe exactly.  Very disappointing for the New York Times cooking.

These looked great going into the oven. However, the filling melted out and slid the dough apart. I just had lumps in chocolate puddles. Reread the recipe and am quite certain I didn’t miss an ingredient or step.

My first iteration resulted in doughy spirals peeking up through a sea of chocolate like icebergs. Freezing longer and standing the spirals upright to seal one end helped but I ultimately resorted to dipping the sticky spirals in powdered sugar to help seal the filling in.  Later I found generations of bouche de Noel mishaps in the refrigerator/freezer, sad reminders that I still long to salvage.

I have made these cookies twice now, the first using the weight measurements and the second using cups like Claire does in the video. While weight is typically more accurate, I found it to be the opposite for this recipe. The weighed dough was much too dry and needed additional liquid to come together. The only way this dough came together properly like Claire's was to use cup measurements exactly like she did. 

The flavor of these is top notch though and a showstopper for any cookie party!

I needed to make these dairy free so my chocolate loving mom can have them. I subbed miyokos vegan butter. I also used Einkorn flour and in the dough used coconut sugar instead of powdered. The dough was a little challenging to roll out but was successful. The chocolate filling was a little too runny at first and squished out a bit when I rolled the first log but had cooled down and was the perfect consistency by the second log, then was a little too hard / cold on the final log and made the dough break a little. I suspect the miyokos containing coconut oil made it a little less forgiving temperature wise. All in all was successful and I have the logs chilling now. Substantial quality control testing along the way suggests the final result will taste great!
The filling leaked out. Used a silat rather than parchment-can't believe that would cause a problem. I agree with many reviews...way to much work for what is basically a chocolate rugelach.
These cookies are time consuming.  Not worth the effort.  The filling seemed to disappear into the cookie so the definition wasn’t there.  Taste just ok,

Okay- these looks AMAZING.  My oven gets fixed this week and my kid comes home from college and we are going to make these!!!!  That said, I don't understand why we should hold the knife at a forty-five degree angle, sliding towards our hand, when we can turn the perfectly vertical knife 45 degrees to the cookie sticks, slice strait to the cutting surface and get exactly the same result.  That's all.  I am soooooooooo looking forward to these!  Thanks for the recipe and video!
I found the cacao nibs at Target, hidden in a cubby under the fresh bananas.

Watched the video twice, followed the instructions, and they turned out WONDERFUL. So stinking cute. And to Claire's comments - the dough is a joy to work with... easy, forgiving, fun. I used to tackle buche du noels and the one's that I have baked were *way* more work than these. Hot tips: cut into 8 sections instead of 6.... makes 48 smaller cookies. I froze before baking, 25 min was perf. Thank you Claire! Haters gonna hate. I love this recipe. These little cuties are here to stay.

I have these formed and frozen.

Should we defrost them before baking? Should the oven temperature or baking time be adjusted?
I didn't. Straight from frozen and into oven. Perfect in 15 mins. But maybe because my cookies were smaller?
Hey Beth!  I baked them completely frozen.  350 degrees, 25 minutes.  Turned out exactly as pictured and they have a nice crunch but a soft interior to them.
you could always experiment with baking just a few from frozen and seeing how they turn out. I ususally chill my rugelach dough for 24 hours so i imagine a slight thaw would be a good idea...
A lot of fuss for less than spectacular results.

Just finished these today. I read some comments after starting them so I was wondering how mine would turn out. I’m a very experienced baker.  I did made them in 3 days. Chilling the dough overnight each stage.  They look beautiful!   Love the not too sweet fudgey flavor and texture!   I would not throw out the dough when trimming!!! I trimmed and refilled the scraps into the strips.  I will make them next year thank you.  I have a Russian Tea cake chocolate cookie that’s very similar with nuts in the dough. Extra work but worth it.   The video is helpful to be watched before starting especially rolling filled log and cutting *****

@Denise Missall what coco powder did you use and how did you keep them from flattening?
I pride myself as usually having things I bake look like the photos. These were not the case. When baked, mine had no discernible colour difference between the dough and the filling. Also they lost the log roll shaped and flattened out a tad losing the log look. Would love to bake again to get it right. Enjoyed the flavour.
For me, these were just alright, not my faves, and I felt like the payoff wasn’t worth the effort put in. They do look pretty though, so were nice in the tin I gifted.
Add a tsp or 2 of cinnamon to the dough!! It really extends the flavor

I really wanted this to be an exceptionally yummy cookie with all of the labor involved but....it was quite disappointing. It's dry and not particularly flavorful. I make about a dozen different cookies every Christmas and I'm afraid this one will not make the list for future years.

I agree with the many who found these cookies underwhelming for the effort involved, even though I don't generally mind multip steps. I found them to be neither sweet nor chocolate-y enough. I took them to my office Christmas party and heard not a whit about them being really good. The other cookie week Christmas cookie that I made, Eric's lemon and turmeric crackle cookies, were better but also not lemon-y enough. I feel like a Scrooge dissing Christmas cookies, but this recipe will be unsaved.
Okay, this is mostly on me for not pulling up the photos to reference, but slicing at a different 45° angle than intended will produce festive chocolate vulv*s

A lot of work for what amounts to brownie batter rolled up in chocolate shortbread, and it seems like you’ll undercook one if you cook the other correctly. A fun little diversion but I wish the end result were worth the effort

After all that time and effort mine deflatted into puddles  while baking...so disappointed. I was even going to make little marzipan mushrooms to put on them, use slivered almonds stuck inside and poking out to look like polypores (shelf fungi), and pieces of curled shaved chocolate to look like branches. Turns out, none of that happened and they all went in the trash...so sad about it

Ugh. These cookies. The flavour was fine, I did like them better once they softened up a bit. But the actual building of the cookie was quite a process.

I followed the instructions and the video to a T and my first cookies melted into sad v*gin*s. Then for the other two logs I cut them the next day, not end to end but with a slight angle in the middle. Also cooked from frozen and for 15 minutes. These held their shapes a bit better. 

I think overall I was thinking these would be a texture closer to a buche/sponge. I’m glad I tried them but probably would wait for the recipe to be tweaked more.
Good, but not the fantastic I would expect from the amount of work.

I am delighted with these pretty cookies. They were a fair bit of work but worth it. We relished the rich and subtle chocolatey flavor and textural variety from the cookie/filling interplay. They are not too sweet, a welcome change from many Christmas cookies. Mine baked straight from the freezer for 25 minutes at 350°, and they subtly slumped but maintained their shapes, as in the video. These have already been lovely for entertaining and gifting, and with a cup of tea by the fire. They are on my list to make again next year.

I’m wondering what I did wrong. Used premium ingredients (Plugra butter, King Arthur flour), and measured them out but my dough cracked during rolling as if there was too much flour. Then when I baked the cookies, they flattened out more than the photos and video as if there was too much butter. Also, my filling never firmed up (possibly the cause of the flatter cookie). Really wanted to love this cookie. Since I’ve got a good amount of leftover coating, I might try it once more.

After cutting the cookies, arrange them on the tray and RETURN them to the freezer for a 10 minutes, and then bake; they can slump to unattractive flatness if baked straight away. Learned that the hard way. Delicious nonetheless.

Loved these cookies. Yes they're a bit of effort to make, but very fun for a holiday activity and I brought them to a party where everyone loved them. I really like that they aren't very sweet, the chocolate really shines.

These cookies are not as complicated as some commenters are making them out to be! They are delicious and totally worth the extra bit of effort, but honestly I thought the dough came together fairly quickly and the rolling/cutting took barely any time at all. Yea, they need to be refrigerated and frozen at different steps but a lot of great cookie recipes require the same. Highly recommend!

very tasty and nice texture.  time consuming, but steps interspersed with the other 4 cookies going that day!  Was concerned about flavor per other comments, so added a few tsp of coffee grounds for insurance
These are delicious and attractive. They are a little fussy and time consuming,  but they are worth the effort.
This cookie recipe is so much more complicated, and takes a lot longer, than making a Yule log.

Post baking notes: 1) Have plenty of space in the fridge for re-chilling dough during shaping, because it will go limp and make you sad. 2) I used a darker cocoa and flavor was good (I might add a small bit of beet root powder next time for color. 4) I used Neilson-Massey Orange extract in the filling -it was terrific! 5)The well chilled cookies flattened in the oven, a little more than I really liked- How do I fix this? 6) Lots of sugar left over.    I will make them again.  Very tasty!

This is a delicious, sophisticated cookie, perfect for a special occasion.  Numerous steps, but none difficult, none fussy.  Dough is easy to handle and it’s a straightforward process.  Well worth it, IMO.

I make cookies twice a year. These were not difficult to make. I followed directions and they did not spread.
Like others, I found them OK at best. Though they did look pretty. next time I would try making a more distinctive filling I’m thinking Nutella

My most challenging bake yet! I agree that rolling out once would make more sense. From step 5 on I did one log at a time. I got lost in the paragraph-long steps so my first filling was just butter and chocolate; I later fixed but can't taste the difference. First two batches bubbled from either too much filling or too much egg white, so I decreased both for third batch. 18-min bake was perfect; longer led to dry cookies. Hope these improve after a few days -- they look pretty but taste ehh.
These cookies were quite a lot of work for a middling cookie. Yes, they look nice but the taste is underwhelming. Maybe more salt would help?
Cookies are a pain to make, and just average chocolate cookies. Not very complex.

Made these exactly as described and it’s a ton of work. They look pretty enough but I didn’t get a noticeable contrast between the dough and the chocolate filling. I also found the dough wasn’t as forgiving as described in the video and found the cookies to be a bit dry. At the end of the day, I don’t think that these are worth the effort.

I liked this cookies flavor but it was very one note, it just tasted like chocolate. I would recommend adding a half teaspoon more vanilla extract take out the cocoa and add in a few pinches of flour, then continue on the preparation, include the Coco nips on top. It makes it look nicer and changes the flavor.
Once you have made the cookies and are now ready to freeze the unbaked cookies, do you have to defrost them to bake them? Or can they go from freezer to oven?
I usually go from freezer to oven, but you should have them on a cookie sheet as your oven is preheating, and you might need to add a couple of minutes.
What oven temperature?
@Julie M. It’s in step 7 if you read the full sentence!!

To everyone asking if they can sub chocolate or cocoa powder for nibs, negative ghost rider if you want it to turn out the way it did. Cocoa nibs are unsweetened and hard. Chocolate would be too sweet and the wrong texture. The same with powder. Even if you use unsweetened, you will be missing out on texture.
Cacao nibs can be sweetened or unsweetened. The recipe should indicate which is used.

These cookies look cute, but it does surprise me that, while Saffitz uses a scale for portioning the dough, she doesn't use it to measure the ingredients. 

I was happy that the printed recipe does specify grams as well as the (less accurate) volume measurements.
My cookies spread while baking and don’t even resemble the final picture, just look like regular shape cookie. They taste absolutely delicious though! Someone has any ideas how to prevent spreading?

@Mateja I have absolutely no authority to respond, as I had a similar problem haha. But I only baked 4 cookies last night, put the rest of the dough in the freezer.  Maybe if all are baked at once, side by side, the rise would be better? I know that method can give biscuits more rise, may help with a butter filled cookie?

When I made the filling, something went wrong :/ Everything looked great as I took the butter & chocolate off the steaming water. Waited a minute, added flour and powdered sugar = still great. Then added egg yolk and the mixture immediately started getting chunky/ separating. Now I have a chunky mixture with liquid on top that refused to be incorporated. Where did I go wrong? If anyone has any clues, I‘d be happy to hear them :)

It sounds like the mixture was still a bit too hot and you ended up cooking the egg yolk, resulting in a chocolate scrambled egg. Perhaps that instruction to wait one minute needs to be a bit longer. Ms. Saffitz might consider updating the recipe to include a specific temperature that the mixture should cool to before adding the egg yolk.

Update! :)
I added pistacchio cream (from a jar) instead because I messed up the filling and it is so so good! Added gold shimmer to the topping and they are very cute despite being super wonky :)
@Larissa It sounds as if it curdled. The chocolate likely was still too hot.

These cookies were not one of the best things I’ve ever eaten, but were in the solid good category. The appeal here is the cute yule log appearance which did take a lot of effort but got lots of “oohs” when presenting. I also found it difficult to figure when the cookies baked enough but the ganache filling was still fudgey.

1. Totally doable without a food processor I used a blender for chopping the nibs and blended the flour and butter with the whisk on my KitchenAid mixer.
2 be careful you didn't overprocess the nibs so they look cute. 
3. I didn't chill the dough was many times as she did in the instructions and they turned out great!
4. The dough is very stiff when cold. If you're worried about it being too sticky, use the plastic wrap over the top while rolling. I put a silicon baking mat underneath

Note you cannot use high quality cocoa like Valrhona because there is not contrast between the dark cocoa and the chocolate filling. Also I followed the instructions precisely and my cookies almost all slumped. They were really unappetizing. Out of 36 I salvaged only 20.
These are wonderful, and will make a lovely addition to a cookie array.
I enjoyed making and baking these cookies. I watched the video before I made them which was very helpful. Perfect for Christmas - fun to give away and to eat!!
Prep time 5 minutes?

I wanted to love these, and didn't mind all the steps because they were easy and there was plenty of rest time in between. But am I the only one for whom the dough matched the color of the filling even after baking? My cookies looked nothing like the photo; the spirals were not apparent and the concept totally lost on my cookie swap friends. The cookies also spread way too much and lost their shape. Wonderful idea -- but really doesn't work if the dough matches the filling...

When I baked, some of the filling leaked out. It’s not catastrophic, but the spirals don’t look as neat - and I hate to see the filling wasted on the baking sheet! I froze individually as suggested and then baked from frozen. Any ideas about where I went wrong and how to avoid filling leakage with the next batch?

I am not as deft or speedy a roller as Claire, so it was a little trickier than it seemed, though not insurmountable. This may help others:
Step 5: The dough, out of the fridge, is very hard to roll and then, suddenly, very soft & began rolling out in a decidedly non-rectangular fashion. It made it hard to get the measurement correct. I ended up using a small tortilla rolling pin to tame it back into shape (Maybe shoulda let it chill a little before handling).  (1/3)

Recipe took four hours and took two people to construct and roll the cookies. The filling melted out when they were baked but they still tasted good but looked like tiny chocolate croissants. Next time would chill the dough and filling overnight and hopefully the filling wouldn’t melt out next time.

Having the video to which to refer was most helpful. I felt that I may have over-baked my first batch so I’ll be more careful with the second. I was happy that the cookies pretty much looked like the photo. My biggest struggle was finding cacao nibs.

@Stephanie S I didn’t have cocoa nibs ( thought I did) so just skipped it. Used dark and milk  chocolate jimmies ( de Ruijter ) , sanding sugar and turbinado sugar in a mix. I threw it all in the food processor. It was crunchy and had elements of cocoa and crunch.

Made exactly as written but added chilling time to the rolled out dough and the filling which did not stiffen up after an hour. It took 22 minutes in a very preheated oven. 25 burned the cacao nibs. It took about 5 hours start to finish. I didn’t like the powdered sugar on the few I covered so left it off, the Demerara was sweet enough. You MUST salt the coating well to have a balanced and complex cookie. I thought it was delicious and worth the effort. It tasted better the next day.

I’d make it again with riffs like add orange extract or roasted chopped hazelnuts to the filling, maybe switch to a darker chocolate and up the salt… lots of fun ways to play with the flavor profile.

These cookies were amazing. I used dutch-processed cocoa powder and still had perfectly defined spirals, so I'm unsure what happened to other commenters' cookies where the dough matched the color of the filling. I added 1/2 tsp of cinnamon and 1/4 tsp of cayenne to the filling to give it a Mexican hot cocoa flavor -- delicious. I don't own a food processor, so I used a spice grinder to break down the cacao nibs & Demerara (I used turbinado) sugar, and mixed the dough by hand. Worked perfectly.
Has anyone tried making micro meringue mushrooms to top these? Wouldn’t that be the coolest? Would definitely take these to the next level in terms of Bouche de Noel concept.
the best!
Mine spread - a lot of work for a big fail

Claire, your video made all the difference! I watched it at least three times and then jumped in. Lots of steps, but manageable with a bit of patience. Took them to a cookie swap and won "best looking cookie," so there's that. Love that they are not too sweet, but crumbly and chocolatey.
I failed on these! Took some much time to only have them drop and look a mess. So disappointed
I love cookie week. It’s worth the subscription price alone!

These were undeniably time-consuming. However, we found them both charming and delicious in equal measure. I would like to try a mint chocolate version, pondering different ways to make that happen: in the filling or in the cookie? Crushed mint candy or mint extract?

I saw a few notes about the taste not living up to the amount of work these are so I added extra vanilla and some almond extract to the filling. They came out delicious and are beautiful, taste very similar to Alison Roman's chocolate shortbread cookie which I love. For those saying they are putzy, yes, this recipe is for folks who like fiddly pretty little high-effort cookies! They're perfect for cookie boxes that need some visually interesting new ideas!

As others have noted, an incredible amount of work for a very pretty but only average tasting cookie. Not too sweet which is nice and very cocoa-forward but a little dry. (Maybe don’t heap your cocoa tbsp’s like me.) I did a double batch, one gluten-free and one regular. I am allergic to gluten and didn’t get to taste the regular so that could be part of my issue both on the effort and taste. But it did work well with gluten-free flour. Also Claire makes everything look easy! Overall I did have fun and do love the look of them but probably won’t make again. A festive, charming and creative recipe though.

The filling takes a remarkably long time to come down to the correct consistency - at least an hour and a half in my experience. If you chill the dough overnight beforehand please account for that when going to bake the next day.
While these are a bit labor intensive, the look, deep chocolate flavor, and the texture make it a really satisfying cookie.

I followed one commenter’s suggestion and used Nutella for the filling.  I thought the finished product was excellent!   This was undeniably time consuming, but I was so happy with the finished product that I’ll probably make them again next year.  My sister was also a big fan.
Do you thaw frozen unbaked cookies before baking them?
I can’t find (or afford) coconibs anywhere so I’m going to attempt to substitute them for coconut and hope they look like snowy logs 🤞🏼
What if you don't have a food processor?

Warning! Don't do as I did and substitute milk chocolate for the dark chocolate because your husband is a philistine and doesn't like dark. My cookies looked perfect going in and came out looking like sad, slumply lumps.

I used ingredients as specified and consider the taste of the cookies, as reported by others, to be just ok. There are much better Christmas cookies more worthy of your time and effort. (And there must have been some serious food-styling to get the cute ones for the photo.)

A 5 for visual appeal but just OK on flavor. Perhaps if I make again I will flavor the filling...espresso powder? mint extract? Also, I have a TON of the cocoa nib and sugar coating left. Anyone have suggestions for using it?

My 13 year old daughter wanted us to make this together for Christmas. 30 min in to this 4 hour adventure, she deserted me and so I was solo and cursing her name. We didn’t have the nibs so I substituted pecans. In the end the cookies were actually delicious and the whole family loved them. The pecans weren’t as pretty but still pretty good and tasted great.

I went through most of the steps and just took a test batch out of the oven. I found them a little dry- not sure why. Also the chocolate flavor was underwhelming. The nibs don't seem to contribute much overall. I used a mini chopper to make them less demanding. It was a lot of work but its a nice idea. Don't think I will do them again. Will cut them up and freeze. Bake on demand. I may streak some frosting, both semi sweet and white threads on them to zing 'em up a bit.

Yes, they’re fussy with several steps but I don’t consider myself much of a decorator and these were a showstopper the very first time I made them! Once you make these, it’s just slice and bake! They are so lovely. I really enjoyed this recipe! I played it a little fast and loose and didn’t trim the rectangles or measure the filling and am still very pleased with the results.
Best holiday cookie ever... Thank you a million times for the video!

I wanted to like this but it was a lot of effort and the cookies turned out very bland. I was suspicious of how much butter was in the recipe and indeed, the filling oozed out and the rugelach slumped during baking. I baked it from chilled but not frozen, maybe that would have been better. The filling needs to be tweaked to have a more impactful flavor and to be less buttery. Maybe a cinnamon roll filling but make it chocolatey. Making that change would compensate for the bland cookie.

Once again I neglected to read the comments before I got started. I had made the dough and filling and then started reading, so I got pretty worried. But I kept going. Chilled the logs (before coating) overnight, coated them, froze them for 45 minutes or more and then cut and baked. They slumped a bit and my seams show on many, but they are good enough to include in boxes for neighbors. Nice cocoa flavors. But I won’t add to my list of favorites.

The cookies are very tasty but honestly not good enough or attractive enough for the amount of work involved.  They also spread a bit in the over losing a bit of their shape. Maybe they should have been chilled again after the cutting.

Reading the notes, I want to point out that we are told these aren't deeply chocolatey as far as the dough is concerned.  But if you want it more so- try a different cocoa.  I make a very dark icebox (do we still call 'em that?) cookie, mixing both Hersey's Dark and a lighter one of quality.  For these, I might sub a tablespoon of dark for a tablespoon of flour.  Another thing that could make these pop is to use Orange extract in the  filling, instead of vanilla (or maybe rum, mint,  ginger...)

I do not understand how the filling, as seen in the photo, is a light colored chocolate against the dark chocolate dough.  The filling and dough in my cookies are both the same color.  No definition is seen. I used the coco powder and semisweet chocolate as directed.
My first batch wasn’t very pretty. My second was fine but only existed bc I’d already doubled it for a cookie swap. I regret choosing this bland fiddly recipe.
Has anyone ‘hacked’ this recipe to doctor the dough enough to fix the spreading issue? I added some cherry jam to my filling and they’re delicious, but look like someone sat in them… any ideas??
can the dough be frozen ? and for how long ?
Making these now.  My cookie dough is darker.  Would you be willing to share the products you used?

I just finished making these. Yes, they took a really long time & were quite involved. I did it over 2 days. I know some found the flavor underwhelming, but I find them nicely chocolatey. I used Dutch process cocoa powder & a 70% dark chocolate that I really like eating for the filling (added a good pinch of salt to the filling). Some did leak a little filling, but not too badly. I'd make them again but not as part of a cookie assortment because of the effort involved. They look cute!

@KS did yours look like her photos? With mine, the dough and the filling when baked were so similar in colour there was no discernible difference between the two. Also, mine flattened somewhat when baked to her timeframe. Curious what you did differently if anything. I’m wondering if I should have used a coco powder lighter in appearance to lighten the dough??

I modified the filling in this by adding roasted hazelnuts I puréed. They are slightly dry so if I made them again, I would add some moisture to the filling to compensate for the addition of hazelnuts. The flavour with the addition of hazelnuts is quite nice. Perhaps not necessary but based on other's reviews, I thought would help to level these up.

I am so excited to bake these! I mostly make shortbread variations at Christmas time bc you can do so many things with them. These chocolate cookies will be a great balance. Just love Claire Saffitz and how well she explains things -- she's inspiring, as are all your cookie folks.

Suchhhh a hassel! These cookies became an all day project! The filling also oozed out while in the oven - not sure if that was just me. But, I think they will look nice on a Christmas treat tray or in a cookie tin.

@CC My dough was definitely darker than the photos due to the Dutch process cocoa. I feel like you can still see the spiral, though. Baked same day, they did slump a bit as the recipe indicates but held their overall shape. I just baked some from frozen, and they did not slump as much. Also, it seems like the tighter you roll them, the less they slump.

I followed this recipe to a T and tightly rolled the cookies but, in the oven they completely fell apart and turned into mush on the tray.  The dough also kept breaking when I was rolling it out and when transferring the rolls, they broke apart. I wasted a ton of time and ingredients on this for nothing.

I followed this recipe as precisely as I could in all respects. Verdict: looks 2/10 (sagged and flattened in baking), flavor 4/10 (chocolate is chocolate, after all), texture 1/10 (unfortunately, very dry mouth feel). I'm sorry, because I really wanted this to be a delicious showstopper, and it was probably some failing of mine that made it not work, but I can't recommend it.

These were very labor intensive and involved three trips to the fridge for hardening up. The Times video was really helpful. I was pleased with how they came out because they are very chocolate/cocoa forward and not too sweet at all. The only part I might change is the ratio of the dusting. The sugar was ground too fine for me while the nibs still were in too large pieces. I’d maybe grind the nibs alone first next time.

To defend why I don’t think these are very good:  I’m experienced, used high quality ingredients, took my time and followed to T.  I watched the video and read the entire recipe twice then read every step again as I went. I have all the equipment. Irritated at the effort to find nibs and Demerara.  They aren’t just lightly sweet, they are practically bitter, and they are dry. A lot of steps for a very blah cookie. I’ve made several Yule logs with less work and they were much more delightful to eat.

These cookies aren’t too sweet and look really impressive (even if you didn’t do the best job with the rolling/cutting)! The one issue I had was the amount of topping this recipe had me make. Maybe I didn’t something wrong but I had sooo much left over, you could easily halve the topping and still have some leftover.

I enjoyed these cookies. That said the appearance of mine was less than ideal. Also, it was a bit of a comedy of errors in making it due to a very hot kitchen in Honolulu, HI. But I actually loved them. Good for the cookie platter at a family gathering.

These cookies were delicious and not as difficult to make as I thought they'd be. I think they were absolutely worth the effort for a large quantity of super chocolatey, pretty cookies, perfect for a party! I made one slight edit, I couldn't find cacao nibs so I rolled the logs in broken up Mexican hot chocolate tablets, which may have made a slight difference. Happy to add this recipe to my repertoire!

Excellent cookies! Recipe isn't the world's easiest, but I made them twice for gatherings this holiday season and they were a huge hit at both. I didn't trim to make perfect rectangles (waste not, want not!) and they still looked very nice.  Off cuts from ends of logs get mashed together into an ugly cookie just for me!

A three star rating because the cookie is good, not great, and the amount of work is large. If these took a normal amount of time then I'd go four star. I would suggest a more moist and interesting filling than just chocolate (something creamed and with hazelnuts perhaps).
Just okay for the amount of work.  They were very pretty, though!
Hi made them once thought they were great! One question, can I use measure to measure gluten free flour?
Mine ended up spreading. They looked great after rolling and cutting. Not sure what caused the spread. Taste was okay but like others said, underwhelming despite the amount of time these take.

These cookies were disappointing. Although the recipe photo makes them look beautiful, and I am a big fan of Claire Saffitz’s past work, these were not her best effort. They tasted dry and chocolate-chalky. At Christmas time, with other sweets available, no one ate more than one of these, and I ended up throwing most of them away. Also, in retrospect, I was mystified by the instruction to cram them all onto one baking sheet. Why? I did so, and some of them drooped together. If you make these, at least do yourself a favor and space them onto two cookie sheets.
Deliciously chocolatey flavor - they slumped too much - didn't hold their shape at all so they weren't pretty.

My favorite cookies of the holiday season. They were a little fussy, but the effort paid off. The two layers of chocolate remain deliciously distinct. I may not wait till next Christmas to bake them again.
I wanted to hate them because they are a pain to make. But they are really really great !!

This cookie does take effort, but if you like shortbread-type cookies, these are worth trying out. - Go longer on all of the chill stages.
- Make sure to sift your powdered sugar used for the filling otherwise your filling will have lumps.
- take all of your scraps (including the end pieces), smoosh into a log, eggwash, and roll in the coating . Slice and bake!

Claire, you've done it again~! The directions were perfect, and the cookies were delicious.  Loved your explanations as it really helped in the success.  And yes, I threw NOTHING away, just baked the scraps and ate them myself for a reward.  AWESOME!!!

Good tip on stocking up on baking ingredients before the holidays. I would add parchment paper, cling film, and foil to the list.

Also, I don't have room in my frig for a big cookie sheet without a lot of rearranging and tossing. But I do have plenty of room in my garage right now, which is a bit colder than my frig.

I didn’t have great results - my cookies spread by about 50-75% and the tops split, so many of them didn’t have the “log” appearance. I used a darker cocoa powder which caused them to not have the color contrast that is shown here. Taste wise they’re just ok. The cacao nibs have a sourness that I find slightly off in the context of this cookie. Fun to make though! :-)

If you follow the directions, they will not spread very much. Just be ready to devote time to these, because they need to be cold before baking. My biggest tip is to not overfill these. I used too much filling on my first "log", and they split on top. I would aim to have a bit of filling left over.

Overall, these are so adorable and tasty!

An impressive and delicious addition to the holiday cookie roster! Its pretty tight to bake them all on one sheet, so I would definitely use two, even a quarter sheet pan on the side. They freeze great once baked.

I used black cocoa for the filling, so the contrast between the dough and the filling was was very striking. I used an immersion blender to mix the filling. At first the filling kept splitting, but a quick blitz turned it into a wonderfully smooth mixture. I think this is the type of cookie you have to invest the best ingredients into. My cookies came out in perfect spirals, and kept their shape after baking. Although very labor intensive, I’d make them again. They are delicious!
the flavor of these are very underwhelming unfortunately. I will likely not make these again, not worth the effort for the finished product despite how nice they look
They came out wonderful. Along with my usual cookies, these will stand out! They take the place of brownies on our holiday assorted cookie plate.

I liked this cookies flavor but it was very one note, it just tasted like chocolate. I would recommend adding a half teaspoon more vanilla extract take out the cocoa and add in a few pinches of flour, then continue on the preparation, include the Coco nips on top. It makes it look nicer and changes the flavor.

These were good! They weren’t too difficult to make but it did take a really long time. If you are having issues with spreading I would make sure that the cut pieces are still very cold before putting them into the oven.

I saw some other comments saying that these tasted too plain, so I halved the chocolate fillings did 2 different ones:
- Peanut butter: I used about half a jar of conventional peanut butter (not the natural onea that separate over time) and added a little bit of flour and egg. I essentially made a cookie dough, but a little looser
- Raspberry preserves: Also about half a jar and some cornstarch.
The added egg/flour/cornstarch was so that the fillings would set while baking.

Another minor 2 additions: I added extra vanilla extract when it called for it and espresso powder to the dough and topping (I’m sure this didn’t make too much of a difference).

I probably wouldn’t make these again casually since it took so long but I would for more special occasions. I think a chocolate hazelnut filling would be good too!

My aunt worked in Italian pastry shop in Newark, NJ for years. To form the shells, they used wooden dowels approximately 3- inch diameter. For the filling they used Ricotta Impestata. 

The reason why people eat cannoli with soggy shells, is that have been filled and sat in refrigeration for a period of time. 
Cannolis are best eaten freshly filled.
Calandra's?  I live in AZ now, and so miss their bread, taralli and cannoli.

Organic powdered sugar (used one for one) is indeed tastier than regular powdered sugar.  I can taste the "chalky taste" mentioned.

Conventional powdered sugar usually has added cornstarch, which is an anticaking substance and often contains GMOs, and that has a chalky taste and texture. Organic cornstarch, on the other hand, uses tapioca starch

Make your own ricotta! Times Cooking has a simple, easy recipe, and its really easy to get the cheese dry enough for cannoli.  Also, the liquid drained from the cheese can be used in soup stock, and many other uses. Our dogs love "cheese water."
Question : The end of Step 8 says to slide off the molds, but subsequent steps indicate the molds are still on. Should that instruction be at the end of Step 10?
It's a mistake. The shells, with the molds on, are fried that way and are slid off after frying.

In the video, Clare says to put the used oil in the garbage if not recycling it.  That is not a good thing to do.  Used oil can certainly be added to compost or poured out in a garden, and the microbes will love it.  Of course, that's not feasible for those who live in apartments, so recyclingis their best option. But please don't put it in the garbage.

My Sicilian mother makes cannoli using a pasta machine and rolling the dough so thin that a recipe for 1 dozen shells makes over 100.  The shells are very delicate and must be filled immediately before eating, but are magical in their delicacy and better than any you’ve ever had, anywhere.

@p murray I use the lasagna attachment to my Kitchenaid and it works really well. Start at 1 and finish at 5. You get nice thin sheets. Save all your scraps, lay them on top of each other and start over at one.
@p murray, this recipe should make at least 30 shells. 1/8” is too think. It should be rolled thin enough so you can see the grain of the granite or wood upon which you are working.
@onemorebitedelara.com, whoops, 15 shells!
If you can't travel to Sicily, watch Detective Montalbano on MHz while making and eating these cannolis!
Modified the filling to be 8 oz of marscapone and 8 oz of whole milk ricotta. Also cut powdered sugar down to 1/4 cup and 1 teaspoon of honey. Delish!

To the commenter bellow with GMOs cornstarch  - GMO corn is created to resist insect pests or tolerate herbicides, it's completely safe for humans and animals,  not different from a regular selection corn. I agree that starchy taste may be a bit annoying,  but the hysteria around GMO should stop.

Thank you for your recipe,  Claire!  I will be using your recipe for the shells, but I will be filling them with homemade whipped cream.  Why, you ask? My godfather was from Palermo and owned his own "shop." He made these, but because I don't eat cheese,  he filled it with whipped cream! I miss him dearly.  When I saw your recipe,  the picture looks just like ones he used to make!

In the video Claire says to save the bottles to fill with the oil and put in the garbage. If your trash is collected by a truck with a compactor the bottle will get squeezed and the oil will come out the bottom of the truck onto your parking lot or driveway or curbside. I've seen this. I bought a product called Fryaway and used it to solidify my leftover cooking oil. It worked.

These were fabulous. A lot of work and mess but worth it. Even though Claire says to serve them the day they are made, we couldn't eat that many, so I refrigerated the filling and stored the shells in a covered container. Still delicious combined the next day.

@Ferguson, my grandmother used to store the shells in a shoebox on the 2nd floor landing of her two flat. It had an enclosed staircase so it was cold and not humid, unlike a refrigerator would be. She could store them for months during the cold season. I can store them for a few weeks in an air tight tin at room temperature.
My mom made delicious cannoli and shaped them around broomstick pieces that had been sanded and cleaned. Don't fill those babies till the last moment, or they will be soggy!!!
Good Afternoon: Bet that filling would also work well in a glass dish topped with fresh fruit.
Can these be made with gluten-free flour or almond flour?

One tip from someone who has made a couple thousand cannoli, is to buy ricotta without any fillers. Avoid any product with guar gum etc. Best quality ricotta will have three ingredients: milk, salt, vinegar.
@Bob, a few weeks in an air tight tin in a place that’s cool and dry.

@Bob,  oops, the comment about storage was meant for Vicki. 
I’ve also made thousands of cannoli and I agree with you but also, it should be very smooth whole milk Ricotta. Some whole milk ricotta are still grainy like Calabro, Sorrento and Polly-O. I like Leoni.

I also use Fryaway--it's biodegradable and works to solidify the oil.  I love the stuff. I get in on line and it's not that expensive.  I had oil spill in my garbage can once and it was a mess I will never forget!
I think I will try the cannoli with heavy cream.  Am not a fan of ricotta...

This recipe was a total bust for me. I followed it exactly and saw the video but the final result was not light and crispy, but dense. I researched other recipes and found that most other recipes use white vinegar to help get it crispy. I tried the All Recipes cannoli recipe after this one and it came out perfect. It’s too bad. Usually Claire’s recipes work great.

I use a pasta maker to knead and roll dough. If you don’t do too much you still get the bubbles. I also fold in some whipped cream into the ricotta as it lightens it up. and it’s ok to use the not fresh ricotta. You can whip it to lighten it and the whipped cream also lightens it. I use a pastry tube to fill so the filling gets all the way into the middle. I like to use my own candied lemon or grapefruit peel instead of chocolate. Chopped very fine. It is so fresh and light.

@tracy, I have to object. It is absolutely not ok to use then not so fresh ricotta. Your ricotta should taste like fresh milk. If it doesn’t make the cannoli when you can get some thats nice and fresh.

Got 15 shells using Atlas pasta maker down to a 4.  Two shells at 3 were too thick and if could get dough to hold together a 5 would have been perfect.  Shells easily come off forms but slight stick if egg seal hot on form too much. Not bad for first try:)
My aunt worked at Ferrara’s on 11th Street and Bloomfield Avenue. Back in the day there was Catherine’s Pastry Shop and Capri’s.
There is no store-bought ricotta in America worthy of cannoli (or any other ricotta use). Not hard to make your own if you have access to raw milk. Sheep and goat milk much preferred.

The problem is (as you know, as you're a chef in Italy) is that ricotta is made from whey, and it takes a huge amount of whey to make your own true ricotta. Yes, you can make a version of 'ricotta' by curdling whole milk, but it's not the same as the ricotta as I've had in Sicily.

@Max Alexander, Rome, MasterChef Italia, goat milk?  Absolutely not. You would never find that in Sicily. Fine quality sheep’s milk only. However, thats almost impossible to find in the US unless you live in New York, LA or San Francisco. The rest of us have to make do with a fine quality cows milk ricotta.  My grandmother had a secret to lighten it that did not involve mascarpone or whipping cream. 

However, that is difficult to find in the US so
THANK YOU for being specific about the amount of salt!!!
Complicated, many ingredients, low yield. Doesn't convince me to make, rather than buy

Impastata ricotta is the key to the perfect cannoli filling. Forego regular ricotta and find a market that sells impastata. You don’t need to drain it and the taste and texture is sublime. You’ll never regret it.
Do you know how much impastata ricotta to use for this recipe for 16oz regular ricotta? Or I suppose, how many oz. the ricotta drains to?
Can the shells be made in advance? If so how long can they be kept - a day or two!
Was wondering regarding the air-bubbles on the shell, could it be caused by air bubbles from egg proteins (albumin) being trapped?

I made these with King Arthur Measure for Measure gluten free flour. Everyone loved them, including non gluten free family. I filled the leftover shells with the ricotta filling, wrapped in wax paper and put into the freezer. My family loves to eat frozen cannoli (depending on the freezer, thaw a few minutes so they are not rock hard) now I can too! Thank you for the detailed recipe and video!
Is it necessary to push the ricotta through the strainer? Or is draining it enough? Looking for tips on that step!
I really want to make these however I do nnot want to use lard. It seems like Coconut may have a flavor that would not be "authentic" ...;What about butter?

These were delicious! The dough was well flavored and the fry time was perfect. I did get more like 10 molds out of the dough. The filling was delicious, but made barely enough for 6 cannoli :( next time I would probably do 16 oz of ricotta combined with 8 oz of marscapone, a rations I’ve tried and enjoyed before. Appreciated the detailed instructions!

My daughter and I made these after she brought back the cylinders from Sicily. The ricotta filling with the orange zest is to die for! Loved the taste of the shells but will need to work on the technique to get more bubbles.

@Claire, try making the dough in your food processor and pulse it until you can’t see any lard at all. It should be well distributed throughout the dough in tiny little micro specs. Then, you’ll get your little bubbles and avoid the big oil filled blisters.

I rolled these out using a pasta maker and was able to get 10 shells out of the dough and there was just enough filling to fill all 10. If you somehow push it beyond 10 you're gonna be out of filling and have a terrible day.
Super easy to make!

The filling came out very runny and it couldn’t be used. I got part-skim ricotta by mistake and drained it overnight and lots of liquid came out so I thought the recipe would work. When the rest of the filling ingredients were added, I had a bowl of delicious cannoli soup. I went back to the store, bought whole milk ricotta and followed a different recipe for the filling that did not have as much added liquid ingredients and had better results.

I used a ricotta from Whole Foods called "basket ricotta." It is thick and smooth, no need to drain or sieve. I used cold butter instead of lard in the dough. This recipe made enough dough and filling for 10 phenomenal cannoli!

Just made these pretty much as directed and they were fab! I Followed the dough recipe(with coconut oil) and a Marsala wine. Let it sit overnight with the ricotta. They fried up beautifully. For the cream I was 4oz short of ricotta so I added mascarpone. Delicious!

Great filling! I must admit I didn’t make the shells - I have a pizzelle iron that doesn’t get enough action outside of the holiday cookie season. Pizzelle rolled around the cannoli tube as soon as they come off the iron is a great alternative!
Any suggestions as to where I can buy the metal rods used to make the cannoli that IS NOT Amazon or Walmart? Other website?

These were really good and the video was very helpful. I made mini ones using a smaller cutter. I ended up with about 22.  Perfect for a party! I didn't wrap loosely like in the video and they still slid off the molds easily.

I too roast the turkey "upside down" for the 1st hour, resulting in very juicy breast meat every time! Having lost the bird to the kitchen floor once too many times, I got some insulated silicone glove mitts vs utensils to manhandle the hot turkey to the breast side up position. So much easier. Don't forget to grease the rack well before putting the bird on it. Helps prevent sticking... Tho nothing is foolproof. Happy Turkey Day!
Place a sheet of parchment paper on the sheet pan.  The turkey won't stick to it and cleaning up the pan is a breeze.

I have always had great luck roasting the turkey at a high heat for the first 25-30 minutes, basting it and then turning down the heat to 350 for the rest of the time basting often...every 20 minutes or so until done. I do the same with whole chicken. 
It is always juicy. Also before roasting, I rub the whole bird with toasted sesame oil first, then fresh garlic.....sprinkle top and underside with steak spice, powdered savoury and sage.

I make a mixture of salt, pepper, and sage (dried, crumbled), and rub it all over the turkey, inside and out.  Then I let it sit in the fridge (or on the balcony if it's not freezing) for two days.  On roasting day, bring it to room temp and then stuff (with room temp stuffing) and roast.  My main secret for a good turkey is to get one that is pasture raised.  It doesn't have to be heirloom, just not one of those cannonballs wrapped in plastic.  They cook more quickly and evenly and taste great.
Question: I have never roasted a turkey on a rimmed sheet pan, I would think the juices from the turkey would overflow the shallow pan. Apparently not?

I roasted a 12.35 lb. turkey on a rimmed sheet pan a few days ago. First I scattered chopped celery, onion and carrot on the pan then placed the bird on a wire rack on top of the veggies, per another NYT recipe. No overflow of juices at all. I found it easier to maneuver the turkey than using a deep roasting pan.

Thank you for the simple, no frills recipe. I prepared the turkey 2 days before Thanksgiving and just left it in the spare fridge. Let it sit for an hour before putting it in the oven.  It resulted in the best turkey I've ever made.

After flipping the 13 lb turkey I set the timer for an hour, checked a little early, and the breast had already reached 160 degrees. I'm still learning my oven, maybe it runs hot. The meat was still moist and absolutely delicious. I felt like a pro! I used Mortons brining salt to brine. The skin did stick somewhat when I flipped it, so I put a pat of butter on the exposed skin. Didn't baste any further.

This has been my method for many years with just a couple of adjustments that make it even easier.  I place the turkey on the roasting pan that comes with the oven (spray with cooking spray).  Start breast side up and leave it without turning.  It always comes out perfectly with plenty of room for drippings in the pan.

I loved Claire's YouTube video so I'm going to trust her and just do it! I had been thinking about spatchcocking my turkey ... some NYT guy claims you can cook a 12-lb turkey in 45 min. So I tried it out on a 6-lb chicken last weekend. Took 1.5 hours! So it's upside down cooking for me this year.

Thanks so much for this simple recipe!! I made a turkey for the first time this year and was very nervous. There are a lot of fussy and intimidating turkey recipes out there and I’m so grateful to Claire for making a simple one that just works so well. I followed every bit of advice in this recipe and now I don’t have to be worried about making a turkey ever again!

Forgot to take it out a day ahead

Gave turkey a 30 min warm bath in the sink

Put parchment paper under turkey to keep from sticking

Stuffed with onion, two lemons cut into thin triangles, crushed garlic cloves tossed with dried gourmet garden poultry herb seasoning

My first ever Thanksgiving turkey after 41 years.   High emotion going into the day, as this is usually Dad's task, along with personal high hopes for success.  This recipe did the trick with minimal fuss and muss, which was just the trick for this nervous chef. I followed it exactly, and the turkey was a hit! High fives all around and a few happy tears.  The bird was flavorful and juicy, and the leftovers have been lovely.
Should solution-injected turkeys follow the salt/pepper step?

I have used this recipe two years in a row now, and I just love it. So simple and straightforward. (I used to do an overnight wet brine, then utilize a roasting bag the day of cooking. I'll never go back.) I follow the directions exactly, and for a 10lb bird it reaches 160*F in two hours. Crip, golden skin. Moist breast meat. Perfectly seasoned. I also adoped Saffitz' suggestion to remove the wing tips from the bird and use them to make homemade stock for gravy.

My husband said this was the best turkey he had ever had. I followed the recipe and YouTube video to the letter but also greased the sheet pan. I used large turkey tongs to turn it over. It was juicy, tasty, and perfectly cooked. I made it was a Trader Joe's 12lb kosher turkey.
Has anyone actually tried this method? I love the ease but seems too good to be true and I’m afraid to risk my pricey organic turkey.
Recommendations for a turkey breast instead of the whole bird?
