This does not sound THAT low work. I simply use a cooking bag (Reynolds). It keeps the turkey very moist and speeds up the cooking time and creates a lot of liquid for gravy. And yes it browns still. I use simple seasoning in and out and no stuffing. No basting required and no flipping it over. Try it!
Will this work for a 15 pound turkey? It was the smallest I could find.
The turkey turned out okay using this method, but the breast skin tore off when flipping the bird over. Next time I’ll line the pan with parchment paper.
First time making the whole Thanksgiving dinner and it was a huge hit. Loved all the make ahead sides. Thank you!!

This was my first Thanksgiving being in charge of the turkey and I was definitely nervous. I'm so glad I trusted Claire. The turkey was super easy to prep (especially following the YouTube video), tasted delicious and not one bit dry, and paired equally wonderfully with orange cranberry sauce and white wine gravy. I accidentally got a fresh, pre-brined turkey so I skipped the salt and only seasoned with pepper and it turned out great.

It seems dangerous and unsanitary to let the bird sit out of the fridge for 4 hours before cooking it. I let mine sit out for maybe 1.5 hours. I also rubbed the turkey with dry onion soup mix instead of just salt and pepper. With those modifications I would make it again.
This method worked like a charm! Was so easy and quick (for a smaller bird). The salting overnight kept the turkey moist and flavorful. I

This was amazing and I will use this method forever, especially the half-upside-down trick.  Our bird was smaller so we mistakenly pulled it late at approx 175-180 degrees measured in the breast meat and after resting it was STILL entirely moist and delicious.

This is the fourth year (not in a row) that I've used this method. I am pretty sure I learned it first from Jacques Pepin. I prefer (if I'm organized enough) to order the bird and ask that it be cut into a split breast and hindquarters (4 pieces total, and I use the back to make stock). But when doing a whole bird, this is the way to go for sure. Works every time. But I DEFINITELY agree with the suggestion to get silicone heat-resistant gloves for the flip.
An internal temp of 150F goes against everything else I've read, which suggests 165F, has anyone gotten an under-done turkey with the 150F suggestion?

"so it can come to room temperature."  

I wish writers would stop telling us to let the meat come to "room temperature" before cooking.  If room temperature is 70 it will take many hours for the turkey to reach 70 sitting on the counter and by that time enough bacteria will have grown on it to make the whole party sick.

This was delicious.  It didn't sit as long as suggested with the dry brine and I could have taken out of the frig a little longer than 30 minutes and I should have checked a little sooner than I did, but overall -- oh, so good!

title: Cranberry Sauce With Orange and Golden Raisins Recipe
author: Claire Saffitz

comments.commentBody:
As someone that collects cranberry recipes and makes between 3 and 5 for each Thanksgiving, I think that this is good.  My wife gave it a thumbs up - which is all that really matters. 
1. I cut the sugar down from 2.3 cups to 1.3 cups (and could have cut it down a little more)
2. I added a bit of OJ so that there would be some liquid for cooking.  No,  I didn't measure - I just added enough to cover the berries in the pot.
3. I used a whole orange - peeled & cut into pretty small pieces.

Made this to change up the routine.  No one loved it.  Too sweet, and the orange peel chunks are too big.  I recommend cutting way back on the sugar and dicing the orange more finely than the recipe called for.

Here's a "no-recipe" cranberry sauce I've been making for years. Pour some orange juice in a saucepan. Add some dried cranberries and pineapple tidbits. Bring to a boil then simmer, stirring occasionally, for about an hour until the berries and pineapple have softened significantly. Add more orange juice as needed to keep it from sticking. Remove from heat when it has reached the right consistency.
A tablespoon or two of Grand Marnier is a wonderful addition to any cranberry sauce recipe.

The best cranberry relish courtesy of my mother: 1 bag of cranberries rinsed and dried, 1 naval orange skin included cut into a few pieces, 1 cup of sugar. Put all ingredients raw into a food processor, puree until there are no chunks left. Refrigerate and enjoy!
This is also my family recipe! We use less sugar now than we used to and call it cranberry chutney!

@rosemyrose

This is the traditional Ocean Spray recipe to a T. They call it a relish.  It’s so good.
Only 1 cup sugar. Zest the orange, peel it and add back 1/2 the orange peel small diced, add small diced apple, thin with orange juice as needed
Way too much sugar. Cut the sugar to one cup. Peel the orange, cut slices to small pieces. Cook over the stove top on low heat, with the rest of the ingredients as written. It’s really delicious!
I doubled this but did NOT double the sugar.   It made a nice tang that will complement my Thanksgiving sides.
Omitted the raisins, used only 1 1/2  cups of sugar, and used orange zest with the chopped up peeled orange made it great! Love the spice and apple cider vinegar taste! New favorite!!
Delicious as written.  Our new favorite!

I decided on 1 cup sugar based on all the comments. Because my cranberries came in 8oz packs I used 2, so had 25% more than the recipe. I added 1cup Maine Root Spicy Ginger Beer for liquid, also gave great flavor, plus 2 Tbls Courvoisier, well because... And omitted the ACV. For additional spice I added 1tsp Chinese 5 Spice and about 1/8 tsp ground nutmeg. Used 3/4 c raisins but would reduce even more next time. Grated the orange peel then finely diced the rest of it. Cooked 25min. Delicious!
With vinegar and all the spices is this more like a chutney than a cranberry sauce?
Claire's mother is named Sauci! I love it — and no wonder Claire became the cook she is. Happy feasting to all!
I used 1 1/4 cups of sugar, substituted orange juice for the vinegar, and cut the peeled orange into small pieces. Success.

Caveat: I'm making this to suit the toddlers and health-conscious adults at my table, so I lowered the sugar to 3 TBSP + honey. I used regular raisins instead of goldens because I didn't want the sulfur dioxide. I kept everything else the same: I used half of a large organic navel orange, chopped into small pieces (raisin-ish sized), and threw in some OJ to sub the omitted sugar. I really enjoy this combination of flavors, hence the 5 stars.

I must have at least a dozen cranberry sauce recipes ranging from raw to cooked, jellies to chutneys, & I have to say this recipe is now at the top of my favs. I followed the recipe exactly (I was going to grind the orange through my kitchen-aid, but didn't). I didn't find it overly sweet, & would say the interplay of cranberries-raisins-orange & zest lends to a depth of flavor & an interest profile that is then knocked out of the park with the addition of cinnamon & cloves. Thanks, Claire!!

New Orleans native here and I have been making my version of this sauce for decades.  My version uses only a half to 3/4 cup of dark brown sugar. I also use one LARGE  navel orange.  2/3rds of the skin provides peels that go into the mix, no pith anywhere. My facilitating liquid is the juice from this big orange, usually a navel. No further raisins or spices. It is well chilled, well chopped, but never pureed,  pure cranberry and orange joy with the brown sugar really enhancing it all.

I cut the sugar as many  mentioned and maybe could have used a bit more than just 1 c, but overall -- delicious!  My daughter had no clue about where I got the recipe and was worried because it had raisins in it, but she fell in love with it and took a bunch home with her.  She said it 'tasted like fall'.  I concur.
This recipe was exactly what my thanksgiving table needed and perfect on my leftover turkey sandwich (mixed with a little Mayo) the next day. This will become a standard feature henceforth!
I made this the past two years. My family loves it, and my SIL asked me for the recipe.
I usually keep some golden raisins soaking in rum and suggest folks try them.  Having gone that far, I also added perhaps a tablespoon finely chopped pickled ginger.  Much more complex.
Puréed the orange and peel, used just one cup dark brown sugar, substituted sweet currants for raisins, otherwise recipe as written, came out delicious!
This is really delicious. I used brown raisins and it tastes great. I actually love the orange. It softened plenty with the cook time.

This is almost exactly my mother-in-law's cranberry conserve recipe except she added twice the sugar and a cup of chopped pecans. I personally cut the sugar back to about 1.5 cups  but Southern tradition is to make it very sweet. The chopped pecans add a nice crunch.

We found this cranberry sauce recipe to be quite tasty!  I did cut the sugar to a little less that two cups per suggestions in other posts and also used the food processor to finely chop the orange and the raisins (again per other posts).  This was very easy and very good I think - super happy I can make it a few days out!  Thanks Sauci for this great family recipe!

If you like orange marmalade, you’ll probably like this! I read the comments and reduced to 1.25 cups sugar and that was plenty. Chopped the orange as finely as I could and left out the lemon zest (might add later). Yum.
Made this today, very yummy and a nice change from the standard cranberry/sugar/water mix, would cut the sugar next time--too sweet.
WAY too sweet, even after I cut the sugar by 25%. This is jam, not cranberry sauce. Use Sam Sifton's recipe instead.
This is way too sweet! I'll go back to the recipe on the back of the cranberries. It calls for just one cup of sugar.
Perfect as is!

Love it. Made the recipe exactly as is, sugar and all. I love all the elements together - tastes like a dessert. It will be a beautiful, glossy, dark red pop of color and flavor on the Thanksgiving table tomorrow!

Thanks to the helpful comments, this recipe is my NEW FAVORITE recipe. Definitely cut down on the sugar quantity (I started with 3/4 cups of sugar and then added to taste. And as someone suggested, I substituted orange juice for apple cider (just eyed it. I think it was between 1 - 1/2 cups). For my own twist, I added diced green apple and threw in some julienned orange rinds. Perfect - I'm telling you. Absolutely perfect.
Clarification? should I use the peel of the navel orange as well?
We absolutely loved this sauce. I made it according to directions but it was a bit too tart. So, I added a couple of small packets of Splenda and it was just right.
We tried this recipe and thought it was a bit too sweet as-is. We added some crushed red pepper to give a bit of heat, and it is great!

Cut the orange with the peel still on. It candies slightly and adds an excellent texture to the sauce.

All in all a huge winner for our family!

Whipped this up at the last minute, cooled to warm and served. Cut sugar down based on other reviews. Otherwise followed exactly. This was delicious and my husband who doesn’t really like cranberries asked for me to save the recipe and make again!
Loved this but will peel the oranges next time.
I really liked this but I agree with other commenters - way too much sugar! I cut to one cup and still tasted plenty sweet to me. Aside from sugar, great combo of flavours!

Excellent! I made the following changes after reading reviews- 

1. Used 1 c of sugar
2. a splash of Cointreau

We truly threw all the ingredients in a pot and simmered them.

I used this recipe for the first time after watching a 2-day NYT shoot of Claire applying her superpowers to Thanksgiving dinner, being inspired by the Saffitz Thanksgiving tradition along the way (community cookbooks FTW). It was excellent. The only variations I made were to finely chop the orange (I confess some of the skin ended up in the compost bin) and to bring my electric burner to medium-high to bring this to a vigorous simmer. Can't get enough Claire Saffitz!

do not change anything about the recipe when you make it. this was the #1 favorite dish at my family's thanksgiving this year. none of us like cranberry sauce before this year, but we are converts after making this.

This was THE dish of Thanksgiving! My family would not stop raving about it. The spices were perfection. My only fear is I will not be able to recreate it next year! Modifications:
used 20 oz cranberries (and still ran out of leftovers too soon)
1 cup sugar
This recipe is super delicious and so easy to make. I stuck to the recipe but did half golden raisins & half dried cranberries, I think that kept it from being too sweet.

Too sweet. I make mine with two bags of cranberries, two juiced oranges, one juiced pomegranate, one cup of water, a quarter cup of sugar. Pour the boiled cranberries onto most of the zest of one lemon and the oranges. Decorate with more zest and mint leaves. It's delicious!
I made this recipe as written and it was a huge hit with my family. Even my dad, who is not a cranberry sauce lover had two helpings. Today he asked me if it could be used as a pie filling.
I agree about dicing the orange into smaller chunks...but definitely will put it on the repeat menu for Thanksgiving!

Made this, but forgot to bring it to the potluck Thanksgiving. I liked it. After nibbling at it for days, I decided to try to convert it to a dessert. This recipe, https://cooking.nytimes.com/recipes/1017817-cranberry-curd-tart Has almost the same ingredients. Worked great.

I made this for Thanksgiving and loved the bright flavor, but it is more like a preserve than traditional cranberry sauce. As written, think of this as a festive condiment to be used in small amounts versus a side. It was wonderful added to oatmeal on chilly mornings. In a revised format, this recipe made the Christmas day line up as well, but as a topping for our morning French toast. This time I doubled the cranberries and kept everything else the same and it was delicious.

I made this per the recipe and diced the orange to cranberry-size pieces. The orange is the make-or-break of this recipe so use one that is nice, juicy and fresh.

I served it with roast duck, roast lamb and mixed it into my morning oatmeal. 

It worked as a filling for small Christmas pies when I ended up with more pastry than mincemeat. Perfect for a small bite. Probably too much for a large pie slice.

I will try it next in oatmeal cookies.

Cut the sugar to a cup.  Zest and juice the orange.  Add a grated tablespoon of ginger.  A clove or two and 2 bay leaves.  Have your liquid to be mostly orange juice.  Simmer , remove bay and clove.  Family favorite

Sickly sweet. I should have read the notes before I made this.  Many of which advise cutting down on the sugar.  I may cook another batch and add no sugar so that I can mix the two.  I would not use more than 1C sugar. Would also add more zest.
Made this as a double batch as explained and it was horribly sweet. Had to add two more bags of cranberries to rescue the batch. Delicious now though so cutting surgar in half I highly recommend.

Made this with one-quarter less sugar and more clementines. The apple cider vinegar gave it a 'chutney' style with a delightful depth of flavor. We had no golden raisins so I just left them out. I needed more liquid than the vinegar provided so I added some apple cider. Yum !
PEEL the orange! It wasn't appetizing to eat orange peel - not matter how finely diced they were.
I disagree. I think the chopped orange peel greatly improves the texture and flavor of what otherwise might be a fairly mundane relish.

Made this with half the sugar which gave it the right sweetness, but it came out kind of grainy from the orange rind. It tastes nice, but I think I’m going to re-make it per the suggestion to use large pieces of peel that get removed before serving.

Took the advice of commenters and reduced the sugar to a generous 1 cup; added sweetened dried cranberries because we love them. Delicious result -- will probably buy another bag of cranberries just to make more immediately! We're taking Thanksgiving to our kids on Saturday, and I'm not sure Hubs and I can be trusted not to wolf this batch right down. Yum-o!

As someone who has never liked cranberry sauce, I am now enlightened.  Even the orange peel and raisins.  Somehow it is savory as well as tart, and really cuts through the stuffing and gravy nicely. I needed every bit of the sugar though.

Couldn’t get to my recipes this year so tried this. It’s so good! Per other reviewers’ suggestions i reduced the sugar to 1 3/4c and will probably start with 1 1 /2 c next time and test and add more if needed. Delicious.

I have to agree with those who suggested 1 cup of sugar in this recipe...at that level of sweetness, it was utterly fantastic, with (for me) a perfect balance of tart to sweet. Two other suggestions: take that orange, de-seeded and carefully scrubbed, and put it right in the food processor and whirl the heck out of it. Second, dried cranberries were great in this, although I will try raisins next time, and there will definitely be a next time.

Used 1 cup sugar, added a little powdered ginger, chopped orange finely, it ended up great.  Next time will use 1 cinnamon stick instead of the powdered cinnamon to add a little more complexity of flavor.

I made this. So delicious. Even better the second day. Ladyfingers from scratch. 

It took 12 min. for the egg mixture to reach 160F (2388' 5") The sugar never melted in the eggs (160F).  When it all came together, it was smooth, bright and impressive.
Very very sweet. Not a bad thing!, but I will be putting this on yogurt and eating it with leftover pie

Thanksgiving morning my Bro-in-law handed over half of a giant Costco bag of cranberries. (“Here!”) Challenge accepted. Found this recipe. Had all the ingredients. Made it per the instructions, except it was Thanksgiving day, so no 24-hour rest period. Everyone liked it!

This was the best thing I made this past Thanksgiving. Pretty easy to put together, tart and delicious. I didn't realize till I read the introduction that the orange peels should be left ON, that isn't mentioned in the recipe.

I made this recipe for Thksgvg and it was delicious. I made it again just 'cuz, but, since I didn't have any raisins, I threw in the last of my dried cherries and chopped up some leftover pecans. I didn't measure, but I think the dried cherries were somewhere between a 1/3 and 1/2 cup; and the pecans also about1/3 of a cup. OMG! I've made lot of cranberry sauce in my life, and this was by far the best I've ever made or eaten! Might cut the sugar a bit next time, depends on the (whole) orange.

I made half the recipe. I didn’t have raisins, so I used half of a regular-sized orange to compensate. Perhaps because I didn’t use raisins and added what was probably close to a whole small orange, I didn’t find the proportion of sugar excessive. I will, however, use less orange and cut it up into much smaller pieces next time, and perhaps adjust the sugar downward (easy to add more as needed).

I think the semi-candied orange peels add an interesting bitter note. I didn’t miss the raisins.

I have to say I absolutely loved this cranberry dish and it will become a regular going forward for me. It was the right amount of sweet and tart. I think in the midst of things I might’ve cooked it just a little bit too long, next time I’m going to shorten the cooking time up.

Every time I cook a turkey, I save, date and freeze the pan drippings in a small container for roux. Then I make stock with the carcass and the roasted vegetables that I had in the bottom of the roasting pan. I date and freeze that broth; then when I'm ready to make turkey gravy for the next turkey dinner, I can pull them out of the freezer and make the gravy a day or two ahead of time. I'm always one turkey gravy a head. Makes a holiday meal so much easier.

@Phyllis. I do the same thing. I find making stock from this year’s carcass and saving it for next year’s gravy is a better use for it than making turkey soup, which I never enjoyed much, after Thanksgiving. Today I’m pulling turkey stock and pan drippings out of the freezer for my make-ahead gravy.

Make a real make ahead turkey gravy by roasting necks, back or thighs in oven with onions until very brown. Them mash the meat with store bought stock so it releases flavor. Roast some more with the stock until flavorful. Remove from oven and discard meat. Thicken with a slurry and this is a great gravy. I make it at least a week ahead and freeze. Then use this or add it to the drippings gravy on turkey day.  So good and less hassle on the day.
Added a Tb of soy sauce instead of salt - elevated the flavor. Really excellent recipe!
My pro tip:  always make ten times the amount of gravy than you think you'll need.  Happy Thanksgiving, everyone!
Has anyone tried using veggie stock instead?

Every year for 30 years. I use mushroom stock made with dried Chinese mushrooms and roasted odds & ends from prepping veggies all week. And in a pinch I use mushroom-flavored "Better Than Bouillon" in a jar, $6.99 but worth every penny.

I have been making turkey gravy for over 30 years, this is the best method ever! Didn’t need to mess around with seasoning or texture. I think the buttery roux makes a huge difference in the flavor of the finished gravy.

For Karen and other GF folks … cup-for-cup works, as does all-purpose GF flour. 
I don’t know about using cornstarch for a roux. A good question!
@Tim I learned from my niece that cornstarch subs perfectly for flour in a roux for GF purposes. Try it!

About "using cornstarch for a roux" -- don't. Even if it were to work, it's pointless. In the recipe, roux is used to thicken the stock. Just use a slurry of cornstarch and cold water to thicken the stock.

There are other thickeners that can be used similarly, including potato starch and arrowroot. The color and consistency are different, from translucent to almost clear, where the roux (can be) as dark as desired and opaque.

So easy and so good, made the final product (pan drippings at the end) FANTASTICO! I have gravy prep phobia so this make ahead recipe has cured me of my fear! (It’s a real thing, you can ask my family) I’m sure that sounds ridiculous, if you can make a roux-good for you!

I have family and friends with celiacs disease, so all my holidays are GF meals.  RE GF flour, I keep on hand a mix of 2 parts sweet rice flour, 2 parts potato starch and 1 part cornstarch for thickening and dredging.  It works beautifully, hubby and I actually like gravy made with this better than AP regular flour.
Will never make Thanksgiving gravy any other way !

Just made this in advance of tomorrow's Thanksgiving dinner. Our guests will include a vegetarian, a vegan and a family member with a dairy allergy.
Thus for the butter I used Miyoko's Plant Milk Butter and for the broth I used Better than Bouillon's Roasted Garlic Base.
Everything else was exactly is written in the recipe.
The result is excellent. Looking forward to serving it.

Veganized this exactly with wonderful results. Used Melt brand plant butter and Better than Bouillon No Chicken Chicken broth. I’ve always made a quickie Thanksgiving gravy recipe but this year I splurged on fresh herbs and wine. Utterly delicious and smooth as silk — didn’t strain because I loved the look of the thyme and smaller rosemary leaves, just removed stems and big pieces.

I made this last night and absolutely loved it. I only needed a half portion of the gravy as it was just for 2 of us. I did not realize I was out of white wine so I used mirin but in a smaller amount and it came out tasting great. I used a combo of both chicken and veg broth made from better than bouillon because that's what I had on hand. This will definitely be my go to gravy recipe from now on.
Julie was thinking of making this with vegan stock. I have done this, it works just fine. To get that silky mouthfeel I used agar, but potato starch, arrowroot, cornstarch might work as well.
Mushroom stock is especially full of umami.

Don't use the sherry vinegar!  This is a great recipe, except for that.  I first made the recipe exactly as written, then threw out that initial batch because the vinegar ruined the flavors.  Determined, I made it again without adding the vinegar, and used only 6 tablespoons of flour.  Delightful!  (My 2-star rating is based on the actual published recipe.)
Where is the recipe for the gravy from your Thanksgiving Start to finish?
This recipe (and roasted stock) is absolutely fantastic. Definitely the most deeply flavorful gravy I’ve ever made. Simple and amazing to have it done before the big day
I just made this make-ahead gravy. It is just delicious. I made turkey stock ahead of time with turkey wings, herbs, celery, leeks, garlic. The sherry vinegar rmakes it really good.

Delicious. Used homemade turkey stock from my freezer, Sauvignon Blanc and about a teaspoon of vinegar, all told. My only deviation was to add a good pinch of dried rosemary to the fresh thyme and sage sprigs (had no fresh rosemary on hand, and not eager to go to the grocery store the day before Thanksgiving). Wonderful gravy. The herbs really add to the depth. Thanks for a great recipe.

It’s serviceable if someone is desperate for something to moisten the meat or you are out of other options. But having made this today hoping to cross it off the list for tomorrow, sad to say I’m seriously underwhelmed. It’s lacking the depth, body, texture, and flavor of a pan sauce gravy from the turkey. Unfortunately, it’s back on the “cook the day of” list.
Balsamic vinegar works much better than sherry. The herbs really make this exceptional.

This recipe is quite tasty, even though we used store bought chicken stock(!) I can imagine we’ll be making it again, and if my partner allows me to make stock (she hates the smell of stock-making) it would be really something. Thanks for this recipe - delish!

Although my gut told be that you need a 1:1 ratio of fat to flour,  I thought I would try this one since I could make it ahead.
Luckily, I had plenty of butter and flour because if you follow this recipe as is, you will end up with a rue that is like a dough. 
Use the 1:1 ratio and it turned out great.
Chicken broth recipe is awesome…no need for drippings.

Made it exactly as written and it was wonderful.  The caramelized onions and cognac really made the difference.  I got lots of compliments on it at Thanksgiving and will definitely make again.  Also, making it ahead was a great time saver.

What caramelized onions and cognac?
While they may be good additions, they are not included in THIS recipe.

I made this exactly as described with Claire’s stock recipe (plus the turkey back bone in the stock from spatchcocking the recipe). 

One of the best tasting parts of the meal. Will make again.
The gravy is simple but has great flavor, especially if made from homemade stock (see related stock recipe from Clair), but, in my opinion, there is too much flour, next time I will reduce by half.
Holy moly so delicious!!!! I used 1/2 bone broth from chicken and 1/2 broth from turkey make ahead in instant pot.

It's the Tuesday before Thanksgiving and my gravy is done!  What a great recipe!  I used Gluten Free flour, a bit extra wine and will add pan drippings for extra flavor later.  Waiting until the turkey comes out of the oven and everything is coming together is the most stressful time to make gravy and invariably, someone trying to be helpful has washed out the small bowl with flour and water I've made ahead for thickener and set aside.  As far as I'm concerned, this is good to go right now!

Amazing! I’ve been vegetarian for 10 years, and this -  if made with vegetable stock - is the first vegetarian gravy that has been edible. Seriously. I’m not a fan of mushroom-flavored gravy or soy-sauce gravy, and this was a delightfully nice, buttery, flavorful gravy for mashed potatoes or…whatever.
Choose your wine wisely, my white was way too fruity and it definitely ruined the gravy
I’ve made this the last three years, and it’s perfect every time. Make double because it goes fast.
Made exactly as written and it was perfection. Didn’t even bother to add turkey drippings and still  so so delicious
sherry vinegar an essential note! tasted before the vinegar and thought it was wayyyy too sagey, but the vinegar balances everything out. yum
This is, hands down, one of the easiest and best gravies ever. I did have to substitute dried herbs for fresh but otherwise made it exactly. Love!
Can you freeze the gravy?
Can I make this gluten free with gluten free flour (cup-4-cup, etc.)?
You can use cup-4-cup gf flour. I prefer to use cornstarch; which makes a very smooth gravy without the grainy taste and texture you sometimes get from gf flour.
Phyllis -- do you use the same amount of corn starch if you replace the flour?

Simone: Cornstarch has twice the thickening power of flour, so when substituting cornstarch for flour in making a gravy, sauce, soup, or stew, use half as much cornstarch; e.g.: 1 tablespoon of corn starch for 2 tablespoons of flour.
Not sure how I feel about the tangy flavor from the wine.   It took a long time for the alcohol to cook out since it sorta simmers.
For a gluten-free gravy, if I use cornstarch, do I need to make a slurry first? I would assume yes, but anyone with experience with this, appreciate if you could confirm.

When I  brine my turkey (Tues. AM) I save all those "parts" (except the liver) and boil them with an onion,  carrot and celery.  Simmer covered for about an hour.  Strain and use this  to make the "make ahead gravy".  Refrigerate.  Add to the de-fatted turkey drippings on Thurs..  Be sure to scrape up those browned bits from the pan!
To brine, put turkey in a clean trash bag in a picnic cooler.  Pour in brine.  Seal tightly, and cover with ice cubes.  I put it outside, but could be kept inside.

Simmered for an hour and it didn't turn into gravy. Used a cornstarch/ cold water slurry to finally get the right consistency.  Could be because I'm terrible at gravy,  could be the plant butter.  Tastes ok.
It's likely the plant butter, which may contain liquids. Try with extra virgin olive oil, which adds extra flavor.
How much is sprig of any of these herbs? Does that mean we’re not meant to remove the leaves from the stems?

I used vegetable stock (half homemade and half store bought) instead since I was doing a vegetarian thanksgiving for some of my friends. I ended up also browning some garlic, shallots, and porcini mushrooms in the butter for the roux (but not actually adding them to the finally gravy) to up the "meaty" flavor and depth. It was the perfect addition to make it veggie friendly but still really flavorful, complex, and delicious!!
Delicious.  Vinegar almost ruined it after I added it, but I had extra stock and added more, and that problem was solved.  Tasted great.

I thought the gravy was fantastic and removed the stress of trying to make gravy and get the food on the table and carve turkey at the same time.
The vinegar does give the the gravy a little bright tartness, like a hollandaise sauce. Once the gravy is on the food, you don’t notice the sourness as much. Personally, I loved it.

gravy always stresses me out- no one in my family can do it and it's always strange and greasy.  This tasted good, especially after adding some turkey drippings.  I always have chicken stock in the freezer so this was simple.

I cooked this because I had never had normal gravy before and I thought I’d try it. Absolutely gross. I have no idea why anyone would want to eat this snot. I like a lot of Claire’s recipes but her taste in thanksgiving food is really bad.
Maybe this was "gross" because you don't have any experience making gravy. You can't always blame the recipe!
Or because you don't know what "normal gravy" is?  I mean no disrespect, but what kind of gravy do you usually have?
It was little bland...but worked out nicely
This was absolutely delicious and so easy to make! I was amazed by the depth of flavor that was achieved in such a short amount of time. This will be my go-to gravy dish going forward.
Beautiful recipe but bland. Can use store bought veggie broth but add a tablespoon of Umami Sauce Black Garlic (japangoldusa.com) for depth of flavor.
Amazing gravy - the sherry vinegar really makes it!!
I used Duck Fat for the base. Less healthy but more delicious!
I bought a cooked turkey breast so I didn’t have any drippings. I used Duck Fat for the base; less healthy but definitely more delicious!
Jamie Olivier’s make ahead gravy is far far better than this. It is a little more effort but I make it well ahead and even freeze some. Yum
Gravy is done so not hassle tomorrow.  And it is the best tasting gravy I’ve ever made.  Thanks Claire!
I followed the recipe, except I didn’t have sherry vinegar. I used sherry cooking wine instead. This is excellent and no stress!
I made my own chicken stock, followed the recipe and didn’t rush the roux, and while it came out smooth, it just didn’t have a lot of flavor.
very nice. recipe is accurate for measurements for proper consistency. well-flavored broth is essential. the wine added something unique and intriguing.
This is so easy and delicious. Always sub Dolin vermouth for white wine in my cooking and it did not disappoint this time! Can't wait to debut it on Thanksgiving!

I agree with the comments that this is the BEST and Easiest gravy EVER and I've been making gravy for 40+ years. I will NEVER go back to making gravy on Thanksgiving. I made turkey stock on Monday and the gravy on Tuesday with fresh herbs from my garden. I did add about 1/2 cup stock while it was reheating and some pan drippings. Everyone said it was the best I'd ever made. 5 stars!

This is the only gravy you will want to make moving forward. Made it exactly as described by the recipe. It was absolutely delicious! This year I took over hosting Thanksgiving, which is a holiday that been hosted by the same family member for the past 20 years... everyone loved it. I actually made Claire Saffitz' whole Thanksgiving menu (minus the desserts) and the whole meal was very delicious.

As presented, this gravy tastes almost exclusively of wine and vinegar.  I was panicking.  But as luck would have it, I had not yet discarded the salty/bloody juice that came out of my spatchcocked turkey while "marinating" in its seasoned dry-rub overnight.  Dumped it in in a last ditch effort to salvage the gravy and wow!  Some of the best gravy I've ever had, and really did taste of turkey.  My go to recipe from now on.  One star as written; five stars as "salvaged".

title: Smooth and Creamy Mashed Potatoes Recipe
author: Claire Saffitz

comments.commentBody: If I make this recipe 2-3 days ahead of Thanksgiving, should I incorporate the butter/milk mixture before refrigerating or do this last step when I’m reheating the potatoes on Thursday?

I have the same question.  Advice for making ahead?
Thank you!

During Step 3: my grandmother taught me to cover the pot with a dish towel, while the drained potatoes sit for a few minutes.  Towel absorbs steamy moisture from the spuds and will give you fluffier results! She was always right.

I’ve made a recipe similar to this one ahead and the recipe called for saving some of the water you boil the potatoes in. My recipe asked for only 1/2c, but I’d recommend at least twice as much, maybe more. Add some of the starchy water to loosen the potatoes when you reheat gently. I’ve been making them for almost 10 years and are requested every year, even when I’m not hosting.
Why scrub the potatoes if you are going to peel them??
The herb infused milk was delicious, but I found it called for far too much milk. I had about 1 cup of extra milk left at the end and my potatoes would have been absolutely dripping if I added it.

OK, but doesn't need to be so fussy. Peel and cut into any similar sized pieces so cooking is consistent.  I always add milk and a bouquet garni to the cooking liquid.  Some people even use a bit of stock.  You can also "hold" the potatoes in the original cooking liquid for some time if you don't overcook to begin with.  The mix in can also include creme fraiche, sour cream and I always use white pepper.  Black is too strong.
Agree, always white pepper!
Use heavy cream and a whisk attachment.A much fluffier outcome.

I hate to tell you Kathy G 3 days is the max you can keep them and I personally wouldn't recommend more than overnight or done in the morning before. (I peel and get ready to cook in the morning and make soon before serving myself). Peeling is a good job for overnight  guests getting in your way or children old enough to hold a peeler. Teenagers could even get them boiling in the pot and let your know when out. Making them in a mixer takes under 10min.
Is everyone but me peeling their potatoes to be mashed? We like the taste of mashed with some skins, and there are lots of health benefits.

I never peel the potatoes.   The skin does, indeed, add a bit extra flavoring and everything I’ve ever read says that potato skins have some great benefits like vitamins.  My kids will skip nearly the whole Thanksgiving meal as long as they have this recipe of mash potatoes - with skins!
Using a potato ricer is key to fluffy mashed potatoes every time.

I have always used an electric mixer just as my mother did. The key is patience and adding liquid a little at a time. At the end you turn the mixer on high for a bit to incorporate air. My kids used to call them angel potatoes because they are fluffy like clouds!
Way too much milk. Turned into soup. Very disappointing

I made the recipe as written (weighed the potatoes, drained well, etc.) on Thanksgiving morning and the flavor was incredible, but they were far too runny. I had to add several tablespoons of corn starch and skipped the reserved milk (only the butter) at the end. Next time, I will use only half the milk/butter mixture at the start and add as needed. They were a little firmer as leftovers. Definitely adding them to the permanent menu -- best tasting mashed potatoes ever!

Far too much liquid.  Like mashed potato soup.  I did weigh my potatoes.  I spent 30 minutes with the flame under the potatoes and lid off trying to dry them out.  Lost the potato flavor because of the excess milk and butter.
Perfect just as the recipe states

Huge fail for me and a humbling disappointment. Despite using only 2 cups milk rather than 2 1/2 it came out like potato soup. Not even several tablespoons of cornstarch brought it back to life. Beware on the liquids for this one! I might also mention the amount of garlic is pretty rich for this one as well.
Prepare mashed potatoes several hours ahead.

I am perplexed by the swathe of conflicting results here. Lots say way too much milk, while others say perfect as is?! I would always use a ricer, for super smooth, soft and beautiful potatoes - I fear they’d become claggy/sticky with hand beater…
Don't use Yukon Golds OR russets. RED potatoes make the best mashed. Have tried them all.

This recipe has way too much milk.
For me, sour cream added to mashed potatoes makes all the difference.
Make in red casserole for full recipe.   1/2 recipe would be enough for 10 with other sides.

There is no need to peel and cut potatoes! Just bake them and take it from there-been cooking Thanksgiving for 34+ people and we have used this method for the last 6 years when I figured it out.so much easier and potatoes are wonderful

I noticed you mentioned several other things like parsnips and sweet potatoes to be used 1/2 and 1/2 with the white potatoes but no mention of my very favorite mixture with rutabaga. It is close to parsnip but much better. Whenever cooking potatoes for roasts or stews or mashed potatoes or any recipe I most often will mix with rutabaga.

The recipe calls for Yukon Gold potatoes which have very limited availability since the potato industry has moved on to much more appealing yellow fleshed varieties.  I don't find any need to use a 2nd variety of potato anyway, it's a further complication one can do w/out.  If you can find Idaho russet Burbanks, that's all you need.  Idaho requires the variety of potato to be placed on every package, typically on the quik-lok on consumer bags or the cartons of loose potatoes.

The Yukon Gold are very plentiful in the Midwest. They’re everywhere-at Hy-Vee, the local grocery chain, Walmart and 10# bags at Costco. I buy both types and these are from either Idaho or North Dakota.
The sign may say Yukon Gold, but it's not likely that is the actual variety of yellow fleshed potatoes being sold.

Great recipe and easy to follow! I made it earlier in the day on Thanksgiving and held it on warm in our instant pot (with the butter on top) and it held perfectly! I will say that the only way this serves 8 is if people are only eating potatoes - this was an obscene amount of potatoes that was difficult to work with. We had 7 people who got generous servings and still barely made a dent. Will probably half it next year!

This is more like a pomme puree. The large volume of milk makes it extremely rich but loosens the consistency considerably. It does thicken as it sits but next time, I would reduce the amount of milk.

New favorite mashed potato recipe! So easy and flavorful, and potatoes turned out fluffy and smooth. Loved the trick for storing potatoes at the back of the stove with milk/butter on top — worked like a dream. And potatoes didn’t take as long to cook as recipe stated, so check on doneness with fork every so often.
Great recipe - turned out light and fluffy, and they reheat beautifully.

I would like to make mashed potatoes just for myself and I dont have large pots anyway. I will estimate what it takes but it would be nice if some of these recipes could have pared down volumes for the single person or the person with a limited kitchen. 

Also the video does not work on my iPad
This recipe is so simple that it's easy to scale up or down as you prefer. For one person, just scale everything down by a factor of 8 (more or less). No big deal.

About 5 years ago I bought the Paprika recipe app.   I use it religiously.   It is simple to download NYT recipes to it, plus automatically retains the URL in case I want to go check something in the recipe or comments and it has a feature that lets me adjust the entire recipe portions at the touch of a button. And…I tend to love reading comments and I am able to copy them and paste them into the recipe notes on the app.   It was well worth my one-time payment!

To the author/editors, may I strongly suggest writing (reserved) beside ingredients on the list that need quantities reserved, for people cooking by themselves for a family dinner who have to scroll back and forth between the sections of this app.
Process works well. But use half as much milk.
As others noted...wayyy too much milk.  Reserve 1/2 of the milk mixture and add as needed.

My trick is to boil about a 1:4 yellow turnip-potato mix.  Comes out with just a touch of golden colour and just amps up the potato flavour without being noticeable. Probably this would need less milk…
Delicious. I just don't believe in using a mixer for potatoes at all. Rice or mash then stir gently is all that's needed?

Stir in a couple of teaspoons of horseradish and a quarter cup of maple syrup before serving.  Adjust to your taste- you may really like this and add more in the future. Use genuine maple syrup of course- supporting our sugar makers here in Vermont.  Check out Baird Farm on line if you want pure organic and delicious syrup.
I followed the recipe to the letter, and these are the best mashed potatoes I've ever made. I also liked that the recipe was sized really well for a crowd.

Some may disagree for serious scientific glutinous reasons! But! I cut and boil and cool the day before then fridge them. In the morning sit at room temp and later proceed with the milk etc and let them hang out on back of stove for an hour or so before serving.
Made this recipe with about 3/4 less milk. Worked great! Use garlic and salt generously!

I followed the instructions exactly, and I mean exact as I’m a retired design engineer, even making sure that my potatoes weight was dead-on, and cut pieces were as close to 1” as possible. My pieces were fall-apart at 10 minutes at a moderate simmer, so test early. I agree with some comments that the milk amount was too much as the serving finish was a bit too “soupy.” They did taste great but next time I’m definitely dialing back on the cooked milk with thyme and garlic.
unless your potatoes are organic or you grew them yourself and trust them as organic, I wouldn't eat peels. peels are well known to be ridden with pesticides @KJ Woodstock

This recipe seems more complicated than necessary.
My variation of Joel Robuchon's famous mashed potatoes uses russet potatoes, butter, whole milk and salt.  I peel the potatoes before cooking, drain in a colander when they are done, put them through a ricer into the original pot, which I then heat to remove the moisture. I then slowly add the butter followed by the heated milk, mixing with a rubber spatula. Salt and pepper to taste. The potatoes are creamy and light. Never fails!
We only needed about half the milk to get a smooth and creamy texture. I recommend not adding it all at once.

I won't lie. This recipe has me on a chokehold. I'm tempted to eat the whole 4 pounds of mash potatoes. I made it for Thanksgiving and EVERYONE loved them! Truth be told, I'm only using Yukon Gold for the total amount of 4lb of potatoes, and it works just fine. Is so good.
What's the point of half russet half yukon gold?

The YG variety is glue-like in texture so adding the russets improves the final product.  YG came into fashion based on the color of the flesh, not the taste or texture.  Make life simple; use the russets, add butter - problem solved!
plain, whole milk yogurt is a great add in place of sour cream. buttery!
I've had great success with holding prepared mashed potatoes in a crock pot. Made early in the morning, I know my masters will be hot at the table.
Has anyone made this with a dairy-free milk?
Best mashed potatoes!

Yummy! Guests loved these. I didn't add all the milk and only a bit at the end before I served. 
Keep in a crock-pot container on warm and they do well until dinner. Add the extra milk/butter at the end and stir. Delish!

Perfect! The BEST out of the countless mashed potatoes recipes I've made for TG, Also the EASIEST! I do most of the cooking myself and the last minute mashed potatoes are always a little stressful - these sat for 2 hours and reheated quickly and stayed delicious.

Did anyone mash the garlic cloves with the potatoes? There were no instructions about the garlic cloves beyond adding to the milk. I took them out.
I was skeptical but I followed the recipe and left the mashed potatoes at the back of the stove while cooking the rest of the dinner.  They stayed warm just as promised!  and they were delicious.

Wonderful! I wanted to teach Pai something about typical US Thanksgibing food.  went to NYT COOKING & hit the recipe jackpot with this recipe Be careful to only put in 8 tbsp of butter in first melt and save remaining 4 tbsp.for lasr melt ar the end. We  put al 12 tbspin first and. Then had to find 4 more tbsp butter to put in. Also, had to use all russets, still was SUPERB!! Family never ate so many potatoes!! Thanks!
Might be good for make-ahead potatoes
these were the best mashed potatoes I've ever made. followed the recipe to the letter besides adding a spring of rosemary with the thyme. absolutely DELICIOUS-- highly recommend!
Excellent.  Will be my go-to mashed potato recipe from now on!  Delish.  Thank you Claire.
Decadent and Delicious!!!!
Process works well but use about half as milk.
Dramatically too liquidy.  I made them "by the book" except that I used all Yukon Gold potatoes.  I should have added the milk slowly so I could stop when the consistency was right.

It is absolutely irresponsible to suggest in Step 5 that mashed potatoes be kept at room temperature for several hours. Mashed potatoes at room temperature are a prime environment for the growth of bacteria and can cause severe food poisoning. I am shocked to see this recommendation in the cooking section in 2024.
These were SO perfect for Thanksgiving dinner.
Mama would say add about a teaspoon of baking powder to make them fluffy.

My brother and I used to go through all this fuss including a potato ricer. I then discovered Costco mashed potatoes in a crock pot. You just start heating them up a few hours before dinner. They are as good if not better then homemade
Too runny!

This is such a great recipe but trust the other comments! Pour enough of the milk mixture in just enough to get the consistency where you want.  If you add the entire amount all at once, it is more like potato soup... otherwise this recipe is so delicious and easy, especially to make ahead and reheat for any kind of meal.

This is the secomd Thanksgiving I used this recipe thinking I had made a mistake the first time. The end dish turned out way to liquidy and the potatoes did not hold any shape on a plate.  Overboiled?  They passed the "done" test so not sure where I went wrong.

I followed the recipe closely because it reminded me of my mom’s method for mashed potatoes. I had a moment of panic when I stirred in the full amount of milk and butter.  It briefly looked too liquid before they firmed after about a minute.  

My mom always made her “creamed potatoes” with the electric mixer, and I have been raising an eyebrow at the recipes that have you avoid it.  Her potatoes were absolutely smooth and billowy.  Her additions were milk, butter, salt and black pepper.  Heaven.

My mom always used a hand mixer for mashed potatoes, and they were unfailingly smooth and delicious! Great to see a respected food writer bucking the current trend and making use of a hand mixer for them!  I have followed this recipe as written twice now.  No regrets!
Would it be ok to do parts of this the day before?  For example, can the caramelized apples be left overnight in the fridge, or perhaps the whole assembled pie?
Should work to chill the assembled pie over night, but to be on the safe side, I’d prepare the pie dish and keep the apple filling separate. Then the next I’d assemble everything and bake it.

Hi, YiaYia! 

You can absolutely make the dough the day before -- or make the whole assembled pie, refrigerate and bake it off day of! But I'd most recommend making the pie a day ahead of time and let it rest at room temperature. It'll free up time and oven space, and taste delicious as the flavors have time to merge.
I made this over 3 days (crust on day 1, filling day 2, and assembled and baked on day 3) and it was the best apple pie I have ever made.

When you are done cooking the apples you will likely have caramel soup.  The recipe doesn’t handle that well.  

Instead, strain the soup into a pot and put the cooked apples aside.  Dissolve 1/4 cup corn starch by adding water to the starch (not the other way round) and add that to the soup and cook until the milky starch thickens to a pie filling consistency.  

Mix that back into the apples and cool and proceed with the baking of the pie filling into the crust…
How much water do you use to dissolve the 1/4 cup corn starch?

@MRL I actually found that the recipe as written worked perfectly. I poured in all the caramel liquid into the pie. The corn starch was enough to firm it up, and I had no problems with leakage. Filling was the perfect texture!

@MRL

I had the same concern about the amount of liquid in the dish with the apples when I took them out of the oven, but I followed the instructions as written and it turned out great. The juices thickened as they cooled, and then the corn starch mixture set everything up really well in the finished pie.
Could I get all the way until the last step (putting it in oven and baking) and freeze it?

I just made this. The apple filling is delicious. This is the first pie I've ever had where I like the filling more than the pastry. I would be sure to not cut the apples too thin or they will fall apart. The recipe says 1/2 inch, and that is the right idea. I didn't have any problems with the caramel. A heavy-bottomed pot may help.

The pastry was pretty easy to make and roll out but in my case, with a metal pan, it came out super crisp. It would be better in a glass pan.

An easy way to avoid crystallization of sugar while making caramel is to put a cover over the pot. The condensation will wash down the sides of the pot. 
Just remember to keep an eye on the cooking ,and Don’t stir it ! A little swirl once it starts to color will do.

This recipe has some holes and flaws that can easily be remedied.

If you aren’t familiar with making caramel a thermometer is handy.  Light Caramel should be 320-300, darker smokier 340… be careful not to burn the caramel as once you get close to that temp it tends to shoot up quite quickly.  I stir the caramel when it’s close to keep down the bubbles and use my nose and eyes to get the right color and flavor.
@MRL This was a very helpful comment. The caramel was perfect. Thank you.
After 20 minutes or so it’s a good idea (in our oven) to protect the crust rim with foil. Can anybody explain why a glass pie pan is preferred?

Glass heats slowly and evenly and you can see the bottom. But my husband got me an Emile Henry pie dish (ceramic) and I have never had a bad pie crust in it. Now if only I could make a beautiful lattice top…
My Emile Henry pie dish (ceramic) has been a game changer. I believe it conducts heat more evenly than glass.
@Moses I have one and love it so I’m going to use it for this, thanks!

The first time I made this pie (Thanksgiving 2023), it turned out perfectly. Second time I made it, I used Granny Smiths at the request of the recipient and it still turned out well. This third time with Honeycrisps turned out soupier. I think the apples are more watery but also I know I didn’t deepen the caramel enough so the caramel was waterier. I meed to get a stainless steel pot and leave my non-stick for oatmeal, smh. Fingers crossed the pie still turns out.
Water & sugar are taking for ever to go dark amber??? What am I doing wrong?
Mine never turned dark amber either, and then the mixture crystallized.

@Vanessa same. I was having issues in a non stick but then switched to a stainless steel and that worked better. Still took longer than anticipated. Mine started to crystallize until I added the butter.

@Vanessa that might be an issue with your heat? I think having the heat too low will make it hard for the sugar to boil. You should see big sugary bubbles. I found the video of Claire making it helpful for visual cues

This is a full day project and I absolutely majorly failed at caramel twice, I never want to make this again but I have to because it’s so dang delicious. I’ve never made such a flaky pie crust that rolled out just perfectly. The apple cider vinegar is the perfect offset to the sweet caramel apples, I enjoyed not having cinnamon for once. Lattice top is the way to go. But please please attach a caramel video to this recipe I am totally lost.
This is the best tasting apple pie I’ve ever made. I used a pie crust recipe which was extra crispy and the results were spectacular. It’s extra work, but worth it!

Made this last night! I used a metal pie pan and it turned out perfectly fine. I would recommend checking earlier on the apples. Mine cooked super quickly and the filling ended up quite smooth. Super tasty though, and held together! Next time might double the caramel to serve on top.
I have made the sauce twice and it keeps crystallizing when I add the butter

I had trouble with the Carmel. I followed the recipe exactly but the Carmel solidified when I added the butter (twice) I rescued the second batch by adding some water and reducing it while stirring vigorously but would like to know what I did wrong
Is the recipe calling for 3lb of apples after peeling and coring or before?
What amount of caramel does the first part of the recipe yield? I’m thinking of subbing in a high quality caramel from the store.
Can this cooked pie be frozen?

This is a fantastic pie. Pastry has always been my weak spot as a baker but this pie crust recipe changed all that, it was very pliant when rolling and flaky when baked. Next time I will make sure all apples are cut at 1/2" thickness and I'll be more patient with the caramel. I think I rushed it at medium-high heat and maybe used too much water brushing down the side as it never turned amber. Even so, it tasted delicious and everyone loved it.

to answer some questions I noticed in the comments:
1. yes you can make filling ahead of time, store in fridge and then use
2. glass bottom is just so you can majke sure it hasn't burned and it conducts heat nicely

this pie filling is my go to, it is gooey greatness!
If I have it in a glass pie plate and I'm taking it from the freezer to a 425 oven doesn't that mean my plate might crack?
After baking the sliced apples (honey crisp) and caramel mixture, I had a lot of liquid left over; too much I thought to pour into the pie…anybody else have this problem?

-My caramel did not crystallize, but took much longer than 5 minutes. If it's not crystallized, keep going. Hardened when added to apples but melted beautifully in oven. 
-Highly recommend arranging apples individually in bottom crust & pouring the cornstarch-juice mixture on top afterwards. 
-I subbed top crust for Claire's all-purpose crumble recipe from Dessert Person. Yum!
-Made crust 10 days ago (freezer), apple/caramel mix night before, assembled & baked day before Thanksgiving.

I loved this! Made the filling and the crust the day before. Pressed dough into springform and chilled overnight. Scooped apples into prepared pan and reserved the liquid and reduced it separately to avoid a mushy crust. Glad I did. Flavor was fab. Next time I’d add cardamom or cinnamon but otherwise it’s terrific as is. Claire for the win as usual!

The caramel went well enough and the filling tasted fine, though soft and very brown. But the crust was a crunchy, tough, nearly inedible cracker. I’ve gotten pretty good at pie dough over the years and thought I’d try something new, forgetting that in the arid SW dough often requires more hydration, not less. I usually use 1 1/2 to 2 times the water I use up north. I can see the merits of this technique elsewhere, but next time it’s back to mom’s classics. Never should have strayed!

Don't take too much stock re: the other reviews. Embrace the extent how the filling ends up kind of apple sauce-y. This was the best apple pie I've ever made, with a wonderfully complex filling flavor. It was a huge hit at our Thanksgiving. (And by comparison, last year's apple pie -- a different recipe -- was ignored in favor of the other desserts.) Thank you, Claire!

Filling was delicious and intensely flavored, but uniform and smooth, with no apple pieces surviving the second bake. If you're fine with that, great! I missed the distinct apple chunks, so I think next time I'll try pre-baking only half of the apples to hopefully get the best of both worlds.

I froze the entire pie solid before baking, and baked without thawing, adding about 20 minutes to the cook time, which worked great.
Very good apple pie. I added more a good splash of bourbon and a little more sugar. In the future, I’ll also add cinnamon. I missed the spice
We found this to be just an OK apple pie and somewhat disappointing given the time it took to prepare and bake. While the apples remained nicely firm, without any spices, it was rather bland overall.

I bought Coop’s salted caramel just because I’ve never made caramel before and wasn’t super confident. I’m glad I did because the caramel I made turned hard  after a few minutes. I also added some cinnamon because it seems weird that there’s no spices.

This is one of the most frustrating and ludicrous recipes I've ever encountered, and makes me excited to cancel my NY Times subscription. The instructions for the caramel led to terrible results, and don't align with actual instructions for making caramel. Plus, the second you pour it over the sliced apples, it hardens! Why on earth wouldn't you add the apples to the hot caramel in the pan?
Yes, the caramel sauce hardens when it cools down, but it should have reliquefied and incorporated with the apple juices during the first baking of the apples.

This recipe made me love apple pie. I followed it exactly but over two days. Used Pink Lady apples and a ceramic pie plate. The caramel sauce took me two attempts. The first attempt crystallized before adding the butter. The second crystallized after adding the butter. At that point I got frustrated and put the gritty mess on top of the apples and put it in the oven, figuring we would just eat a gritty pie.  It came out fine after a little stirring. The oven heat and juice from the apples dissolved the crystals. I put the Apple caramel filling in the fridge overnight with the pie dough and continued with the recipe the next day.  It came out of the oven looking like something from a pastry shop. NOTE: do not throw out the pie dough scraps. Collect them, roll them out and cut them, and sprinkle with Demerara sugar and cinnamon and bake with the pie until done.  The dough is so good I had a request to make it again just for those “cookies.”

this baked way too long - cooking the apples for 45 minutes then baking the pie for 90 minutes left this as an applesauce pie. There was no bite or structure to any of the apples. plus, there was no caramel flavor at all. disappointing.

I think it allows you to see the bottom crust to assure it’s baked thru.
My guess, anyway.
a glass pan conducts heat differently - takes longer to both gain and lose heat, and so changes the way the pie bakes. i am guessing it helps it to bake more evenly!
I am following exactly but Carmel keep solidifying when I add the butter what am doing wrong
when i have this problem, i typically find that whisking vigorously works well. I am sorry you had this problem.
Why freeze the pie before baking?
Am I reading the baking time correctly?   Is it a total of 1 hour and 50 minutes?
Would this work with vegan butter?

My pie was done after 1hr of baking.

Be careful with the caramel. During the baking process the caramel is darkening more. Next time I’ll rather take it off when it’s amber, than dark amber as described here.

Anyway all together totally worth it and really delicious.

@NotMyRealName a glass pie plate is better than metal because a) it heats up and cools down more slowly, which is better for pastry, and b) you can see through it and gauge whether the pastry is properly browned.

The end result was incredible--a huge hit at a thanksgiving party. 
That said, a few notes--it really does take all day.
The caramel took forever--my stove may have just been too low. And once I added the butter and salt, it hardened into crystals immediately, was not pourable, more liek brown sugar. Still, I crumbled it onto teh apples and baked, adn the taste was incredible.
Also, the high heat burned the top crust in 10 mins. I had to take it off and make a whole new crust. Worked fine at 350

This was my first attempt at fruit pie.  It's cheating and lazy, but I used store-bought pie crust. I brushed it with egg and sprinkled with the demerara sugar, as this recipe said, but wasn't on the instructions of the pre-made crust.  Maybe it was luck, but I had no problem making the caramel.  It gets hard rather quickly as it cools, but I managed to get it into the apples in time.  I put the oven on high for 20 minutes, followed by an hour and 5 minutes at 350.  The results were perfect.

Ugh, the caramel. I don’t know what I’m doing wrong but have made it three times and as soon as it hits the apples it turns hard as a rock. Following the recipe to a T. I don’t know what to do at this point as I have ruined tons of good apples.
My caramel hardened too. Not to worry though-it melts into the apples in the next step when baked at 300 degrees. Good luck!
I also had trouble with the caramel seizing though it happened when it hit the apples. I put it in the oven anyway and am hoping for the best.
My apples created a bunch of juice and it was still "juicy" after adding the corn starch.  Is this ok?  Does it need more cornstarch?  Should I just use a slotted spoon?  LOVING this process!
Cornstarch mixtures thicken with heat, so the juices will turn into a delicious jam in the oven :)
My caramel hardened when it hit the apples too but after an hour in the oven was nice and dissolved! Keep going!

I’m going to have practice caramel making.  I justes added the crystallized to the apple. Worked fine. It crystalized like others mentioned.  It me six large Granny Smiths.  I added cinnamon and nutmeg to the cornstarch mixture.  I’m cooking the last hour now.  Can’t wait to try.
What is the point of freezing the assembled pie before baking?
It keeps the top crust neat and sharp. (She discusses the technique in her recent Youtube Thanksgiving video.)

The recipe doesn’t have enough butter to not harden when you put it on the cold apples…. As some more butter, cream or a tablespoon of baking soda to interfere with the candy hardening so you can coat the apples.

I cooked the apples at 325 for 45 minutes stir them several times to coat the apples and dissolve any caramel crystals.

I think I need a lesson in making caramel. 3rd attempt with this recipe and others. It barely turns amber after 5 mins and never darker. Oh well, maybe there’s such a thing as white caramel. Dumped it over the apples and probably can’t go wrong with the ingredients! Very excited to taste the flakey pie dough though, which looked like it will turn out pretty nice!

If making the apple filling a day ahead, would you recommend completing all steps (incl cornstarch & vinegar) before placing in the fridge overnight? Or alternately add the cornstarch & vinegar right before baking?

Thank you MRL. The info about the caramel temperature would have helped so much/ should have been in the recipe. Mine took 20 minutes to get to anything like amber. On the upside even with the butter it stayed liquid when I poured it on the apples.
There was so much liquid with the apples after bakingI put into the pie… the tripe didn’t say what to do there either. We shall see… proof of the pudding shall be in the eating!

My caramel was as hard as a rock when I added it to the apples but it ended up softening some when I put it in the oven. I raised the oven temp to 325 and put them in for a full hour. There were still some caramel chunks so I put them in a glass bowl and microwaved them with some water until they melted nicely and added them back into the filling

Carmel took 20 minutes then I turned heat up and it browned. Carmel solidified when poured on apples and was difficult to "gently toss the apples". Apples turned to mush and soup after step 3. Given consistency of filling it was not enough to fill the pie so we made a modified galette instead. Not at all what I had imagined.
The recipe for the caramel was horribly inaccurate. I tried twice with terrible results. I finally made caramel from another recipe then continued on. please update it.

Amazing recipe! My only note is that I ended up putting foil over the pie about 45 minutes in because the top was already a deep brown. Would recommend setting yourself 15-20 minute increment timers to check how the color of your crust is developing and ensure it's to your liking.
I rarely do this but followed the recipe exactly and it is amazing.

Used Turbinado sugar instead of Demerara because it is easier to obtain and less expensive in my city.  A thoughtful pie that rewarded me applause from my daughter in law who is a careful eater.  The work of this pie is best spread over 2 or 3 days but worth it.  What a gorgeous thing!  Everyone took instagramable photos of that crust!

Super fussy recipe and a disaster in total. Lucky for us we had no guests! Both burnt and too much butter. We are lucky our teeth are still in place. Thankfully, our happiness on Thanksgiving does not depend on the dessert.  Yuk.

I followed directions exactly & I'm really disappointed in this recipe. No spices at all. Caramel alone does not carry this pie as I imagined it would. And the instructions have a typo about how long it takes to caramelize the sugar water. It took me 26 minutes.

Followed directions pretty closely. Good flavors. But, I prefer apple-based desserts to retain apple integrity-this wasn’t quite apple sauce but not far from it. The idea of baking apple slices for 2 to 2.5 hours (even half inch) will do that-baked apples take less time! If you like the contrast of crunchy crust and mushy apples, this is the recipe for you. If you want defined apple slices, bake or cook the apple/caramel mix for only 30min and the pie for 1 hr.
I followed the directions to a T, and my apples completely became applesauce. The flavors were wonderful but I would not precook the apples for the length required in the recipe.

This apple pie recipe was a big hit  this Thanksgiving. It was apple pie to the next level. A bit labor intensive but so worth it!  The caramelized apples were so tasty. I did have some issues with the caramel hardening in a clump while in the oven so I put the apples in a bowl to cool and kept stirring the caramel and apple juices until they melted together, then poured over the apples before putting in the fridge which worked.

Delicious pie. My sugar never caramelized so I went ahead and followed the recipe despite that and the pie still tasted great. The crust was nice and flaky. I made the pie over 2 days and prepared the filling and the pie crust on the first day and then assembled everything and baked it on day 2.
It wasn’t terrible and I’ve made better with half the prep time. Could not taste the caramel. It was a lot of work and not worth the return on investment.
Hands down the best apple pie I’ve ever made and eaten.

This is the first time I have ever baked a pie, and even though I'm a novice with no fancy tools, it came out beautifully. At our Thanksgiving dinner it was a hit, people came back for seconds. I watched the video they linked to repeatedly, esp. for the caramel and pie crust part. This pie is not too sweet, with caramel notes, structure holds up, great texture. It's really perfect. Takes time but not as hard as I thought it would be! You can prep dough a day or two in advance and keep in fridge.

The only challenge I had is the caramel hardened when poured over the apples.  After apples were ready to come out of the oven I continued to stir the caramel with the apple juices until caramel melted.  Worked perfectly.  Thank you to Carol for suggesting the Emile Henry pie dish! The recipe is a keeper.

It's...OK. Baking the apples twice leaves you with applesauce as filling.  The taste is interesting,  but not that of apple pie. Really,  apple pie is a classic. Why mess with it?  If you don't have a basic recipe,  try James Beard.  This was worth trying.  Once.
Love Claire but the this was such a miss. 40 minutes precooking and 1h20 in the oven turned the filling to mush. Had to serve just ice cream for dessert :(

The caramel glopped up when I added it to the apples.  However the oven heat and apple juices will liquify the caramel in the end.  Very delicious filling.  I used Northern Spy apples for their tartness.

I have to agree with D. Not a fan of this pie. Made sure the caramel was just the right color before cooking with the apples. Final pie result was I thought the apples tasted bitter due to the caramel picking up more color when baked with the apples. Also, I don’t like the needlessly complex crust technique. I didn’t find it any flakier than traditional method. Maybe even less so. I like Claire’s recipes. I have both her cookbooks, but this won’t get baked again.

This was by far the best apple pie I have ever made after years of searching. I, too, had problems with the caramel hardening and clumping in the oven but kept going and I am glad I did. I turned the heat up a touch and stirred the apples more frequently and the caramel did indeed melt and coat the fruit. Pull the apples from the oven as soon as they are just tender and sauced in the caramel. I baked the apples the night before and refrigerated to break up the work. Added cornstarch mix in AM.

This was the best pie crust I ever made. I generally find that using the food processor yields a dough that's easier to handle but too overworked. However, I followed Claire's instructions to a tee and, while it seemed super crumbly at first, it was miraculously easy to roll out by the time I refrigerated and pounded the dough - no sticking! The result was visually appealing and super flaky. I'm pretty sure I messed up the caramel and didn't want to redo it but the filling was still delicious.

If I were making this recipe again, I'd cut the apples into thicker pieces and pre-bake them for less time.
I used 4 Pink Lady and 2 green apples cut into half inch thick slices, but they pre-baked in like half the estimated time. I should have pulled them from the oven, but went with the recipe's timing.
The result was a very tasty apple pie, but that had what was effectively apple puree as a filling. No apple chunks at all. It tastes good, but there was too much textureless apple goop.

I completely failed at this. I was unable to make caramel. First batch kept reducing and reducing and never turned brown. And stuck -- REALLY stuck -- to my pot and spoon. Next batch, different pot, and it again wasn't turning brown. I gave up.
I must have overcooked the caramel but it didn’t look past the amber stage. It created a bitter filling when added to the apples. Pie was ruined. Won’t make again.

I wonder if the caramel sauce in this pie would behave the same way the Bannoffee pie did. It did not keep well in the refrigerator and I spent the better part of a Saturday cleaning a wonderfully tasty caramel off of the bottom of my refrigerator (was done as a tart so it didn’t stay in a pan).

The fact that it liquefied after less than 24 hours, would have me worried about the caramel sauce here. Does it need to be stabilized somehow? Cornstarch? Arrowroot? Gelatin?

I made this pie exactly as written. It was excellent for sure, except my Granny Smith apples ended up as applesauce!
What could have happened? The recipe said these apples would be fine.
Can I use a caramel sauce instead of making my own? Last TG, I got the caramel sauce to work on my 2nd try. Love the recipe but  Would rather use a ready-made caramel.

In a first for me, I used a glass (brand new Pyrex) pie pan (as suggested in the recipe), spent hours prepping this pie a day in advance of Thanksgiving, set it in the freezer in the penultimate step (as suggested in the recipe), then put it in the oven to bake, only to find the glass pie plate shattered into a million pieces by the time I was ready to lower the oven temperature.  Perhaps there was a flaw in my pan or user error on my part, but I’d be surprised if I’m the only one suffering from thermal shock on this one. Quite a colossal waste of time and disappointment.
Easily the worst apple pie I have made in the last 20 years. I’ll just go back to the much simpler, easier, faster and tastier recipe from Joy of Cooking.

This was the best apple pie ever! The crust was a dream to work with, no breakage, rolled out easily even though it was cold. I think I could have let the caramel cook longer, but still fantastic. Used mostly honey crisp apples and one lonely Mitsu. My daughter thought it needed cinnamon, but I thought it was fine without.

Loved this pie and didn’t find it to be as faffy as I feared from the 4  hour time estimate. I highly recommend watching the video of Claire making the pie, especially the caramel part. I followed the time instructions very carefully and had no problems! The only adjustment I made was that I didn’t add Demerara sugar. I think using smaller, firmer apples was helpful as I didn’t really struggle with a soupy filling as some other people mentioned.

This turned out tasty. No actual caramel, just a caramel flavor to the apples. I poured out most of the extra liquid after the apples cooked and cooled. Way too much liquid for a pie. I made it in a 10 inch pie pan. There were not enough apples by volume to that size of pie. Totally solveable by using a 9" pie pan. Claire Saffitz video about how to make pie crust was the best! I watched it once then followed it as I made my actual crust. https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=DcCa_dHHu30
This was such a hit on Thanksgiving, will be my new apple pie go to. And loved this crust!

Please change the name to Apple Sauce Pie since everyone who tried it thought that is what I had made. And as for the caramel, no one could taste it. It seemed worth trying, but it was truly not worth all the time it took for a mediocre dessert.

Fan favorite! You really don't miss the cinnamon. I did use Melissa Clark's Pie Crust recipe instead since it uses a food processor so it quite a bit quicker in my opinion. Also be sure to not to fully cook the apples before assembly, especially if you are planning to make across different days. There is a fine line between crunch apples and apple sauce. Overall this is a hurry up and wait recipe.

I don’t really like apple pie but my partner requested it for his birthday so I made this and it was delicious. My caramel sauce seized when I added the butter, but I scraped as much as I could of it over the apples and refrigerated overnight after baking them and the caramel just melted into the juice from the apples and it was fine. This will be my go-to apple pie recipe from now on.

Similar critiques as others: apples were pretty mushy with little-to-no bite and caramel flavour was lacking. I didn't have issues with making the caramel sauce though and my pink ladies didn't release too much liquid during the initial bake. However, the pie crust browned too quickly (using CS's own recipe); I'd suggest cutting the egg wash with water or milk/cream. My pie was only in for 20 min @ 425F / 40 min @ 350F and it was quite dark. Still well-received at the dinner party though!

The caramel was really frustrating to make. It was clumpy and refused to melt in the oven, even though I kicked up the temperature to 375! I ended up spooning out caramel clumps and the apple liquid and melted it together on the stove top and poured it back over. I tried a bite and it's fine, I guess, we'll see if this was worth it when the finished pie is done.

I pre-baked the bottom crust with weights for 10 minutes before adding the filling and top crust. The dough of the bottom crust must overhang the pan (it will shrink) and you need to cover the edges with foil in the pre-bake. I had no problems getting the raw top crust to stick to the pre-baked bottom. This made for the most amazing bottom crust! This recipe is absolutely fantastic.
Is there perhaps, a recommended caramel sauce that could be purchased and added to the apple mix? As a cheat?

Made as instructed, made the filling and crust the day ahead and assembled and baked early on the day of. Did have to make a foil protector for the edge of the crust. Absolutely killed at Thanksgiving - winner winner
