Thanksgiving with Joshua. Very good. Chill, chill, chill and
My caramel seized up :(

title: Lemon Cream Pie With Honey and Ginger Recipe
author: Claire Saffitz

stats.Total Time: 1 hour 40 minutes, plus at least 4 hours’ setting
stats.Cook Time: 1 hour 30 minutes, plus at least 4 hours' setting

comments.commentBody: It would be great to have a measurement for ginger AFTER it's been grated-- tablespoons, ounces or grams, for example.
Where does it say to grate the ginger?
There is no need to grate the ginger. Since it's steeping and strained out at the end, strips with a lot of surface area is fine.

It states in the ingredients list that the inch of root ginger should be thinly-sliced - and then that thinly-sliced is used to infuse the cream. I don't know why you'd need some weighted measurement for this process. Nowhere does it say to grate any ginger.

I used Anna’s Swedish Ginger Thins and they worked great for the crust. I also candied the lemon peels in the oven after taking out the crust and let them cook while I prepped the filling. The timing made much more sense than doing it at the end like the recipe suggests.
Making the filling I decided to grate in the ginger and lemon zest and it came out so delicious. I also did a meringue topping rather than cream and it was great.
Learned from a produce manager at a Safeway in Hawaii — freeze the ginger before peeling then grate with a Microplane. Works like a charm. Some places (Trader Joe’s) carry frozen grated ginger.
Effie’s ginger biscuits are great and so are Midel’s!

A word to the nervous chef, my pudding took almost 10 whole minutes to stiffen on the stovetop. At minute 6 i almost gave up but decided to just have faith. Then all of a sudden it came together beautifully.
Also as one person suggested i used grated lemon zest and 3 defrosted cubes of grated ginger that i buy at TJ's. Might up the ginger amount next time bc mine came out very lemon forward.
Happy baking friends!
@Sj Same.  Mine took much longer likely because I followed the directions and kept it at medium.  I turned it to medium high and it stiffened up swiftly.

Just want to spell this out for people (like me) who might read the recipe and notes and still not notice this: there are (at least) two different kinds of ginger snap cookies. There’s the thin wafers that this recipe calls for and the thicker, more cookie-like ginger snaps. I used the cookie style ones and they are far too dense and moist. Still tasted fantastic, but the crust was almost like toffee.

Made this pie 3x (who doesn’t want to make sure it’s perfect for the holiday feast?!). Used Anna’s ginger thins. First 2 pies, received comments that they loved the pie but couldn’t taste the ginger. Third try, I juiced a 2-inch ginger piece & added the juice to the creme fraiche topping. Everyone raved about that one. Thanks NYT.
Can’t wait to try this! Just to clarify is this with lemon zest or the peels?
Recipe says: lemon zest, removed in strips with a vegetable peeler. The result will be peels of lemon zest.
What brand of ginger snaps do you recommend?

A big hit! And very easy, too. We were doubtful about using the Nabisco brand gingersnaps, in the yellow box, but they were absolutely perfect. The custard comes together on the stove slowly and then all at once, so pay attention and be ready to remove from heat!

Agree with others who report the filling took longer than indicated to thicken on the stovetop. I used creme fraiche for the topping because I'm not a sour cream fan but still worried after tasting the finished topping that it would be off-puttingly sour. But in combo with the super sweet/tart filling the topping is perfect. Any added sugar would absolutely have been too sweet.

Delicious! I had trouble with the crust. Used Midel's ginger snap cookies and 4T butter, it hella slumped. Did a 2nd try with 2T butter (as recommended), and was better but I still had to press a spoon to shove the crust back up the well. Worked okay. 

Loved the lemon! Didn't use ginger. Added a layer of dark chocolate ganache on top of the chilled lemon (maybe 150g whipping cream to 120g 75% choc, salt) for 1/3" layer of choc. Loved the creme fraiche/whip, added 1T sugar.

I just made it again, but with Anna's Swedish Ginger Thins, and the crust was perfect! Even with the 4 T butter that the recipe says. These cookies reacted so much differently than the Midel cookies. Also, I didn't realize the first time I made this that I was supposed to put the pie pan onto a baking sheet in the oven. I did that this time. I'm not sure what that does differently, but the crust is great.

Any considerations for subbing coconut cream in for either or both the filling and topping? Instead of heavy cream? Feel like the flavor would pair well with lemon and ginger; but not sure if it will cook or set the same way as with heavy cream?
JT - you can’t make it without ginger, honey, etc. Make another pie or just follow the amounts written in the recipe.

Made this two nights ago following the method of lemon zest strips and sliced ginger (matchsticks) but I waited about 30 seconds longer than I should have to get the pudding off the stove and was left with a very thick pudding and trying to sieve that through with all of the peel and ginger was a nightmare! It still tasted good but the textures were all wrong, including the crust which absolutely did not need 4 tbs of butter!

As a follow up: I remade this recipe today using a fine lemon zest and grated ginger so that it wouldn’t pose a problem when I put the pudding through the strainer. This time I got the pudding off the stove on time and it easily went through the strainer, which held back most of the solids. The texture is infinitely better. Also halved the amount of butter in the crust and that came out much better too.

Used twice as much ginger as the recipe called for, could not taste any ginger in the custard at all. It was delicious, but I missed the ginger flavor. I took it out of the fridge about an hour before serving it, my custard had set (I thought), but it was very loose when I served it.  Next time I’ll serve directly from the refrigerator.  For the effort involved, I think a dish of lemon curd with whipped cream and ginger snaps on the side would have been just as good.
I'm going to try this with Tate's ginger cookies. They're delicious, very gingery and gluten free so everyone in our family can eat it!

This was pretty good! The ginger doesn’t come through in the custard though. Might need to cut the ginger into smaller pieces or grate it instead. The candied lemon slices look nice, but aren’t really edible and make cutting the pie a bit hard. The sour cream whipped cream is delicious. The crust was a bit hard, but I think I compressed it too much or made the crumbs too fine - would be more careful about that next time. In the future, I might add candied ginger on top instead of lemon.

I made this pie non-dairy by subbing NutPod creamer for the heavy cream and making coconut milk whipped cream for the topping. It turned out delicious! Next time I will grate the ginger and lemon in the custard to get a stronger flavor. Also I used Stauffers SNAPS gingersnaps for the crust and it was amazing! Will definitely make again.

Can confirm - don't make it with thick ginger snaps. The crust slumped but I made it work! The filling is good, but didn't taste like ginger at all. Overall, a good lemon cream pie - but would add more ginger or slice the ginger smaller to get ginger flavor.
Could you save time by using a pre-made graham cracker crust?
Totally! I almost did after I failed with the first crust that I made.

This was a hit. The perfect thing to bring when someone else is cooking dinner. A bit too complicated if you’re making the whole meal.
I thought the roasted lemon slices looked unappetizing so I slivered up some candied ginger to scatter over top - it looked great and added an extra hit of spicy ginger. The honey drizzle is a must.

The filling could have used a bit more ginger. I think grating rather than slicing the ginger for the filling would yield a stronger ginger flavour.

This was a great pie but a bit skimpy on crust. I am going to make for Thanksgiving but double the crust recipe. Used Tate’s ginger snaps, which were delicious (and they also make a gluten free version). My son declared it the best pie crust he ever had.
Great recipe! The lemon pudding didn't really taste like ginger, but the crust did and I put crystallized ginger on top for garnish. I used ginger thins from Trader Joe's and they worked great.

This was the favorite pie at our Thanksgiving table yesterday. Anna's ginger thins were perfect for the crust and the custard cream was absolutely delicious. The lemon slices on top, while pretty, weren't really edible. Mine burned the first time as directed so I lowered the heat to 300 which worked much better. But I think next time I will fully candy them and/or grate lemon zest over the top of the cream instead.

The flavor was delicious. I had a hard time getting the pieces intact out of the pan; the crust (which I made with Anna’s gingersnaps) stuck firmly to my glass pie plate. But the flavor was fantastic. I didn’t love the candied lemon slices. The flavor & texture are gratuitous imo. I’ll omit them next time, and perhaps dust the whipped topping with lemon zest for color.

The crust recipe is from Claire Saffitz’s book Dessert Person. Biscoff cookies work well. I thought the pie was delicious and a refreshing end to Thanksgiving. I do agree with others that the lemon rounds on top were basically inedible, although perhaps I did not slice them thinly enough or bake long enough.

I had no problem with the crust. I used Anna's ginger snaps and baked for 13 minutes. I grated the lemon zest and ginger as others had suggested. The advantage of slices would make straining faster but I went with grating for the taste and will do so again. Everyone who suggested skipping the lemon slices on top was right. I made them and they were pretty but unpleasant to eat.

I’ve made this twice. Both with and without the crust. It is one of the most delicious pies that I’ve made, especially during the holidays. Light, but still feels decadent, truly delicious. I made it with gluten-free ginger snaps, and it still turned out great.
What gluten free ginger snaps brand did you use?

This recipe is a keeper. Heed the advice to be patient with the lemon filling - it will thicken. 
Thank you for the advice for Anna’s ginger cookies. They worked great.

I really liked the lemon custard in this; very similar to lemon curd, but creamier. Took about 10 minutes to thicken on the stove. I had graham crackers on hand so I used them instead of the gingersnaps, but the ginger in the custard by itself did not come through at all in the final product-- so I would definitely use the gingersnaps next time. If I were to make this again, I would also add something to vary the texture. It's a lot of creaminess with little to break it up.

This is a good recipe, and I’m a fan of Claire Saffitz. My only disappointment with it is that the ginger flavor was a little too faint. When I make it again, I’ll finely chop a couple tablespoons of crystallized ginger and scatter it on the crust before I spoon in the custard.
Guests LOVED it.  Based on other comments, we doubled the amount of fresh ginger, and the balance was just right.

So delicious! I made this with a graham cracker crust and no ginger in it because I’m not a ginger person. I also only put whipped cream with a pinch of sugar whipped in on top (no sour cream). It tasted just like key lime pie but with lemon instead of lime. Will definitely make again.
Any chance you could sub in a thick oat milk creamer for the heavy cream?

That might work, but I think it may be better to use something that has more fat.  I am going to use 1 cup raw cashews blended with 1 & 1/2 cups almond or oat milk.  If you don’t have a high speed blender, soak the cashews in hot water for 15-30 minutes beforehand and keep blending until completely smooth (no grainy bits).  Full fat coconut milk is another good option, but it might give a slight coconut flavored undertone…which I don’t think would be bad with this recipe…just different.
Viable substitution for heavy cream?  Greek yogurt or...?
Any brand recommendations for the gingersnap cookies? The recipe description isn't very helpful.

Since the recommendation is for a crisp, wafer cookie, I'd recommend Anna's Swedish ginger thins that are available in the grocery store.  Or Moravian ginger snaps but those are often seasonal and harder to find.
25 minutes was too long for my lemon slices, (maybe they were too thin?). I would definitely keep a watchful eye the next time
Does this pie freeze well? I would love to make it in advance!

Is there any way to make this the night before to save time? Even partially, like prepare the filling the night before, chill, then add it to the pie crust to set before serving the following day? It says best served within 24 hours, just looking to keep the crust as crisp as possible.
i have the same question...i cant imagine why not but do wonder
I’m having a hard time visualizing how you could use your fingertips to work sliced ginger and strips of lemon peel into something fine enough to look like wet sand. Anyone done this yet?
Really good for a sore throat too!
I added a little powdered sugar to the cream topping. It was a little tart for me. Overall though it’s delicious!
Haven’t tried this yet but wonder - what do you do with all the egg whites? Couldn’t you use them for topping and forget all cholesterol laden cream?

Dorie Greenspan has a great tiger cake recipe that calls for 6 egg whites: https://cooking.nytimes.com/recipes/1022947-chocolate-and-almond-tiger-cake I freeze my leftover egg whites, thaw in fridge or in a dish of warm water if i'm in a hurry. they come back perfect.

can the filling can be prepped day ahead and stored in fridge, then assembled with crust on serving day? I don't have a 4-hour window to chill this on the day it's served and dont want the filling clagging up the crust overnight.

Made this tonight for Friendsgiving and it was deeeelicious. I baked the crust and made the lemon filling the day before and it was not at all soggy. I did make some substitutions: 1) gluten free Graham crackers due to celiac, 2) after tasting the whipped cream and crème fraîche mixture I added about a tablespoon of sugar, and 3) I was too lazy to make the lemon topping so I used some finely diced candied ginger. So great! Got many compliments.

Delicious, but I thought the lemon slice garnish was inedible, and didn’t contribute much. Next time I’m going to sprinkle candied ginger on top. In my version, the honey flavor overpowered the ginger flavor.

i have mixed feelings about this recipe. the custard was delicious and the crust as well, but the sour cream just ruined the topping for me, it was heavy and ran into the shell when cut. Also the sliced lemon decoration is a waste if it cant be eaten. I did make the custard a day ahead and assemble day of. The crust stayed crisp and needed all the butter, using Lars ginger snaps. A layer of candied lemon rind on the shell would also prevent it from getting claggy if held longer.
It was a hit and I have made it again upon request. I followed the recipe, no substitutes. T
pie didn't set overnight, remained basically liquid. tasted great, but unusable

This was a great addition to our Thanksgiving feast! Tweaked it to accommodate the younger palates in our group by adding a bit of confectioners sugar to the whipped cream omitted the sour cream. 
Also after reading several reviewers regret the lack of ginger flavor coming through in the custard, I decided used the microplane to finely grate the zest and ginger root. The fibers filtered when sieved after cooking and we definitely tasted the ginger! It brought a nice complexity to the lemon.
Not sure how the lemon slices for the topping are supposed to bake, looks the juice of the lemon burned. We tried some other methods in the toaster oven and still the juice bakes out and burns

Delicious!
The ginger was not very pronounced, but nice and lemony. 
The crust was more finicky than I anticipated.  I used some thin ginger cookies I found at Whole Foods.  
Every part of the recipe needed more cooking time.
This was the big hit at Thanksgiving. Next time I make it, I will try grating the ginger to get a bit more ginger flavor in the pudding.
Absolutely delicious! Pudding took about 15 min to come together instead of 3 on our glass electric stove. Also added 1 tbsp to the whipped topping to sweeten it slightly.

Super good! A very bright but not too tart lemon flavor and not as cloyingly sweet as some lemon meringue pies, nice creamy lemon. The ginger was hidden but I might not have used enough. Served with plain whipped crem.

The tastes were great, but there was a major problem with the pie filling. It does not sit right and it was liquid. Even though I put it in the freezer it was still to thin and was runny. I suggest probably have a more egg whites & a bit more corn starch.
The crust was delicious, but it needs to also be made with very hard crusty type of ginger snaps do not use voice ones. And it needs more ginger snaps in it but it was delicious.
I think you may have needed to cook the filling a little longer. It went from liquid to thick quickly with enough time.
This pie has trouble setting properly. It runs very thin. Just be aware

Tate's GF gingersnaps are great but have tiny pieces of candied ginger so crust has bumps. Crust slumped although the flavor was phenomenal. Full 2t. of vanilla overwhelmed the lemon flavor so I'd cut that in half next time. The ginger flavor in the filling was barely discernible. One idea would be heating the cream to 105-110 and then letting the ginger and lemon sit in the warm cream for an hour before straining them out to bruise with the sugar.

this pie was a HUGE hit at Thanksgiving dinner this year - made it exactly according to the recipe with no substitutions and I agree with the other comments saying that grated ginger is probably better here instead of sliced. Pie came out super lemon forward and I couldn't taste much ginger with the exception of the crust. I used Trader Joe's triple ginger snaps and they were perfectly suited for the recipe. 11/10 would make again!
This was amazing.  Best dish at Thanksgiving and we had a lot of great choices.

I made this exactly as directed. It was almost excellent, but everyone thought it was just too lemony. It was very sour, which I almost never say, as a huge citrus person. I would make this again, but I will slightly reduce the amount of lemon next time.

Great addition to the Thanksgiving pie lineup. I listened to the other comments about it not being gingery enough and grated the ginger instead. With the gingersnap crust and the grated ginger you get a nice ginger flavor although the lemon is still very strong.

this was just fine; it wasn't the greatest pie ever. i think leaving out the honey would have made it better (the honey just flattened the flavor a bit, and it fought with the lemon and ginger). i wouldn't make it again!

Following others advice, I used thin ginger snaps.  Perfect!  Custard came together quickly (watched my grandma make homemade pudding on the stove so I knew what to watch for).  Pie set in fridge - 2 hours with chilled crust and was delicious.  Substitutions:  used 1/16 c maple syrup and 1/16 c honey which I felt gave it enough sweetness.  Added extra fresh ginger as well.  Served with Meyer lemon thin cookies instead of lemon slices.
A big hit at my friendsgiving! Will definitely make again!

This was a fabulous pie, the clear favorite of our Thanksgiving desserts.  I will definitely make this again.  The only issue I had was that my lemon slices didn't come out great.  I think I may have cut them a bit too thick, next time I'll be sure to slice them as thinly as possible.

I made a 7” version of this which was half a recipe.
I used graham crackers because  couldn’t find the right ginger snaps but added extra ginger. I have made graham cracker crusts forever with no egg so left it out here; the pie is plenty rich.

We loved this pie and the sour cream/ whipped cream topping was great. I was too lazy to make the slices lemons, added a little crystallized ginger on top. Five stars.
This filling and topping were so good. But I’d swap out a butter crust. The ginger snap crust ended up mushy. But I will definitely make this again!

Used Tate’s ginger cookies for the crust - expensive but worth the cost for a delicious and non soggy crust! Recommend letting the sugar/lemon/ginger mixture to sit for several hours if you’ve got the time. We also set the strained solids in the cream before whipping the topping to infuse the flavors. Grateful for the comment about the curd magic happening around 10 minutes—3 minutes is not nearly enough! Keep whisking.

Loved this. Served for family Thanksgiving and got so many compliments. Only had an 11” tart pan on hand so made 1.5x the recipe and it worked. Added more ginger than the recipe called for and still couldn’t taste it. Next time I will add more.
My sister made this for Thanksgiving. Oh my gosh it was SO good. Like a lemon meringue pie but so creamy. I loved it! Very pretty too.

My pie is delicious, however the crust is hard and tough. I found my answer below, I think, "There’s the thin wafers that this recipe calls for and the thicker, more cookie-like ginger snaps. I used the cookie style ones and they are far too dense and moist. Still tasted fantastic, but the crust was almost like toffee."
Also agree that it took far longer than 3 min. on the stove for the filling to thicken & boil.

Tried making this today. Everything is incredibly tasty, but...

Unfortunately the pudding/custard (whatever it is) didn't set and was incredibly runny after sitting in the fridge for 4-5 hours. I kept whisking it on the stove for ~5 minutes, and even though I never got it to the thick texture the recipe describes, I figured that had to have been enough. I guess not! I see several comments about whisking for longer, so I'll have to do that next time.
This wasn’t anything special, needed a lot more ginger, we couldn’t even taste it. It just tasted like a lemon meringue

Delicious pie that my family and friends love! I would definitely double the amount of ginger as others have said it’s very lemon dominant and triple the amount of time for the pudding on the stove for it to thicken. I’ve made this twice and the first time I only did the 3 minutes and it never set so I had to freeze it! But it was still tasty. I used Trader Joe’s triple ginger cookie thins which are inexpensive and yummy. I would appreciate the amount of cookie crumbs in cups versus ounces or grams. Not helpful.
@djfrantzen buy a kitchen scale. They aren’t expensive and makes your baking results so much better.

Really yummy pie. Rich but not too sweet, especially with the whipped topping. I used Tates gluten free ginger zinger cookies and crust turned out ok, a little tough and clumpy because of the crystallized ginger. I tried grating the ginger and added a packet of Trader Joe’s ginger powdered drink and still not a super gingery flavor, I bet I could have doubled the ginger to really have it come through. But all worked out because not everyone at my function 
is a huge ginger fan.

I can't fathom why you'd freeze ginger before peeling. Have you ever tasted the difference between fresh squeezed orange juice, and orange juice from frozen concentrate? Same concept. And ginger microplanes very easily.

I personally find that fresh ginger when frozen whole is much easier to microplane and the flavor isn't compromised, although I those frozen cubes of pre-grated ginger don't work as well as fresh flavor-wise
@Fresh Ginger Freezing does not itself create a "concentrate", so the comparison is rather apples to orange juice.
I have to chime in and say it took about 8 minutes at med high on a new gas range for mine to thicken. Other than that, easy and popular at the first BBQ of the year.

Our guests loved this. We doubled the amount of ginger and the balance was perfect. Also, I used about half of the cream topping for the pie. It was helpful because it cut the sweetness a little bit. We served limoncello with it – I highly recommend that.

I just wanted to check that it is balanced without sugar in the topping? I know there is honey drizzled on top but wouldn’t it be a little blah with cream and sour cream on top without any sugar in it?

I have made this many times and it is better than I remember each time. Don’t skimp on the ginger- and grating it helps as well as a little lemon zest. It doesn’t have as much flavor if you strain the ginger out.

Three minutes on medium high is not long enough for the custard to set. This took me two attempts, and 7-8 minutes was the magic window. When it sets, you'll know. If you're not sure, keep heating and stirring.

title: Caramelized Brussels Sprouts With Lemon Recipe
author: Claire Saffitz

comments.commentBody: Wait, I thought the author was trying to make a simple and basic Thanksgiving meal! Separating the outer leaves from the core of a Brussels sprout to cook them in stages is not happening while I am trying to make gravy, mash potatoes, carve the turkey, check on the rolls, dish up the cranberry sauce and get it all plated and served before the food is stone cold. I won’t even add zesting a lemon over the hot sprouts pan!
You can prep the sprouts ahead of time!  And if you're still zesting on a box grater, definitely buy yourself a microplane zester - it changed my life!  :)

Well, this seems pretty simplistic. Roast the cores. Roast the leaves.
There is at least a glaze, mainly balsamic but probably more complex, that is developed during the cooking process by the restaurants that really master Brussels Sprouts. Those Sprouts that make you drool through the entire appetizer. 
That technique is what needs to be shared.
@stevrino Restaurants oftentimes throw the sprouts into a deep fryer. That’s how the get them so crispy.

Question regarding roasting instructions.  When the core parts are done, “ until the tip of a paring knife slides into the cores with just a little resistance” are those put into a bowl and set aside until the leaves are baked or are the leaves put atop the already cooked cores, then all are roasted together?  Or do I need another roasting pan just for the leaves?

Great questions on the technique. Seems what is recommended here is to do the cores, move them to a serving dish, and then do the leaves. What's missing is the process that changes simple heat-based caramelization to a flavored caramelization that is irresistible.
No. The leaves are roasted in the same pan, over the cores.
Great tips on how to prepare this dish. Thanks for the refresher on technique.
I preheat the cookie sheet in the oven to preheat prior to adding the brussel sprouts, made a big difference for me...

Making my second batch!! Separating the leaves during cooking makes all the difference!! People fussing about they don’t have time for this? Super easy and can be separated ahead of time, use a micro plane.. I mean it doesn’t get any easier? And spatchcock the turkey and save all that oven time!
Actually the perfect roasted brussel sprout recipe. Simple and extremely tasty.
These were good but a lot of work for an average return.
Preparation is tedious & quite long but these are the best roasted brussels sprouts I've tasted!!
My 13 year old daughter said these were delicious and she rarely eats anything green.  I have to agree with her.  Taking off the outer leaves makes a big difference.
I made as directed and it came out great! Served with a couscous salad and roasted mushrooms for a light dinner.
Great basic method for roasting brussels sprouts.  I have also followed these instructions but used other seasonings during roasting for variety.  Always good.

Seems a safe bet that Claire has multiple ovens. But if you only have one, like many/most of us do, you can only crank it up for that last-minute leaf-crisping blitz, if you have nothing else finishing up or staying warm. Still, I'm gonna try to pull these off for Thanksgiving -- unless my sister-in-law, who's hosting, vetoes it. I'm happy to be the sous chef this year, rather than the lead, even if it means being overruled!
Very nice recipe!  Spreading the leaves over makes for a visually and texturally interesting dish, and the lemon, lightly done, is perfect.
these are SO GOOD. the peeling is tedious--a good task to do with others. i've really struggled with making brussels sprouts and i'm so glad i've found my recipe!
This delicious. It’s not nearly the time drain you might think.

I'd like to put in a plug for looser sprouts. The larger less tightly packed ones are better tasting to me. I won't even buy the tight ones.They go nicely cut in half or 1/4's depending on size,with a few minutes in the microwave. Then EVOO and salt.

A simpler method:
Quarter the sprouts.
Heat pan with olive oil to Med High, add some chilli flake.
Sear sprouts, they should occasionally go "pop".  Don't over crowd and steam them.
Turn and brown and after one or two turns, add garlic and a bit more oil on the garlic.  It will cook

When still a bit crunchy, add either two splashes of water or white wine to steam them off.

Remove from heat.

Finish with:  Cold butter, lemon and salt.  Toss, done.

Notes;  Adding the salt last is best.
Too much lemon
I had a similar prep of sprouts at an Amazon Christmas party, but the last minute glaze was maple syrup.  The sweetness balance the sometimes bitter taste of the sprouts perfectly.
If I make them ahead of time, what is the best way to reheat?
The outer leaves are spotted and grungy.  Do you really want to cook with them?
Fresh sprouts will not look like that.

I followed the recipe but susbstituted Tajin for the lemon, salt and pepper, mixing it with the oil. A second time, I did the same but usted a heavy cast iron skillet on the stove top. Both times were great. Separating the outer leaves and adding them later makes for a better dish, leas charring and more caramelization.
This was really good!
Made for thanksgiving- easy and tasty
If there is a 30-35 min cooking time, then a 10-15 min cooking time, why does it say total cooking time is 1 hour, 20 minutes??  Am I missing something?
Can you make them the day before and reheat while turkey is resting?
Has anyone tried this with frozen brussel sprouts? If yes, how would you adjust the roasting time—and would you thaw in microwave?
Can you do the first roast ahead of time, then do the final roast with the leaves just before serving?  We are juggling oven space so this would be really help.
This was great. Will make Brussels sprouts this way from now on. I don’t cook the outer leaves though I’m sure that would be good too.
Can I take off the outer leaves and discard them? In past roasting experiences they always burn!
Belated, brought this to a Thanksgiving potluck, mostly to get more veggies on the table. It was a huge hit, simple and exactly what's needed to balance out the other rich dishes of the day.
Just too oily for me
As someone who makes homemade broth on the regular, this recipe really takes it to the next level.  The depth of flavor was noticeable. Only making broth this way from now on.
This recipe should be included on its own in the Claire Saffitz Thanksgiving Recipe collection. Also, it’s amazing and my kitchen smells so good.
can anything be done with all the chicken parts once it's been strained?--feed to the cat?  make chicken salad? or should it be tossed
Toss it. All the flavor has already been extracted.

This is my go-to bone broth recipe (doubling the simmer time). I always add some chunked up daikon, sliced ginger, and sometimes dried mushrooms. I also double the recipe, roasting on a half sheet. It works fine to pour the wine on while still hot and scrape all the goodness into my big ol’ stock pot. Thanks, Claire!!
okay sorry to be stupid but how is this chicken gravy if there is no chicken in it, and why would anyone have turkey giblets and wings on hand *before* Thanksgiving? I have never made gravy...
You can use chicken or turkey parts to make the stock- then you end up with either chicken or turkey gravy.  Most markets sell turkey wings.
Recipe includes 2 pounds of chicken wings. The recipe is for broth to be used when making the gravy.

The recipe calls for chicken OR turkey wings. Chicken wings are usually easier to find and probably cheaper. You usually buy a turkey before Thanksgiving. When you get the turkey and start to brine it (usually a few days before thanksgiving) you'll take out the neck and the giblets. So - this is meant to be done a few days before Thanksgiving. This is a recipe for stock, not gravy. You'll use this stock to make your gravy. She's got a good recipe on here for the stock too :) Hope that helps!
This is excellent.  I've used it (and changed it as necessary) and it is always very very good.  Passing it down to my children as my own.  :)
@Karen That’s a legendary move and I’m stealing it!
Making it now.  Be aware that turkey wings are much larger than chicken wings.  I ended up cutting the drummette off my 2 wings to get down to 2 lbs.
Lid or no lid when roasting? Assuming no lid to maximize browned bits but will keep a close eye on the veg

This broth recipe was simple to prepare (the day prior to Thanksgiving w/ very light seasoning), delicious and from now on, will be a staple on my Thanksgiving preparation menu. My family raved about the tasty flavors of the gravy and bread stuffing; both of which were made using generous amounts of this broth.
What could be used as a substitute for white wine?
I made this last night - full week before Thanksgiving. Do I need to freeze it or can I just keep in fridge? Smelled and tasted amazing.
If you want to keep it for a week, you should cool it and then freeze it

If I make this today, am I good for T-giving?
I’m trying to get as much done ahead of time as I can.
I’m surprised that it only holds for a couple days in the fridge.
ND
Can I use a chicken carcass instead of wings? I have one ready for a stock that would be great to use.
I did, and definitely not as gelatinous or AMAZING as everyone else's broth seems to be but still tasty.
We get Costco roast chicken all the time, and save up all their carcasses and use those to make stock. Works great!
Great, classic stock recipe. The only thing I added to this was the turkey back bone from spatchcocking it. When set up in the fridge the stock was like jello, just how it should be.

Incredible flavor and gelatinous broth .  Used 10 chicken wings, no giblets.  And when preparing the gravy, the roasted turkey drippings.  Now my go-to whenever we need great gravy.  And when is that not the case?

Second “zcooks”. A revelation to a regular broth maker. Used mostly rabbit - poor thing was too old for anything else lol and a turkey neck. Less gelatinous bc well… rabbit, but delicious and versatile
Best broth ever. Made it in March from frozen organic chicken backs—the fragrance and flavor are beyond any broth I’ve ever made. Thank you!

Fabulous recipe, and also very versatile. We’ve adapted this recipe to use as our go to scraps recipe and it always turns out amazingly! It’s great as written but we triple the peppercorn when making it specifically for Italian wedding soup and it’s phenomenal.
Instead of water, can you use chicken broth? Or is that bird overload?

Yes, that's overload.
This recipe is to make chicken broth. You don't need chicken broth to make chicken broth.

Big learning experience for me! 

Salt only at the end. 

Salt early → flat flavor because it suppresses reduction.
Salt late → deeper, brighter flavor.

This is Claire’s pie crust technique from her book What’s for Dessert and it makes such a great crust! So glad to see it published here. Before anyone feels the need to say their technique is better, please try this out! She has a video on YouTube with King Arthur showing how it’s done.

I've been making all butter crusts for decades. Perhaps, the easiest route to a workable crust that is flaky, buttery, irresistible is using iced vodka instead of iced water.

The reason is simple science: Water bonds with the glutens in starch; therefore, the more water, the less flaky the crust; however, without enough liquid the dough can be too crumbly. Iced Vodka allows for a workable AND flaky crust and almost all of the alcohol cooks off.
Years ago I heard an interview on NPR’s Fresh Air with Christopher Kimball of America’s Test Kitchen/Cooks Illustrated.  He recommended the same thing.
@Vince Sounds great by I never had any luck with vodka. It makes the crust stiff and craggy.
@FY I - agree. Once you get the hang of a light touch, you’ll never make crust any other way.

I always make my pie crust dough in the food processor.  The secret is to stop the food processor the SECOND the dough stops being fairly level in the machine and gathers itself into a ball (there will be bits still not incorporated).  Then a few quick squishes on the counter to get it all stuck together, wrap, into fridge for at least 15 minutes.

I also start with just a bit less ice water than the recipe suggests.  Then if the dough doesn't gather quite quickly, I add a bit more.

Bibi:  food processor is the way to go.  Once you cut the butter into small chunks and add ri rhe flour, 2-3 good pulses gets you your pea sized pieces of butter in the flour.  Now you can add 1/4 cup of ICE WATER.  Too much water and the dough is soupy, too little and the dough is crumbly.  Run the foon processor unti the dough forms a nice ball.   You are ready to roll out.  I use the Jacques method and rollout the dough ball without refrigeration.  I get a flaky crust and very time.
Good, but too much water to add at once. Add a few tablespoons at a time until it has the right feel, then stop, no matter how much water is left over. Pie dough is finicky.

for FP mixing, I give the dry ingredients a couple of pulses to blend.  Cube the butter and let freeze for ~ hour.  pulse butter w/dry until butter looks like pea-sized lumps (~ 10-14 pulses).  Add ice cold water all at once & pulse 4-5 times - dough will still be scraggy. 
Turn out/scrape onto a board and press/form into a flat disk for chilling
@Irene G. This is the method on AllRecipes and it is easy and the result is excellent.
How is 11/2 cup of flour 202 grams when a cup of flour is 125 grams?

@Carlton there is some disagreement about the weight of a cup of flour. The standards are 128gm, 135gm, and 140gm, depending upon who is writing the recipe. Based on her final weight of 202gm, it looks like this is calculated on a the 135gm/cup standard.

Been making pie crust since I was eight or nine. Four simple ingredients 2/3 cup cold Crisco, a few Tbs ice water,  2 cups AP flour and pinch of salt salt.  Cut crisco into flour/salt with pastry cutter or 2 dinner knives, stir in ice water a tbs at a time until it starts to form a ball.  Wrap in plastic wrap and put in fridge for 30 - 60 minutes, roll out between 2 sheets of wax paper, peel off one sheet, flip pastry into pie dish, crimp edge and you are done.

Many of the folks commenting here have their own way of making a crust. So do I. However, I am not sure why disclosing that, briefly or in depth, is helpful to  those of us who think this crust sounds pretty interesting, are considering trying it, and are looking for insights from those who have tried it successfully.

@stefanie

Many of us would likely prefer butter over hydrogenated Crisco.

I'm so very happy this recipe doesn't require the use of a food processor!! I get so tired of Perfect Easy Pie Crust recipes that rely on having one: you're using a food processor. Of course it's "easy." So thank you, Ms. Saffitz!
Please make this at least once the way she instructs! So very flaky. I omitted the sugar for a crust to top pot pie and it was amazing

A pastry blender works much better and faster than a bench scraper.  It has more cutting blades and its shape makes it easy to work the butter and flour mixture while it's still in the bowel.  That way, you can add in the water and form the dough right in the bowel instead of having to dump a loose mixture of dough clumps onto the work surface.  Much less of a mess that way.

One more vote for the recipe as written - have been making it from Claire's book for the last year and it's delicious and versatile! Works well for galette and other flaky pastry in addition to pie crust. 

I sometimes wrap and chill the dough for a while after it initially comes together, before doing the stacking/flattening step and chilling again - helps it cohere without adding more water.

I made this crust today using the Youtube video. Love the technique of pouring the dough onto the countertop. Mine dough was very dry with these measurements so I added a few extra tablespoons of water as it wasn't coming together the way it should, and it turned out great! I think this recipe is a great guidepost and technique but seasoned pie makers will tell you pie dough his finicky and IT will tell YOU what IT needs, so keep your eyes open and be ready to deviate from the recipe as written.

This recipe worked like a charm! I’ve been using my mother-in-law’s recipe that has both butter and Crisco in it. This is better, don’t tell Mom.

Stacking dough quarters is brilliant! I also smeared the butter into the flour with the heel of my hand, I forget where I learned that but it really helps with lamination.

Thank you Claire!
@Christine - The heel-of-your-hand smearing technique is in Julia Child’s method (which also includes Crisco) in “The Way to Cook.”

This technique is and isn't more or less what I do. And it doesn't really matter what I do because it seems that everyone has their own favorite technique. I keep reading about other's method because I am open to change. But the only thing I'm not going to change is my hands on methodology. I do not want to use a food processor. It's important to me to do it by hand with simple tools.
This is a recipe for pie dough, not a recipe for pie crust.

To keep the butter from melting, I wear glove liners under nitrile gloves. Then, when I press the butter into the flour, my warm fingers make no contacts--just the cooler insulated gloves. Has made great laminated pie crusts like this before.
i get flakey crusts every time with lard. no chilling, no layering.
You have made a simple process incredibly complicated. Flour and fat, keep it cold. Tada.
I concur. It felt like it was trying to be too artisany.

Used this recipe twice for pecan slab pie. Followed it exactly and watched videos twice. Weighed ingredients and did not overwork dough. Came out flakey and tasty, but tough as heck. Could barely cut with chefs knife. No idea what I did wrong, but am moving on to different crust recipe for sure at this point.
Has anyone tried this with vegan butter?
I don't know about vegan butter, but you could certainly use butter-flavored vegetable shortening (e.g., Crisco) instead of regular butter.
I’m German and read this recipe. But it ended without baking? Like do you eat it raw lol? Where is the baking? Our cakes and pies look completely different, but this was a weird raw ending

Many pie recipes call for unbaked pie crusts.. that’s just the recipe for the unbaked pie crust.
Have a look at the recipe for the caramelized apple pie for the baking times.
A useful tip from 1960's CA State Fair: substitute 7-Up for the water. It prevents a tough crust if you must re-knead.

No pie crust recipe can beat J. Kenji Lopez-Alt's. He uses a food processor, yes, but with a specific (easy) technique that ensures 100% consistent and deliciously flaky results. There are no recipe instructions less helpful than "until the largest bits are about the size of a pea." It's purely subjective and doesn't describe any pie crust I've ever made. This is absolutely the only way to go, and once you try it, you'll never go back: https://www.seriouseats.com/easy-pie-dough-recipe
Can you make a pie crust without a food processor, or should I just keep buying the pre-made? (Somehow my grandmother must have!)

Indeed you can! If you cut the cold butter into smaller bits, you can get by with a fork to mash the butter into the flour, or better, a pastry blender (inexpensive, hand-held tool). I still use the pastry blender as often as I do a FP - both work fine. The trick is to add the water gradually, mix lightly, and stop adding water as soon as the dough comes together. After decades of making pie crust, I still find each is unique - the ability of flour to absorb water varies quite a bit.
of course you can make a crust without a food processor - in fact, the recipe you are commenting on doesn't use a food processor.
This recipe doesn't use one! Check it out.

The directions regarding ice water are confusing. The ingredients list half a cup or 120 milliliters. The directions say add a third cup of the ice water straining out the ice and discarding the remainder. Are you worried someone is going to put ice cubes in the flour. Maybe that happens, but I almost put in 120 ml ice cold water, it never occurring to me that I should prepare a half cup of water with ice in it then extract a third cup of the water. I thought ice cold water would do.

I watched Claire’s YouTube video and also read the more detailed instructions for this recipe on the KAF website, and thought I’d followed everything exactly, but turned out terrible for me—flaky yes but extremely tough.
Did you follow the recipe from King Arthur? I noticed the KAF recipe says 450 grams of flour while the what’s for dessert book says 405 grams.
Ummm... i don't have either of the brands of kosher salt.  So what do I do?  All the other ingredients are given weight values except the salt.  Dumb.

Tim, if you have regular Morton table salt (other table salt brands should be similar) use 1/2 teaspoon in this recipe in place of 1 teaspoon Diamond Kosher.
Here’s what I use for salt conversions:
1 tsp. table salt = 1 1/2 tsp. Morton kosher salt = 2 tsp. Diamond Crystal kosher salt
BTW,  Claire’s book she says she uses Diamond Kosher and that the conversion for Morton Kosher is actually closer to 1 tsp. Morton’s Kosher for 1-3/4 tsp. Diamond, but she uses a 1 to 2 ratio for ease.

This method, as opposed to using the FP, results in larger bits of butter which results in a much flakier top crust. I done it both ways many times. The stacking is also key. Just try it her way once.

Question for all you European butter lovers out there: Have you tried this with European butter? Do you have any tricks? I live in Germany and I want to make this, but every time I bake American recipes with the butter here, it's a flop.
Unclear. Is this a recipe for a single-crust pie? Her video shows her making a double crust pie and creating two rectangles to chill. This recipe suggests it's for an open face pie.
It says in the first paragraph that you can double the recipe to make two crusts.

I grew up with adults using Crisco.
There are health reasons that I, along with others, don't use Crisco.

I started making pie crusts before I was 10. I am now 68 and haven't used Crisco for decades.

If you only intend for 80 ml of water to be used. Only say 80ml water. Not 120.
even on second attempt with correct amount this did not turn out.

I made this for four pies this Thanksgiving, and just had the first one - sadly the crust turned out absolutely terribly. Tough, chewy, etc - I followed the instructions as written but sadly did not get a good crust.

Comment on the question raised by Carlton regarding weight vs volume of flour. The answer is probably the brand of flour used. King Arthur flour has a density of 120 g/cup. Bob’s Red Hill flour is more dense. It is 136 g/cup. If the flour used is the more dense brand, that gives a weight of 1.5 cups equal to 206 g, which is nearly equal to the 202 g cited in the recipe. This begs the question of how to follow the (confusing) direction in the recipe. Any help?

We made this exactly the way it is written and watched her video several times. 4 pies were basically inedible due to the toughness of the crust. We had to lift the pies out of the pan onto a cutting board and saw at them with a serrated knife. No one could cut their crust with a fork (or knife!) so we just scraped the filling off and ate it without crust. We have no idea why. We could see pieces of butter in the dough and barely handled it. It was flaky, but the flakes were very chewy.

I was excited to use this recipe and technique and also watched the video. I make great pie crust, but I was intrigued with the method, and I'm always looking for ways to improve. Sadly, I am in total agreement with E. I ended up with 4 pies with very tough crusts. Every one of them was so tough it was quite difficult to cut through the crust while slicing the pies into servings. And the tough crust made for very chewy eating. I'm going back to my old recipe.
Not very good recipe. Crust came out crunchy, not flaky.
Best pie crust ever. So, so delicious and flaky. Almost like puff pastry. Thank you for this fantastic recipe.

This turned out like puff pastry for me! It’s not bad, just unexpected for a pie. 
Lessons learned (after 2 attempts): 
1) use 1/3 cup or 80ml water, whichever is the smaller. 
2) follow the written instruction more than the video (I.e. the size of chickpeas, the size of peas). The first time, I was OCD about replicating the video (I.e. adding water after breaking butter chunks in half, leaving sizable pieces of butter), the result was a disastrous bread dough interspersed with butter chunks.

I have made this twice as directed. It makes decent pie dough, but it requires a lot fussing around. I have made A LOT of pie dough recipes over the years, and this didn’t yield a product that was superior to other recipes that don’t require the lamination step. Also, like others I have found the resulting crust to be very flaky but not as tender as I’d hoped. Finally, it did not freeze welI for me. I suggest Cook’s Illustrated, Martha Stewart, or Julia Child (Baking with Julia) instead.

Is this recipe for a one crust pie or a two crust pie? 
Thanks- planning on giving it a try this Thanksgiving.
This is for a one crust pie! (At least, according to a recipe with identical proportions in her book Dessert Person).
I made this for pumpkin pie twice. My new go-to pie dough. It’s flawless - perfectly flakey and tender (like me). No need to change a thing!

If you’re like me and look at the ingredients list of a recipe as a guide for what to put in a recipe please note that “1/2 cup” of water is not what the recipe calls for in the actual directions. “Use 1/3 of a cup of water and discard the rest”. Would have saved me if the ingredients list was the ingredients.

Love this method. Forgiving and easy for beginners— it’s a hands on, like playing with play dough. I appreciate a no special equipment method. Can be done quickly! 

I used ice water, and it turned out beautifully.
Can you make this in food processor? I’m new to pie crusts and would like some advice.

I’ve made pie crust in the food processor and it works amazingly well. Blends up very fast and no strain on your hands. The trick is not to do it for too long or the crust will toughen up. Do a few quick pulses and check it. That might be enough
I always make my all butter crust in a food processor. I like the consistency of the mixture and the ease of forming the dough.

I'm a bit confused by the "roll over the wrapped dough with the rolling pin to flatten and force it to fill out the plastic or food wrap" - How thin are you supposed to roll it at this point? just a tad, to be rolled out after chilling - or rolled to fit the pie pan, and then chilled???

2 thoughts: A) I've been using Kenji's updated (post-vodka) trick in the food processor, which holds back 1/4 of the flour to coat the tiny bits once they're formed; hasn't ever failed me in probably 50 (double!) crusts now. I'd love to try Claire's technique to save hauling out and cleaning the food processor, but with that reserved-flour twist and see what happens.  And B) this reminds me of a GREAT rough-puff (@BonApp) I love and - looking it up - surprise! it's from Claire! (but uses milk).
In all my decades of making pastry doughs, I have never gotten the hang of rolling a square piece into a round. Can anyone help with this?

someone else mentioned that there is a youtube of Claire showing you how to make this dough. I just watched it and I'm not kidding when I say it's pie-making life-changing. So many things I now understand. the video is for her apple pie but most of it is just on this dough technique. https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=DcCa_dHHu30
@Jen great idea putting the video of Claire showing how she makes the dough !

Interesting to hear about FP use for pie dough. Is it easy to over process and get a rubbery dough?  I agree with the water quantity in the recipe. As has been mentioned previously,  I usually add 4-5 Tbs of ice cold water initially and then one Tbs at a time until I get the consistency I want in the dough.
Hello, we are new pie bakers here- so it looks like this does not need to be par baked prior to putting in the filling?
Whether or not you prebake your crust depends more on the filling you will be using.

I am intrigued. I'd been an avid FP with frozen butter cruster for over a decade. Then one day I worked refrigerated butter into my flour with my fingertips because I didn't want to drag the FP out. If SoCal can stay cool for Thanksgiving I'm going to try this method.

I always use my hands to combine the butter, shortening, flour and salt. Note the shortening use. I find it easier to work the dough, plus I prefer less butter. As for using your fingers to breakup the butter and combine the shortening, it’s easy and therapeutic.  Works every time. I learned this technique from Julia Child. And yes, add cold water sparingly.
Substitute cake flour for 1/3 of the AP, use ghee instead of butter, and use a mixture of leaf lard/ghee 50/50. Cut the leaf lard in first.
I am allergic to gluten. Can you use gluten free flour in this recipe?

I am sure it would work just since that is the point of good flour replacer.
Why don't you try it, see, and let us know your results which I am sure will be just fine.
This recipe has changed my pie crust making — it’s so flaky and gorgeous!
This was easy peasy and the result was a super flaky pie crust. No fm needed. I went by the grams and the 202gr worked.
Can you make a gluten-free pie crust?
This is the best pie crust recipe I’ve used, and yields the easiest dough to work with by a mile. I did skip the bench scraper technique and it still turned out great.
How big is the plastic bag I roll the dough into? Sandwich? Quart? And if no plastic back then how big a disc (and how thick) is the dough when you put it into the fridge?
1-1/2 cups of flour is 180 grams. According to the flour packaging 1/4 cup is 30 grams. Be sure an correct this - that's a lot flour for 10 grams of butter. Someone is going to be very frustrated!

There is something wrong here. The flour weight (202 grams) seems high for 1 1/2 cups of flour AND the same recipe printed on the King Arthur site (with video) calls for 3 3/4 cups (450 grams) of flour. On King Arthur version, she doubles the recipe but still, doubling the flour in this NYT recipe would mean 3 cups, not 3 3/4 cups. All the other ingredients are the same. So, that means the amount of flour is off somewhere. It seems like people have tried this as is so, I’ll just try. Woo hoo!
This is the best pie crust I have ever made!
This was so good! The bit of lamination made it really light.

I probably didn't do the first part right. Perhaps I would have more success next time. My old technique of using a wire pastry cutter is better at incorporating the butter into the flour. I would like to try the layering thing another time though.
I followed the recipe measurements exactly and made 4 pie shells.  I used my food processor however. The recipe calls for too much water. Very disappointed.

I wish Claire would chime in on the flour discrepancy.  I just made it as written using  202g of King Arthur AP flour but had to add an extra couple of teaspoons of water. Next time I would use 180g which, as people have noted, is the correct weight for 1-1/2 cups flour (per King Arthur Baking which is Claire’s preferred flour). I used 1 tsp Diamond Crystal Kosher salt but, if you have table salt, use 1/2 teaspoon. I think the technique is excellent!

I really like this recipe, but I am having a lot of butter melt and it is ending up at the bottom of my oven.  Anyone else have this problem?  I chilled the dough for several hours, as directed in her video.
this dough looked amazing ... and tasted like card board.  Have no idea where I went wrong.  Will go back to my dough that falls apart but tastes fantastic.

I'm a baking novice but got ambitious for Thanksgiving. Made this recipe successfully three times, for Claire's caramelized apple pie and pecan slab pie. I highly highly recommend watching the video linked above and finding the part where she walks you through making the dough. Seeing how she works the butter into the flour with a dough scraper and knowing what the texture was supposed to look like at each step was immensely helpful. Also, who knew making pie dough is like free therapy haha.

This was my first time making pie crust and it was a blast. I loved the messy with little cleanup approach this dough had. I used this recipe to make mini pies and they came out great, buttery and flaky. One note, make sure you're only using 1/3c cup water in the dough. The ingredients call for 1/2 cup, but you won't need it all.

This makes a fantastic dough - easily the most workable pie crust I've ever made. I could be wrong, but I believe she's added the lamination step since What's for Dessert, where she has you chop it into fourths then pile them on top of one another? Either way, the result is a dough with chunks of butter of different sizes, layered on top of one another so that it's easy to roll and so flaky!
Chilled in fridge several days before making

The King Arthur recipe calls for 450g of flour for a double crust recipe. This NYT recipe calls for 202g for a single crust which equals 404g for a double crust. Is there an explanation for the discrepancy?
This is the best pie crust recipe I have ever made! Comes out perfect every time. And taste delicious!!

If you’re making this pie dough in hot/humid weather—the food processor is unfortunately the only way to go. Otherwise, the butter will not stand a chance unless you have AC in your kitchen. Even with frozen butter and the processor, the dough will not look crumbly like in the video of Claire making it. You can’t avoid the butter getting a little melty, so just work fast and get it into the freezer asap. Roughly 100ml of ice water should be enough to hydrate the dough in hot weather (30 deg C).
Don’t like. Lost structure while baking

I am a big pie lover. This is by far the best pie crust I have made, and have tried many versions over the years. This technique has better results than using a food processor, pastry blender/cutter, vodka... her method of mixing in the butter, plus stacking the pastry like with puff pastry, makes it every flaky, & it is very easy to roll out. Don't knock it until you have tried it ...compare ! She explains the science behind her recipes in all of her videos too, they are great.
I am 60 years old and I finally made a good pie crust! Actually, it was great if I say so myself. Thank you Claire. The video was key to getting me to try.

I’ve done this type of simple crust before (with butter or crisco). It’s fine. Though sometimes too dry.  I prefer King Arthur’s pastry dough recipe that uses butter but also adds some sour cream. Much more flaky and easier to work with. I always use a food processor - much easier & faster to do.

This recipe has some great tips! Using a bench scraper instead of a food processor helps keep the dough cold, and rolling the dough in plastic wrap prevents cracking—both are super helpful techniques. I also recommend her video where she explains her reasoning for using these techniques instead of using a food processor.
Outstanding!!!! Never going back to traditional pecan pie.  Less sweet and so good!

Coming from the perfect pie crust recipe after a frenzied morning before Thanksgiving blind-baking-disaster, this recipe is a godsend. I was skeptical about the quantity of water being added at once, but mixing it in with a fork is key. PERFECTLY hydrated while maintaining good texture. Followed to a T, except I only chilled for about an hour before rolling out, freezing in the pan, and baking, which still turned out great. I might refrigerate for longer next time, but genuinely couldn’t be happier with this recipe!
I have failed at pie crusts for years until I found this recipe. My self confidence is restored after a successful fudge pie using this crust

This was my first attempt at making pie dough successfully without a food processor. 

My only note is to either used salted butter or double the amount of salt. 

I personally found 1/2 teaspoon of even Morton Kosher salt to be an almost flavorless pie crust.

This dough came together so easily. I used it to make a free form apple and pear tart. It turned out very flaky with no soggy bottom. I’m going to double the recipe next time to make a blueberry slab pie. I used kerrygold unsalted butter.

It was also super easy to roll out. This is my new go-to pie crust.

I made this quickly in the stand mixer this evening, only chilled in the freezer a bit, and rebaked for 15 minutes at 425. it was good even though I didn't follow the instructions cutting the butter in properly. I'm pleased that it was really ample for a 9 inch crust.

easiest dough recipe to make by hand and is very versatile.

tip: you won't end up using the entire 1/2 cup of water. I add in the water one tablespoon at a time and judge how pliable I want the dough to be. if your dough ends up too wet, just add a bit more flour, one tablespoon at a time.
The flour measurement is way off and the grams don’t equal one and a half cups - had a hard time w this recipe, I agree the water into the recipe should be added as needed

Maybe it's the technique or butter (I used Kerrygold) or a combination of both, but this is the first pie crust I've turned out that didn't crack at all and was super easy to work with. In the past I've used the food processor, but I suppose incorporating everything manually prevents the dough from being over-worked. I bet using a pastry cutter would make it easier to break apart the pieces of butter and keep it even colder.

When doubling the recipe, I followed the directions too closely which did not adjust for the larger batch and accidentally stacked all the (8) pieces and formed into one disc. Can I still split into two separate crusts or is it too late? I used so much of my butter I’m hoping I don’t have to scrap it! 😫

I was intrigued by the chopping technique but this did NOT work for me. This crust leaked a ton of butter when baking, shrank, and was though as heck. I normally rub the butter into the flour before adding water. then much of the flour is blended with butter and you don't add so much water to make a dough. here it seems like Claire just chopped the butter and it didn't smear or rub it into the flour.  this for me resulted in lots of pockets of plain butter surrounded by flour and water, which developed a ton of gluten even though I was careful with the chopping technique. anyway I followed the directions and the video as best I could, but I'm sticking my normal recipe.

The technique threw me a little…it seemed to take forever for the dry flour to mix into the rest of the crust. But the proof is in the finished crust, which is the flakiest I’ve ever managed, even the next day.
Is Demerara sugar materially different in taste from regular light brown sugar allowing me - a relative novice cook - to substitute the latter for the former?  Thanks.  Tom
Demerara sugar is quite coarse and dry.  When it's sprinkled on top of a pastry before baking it melts just a little and gives it a lovely crunch.

If you hadn't seen already, highly recommend watching Claire go through her Thanksgiving recipes (which includes this one) on the NYT YouTube page. Lots of extra explanation from her and it's super useful!

Watching all Claire did in the video made me tired!  So much to do.  There is no way anyone could do all that with a houseful of kids, relatives, etc., even over a couple of days.   There are easier ways.   That said, I'm planning to make this slab pie.
Could this be done with puff pastry instead, considering it's so shallow?

I don't understand the reasoning behind adding an ice cube to the browned butter.  There are other ways to cool the butter without adding water-and ice cubes are very inconsistent in sizing. Just say cool the butter and add 2TBSP of cold water (or whatever the amount needs to be).

Otherwise the recipe sounds great-almost like a frangipane made with pecans instead of almonds.

@Julie when you brown butter, a little bit of water escapes via steam, so adding an ice cube to browned butter not only cools it down and stops the browning, it also reintroduces that displaced water into the butter. Not sure if it’s 100% necessary, but that’s the logic.
The ice cube is to replace the water that was in the butter, which evaporates during the browning process.

Do NOT make this in a metal quarter sheet pan without lining the bottom with parchment or buttering/flouring. I just made this and it not only wouldn’t be loosened but completely fell apart when prepared as described.
I love pecans but not the toothachy sweetness and gloppy texture of pecan pie. Can’t wait to try this recipe!

I made this and served it yesterday for an early Thanksgiving. The recipe is really well written and I followed it to a tee, using a metal quarter-sheet pan.

To answer a few questions here:
- You cannot replace the demerara with brown sugar. You could use turbinado though.
- The dessert is not very sweet.
- The dessert is "drier" than pie, but the guests really liked it. I did serve it with unsweetened whipped cream.
- The pastry worked out just great. Do not par bake, not needed.

TG - I've made a similar recipe for years - a family favorite - and I strongly recommend sticking with the flaky pie crust. Puff pastry does not have the body or the texture that are a key part in the recipe's success.
Beating an “X” in the dough before rolling it out is supposed to help to create the rectangle shape.

Delicious crowd pleaser. Made twice and learning #1. def cover after 30 min bc pecans became too browned. #2 don’t over-blend demerara and pecans in the food processor bc it results in filling becoming too runny which causes the pecan topping to sink into the filling instead of sitting on top. A coarser grind is ideal. Otherwise great recipe!
I've made this slab pie the way I do all slab pies - with puff pastry.  SO much easier, and to my mind, better tasting.  Try it and see!

Am looking forward to trying this recipe, but surprised the weights used in the ingredient list are not carried into the recipe. 1 1/4c pecans instead of, what, roughly 135 grams of pecans in the food processor, the demerara measurement? I don’t understand providing weight if not carrying through to the instructions.

This came out perfectly. Followed recipe exactly and made in a quarter baking sheet. Be warned, though, line the bottom of your oven with foil, or bake on top of another lined baking sheet, as our kitchen got smoked out. Next time, I'll not only do that, but use a baking pan with higher sides. It fits beautifully in the quarter sheet, but the higher sides might prevent the molten stuff from escaping.
@RR I think your oven might be dirty.  There isn’t anything happening in the baking time that would naturally create all that smoke

Demerara sugar will measure differently than brown sugar because it is so much coarser than brown sugar. If you swap it for brown sugar in the dough, expect the dough to be different because they aren’t equivalent measure for measure. You could try it out and see what happens, or get some demerara sugar! It’s a yummy texture for topping cookies and such anyway.
Using puff pastry was a mistake. Stick to flaky dough.
Not worth it. Very fussy to make and the payoff is minimal. It was delicious, but I won't make again. A traditional pecan pie is better tasting and easier. That crust was really fun to make though!
This turned out dryer than I wanted. Watch the cooking time. Crust was great but if you’re going to eat this many calories say go for a pecan pie and use maple syrup instead of karo!

Overall the quality of the recipe is sky high as you'd expect from Claire, but overall it wasn't really what I was expecting.   She describes in the video as basically being the top layer of a pecan pie, but this is more like a pecan bar.  I was worried it'd be too sweet like most other pecan pies....but to my surprise it actually wasn't sweet enough.  If I were to make this again, I would add more sugar to the topping so that the pecans actually candy.  As written, they're barely sweetened.

I enjoyed this very much. It is a bit of pain to make - there are a lot of steps, but we found the result was worth it. I did not use the ice cube. Instead, I added 1 oz of cold bourbon to the browned butter. I think it added a little bit of warmth to the pie, but definitely did not give it a bourbon flavor. I also baked this in a 9x13 glass dish with parchment paper on the bottom. Cooked well and came out cleanly.
Yo make it easier, do you think a puff pastry crust would work?
Has anyone tried this with a gluten free crust?
Wondering. Would it be possible to line baking sheet with parchment with a “hangover” to facilitate lifting out of pan?  The turnover sounds like a disaster waiting to happen!

Re the comment from camille about lining a quarter sheet pan with parchment - I'll do this in a 13x9 pan, too, to make removing the pie easier. No turning it upside down and back again and risking breaking.
MLT43  1 teaspoon of Diamond Crystal Kosher salt is 3 grams. Happy baking!

Made this following instructions exactly and using Claire’s flaky pie dough recipe. I really wanted to love this, but honestly, it was underwhelming. The filling I tasted from the back of the spatula was delicious, but it really lost something in the baking process. Everything seemed to go according to plan, but end result was just kind of heavy and didn’t taste like much of anything. And the dough turned out very flaky, sure, but no tenderness at all. Next time, I’ll stick to Melissa Clark’s.

Yes, Claire really made our Thanksgiving with this Pecan Slab Pie! Standard pecan pies are like treacle, overwhelmingly sweet.  This one was just sweet enough and then the texture of the ground pecans in the custard brought more flavor. i dyrup idn't have Demerara sugar so drizzled maple syrup on the pecans.
Also making the pie crust was easy, and it was flaky and light.
BIG HIT!  Will make this one again and again!

Disappointing, don't think it was worth the effort.  I did exactly what the recipe stated, to cover it after 30 min. and continue baking, which I did for about 8 more minutes.  But, I think I should have taken it out at 30 min.  It felt like the filling, which was yummy on the spoon, seemed to disappear in the baking.  Another reviewer said the same thing and I agree.  Perhaps it would be better in a round pie pan so the filling is thicker.  But I'm not sure I would even try it again.

This got rave reviews. Channeled my inner Martha and baked it in a rectangular tart pan with removable bottom and arranged the pecans in rows on top of the filling. Easy to get out of the pan and looks great. If you use this type of pan, put a sheet pan in the oven when you preheat and put the pie on top of the hot pan to catch any errant butter and get better browning on the crust. Used turbinado sugar and sprinkled with a little Maldon sea salt.

This was a huge hit at our Thanksgiving dinner! I love pecan pie but it can often be way too sweet or with too gloopy of a texture; this recipe is really well balanced for pecan pie lovers who feel the same. I had more than one person come up to me after dinner saying they usually don't like pecan pie (or pecans!) but they loved this, or that it was their favorite dessert from the table. From the topping to the filling it's delicious.

...one more note that I also needed to cover the pecans after about 30 min, came out perfectly. I would not use anything larger than a 13x9 pan. I did, and the filling to nuts/crust ratio was a tad thin. Better to go a little smaller than larger, if you don't have the right pan handy. In her nytimes video (ideal Thanksgiving start to finish - 2023) Claire mentions a quarter sheet pan is fine as well. You can cut these into smaller bars, can eat just like a cookie.

made this according to direction and it was amazing!! id never had pecan pie so i took a shot in the dark making this for thanksgiving but it was very well loved. super simple recipe and the filling even fit in my food processor which is about half the size of the one she uses in her video. will definitely make again

Took this to a thanksgiving dinner where most guests “hated” pecan pie.  It was a huge hit and everyone asked for the recipe.  I used a porcelain bake dish since I did not have a metal pan in the right correct size. It came out beautifully!

I made this for my family for a Thanksgiving dessert. Everyone said it was the best pecan dish they had ever had!  Especially for those who don't care for the gelatinous texture of regular pecan pie, it was a huge hit. The pecan/browned butter cream was terrific!

I followed Claire's video for a preview of the recipe, and had none of the issues reported by some other commenters.  The filling never threatened to overflow, and the pie did not stick to the quarter sheet pan I used.

I made this per instructions though used turbinado sugar rather than Demerara since that's what I could find.
I used a quarter sheet pan which I lightly buttered. No issues with sticking.
It turned out great!

This slab pie is a great concept for a Pecan Pie.  I did make two of them for different gatherings.  The most common reaction was to find the pie was a bit on the dry side, which I would agree.  The flavor overall is excellent.  Would adding a cup of half-n-half or heavy cream with two more eggs do the trick?  Just thinking.

We have been looking for a long time for a pecan pie that uses less corn or maple syrup, because they are too sweet. As promised, this is a pecan pie that is less sweet, and all of us loved it. The only modification I made was to use 250 grams of pecan halves for the topping (at the time, this was all I had) and to grind the remaining 200 grams for the filling. The pecans halves completely covered the top, and the filling was nuttier.

Because of the comments about over-browning, I toasted most of pecans (320 grams) for only 3 minutes and toasted the ones processed in the food processor (133 grams) for the full 8-10 minutes. You could use chopped pecans for the smaller amount if they are cheaper. I had no problem with the pie sticking to the pan.

I have made this twice.  It's fantastic.  The first time I baked it in a 9X13 Pyrex glass baking pan, came out great.  Second time, used quarter sheet pan and like several other commenters had a disastrous smoke fest in my kitchen because so much fat went over the edges of the pan and onto the bottom of the oven.  And, when the slab was cool enough to look underneath, I discovered the bottom was not golden and flakey like the one I make in the glass dish, rather pasty and pale. Still tasty.
Made this with graham cracker crust. It was delicious and devoured by all at Thanksgiving.

Found the overall pie to be a little dry, though I really enjoyed the flavor and lower level of sweetness, and I was glad it wasn’t gooey like a traditional version. Next time I will experiment with a higher ratio of frangipane filling to the pecans on top.
This might be the missing treat that I have been looking for.  Wow
What is the beating of the dough for?  Can't you just roll it out in the ordinary fashion?
I am so excited about this recipe! For once I don’t have to edit a recipe to reduce the sugar! Thank you! I’ll try it as soon as I get my next shipment of pecans. Yum.
The water from the ice cube will have separated from the butter.  Is it supposed to be added to the filling along with the butter?
Is one recipe of "Flaky Pie Crust" enough to fill a 13 x 9 inch baking pan?
Yes it was! I used that recipe for my crust and it nicely filled my 13 x 9.
Would this work with oil or melted margarine instead of butter? Asking due to milk allergy in the household.
I too am mystified by the ice cube.
Agree with Julie's comment - why add "one ice cube?"   Otherwise, recipe is very similar to my State Fair award winning pecan pie recipe.
Hi! Very interested in your state fair recipe.
Can date sugar be used ?
To Roald- I’m wondering the same thing about the beating vs rolling of the dough. Anyone understand this?
My child wants a pecanless pie. Would this recipe work without the pecan halves?

I would not recommend making a pecan pie without pecans. However, for a similar feel, look up Smitten Kitchen's Black Bottom Oat Pie, which has a pecan pie-like filling but with oats instead of pecans.
This recipe is literally all pecans. Without the pecan halves it would just be some sugar and butter and egg. Just pick a different pie.
Beating the dough when it comes out of the fridge just helps it loosen up for when you actually roll it out.
Wondering if anyone has a way to make this without the eggs for my vegan daughters. Always trying to figure out how to make some holiday favorites for them.

You could use vegan butter (such as Earth Balance or Miyoko's) instead of dairy butter, & "flax eggs" instead of the eggs (for one egg, combine one tablespoon of flaxseed meal with three tablespoons of water & allow to sit for about 5 minutes). I'm not normally a huge fan of flax eggs, but I think in this recipe the somewhat nutty taste of flax would not be an issue. If you don't want to do flax eggs, you could try cornstarch as a thickener in the filling, & non-dairy milk instead the egg white.
Can this be made ahead and frozen if traveling to Thanksgiving?

For anyone that's made this- how sweet is it?  Like is it a balanced sweetness or is it pretty saccharine like most pecan pies?  Thinking of making it for Thanksgiving, but certain members of my family won't eat super sweet desserts.
Can this be assembled the day before and then backed off the day of?
Can this be made and frozen?

Would anyone who's made this recommend par-baking the crust? I almost always par-bake, I'm surprised Claire didn't do it in the video. Is the 40-50 minutes enough for the crust to bake completely? I'll be making in a 10-inch circular pie plate so will have slightly thicker crust. Thanks!
Can I use the pre-made pie crusts instead of making my own dough??
Can the filling be made ahead? How many days will it last in the fridge, can it be frozen if made way ahead?
In the tip section, she said that the filling can be made 2 days ahead.
