Has anyone tried adding chocolate to the pecan cream?  Swap 1TBS of the flour for cocoa?  Or just add an ounce or two of bittersweet chocolate?

if you're going to add chocolate I would stay away from cocoa or any unsweeetened chocolate and go for a 65-ish% bittersweet chocolate. If it were me I'd sprinkle chopped chocolate along with the pecan topping. Yum! Might try it ;)
What is the best way to make this without a food processor? Thank you!
Can I substitute dark muscovado sugar (which I have) for the demerara (which I do not have) in this recipe?
Can you make this in a round pie tin instead? How would that be different?

I just over “toasted” the pecans. Will try a new batch after diligently watching them.  

Realized I do not have an appropriate sized serving plate. Has anyone made in a large pie plate?  My paella pan?
I cut them up into  3"x3" squares. So you can just array them on a dinner plate.  You can eat them by hand
Can someone please weigh and report back the Kosher salt measurements in grams? Hard to find Morton’s and Diamond Crystal in Europe...
Is the inverting a necessary step?  Can I just cool and serve in the 9x13 pan?
Yes especially since it can be cut and eaten by hand as slices. Best to let crust dry out.
I just made this with puff pastry and it smells fabulous. I’m taking it to a Thanksgiving dinner tomorrow and can’t wait to taste it!! Thanks Claire. I loved your Thanksgiving meal presentation!
Shout out to @Laura for the polite and constructive response to the inquiry about making a pecan pie without pecans.

Prepared this to a T and it came out perfectly. 

Used good European fat for the butter and Cairnsprings Edison (T-85 AP) for the pie crust. Had to cover the pecans at the 30 minute mark, but came our beautifully and like Claire said, it’s the best part of the pecan pie without the overly sweet gooey mess.

CAUTION - watch this like a hawk as it bakes.  Mine was probably done in 20 minutes - but unfortunately I covered it and kept on going as the recipe says 40-50 minutes.   WAY too long for my oven.  Very sad - looked like it would have been amazing.  I'm still giving it 4 stars as it *would* have been delicious.

This slab pie tastes great. I followed the recipe as written and the filling is lovely—not overpowering like pecan pie can be. Plus, the demerara sugar and salt on top makes it sing! My crust did not puff like the photo, probably because my dough was a bit too dry (due to my inexperience; I should have kept cutting in the butter and maybe added another tablespoon of water). Would make it again!
Amazing!! The perfect nutty & some sweet for those who have picked out the middle of cloying pecan pies.

This isn’t a slab pie it’s HALF of a slab pie!  If you’re hoping to use your beautiful Made In Slab Pie Pan designed by Nancy Silverton (the inventor of the slab pie!) the recipe isn’t big enough. I know, I should have measured, but I also know better than to use a NYT recipe.  You guys always have to change things to try and prove you’re the smartest people in the room.  Shame on me for getting sucker again.
Too much work for me. Demarara sugar is not a household item. So many steps; so many dishes to clean. Won't make this again. Good luck to those who are more committed and have more time than I!

We used Turbinado sugar in place of the Demmera and it had a nice sugar bite to it, much like Maldon salt has for a salty note.  The pastry takes a lot of time, but overall this was not difficult and was a HUGE WIN.  The only reason it is not 5 stars is that I save 5 stars for things that can be made  quickly.  So much better than Pecan Pie, especially for folk who are trying to be thoughtful about sugar consumption. We sliced into smaller slices as it is rich, served with whipped cream.  Yum!

I halved the recipe and made it in a 9” round cake pan, dark nonstick metal.  It looked great and came out easily, but was dry and hard.  If I make it again, I might try baking it 30 mins.  I also ended up with several more pecans than needed after placing them in concentric circles.

I followed the recipe exactly.  Even measured the oven temp. I ended up with an uncooked center of the bottom. Everything else was perfect. Flavor is extraordinary.  Not sure how to fix the soggy bottom problem.
Very easy and very good. None of that corn syrup goo( although that IS yummy) but this is a great -eat out of your hand- pie. I used more than a TBS of Demerara on top - more like 2 or 3

This was the hit of the Thanksgiving dessert buffet!
Made exactly the way the recipe stated. 
I didn't to use any parchment or foil on the bottom of the 19x13 metal baking pan. It has higher sides than a sheet pan, so I just cut the edges down to 1/2". 

Popped out of the pan perfectly and looked great on a pretty platter presentation. 
Everyone enjoyed the less-sweet, bar-like consistency. 
A winner--a new Thanksgiving addition.

This is a perfect pecan dessert! I’ve failed at finding a pecan pie recipe that works as egg, dairy, and gluten free—until now! The caveat is that the frangipane texture is different, but luscious! I made a vegan rough puff for the crust, used Vegg egg replacement, and aquafaba instead of egg whites for the topping. Look at the pic and notice the level of sugar on the pecans…don’t skimp on it or the salt.

Made as written, except I swapped turbinado sugar for Demerara sugar because I could not find the latter. It was absolutely delicious and a total crowd pleaser at my family’s thanksgiving. I made it in the quarter sheet and flipped it onto a cutting board no problem. Next time I will slice it into squares in advance because it takes a sharp knife and some patience to slice.

This was a hit with my guests. I used a gluten free pie crust recipe due to gluten allergies. Thus, the crust wasn’t as flaky as I would have liked, but it was still a great dessert. It was a nice alternative to traditional sticky sweet pecan pie.

Amazing! Flavorful and not too sweet. Followed the recipe almost exactly (including pie dough), except for the sugar. Used muscavado since I couldn't find demarara where I live, and it turned out excellent. I arranged the pecans in a neat row (meditative!) which then made it easy to cut the tart into little squares.

Excellent first time! I watched Claire’s Thanksgiving video and decided to give this a go. Her pie crust recipe was perfectly flaky and the pecan filling much less goopy without corn syrup. I substituted an organic cane sugar, with a coarse crystal for the Demerara, measuring same amount.  The resulting natural sweetness was preferred. I covered the top halfway to prevent over browning of the already toasted pecans.  This is a new favorite for sure!

This was a huge hit for Thanksgiving.   It traveled well and the grab and go size of the bars encouraged grazing both before and after dinner.   I’m planning to make it again for our office party as it seems better suited than a gooey pie.    I’m a ROD pecan pie fan but this may actually taste better (sound of generations of my Southern ancestors spinning!)  It does take longer to make but using a high quality store bought crust helps even the marginal taste benefit to time cost.

Hmm.. Made this for Thanksgiving. One of my guests said, "is this dessert or is this breakfast?" This is not sweet enough to be pie. It's as far over on the unsweet side as traditional pecan pie is on the sweet side. 

OTOH, Claire's pie dough is incredible.
Using a large mezzaluna style pizza cutter helped with cutting tidy squares.
We found the finished product to be far better the day after baking. If served fresh, it is a bit tough, even though flakey. The second day is flaky and softer, closer to a croissant.

This was a big hit with my southern father in law. I made it in a glass 9x13 Pyrex that I lightly greased with butter and it took slightly longer to brown on bottom than recipe indicated but otherwise worked great. I will cover earlier in process next time because the nuts on top got a tad dark. I LOVE the crust recipe and ended up using it for my apple pie too. It was easy to transport in the glass pan and could easily be made a day ahead as well. A keeper imo!

Made this for Thanksgiving.  Followed recipe exactly with the exception of using phyllo for the crust.
Big hit!

This is now one of my favorite desserts. Next time will just get 12 oz of $$ halves, the rest can be much cheaper pieces.  I tented after 30min and the top came out perfect.  I wish I kept it in the oven for an extra 5 min. The side crust was just a blond golden ( video had darker sides). This extra time would improve the bottom crust which was not flaky at the center.  The outer 3" perimeter was perfect. I may play with adding a shot of cold bourbon in lieu of the ice cube.

For step 2, I browned 140g of nice butter and forewent the ice cube. For step 3, I used a vitamix to blend ~134g of toasted walnuts with organic cane sugar, still very tasty! For step 6, I also used walnuts and rubbed their skins off for my topping to avoid extra bitterness in case I overdid things in the oven. For step 7, depending on how much you toasted your nuts, I might check sooner than 30 min to cover your tart with foil.  It turned out amazing and I plan to make it again! Thanks Claire
I bought pie dough from a bakery. Likely the crust was not as flaky as this recipe would be.  The slab pie was delicious nonetheless.
This pie was so good. Made it in a quarter sheet pan and it was absolutely perfect, it popped right out of the pan like a dream. I’m planning on making this every Thanksgiving.
I may try this first with commercial refrigerated dough that I’ve found pretty reliable.  (Claire Saffitz says that’s OK!). So how to adapt a round (or more) to fit the sheet pan?

This recipe was a huge hit at Thanksgiving. The pecans got a little dark on me in the final bake (should have tented) but it was still great and did not taste bitter at all. I will definitely make this again!

Made this EXACTLY as the recipe was written, except I started late at night and was afraid to disrupt the neighbors with my food processor so my pecans were ground just to the size of large panko breadcrumbs.
PERFECTION! The crust was extremely flaky and buttery. (Mine was way thinner than the recipe photo, though...) The slab popped right out of my 9x13 cake pan with no parchment/foil and was very sturdy.
I now have it sliced into 12 squares and ready to go to be enjoyed with family! Yay!!

I am sure I must have messed up some where with the recipe.
The pecan topping was delicious, but actual filling was skimpy. My crust was stuck to the pan and I could not get the tart released after baking. I double checked the recipe and it does not instruct to grease the plan.
The crust turned tough for me.
I was disappointed with this recipe.

I made exactly as written and it turned out fabulously! Flaky crust, deep buttery and nutty flavors without that gloopy cloying sweetness from traditional pecan pie. I bought demerara sugar to stay true to the recipe- though pricey, I think it the flavor it imparted worked well with the overall recipe. I may try turbinado the next time I make the recipe since it is much more affordable

I made this for Thanksgiving and the flavor was perfection, but I was a little disappointed by the texture of the filling. It came out drier and more like a baked dough than a custardy filling. Maybe this was because of the two tablespoons of flour? I was also one of the people who didn't add an ice cube to my butter, but just stuck it in the freezer, so maybe that was it? Or I simple over-baked it? I'm going to tweak it and try again.

I made this instead of pecan pie for Thanksgiving and it came out looking exactly like the photo. Tasted good, just not great. Looks a bit dry in the photo and YES, we all agreed that it seemed a bit dry to eat. In the end, we really missed the gooey pecan pie that we all love. If you love pecan pie, skip this one and just make it. It is about the same amount of work.
So yummy. Most flaky crust I have ever made.
Made this twice and love it but will seek out a different crust recipe for it next time I make it. The linked crust recipe is tasty and flaky but so tough I couldn't believe it.
Perfectly delicious (and so much better than the traditional version which I find cloying.). A definite crowd pleaser.

I made this for Christmas dinner and have a few thoughts: no one else mentioned this, but I felt the ratio of pecan topping to filling was off. Not enough filling. Perhaps I read the recipe wrong! I will definitely make this again, though, since it is a wonderful much less sweet alternative to traditional pecan pie.
Delicious and a winner at Thanksgiving with my family over traditional pecan pie.  Have made it since by request for parties.

Made a half recipe of this and used a 9 in spring form pan.  It was ready in about 35 min.  Came out great and I would definitely make it again as a pecan pie substitute.  As other folks mentioned not too sweet.  Note I used half brown sugar and half granulated sugar for the filling.  I would place a sheet pan under it next time as the bottom was a little browner than I would like.

I would ideally love to change up the filling a bit to modify it to a maple chocolate bourbon interior. Any ideas for measurements or substitutions? Any help or suggestions would be greatly appreciated.
Really good flavors, but the crust/filling/pecans ratio left it a little dry for me. Next time I will try with 1.5 times the filling.

you can replace the demerara sugar with brown sugar (i used dark)! just don’t pack it when measuring, and do a 1:1 replacement. it is quite moist doing it this way. i would use the recommended pie crust recipe though

A hit!!! I used turbinado sugar and it worked great. I also added 2 teaspoons of corn syrup to the pecan filling because I wanted it a little sweeter and homage to the classic pecan pie. It was great and held up really well

This recipe and pie crust also split in half perfectly to make two smaller slab pies. I used two 1/8 sheet pans. Cook time was still the same no stability issues or anything. I did over toast my pecans so after a bit of time in the oven I had to lightly cover with foil to keep them from burning.

I loved this! I used puff pastry left over from another project as I mostly wanted to try the filling. The only change I made to the recipe was adding bittersweet chocolate chips, about 1/2 cup to the filling and another 1/2 cup to the topping. As mentioned by others, just add enough ice cubes to the butter to get back to 113g. I'm not sure it's necessary, though. The filling was pretty runny until I blended in the chocolate. Use 135g of pecans for the filling, and I used turbinado sugar.

I make this yummy recipe with a graham cracker crust, (recipe on box of grahams) which puts it over the top of deliciousness. I’m almost sorry I made it for a “Friends Thanksgiving”, because the drumbeat for another and another comes as each birthday approaches!

Made this slab pie multiple times exactly as written except for the sprinkling of demerara sugar on top. Not only is it delicious, it's so sturdy and  turns out beautifully out the pan every time. I like to slice it up and pack the slices to take to outdoor events. It's really brings together the best parts of a pecan pie!

Very much enjoyed this tart/bar. For me, I would cook on lower rack of the oven to nicely brown the the bottom of the crust, as I do pies. Next time I would roast only the nuts put in the filling as of course they were too browned, even with foil cover towards the end. For me 40 minutes was almost too much time. Would like the fulling a little juicier so may try to add a little something to cream that next time.

Careful: not the recipe from her pecan pie video, but from her thanksgiving video.

CRUST: 2 c ap flour, 1 tbsp sugar, 1 tsp DC, 14 tbsp/283g butter
20 min w/ weights @ 400, 20 min w/o @ 300
FILLING
12 oz (400g) pecans, toasted
1 c brown sugar, 6 tbsp butter, 2 tbsp corn syrup, 1/2 c cream: cook until golden add 1/2 c cream
Whisk 4 large egg equivalents, stream mixture in, add 1 tbsp van, 1 tbsp cocoa powder, 1/2 tsp instant coffee, 1 tsp DC. 
20 min @ 300
Making this again this year – 2025. Our family had this 2024 Thanksgiving and it was fantastic.! We followed the recipe exactly as written and used the link for the flaky pie crust.
Made two days ahead, tasted great if a tad dry (should have covered 30 minutes into baking but forgot)

Adjustments for a pie plate (deep dish). Add a tbs of bourbon and the zest/juice of an orange (reduced ) to the hot butter instead of ice. Crimp the crust and blind bake with weights on 350 for 12 mins. Remove weights, prick the base with fork, and bake uncovered for 6 mins. Mix half the pecans with egg white/sugar. Put the rest in bottom of crust, cover with the filling, and top with egg white pecans. Bake at 375 to internal temp of 190, about 1+ hour, covering as needed to avoid over browning.

My pecans never puffed so I ended up cooking it way too long waiting for that indication and it was a big waste of time and ingredients. The whole thing ended up very dry and hard. I love Claire's recipes and was so excited for this.
Great. Not too sweet.
Tasting the filling before baking, it was one of the best things I have had in a while. I think it would taste really good as an enriched bread filling as well.
I was hoping to love this as much as the other commentators. It wasn't sweet enough for me!

I didn't care for how dry the interior 'cream' layer was. The 'flaky' crust on the bottom was thin and brittle, a bit like cardboard and difficult to cut. Overall flavor was ok, but honestly, if you're going for pecan pie, make a traditional pecan pie.

Next time, I would not wet the petitas with maple syrup and pumpkin puree. They sunk into the puree and became soggy. I might try sprinkling salted toasted petitas onto the puree after 10 minutes of baking or at least gently sprinkle onto the topping before going in the oven. 
Now back to making a regular pumpkin pie to satisfy my taste for the delicious puree and crust! Full transparency, I not pre-bake the crust as recommended.

Excellent taste and texture of the custard filling although the use of dark sugar produced a somewhat unappealing dingy color. The pumpkin seeds were a disaster and distracted from the tasty filling. The seeds were chewy and very different from their popped state before coating. They sunk into the filling unlike the recipe photo. And there were so many of them that they overwhelmed the pie itself. I will never use them again. If you want some crunch, try using fewer seeds or try toasted pecans.

I thought this pumpkin pie was very good! I’m a big fan of pumpkin pie and have been making them for years. I liked this version because it uses maple syrup and butter and cream and is very easy to put together.  I used my own crust (Ovenly recipe) and did not pre-bake it.  Pumpkin pie doesn’t last long enough in my house for the crust to get soggy!  Try this recipe - its delicious!
Could I use coconut milk instead of heavy cream?
Naw, I'd suggest pureeing the Portugese sausages and using them instead of either coconut milk or heavy cream.
This is a pumpkin pie recipe.  Not sure where you are getting black beans or Portuguese sausage.
Discard the scraps of pie dough? Not on your life! Sprinkle with cinnamon and sugar and bake…enjoy!
In the video, Ms. Saffritz makes this recipe with Demerara sugar, and specifies why. But now the recipe calls for brown sugar. Disappointed to not find the actual recipe here.

The pepitas were good and looked cute, but I don't think they added anything that pumpkin pie is missing. I'm glad I tried it but probably wouldn't add again. I did not have a problem with them sinking. Parbaking the crust is definitely worth it.

Regarding the pumpkin pie with the pumpkin seeds, I just think it looks unappetizing, it looks like an infestation of some sort.
I love the recipes from Claire and I have 2 of her books. I think my grandkids would freak out at the look of this, sorry
chop fine & sprinkle on top, as another reader, Judith, suggests, but for a different reason--?
Yes,. See ingredient substitution guide just above the start cooking button

do you have illustrations (or video) of the crust-making technique?  ie your method for crimping the edge -- I don't understand how you move from tucking the crust under the edge to then crimping it?  Also, if you par-bake til the crust is golden brown, won't the edges of the crust burn when you bake the pie for an additional 50"?
there's a youtube video where she bakes a whole thanksgiving and makes this pie! you can see the crimping technique there. it is on the NYT cooking youtube
I followed the crust and pie recipes exactly … I think. My result: good flavors and inedibly bad textures. Overbaked extremely tough crust, soft sludgy maybe underbaked filling. So sad!

Won’t make again. The texture of the pepita topping is disgusting. Inedible. Like eating bugs. There was also a bitter flavor to the pumpkin custard which managed to simultaneously be bland. Not enough pumpkin flavor. The custard looks dingey not bright and pumpkin-ey. Her saving grace is the crust. The method produces delicious flakey pastry, golden even on the bottom. It is also the easiest most efficient way I’ve found to make pie crust. Back to my old pumpkin pie recipe.

Pretty but didn’t come out super tasty. Looked stunning, but I found pepita flavor on top was hard to execute. I had issues with the pepitas sinking through the pie filling at first, so I put them on top of the pie after 20 minutes of baking. Since I could not find raw pepitas in the store, only already-roasted pepitas, I thought less roast time for the pepita topping would be fine, but it was not; the seeds were very chewy and not that flavorful.

I did not make this exact recipe, but I did use the pepitas topping idea for my pumpkin pie this year.  I did toast them, then toss them with the maple syrup and some of the filling.  Unless you are serving this entire pie the same day it is made, I wouldn't recommend topping it with the pepitas.  They lose their crunch after being refrigerated, so you end up with these soft, sorty slippery seeds marring your pie.

I was disappointed with the results and don’t feel the time cost/benefit was worth it. The flavor was nutty but needed a bit more sweetness. The crust was fine but too fiddly (between refrigeration and prebaking at various temps) to make it worth while. I did think the pepitas was a great addition. 
Likely going back to my original recipe (but keeping the pepitas).

Followed the recipes closely and got an extremely tough overbaked crust with soft sludge filling. Thought maybe the filling was underbaked, scraped it out of the inedible crust into a fresh pan and back to the oven but no, even after another hour still mush, if tasty. What a waste of ingredients and time.
sorry to hear this, I followed instructions exactly and had perfect results. seems like its worth checking to see if the quantities were correct.
Has anyone tried this without the pumpkin seed topping? Wondering how the filling would be without the seeds

I forgot to add this to my previous post:  I made this pie without the seeds.  Pumpkin pie and seeds just don’t go together in my opinion. Pumpkin pie should be creamy.  The filling was delicious without the seeds.
Has anyone frozen this or other pumpkin pies with success?
@Jonathan - In the Pumpkin Pie segment, CS uses dark brown sugar. Demerara is used in the Pecan Slab Pie.

I used the crust recipe for pies, and made 4.  Have baked only one so far, a pecan pie. For me, the crust was difficult to maneuver.  Became too wet. Tried one with less water, but I think it will be too dry when I roll that one out. Rolled out to 13" diameter, but it was so thin, it tore.  It shrank of course after blind baking.  I am not sure what went wrong.
I'm confused as to how the crust does not end up burned. If you follow the instructions for pre baking, the crust ends up in the oven for almost 2 hours!

Hard to feel confident about the oven temps at each phase. Step 1 toasting the seeds is at 400 degrees. Step 4 Crust bake with weights is 400 degrees too? Please confirm. Then 325 degrees without weights to brown the crust. After that there is no mention of the oven temps for Step 7, when the filled pie goes back into the oven. Is that at 325 also? Please confirm. This is stressing me out on my busy Thanksgiving morning. Opps! Afternoon.
I’m confused about the oven temperature in step 7 as well. Please clarify!
Needed to be a little sweeter, although some of us like it that way. Crust was good. Pepitas were divisive.
I skipped the pepitas. The filling is to die for delish!

My pepitas sunk into the filling and became soggy. I'm not sure what I did wrong. Going forward, I think I will just make a regular pumpkin pie, and then make a separate crumble to serve alongside if I want something crunchy.
The dough is fantastic, the pumpkin pie filling is good but I didn’t care for the  pepitas were too much and if you made a day ahead, they lost their crunch.

Made this for Thanksgiving this year and regret it. The custard was too sweet, and, as so many others have noted, the pepitas added nothing-if anything, they sank into the pie were soggy instead of crisp.And, quite frankly, the pie looked pretty unappetising full stop. Fortunately one of my guest brought a normal pumpkin pie-that one disappeared quickly and I ended up binning about 2/3s of this one.
Followed the recipe except for using a Rose Levy Beranbaum crust. Pepitas add needed texture to pumpkin pie. Will never go back! My pie baked faster than directed for a softly set custard. A keeper.
We liked the filling but not the pumpkin seed topping

the filling was the perfect flavor and silky smooth consistency, but i wasnt crazy about the pepita topping. personally i would probably prefer a praline esque topping, but thats just my preference! it also took about double the time to bake.
I made this pie and it was delicious. The pepitas turned out light and crunchy. They elevated a rather ordinary pumpkin pie to a higher level. This recipe will be in my recipe book for years to come.
What temperature did you bake the pie at once you added the filling??? 325? Please respond lol
I made this pie, without the pepitas, and it was not only delicious but also beautiful! It’s gone now but it even looks heavenly in photos~
I loved the custardy texture of the pie, but it lacked a bit of sweetness for me.  Also, I would have the pepitas.

Ran out of time on Thanksgiving to add the pepitas in top. Otherwise I made exactly as called for and it was hands down the best pumpkin pie I’ve ever made. This will be my yearly Thanksgiving pumpkin pie recipe from now on!

stats.Total Time: 2 hours 20 minutes, plus at least 4½ hours’ setting
stats.Cook Time: 2 hours 15 minutes, plus at least 4½ hours' setting

comments.commentBody: Would have loved a side view of this.

Made this recipe today as a Thanksgiving audition; for once I did not make any alterations. The cheesecake portion is easy and delicious, not too sweet. The cranberries were pleasingly tart; might increase the amount of glaze next time for a glossier presentation. Requires significant cooling, so definitely make a day in advance.

EP  Since you made this a day in advance, how did the crispiness of the crust hold up when serving it the day after baking?  I'm in the process this weekend of the "audition", as you so cleverly name it, too.
What kind of cheese can I substitute for goat cheese? Such a beautiful cheesecake for the holidays. I really want to make this.

Megan,  You ask what can be subbed for the goat cheese.  See Joanna's comment on this thread that she is considering using mascarpone instead of goat cheese.  That is going to change the texture given it is more fluid than chevre, but it will add a little of the "tang" goat cheese provides.  You might also just use another 12 oz of cream cheese.   Or try labneh, a yogurt cheese, for the tang if you can find one solid enough.
Has anybody made this without the gelatin? If so, what did you use?
I'd like an answer for this also. I'm a vegetarian, so the gelatin is a no go for me.

We found a box of plant-based gel by Simply Delish at a local 'natural' grocery store chain (right next to the boxes of jello).  It's raspberry flavor, but thought that with the small amount used it would go well with the cranberry.  Cooling now, so we'll see if it helps set the top.   You could probably also mix corn starch in some hot water and then add to the juice mix to try to thicken the topping.

Great cheese cake recipe! When measuring out the ingredients for the crust, I didn't feel that it was enough, so I doubled the quantities of the crust. I also read a few comments that suggested doubling the glaze, but NOT the cranberry amount. I also doubled that too!
So in total, I doubled the crust and the glaze, it was fantastic! So glad I doubled the crust and glaze. When chilling the glaze and waiting for it to thicken. That took forever, so prepare yourself for a long process.
For gluten free crust I imagine a pecan crust with GF all purpose flour would be delicious
David Tanis has a wonderful hazelnut crust that would probably work really well here. He uses it in a showstopper recipe for a cranberry curd tart that never fails to get rave comments.

I've made several regular cheesecake recipes by using gluten-free graham crackers and all have turned out well. I plan on doing this one as well. I do sometimes use an equal amount of ground nuts, usually pecan, and graham crackers crumbs, especially  on milder flavors.

I suspect his recipe would get more stars if it were written better.   Things I wish I new.   

Tart = THIN 

The gelatin didn’t set for many of us so maybe that direction needs to be revisited.   The topping process took longer than expected.  

Tart = TART A bit more sugar for the topping would be nice (it’s like it was written for sweetened Ocean Spray cran juice)

Don’t be afraid of the goat cheese, you can’t taste it.    

You NEED a mesh strainer. My mom only had a colander.

I give it FIVE stars. Everyone loved it, including me. I was a little concerned that the glaze wasn't setting up so give yourself plenty of time to let it set. I am going to try increasing the amount of gelatin just a touch the next time I make it.

I made this for Thanksgiving, Christmas, and New Year's parties this year and it was a big hit. The syrup takes a lot more time to thicken than I thought (not specified in recipe) so pull up a seat to the stove and prepare to be patient and stir for awhile. It does eventually thicken and reduce then sets nicely with gelatin. Original crust was plenty, doubling glaze was better. I couldn't get unsweetened pure cranberry juice so used a blend and it worked just fine.

I made this step for step. Was a bit concerned about how liquid the filling was but it cooked perfectly. Removed cranberries after hearing first pops.   First time issuing gelatin, runny going on but it set up. Made the cake on Wednesday night and did the topping Thursday morning. It was devoured, even after all that food. BAKE THIS.

Pretty happy with the cheesecake and the tart cranberries contrasted nicely. I made the following adjustments:
- used 8" cake pan (didn't have 9") so my cheesecake was taller and I didn't run into the issue of not having enough cranberries across the surface
- used 3/4 tsp gelatin. it did end up setting nicely after 4 hours in the fridge
My only disappointment was that the crust was soggy, not sure how to fix that.
I managed to prepare this in the allotted 5 minutes of prep, no problem.

Any thoughts on trying this with blueberries instead? I know they'll handle the heat differently. And "blueberry juice" is not as close at hand as cranberry juice. Anyhow. I can imagine a lot of delicious trial and error on the way to an adaptation.
Find a recipe for blueberry topping and put it on this cheesecake.
Would mascarpone work as a substitute for the goat cheese?     This looks amazing but I'm serving goat cheese haters.
Why not look for a cheesecake recipe that uses mascarpone? You could put this cranberry topping on that cheesecake.

Any cheese with a similar water content should work. I'd just go with cream cheese, which would give it a more classic flavor profile, or a really well strained labne (so it's stiff and not too watery) if you want a similar tanginess to the goat cheese.

Joanna, You ask if mascarpone will work to sub in for the goat cheese.  (Alas that you are serving goat cheese haters because the chevre in this adds that nice tang.)  I don't know if the texture will be right using mascarpone for all of the goat cheese given mascarpone is so much more fluid.  I guess if I was going to try it, good idea for mascarpone 'tang', I might do 12 oz cream cheese to 4 oz mascarpone and see what happens.
You had me at goat cheese! Definitely making this for Thanksgiving
Recipe reads as though crust is only on the bottom. Is this true? Side view would have been nice.
Crust on the bottom; cranberry topping on top. No side view needed.
Five minutes prep time?
Substitute a crisp, gluten-free, cookie for the ones in the recipe. Otherwise look for a crustless cheesecake recipe and add the cranberry topping to it.
can I just use ALL cream cheese, no goat cheese- or substitute ANY other cheese for the goat cheese--sorry-will never learn to love goat cheese.

Someone once said to me, "I don't like goat cheese." My response? "Which one?" A shame the recipe was not more descriptive. "8 ounces goat cheese" is vague. 

My guess is that it should be fresh goat cheese—in bulk or the earliest form of a *crottin de chèvre* or log. A fresh goat cheese will not yet have a rind. I would think what you gain with the mix of cream cheese and fresh goat is more tang. Other recipes which develop a similar depth & structure often include sour cream or marscapone.
I’d love any suggestions on a vegetarian friendly substitute for the gelatin! Thanks!
Does the gelatin have to be the animal product kind, or would agar-agar work? It usually doesn't set as firmly as the "regular" sort, but I need to make a vegetarian version of the cheesecake.
In step 4, what is meant by “slightly aerated?”  Should the filling be slightly stiffened or very liquid at this point?
Just finished step 4. Mine was very liquid. The recipe says scrap across the crust. There was no scraping more like pouring. Cooling now and looks like it cooked ok.

Fantastic taste, impressive look! This one is a bit of a labor of love, but if you allow yourself the time, it is truly rewarding. I checked on my topping three hours after it was in the fridge covered, and it still wasn't set. Took the whole night for it to get where it should be. 

I had read in the comments that the cranberries can be a bit tart, so I sprinkled them with additional sugar while they were resting on the plate, after being taken out of the saucepan. Worked great!

This was delicious and not too much work. My only suggestion is to place a piece of wax paper between the measuring cup and the dough when flattening out the crust to avoid any sticking between the two.

Good thing I put a sheet pan under this when it went to chill. The cranberry jelly went everywhere and did NOT set. I don’t know if I didn’t chill it enough over the ice bath? But I ended up using a turkey baster to get the loose jelly out of the sheet pan and put it back on top of the cheesecake.

I have made this twice now. I  used more cranberries because I felt it needed more coverage. I lost a few  in the cooking process but they also stick together. 
It’s a longer process to include the proper cooking/cooling time but so worth it.
Made with no alterations. Quite tasty, but the cheesecake to topping ratio seemed low - tart was quite short and flavor was overpowered by the cranberry. Might 1.5x the filling next time.

I’m not sure about these last few topping steps. My cranberry gelatin mixture never reached “heavy cream” texture, even with adding more gelatin. So, when I poured it on the tart it ran off the top and down the sides of the pan, leaking though the bottom. As a result, I have a layer of gelatin around the side of the cake, but not on top. Not pleased with that but we will see how it all tastes.
In lieu of gelatin. I added 1/4 cu cranberry simple syrup to 1/4 cup apricot jam. Brushed it on as glaze. Worked well, looked good.

This cake took 3.5 hours to cook in the oven. I followed instructions as in the recipe, but cake was no where near done after 2 hours at 250 degrees F. I added another hour at 350 degrees and kept in the oven for an additional half hour. I had to make the topping separately in another 9-inch pan, because of the 3-hour chill requirement. So much trouble! Hope it's worth it.

Recipe followed without improvisation.  This is a very thin tart.   The cranberry topping was a time consuming challenge and very tart.   The crust was soggy.   

The end result was not as pretty as the picture but still eye catching, The flavors blended nicely and less cheesecake was unexpectedly nice after a big  Thanksgiving dinner.  For that reason I might try again.  

Trader Joe’s cinnamon grahams.

Made this again for Thanksgiving but doubled the proportions for the glaze; unfortunately I forget to get gelatin and used Bakol Gel, which I use for vegetarian strawberry pie. Despite careful measuring, did not fully set and ran when released from the pan (still tasty, though). For those hesitant to try because of the goat cheese--I would never had guessed this had goat cheese in it.

Made as directed, using Nairn’s oat grahams for a gluten-free guest. The cheesecake layer was a tad shorter than anticipated and next time I’ll probably add just a touch more gelatin to keep the topping from running. Overall, delicious, not too sweet, and will make again.
No goat cheese next time, use whole bag of cranberries and use paper on sides

It's a tasty tart-not a real cheesecake. Used about 2/3 ginger snaps and 1/3 garham crackers for crust-but the recipe should be doubled. Also, used agar-agar instead of gelatin; I suggest   tripling the glaze topping recipe. I did not soak the agar in cold water, but instead, after I removed 12 oz of cranberries from heat, once the sugar was dissolved, I slowly added about 1/3 teaspoon of agar-agar to the heated syrup mixture while stirring constantly-Agar gels fast so move quickly to assemble.
so good geletin was a little underwear but was loved by all
Made this recently, and it was delicious. Recommend making 2-3x the amount of glaze.

This was delicious, but I had some problems. I used Spekuloos cookies for crust; since there was no instruction to oil the pan first, I didn't. The crust adhered to the pan like cement; I could hardly scrape any of it up when cutting the cake. 
Also, I spent valuable cooking time trying to get the glaze to set. Finally, I just cooked the liquid down with the cranberries and  orange zest until it was a compote. I added some candied lemon peel and candied ginger, and spread it on the top.

To make for a crowd, I doubled the crust and filling recipe and tripled the jelly portion of the topping (doubled the cranberries) per some commenter suggestions. It worked well in a 9 x 13 casserole dish! Then I just cut them into bars. Super yummy, balanced dessert perfect for a friendsgiving :)

Made this for Thanksgiving and it was absolutely delicious. The one step that gave me trouble was the cooling of the gelatin glaze in the ice bath. There was no time direction and several comments vaguely mentioned that it took forever to set. Mine certainly never reached the consistency of heavy cream. In the end, I poured it on anyway and left it to set overnight in the fridge. It turned out great! Just cool it in the ice bath until it's cold to the touch and give yourself overnight to set.
What kind of goat cheese is best for this?

Any kind of soft, fresh, chèvre style—the kind that’s rindless and usually packed in a plastic-wrapped tube. You want a mild, smooth goat cheese for this, not a pungent one that’s been aged with a rind. Laura Chenel makes an good version, as does Trader Joe’s.
Could regular cranberry juice cocktail work if the sugar in omitted? Thanks

easy with excellent results,  When you ask "Could regular cranberry juice cocktail work if the sugar in omitted?" do you mean artificially sweetened / sugar free cranberry juice cocktail?  No, that wont' work here.  It specs unsweetened juice which is entirely different.
Can you make this without a crust? We have lots of gluten free folks in the family.

Mazalea,  We have celiacs in the family too so I understand the need to observe gluten free meal times.  You might be able to do this w/out crust but noting the specs for the crust include graham crackers as an option, have you tried Pamelas brand gluten free graham crackers to use for a crust like this?  They are pretty darned good and they work well in applications like this one.  Almost all major grocery stores carry them.
Costco goat cheese for a mild and well priced combo. Grabbed a box of blueberries to sub the crans. Wait? Is this in the healthy dessert category!? :)
I second Megan's and Joanna's questions. I'm one of those goat cheese haters, but I'd love everything else about this!
I have a great Blendtech blender (similar to Vitamix). Assume that would work instead of a food processor, right?
What would be the best way to make this vegetarian? Is there a cellulose version of gelatin? Cornstarch or other thickener? Pectin maybe? I can research but thought someone might know.
This might help: https://www.spatuladesserts.com/substitute-for-gelatin/
Could I make ahead and freeze this? That is the key question!!
@Barbara Bolles.  Look for parve kosher gelatin — it should not contain any animal products.  It’s available online.

Parve kosher gelatin is not vegetarian. It is typically made from fish bones. Agar agar from seaweed is a vegetarian substitute. It has different properties from gelatin and can't simply be substituted 1:1 for gelatin. It would probably take some experimentation to make it work here. Please comment, anyone, if you try it! I'd be inclined here to skip the gelatin and try to glaze the cooked cranberries with jelly (like for a fruit tart), or dress them up with sparkly sugar.

Regarding cake strips: may I recommend an alternative. Place a flower nail (Ie what you'd use to pipe a frosting rose) with the flat side down, in the middle of the cake batter before it goes in the oven. The heat of the nail will result in a higher temp in the middle of the cake and better ensure a flat cake top. Be sure to rub butter under the nail bottom before place in the batter so it pulls out easily when you invert the pan. I find flower nails easier to procure and less work than strips.

For those who asked, cake strips are strips of fabric with a shiny silvery exterior and velcro closures. You soak them in water and then wrap them around your cake tins before putting them in the oven. They help the cake layer to rise evenly and keep the layers flat.

@Ellen Tabor, the best cake strips are made of cloth, not the shiny metallic ones. The purpose of cake strips is to insulate the pan, allowing the batter to bake more evenly and slowly along the sides. This ensures that the edges rise fully and prevents doming or cracking in the center. When paired with the right cake pan, cloth strips also help avoid a thick, dark, dried-out crust forming on the sides and bottom of the cake. The shiny metallic strips conducts too much heat to be effective.

Fabulous chocolate cake, a recipe for the ages. While everyone has their own idea of the chocolate cake archetype, this has a lightness to offset the richness of flavour, no less so than a flourless cake. For that reason, those who might be worried about loss of height from trimming the domes, the recipe has already accounted for this- since the cake layers  puffs up proudly as sponges , after trimming,  the end result was still at least 4 inches proud-a layer cake in search of a celebration!

This recipe is very close to the classic Hershey chocolate cake recipe on the cocoa container with no crème fresh needed. Also rather than use boiling water (as the container says) sub hot coffee.  Therefore, no crème fresh needed, less eggs, and for the comments on how to make without coffee, the original hot water works.
The icing… again use the Hershey container recipe ( my splurge is to use Irish butter).

@Mdresearcher do people ask for the Hershey's recipe or this one? What's the point of having a NYT subscription if you're just using the Hershey's recipe anyway? Did you actually make this recipe? lol
@Mdresearcher Everyone’s a critic. This recipe is just different from the Hershey one. With the creme fraiche, it’s richer and the tang helps offset the sweetness.

Total baking time: 25-35 minutes, but rotate the cakes at the 20 min mark. 
Clarifying here for my future self and for others who found the baking time instruction to be quite confusing as written—it seemed the be directing an additional 25-30 min after the rotation. Watching the video on YouTube provides a clearer baking time direction (25 - 25 min total).

If anyone is curious I turned this into a 9x13 single layer cake and it worked pretty well. I turned the heat down to 325F and it cooked for about 35-45 minutes total. It turned out great! (I needed a cake for 20 people and thought a 9x13 would work better than slivers of 8" tiered cake)

@judson i made this as a 9x13 this weekend for my brother’s birthday and this info was super helpful! i baked mine for exactly 45 mins and it was great (could maybe have gone a tiny bit less). thank you!

Can the layers be frozen? She mentions that in the video. We love “snacking cakes” - one layer rounds or squares that last a couple days - perfect for two people. I’d love to make three layers, freeze a couple, and eat them one at a time, making a small batch of frosting for each.

You sure can. I've frozen wedding and birthday cakes and pulled out slices at a time for snacking and they still tasted delicious. Just make sure to wrap with plastic wrap and then a layer of aluminum foil. Here's some info about it from Wilton. https://blog.wilton.com/how-to-freeze-cake-and-other-baked-goods/
Folks that comments on recipes that have been developed by absolute professionals with extensive careers and cookbooks and recommend a Hersey recipe are just plain wrong.

Don't knock it 'til you've tried it. I'd suggest that the Hershey's people are also absolute professionals who know a little something about chocolate desserts. Safitz's recipe is actually quite similar to the one on the Hershey's cocoa can. Ina Garten's recipe for "Beatty's Chocolate Cake" is another slight variation on Hershey's. Maybe I have an unsophisticated palate, but I've tried all three, found little difference among them, and enjoyed them all!
If you use cake strips, the cakes will bake evenly and flat so you don’t need to level them! Best cake trick!

To those chanting that its the same as the Hershey's recipe, the difference is in the technique- reverse creaming method-fat with flour first before liquids, is what creates the unique tenderness of the cake.
What is a cake strip? (To keep a cake level)
https://www.amazon.com/Wilton-Bake-Even-Prevents-Professional-Decorating/dp/B00C1LU8SA Cake strips are a cake baker’s friend

Fabric strips that you wet and wrap around the cake pan.  Keeps the sides cooler as the cake bakes, thus ensuring that the middle and the sides bake at the same rate.  This prevents the middle from rising higher than the sides.

I make crème fraiche with a couple tablespoons of full-fat yogurt (I always have that but almost never have buttermilk) added to heavy cream. Mix in a covered jar and let it sit at room temp till thick, 1-2 days.

I used this German buttercream to top vanilla cupcakes. Don’t let the frosting’s light color fool you - it’s a rich, intense chocolate. 
Only modification I made was to the method: I added all the hot milk & sugar to the cocoa/flour/yolk mix, in 2 additions. Then I put that custard mix back over the low flame. 
Amount was sufficient to generously top 18 cupcakes, plus leftovers to use as a filling for the angel food cake I’ll make with my surplus egg whites.

The directions for the frosting are unnecessarily complicated. Here is how to simplify with the same results: in a small bowl, whisk the flour, sugar, cocoa powder and salt. In a saucepan whisk the egg yolks, milk and vanilla. Whisk the sugar mixture into the milk mixture in the pan and, while whisking constantly, bring to a boil on the stove set at medium heat. Then follow the rest of the recipe as printed. Much easier, same delicious texture and taste.
The batter is very liquid, consistency like pouring cream. The miracle of baking is that the end result is an almost unmanageable loftiness ( trim the domes!)
Can you recommend a substitution for the crème fraiche?
Sour cream. (As noted in the accompanying article.)

Make your own creme fraiche;  to each cup of heavy cream, add 2 tbsp full fat cultured buttermilk. I do this in a jar, shake it a few times to mix and then let it set on the counter for 24 hours or so. It will become tangy and thick.  As a sub, you might try full-fat yogurt or reduced fat sour cream.

@KrazieJane A heating core does not prevent doming. Heating cores are used in deep (3” or deeper) pans or pans 10” or larger to ensure the center bakes evenly without over-baking and drying out the outer edges of the cake. In fragile foam-based cakes, like chiffon, a center core provides support necessary to keep the center from collapsing.

Doming occurs due to excessive heat, causing the cake batter in contact with the metal pan to bake too quickly. When this happens, the batter in contact with the pan sets prematurely as protein denaturation and starch gelatinization are triggered. Meanwhile, the center, still raw, continues to rise, resulting in a cake with low sides and a domed center. If too much heat is conducted, the center eventually cracks, like a volcano.

A thick crust on the cake layers is another sign of excessive heat.

To prevent doming and a thick crust, use uncoated, light-colored metal pans and damp cloth baking strips. Dark pans, anodized aluminum, and non-stick pans conduct heat too intensely for cakes.

Damp cloth baking strips provide insulation for the pan, delaying the setting of the sides and allowing the cake to bake evenly. This helps the sides rise fully, producing a level cake with a high rise. 

Most cakes bake more evenly at 325°F rather than 350°F.

For the benefit of those all over the world who cannot easily source any brand of kosher salt, or who simply choose not to, images of the packaging found online suggest that 1 tsp of Diamond Crystal Kosher Salt is 2.8 grams, and 1 tsp Morton Kosher Salt is 4.8 grams.  No guarantees, but the best numbers I was able to come up with.
What if I used ordinary table salt?
¾ teaspoon of table salt is equal to 1½ teaspoons of Diamond Crystal kosher salt. Cooks Illustrated worked this out years ago.

Diamond Crystal Kosher salt is 2.8g per tsp; Morton's Kosher salt is 4.8g per tsp; and Morton's Table salt is 6g per tsp.  So, 1.5 tsp DCK = 0.875 tsp MK = 0.7 tsp MT.  For this recipe, you could  use 3/4 tsp and call it close enough (you'd be over-salting by ~7% with MT, or under-salting by ~14% with MK).  Or, you could measure by weight and add 4.2g of any (NaCl) salt in the world, and you'd be spot on.
Can you make this batter into cupcakes? How long in the oven?

When measured by volume, Morton salt packs more densely than Diamond, making it about twice as salty. For consistent measurements across brands, either weigh it with a scale, or use half the volume of Morton. Found this explanatory gem in her bagel recipe….

It's a great example  of an American style (vs European nut-based) chocolate cake. Very light. Used some of the frosting to pipe rosettes around the top and scored the sides.  Looked very professional - everyone raved about it.

This cake looked fab that I tried to do this as my very first layer cake. As a german I used the gram-measures … and am disappointed about the buttercream frosting. It is way, way too liquidish and not at all fluffy like in the video. The three layers slipped when I put cake with first frosting into the fridge (and it takes veeery long to harden). What have I done wrong? How could I modify the buttercream recipe in order to get this dense fluffy texture?? Thank you for giving tips about this  …

I guess my only real critique is an 8" round deep pan is probably the hardest pan to buy. I could have bought twenty 9" rounds but have to order the 8" online where I live. I do see people asking for a 9" conversion, which makes sense with the trouble I've had.
I'd like to make this cake, but I wonder if it will be just as good without the coffee, which I can't drink. Has anyone tried it without coffee?

I used decaf coffee, if that helps. You cannot taste it but I think you'd miss it if it weren't there.

Strong black tea might work but I don't know.

Hello! My response may be too late, but just use hot water :) The purpose of the coffee is to enhance the flavour of the chocolate even further, therefore if you can't drink it, the cake will still be fine - it will still be chocolate cake without a magic ingredient. I put coffee in my brownies recipe too.
Any substitutions for the coffee? I don’t drink it, so I don’t have any.
Can you taste the coffee in the cake? (Asking for a chocolate lover who dislikes coffee.)

I’m a caterer and I’ve been searching for a perfect chocolate cake. A cake for special occasions. Many of my jobs are high end dinner parties.
Yesterday, I made this wonderful chocolate cake. It looked beautiful and tasted even better. It’s light and delicious. Even better, the German buttercream frosting. I have avoided making buttercream frosting because of the stress of transporting such a fragile cake. With the German buttercream, I was able to freeze the cake prior to delivery. Perfection!!
This is an overly complicated recipe resulting in a cake that is neither particularly flavorful or moist. Very disappointing.
Can you make the frosting or the cakes ahead and refrigerate then assemble?

On cake strips - I've tried purchased ones, but this DIY version is better - a length of aluminum foil long enough to go around the pan plus a little, folded over a couple times with well-dampened paper towels inside. Wrap your cake pan. It levels layers beautifully, without cutting off a dome of delicious cake. For a demo - see the Preppy Kitchen on YouTube.

Great recipe. Just made this as cupcakes and they are flavorful but still light and airy. Buttercream has an excellent texture. It’s a little too chocolaty for my taste (overpowering the cake), but still VERY good. I might not use the cocoa powder next time and just use melted chocolate. Berries work really well on top for a little pop and tang
I can’t get cake flour (I’m in the UK). Any suggestions what to use? Would a mix of all purpose flour and cornflour still work?
no - don't sub cornflour! just take out 2 TBSP of flour for every cup of flour called. Cake flour just has a lower protein content -- corn flour will alter the texture.
Sponge flour.  Self-rising, so leave out the extra chemical rising agents.
Could I substitute date sugar for granulated sugar?

I made it. Frosted two layers and froze the third and used American vanilla buttercream for the frosting so I could decorate it. My family LOVED IT but I found it not chocolatey enough. I'll make it again for them, but maybe frost with the chocolate frosting in the recipe or just plain chocolate buttercream. I might have overbaked it by a minute or so...
Ran out of energy and time and frosted it with Trader Joe's buttercream frosting and it was great! I wanted white frosting since it was for a birthday cake.

For all of the column space devoted to assuring bakers that this cake is not too sweet, it is. Less sweet than most recipes, but would still benefit from a bit less sugar in both cake and frosting. As others have noted it is comparable to Hershey’s Black Magic Cake (which is the recipe that Ina Garten uses, too, and which I make with 1/3 less sugar than the recipe calls for), though a lighter crumb. Overall a very good cake and nice to try something new-ish.

One year ago I made the worst chocolate cake of my life. Today, I sought redemption and found it in this fine recipe. Great cake - easy to make, just time consuming (as all three-layer cakes are). If this is your first cake rodeo watching the video will be instrumental to success. Only note: the gram conversion for the liquid measurements is off (up to 120g off, I found); use the measuring cup amount for those and you'll soon find yourself in plush chocolate cake heaven.

I guess I like a cake with a little more substance.  The crumb is extremely delicate.  Amazing how much it raises from the liquid-ee batter.  Really a science marvel.   Husband loves it.  I will stay with Ruth Reichel's Big Chocolate Cake.
Cake flour isn't a thing for us here in the UK. Is there a property of this that makes it essential to this recipe or will plain white flour do?
Cake flour is a lower-protein flour, so it has a more delicate crumb. You can add a bit of cornstarch to all-purpose flour to give it a similar texture, but you could just sub it out 1:1 too.
The closest thing is sponge flour, but it's self-rising so leave out the additional chemical rising agents.

You can make your own cake strips from aluminum foil:  for each cake pan, tear off a length of foil longer than the circumference of the pan.  Fold it lengthwise until it is the height of the pan.  Wrap around the pan, and anchor with paper clips (NOT tape!!!).  I’ve been using the same ones for several years; handled carefully they last well.  I leave the paper clips attached and carefully slide the foil off the pan for use the next time.
I did this and still had domes to cut off. I won't do this again. I'd rather snack on cake trimmings.

Can I use cake flour that says “ self-raising cake and pastry flour, premixed and pre sifted; salt and baking powder already added.”?
Thanks.

I Bob, most likely. Do you know how much baking powder is added per cup of cake flour? Then  you could subtract from the baking powder in the recipe. From what I read online it's usually about 1-2 tsp per cup.
The frosting is a keeper, but the cake itself is bland and dry. Try Bernadette Gee’s chocolate cake from the New Zealand Magnolia Kitchen. Easier and better flavor.

I made this cake with a few (minor) alterations. I used 3/4 c sour milk made from a splash of vinegar, 1/4c milk powder, and enough water to fill to a 3/4 c line (I rarely have milk on hand). Making up for the extra liquid, I added an extra 2 tbsp flour to the cake recipe. For the buttercream, I substituted the 3 tbsp with 1 oz unsweetened baking chocolate. I added the unsweetened baking chocolate to the semisweet chocolate (I used chocolate chips). It was DELICIOUS! So light! Not too sweet
It would be nice if ALL ingredients were shown by weight (in metric) and if temperatures were included (e.g. for cooking the frosting, internal bake temp.)

Delicious cake, and forgiving too! 
I made it into cupcakes, I forgot to bloom the cocoa and just dumped it in with the dry ingredients, didn't have enough dutch process cocoa, so used a combination of dutch process, natural, and black.  I put a swiss meringue buttercream on top of them, and super delicious!  
This recipe is a keeper.  Thanks Claire!

I made this cake for a friends birthday and despite not having access to the correct pans and a few other things, it was wonderful. I used two larger pans instead of the 3 8" called for and baked it a few minutes more. I also couldn't find creme fraiche so substituted heavy cream and Greek yogurt. The oil makes it moist and delicate. Although most of the cake was eaten that night what was left over stayed very fresh for a few days.

As a novice baker I thought this would be a good recipe to try as it has very detailed instructions. My cake came out delicious but just on the salty side. I will probably try to use ground kosher instead of diamond crystal and reduce the amount. Also I recommend turning the cakes more often, starting at 5 minutes. And a minor complaint about a recipe that uses weights and 3/4s of the way in starts using measuring spoons instead. Consistency is appreciated by us novice bakers.

The recipe was a perfect b-day cake for my F-I-L. It was moist, light, had right frosting to cake ratio, and was delicious.  I did have to light trim. Highly recommend watching the video.  This was super helpful. Used sour cream in lieu of creme fraiche.

Step 8 is confusing, particularly the 5th sentence ("Adds the yolks...").  If you mix the cocoa powder, flour, salt and sugar and whisk, then add the 4 egg yolks, you CANNOT "whisk more vigorously until the mixture is pale, thick and smooth." Even after you add the warm milk mixture and whisk, you cannot achieve "pale, thick and smooth" because there are bits of cocoa/sugar/egg yolk that make brown bits. Is Step 8 really correct? Would it be better to whisk in the warm milk BEFORE the egg yolks?

Six years ago the NYTimes printed Yotam Ottolenghi's "World's Best Chocolate Cake". I made it and thought "Meh, I'll stick with the old one I grew up with."  But THIS one really is fabulous. I am a convert. I skipped the multi-step icing application and just trimmed the domes and slathered on the icing. Taste is all that matters to me and this one has it. The Vermont Creamery Créme Fraîche is 226 grams so I used a tiny bit of whole milk Greek Yogurt to get to 240 grams.
Yes! This cake is absolutely delicious! 10/10

Also, this is the third time I’ve made this cake. The second time I used cake strips and got a level, higher rise. I felt it threw the cake/frosting ratio off. I've made this several times and submit the following suggestions: decrease salt in frosting to 1 tsp rather than 1 1/2. Here some information about salt and differences; 
Part I - Kosher salt and table salt differ in crystal size, additives, and density, impacting how they are used in cooking. Kosher salt has larger, coarser, and less dense flakes, making it easier to handle for even seasoning and control, and is typically additive-free. Table salt has fine, compact grains, includes anti-caking agents, and is often fortified with iodine to prevent deficiency.
