I followed her steps to a T, used sour cream as a creme fraiche substitute, and the results were phenomenal. Can't wait to make it again.
Made this delicious cake for a birthday party. Raves everywhere. Has anyone experienced the cake tasting a bit salty? It was not a bad thing but just curious.

Fantastic recipe all around. Only in the US would this yield 10 servings - this could easily serve double the amount, the cake is HUGE.  I substituted half peppermint chips for the semi-sweet chocolate in the frosting bc my kiddo wanted a mint chocolate cake and it was a delicious sub.

The frosting is fantastic! I do feel the need to put the hot mixture through a sieve though and it produced enough for me to frost two 8x11in cakes plus a few cupcakes. I do also reduce the salt slightly because I sprinkle it with flaky salt before serving.

I had a question for anyone who might know the answer: do you think I could make this cake with 9 inch cake pans with 2 layers (or still three if that would work)? That’s all I have and this cake looks so good.
subbed crème fraiche for sour cream, such a light and airy cake, not overly sweet which I greatly appreciate
I just made this cake and it was so good! It was moist and spongey and so chocolatey.
Can anyone tell me the height of this cake? Trying to figure if it will fit in my 10” cake carrier
Has anyone tried making this into a tiered cake? This is my favorite chocolate cake and want to make it 2 tiered for a party but worried about the stability since it’s oil based
Has anyone had success baking this at altitude? I'm at 7000 ft and this cake reliably collapses in the middle (rather than doming). It's still very delicious!

I have only ever baked this cake at ~7,000 feet. I always adjust for altitude using recommendations from Jessica Reed's The Baker's Appendix and have not had an issue with the cake collapsing. I think you can find the altitude recommendation online somewhere! I also find that this cake does not tend to dome, but comes out rather flat on top.
@Eatsustainably the same thing happened to me. I’m also at 7k feet. Any tips anyone?

Made as written but had to sub in sour cream, used a 1/2 t of KA espresso powder to amp up the coffee flavor, and Plugra butter for the frosting. The cake was moist yet not too sweet. The frosting was just the right amount of sweetness. Together, it was one of the best cakes I’ve made and my family agreed. I think I have finally found my go-to chocolate cake recipe.
Great recipe!! Moist, fluffy and great flavor. This is going ti my files of recipes.

stats.Total Time: 2 ½ hours, plus about 10 hours’ chilling and resting
stats.Cook Time: 2 hours, plus about 10 hours' chilling and resting

comments.commentBody:
Delicious rolls and so easy to make. To freeze, complete to step 10. Once cut, place extra rolls on a parchment-lined sheet, cover with cling, and freeze. Once frozen, portion in freezer bags. Divide icing and freeze in containers.

8 To 12 hours before serving, place frozen rolls in prepared pan (step 7), cover with buttered foil, and place in frig. Thaw icing. In the AM, remove from the frig and rise for 2 hours before baking. Follow the remaining directions for baking, cooling, and icing.
@Kerry W Thank you for these tips, they worked perfectly at Christmas!
@Kerry W FYI - my frozen rolls didn’t rise following this method after a little under four months in the freezer.

FYI, you can absolutely do step two in the microwave. Nuke it for twenty seconds, stir, nuke it again, stir, go for a third round if necessary to achieve a thick paste. I make challah this way, and it is an absolute miracle. Produces  an amazing, light, professional bakery texture.

Christmas Morning Mods: On Christmas Eve afternoon, I made the dough as directed and allowed to double in size at room temperature. Rolled and cut 14 rolls, divided into 2 prepared 9"cake pans. Covered in plastic and placed in the fridge for 2nd proof overnight. At 6am, rolls had doubled, removed from refrigerator and let come to room temp while preheating the oven. Baked both pans as directed. So tasty! Not an expert but I don't think they were over proofed. Very soft and fluffy!

Delicious. FYI, timing adjustment I’ve done with success at sea level and 7,000 feet: make dough in morning, slow rise in fridge all day, assemble in evening, slow rise in fridge all night, bake in morning (after a pretty much the same assembled rise as in recipe to bring to room temp )
The Speculoos spread filling from Claire’s Dessert Person Babka recipe would work here: mix 1 cup (280g) of Speculoos spread with 2 tbsp melted butter, 1tbsp cinnamon and 1/2 tsp kosher salt

These are great! It's a rich overnight brioche dough with tanzhong added to keep it moist/fluffy. I dislike speculoos so subbed the filling with cinnamon/sugar and blueberry filling (2c blueberries lightly cooked down with lemon juice and cornstarch to thicken). The dough handled beautifully while rolling out, and was very easy to come together. I only have glass pans and opted for a taller sided 8x10 dish so i could only fit 6 plus a baby end piece. Remaining rolls i froze. Came out beautiful!

I made this recipe up to step 10 having cut them into 16 rolls and arranging them into two 9” square pans of 9 (I stuck the end cuts in the center.)  One pan I covered in buttered foil and froze. A week later I moved it to the fridge the night before and then let come to room temperature for about 3 hours the next morning. Then baked per the remaining steps.  The second round came out as tasty and fluffy as the first.

My first time trying to make cinnamon rolls and they turned out better than I could have ever imagined!!! 
I didn’t want to use the cookie based filling, so I stuck with a tradition cinnamon roll filling: 1/2 cup unsalted butter (1 stick), 1 cup dark brown sugar, 1 1/2 teaspoons ground cinnamon, 1/2 teaspoons salt. Wow. The only cinnamon roll recipe you’ll ever need. They were gone within the hour. Truly magnificent!
Can you just use the Speculoos Spread (peanutbutter section of market) instead?

@loves to travel

In her video she specifically says she uses the cookies because they give the filling some substance, as opposed to sugar/butter/cinnamon. I imagine cookie butter is a bit too liquid to accomplish the same goal and could sort of melt away a bit (even though it’s literally made of the cookies, it processes them down so much). There’s usually a reason recipe testers use specific ingredients like this, so best to follow if you’re looking for the result they are after.

I made these successfully with a hand mixer that had a dough hook attachment. It was exhausting to hold it for 30 mins so I don’t necessarily recommend this route but wanted to post that it could be done.

This is a less sweet almost brioche like cinnamon roll. Very good, but I found myself missing the standard more liquid icing. If I make them again, I will swap the cream cheese icing for a more liquid glaze type icing like the one in Alison Romans cinnamon roll recipe.

Make these before you review them, please!! So perfect, I was kneading by hand which took a little longer but was definitely doable. Also made a little extra filling and they were absolutely delicious.

@sandra I also didn’t have my Kitchen Aide-so did everything by hand. They turned out quite well for the first time making cinnamon rolls. Thanks Claire. I’ve made Claire’s croissants and they are terrific.

That’s a lot of steps but well worth it,  I gave my husband a choice of homemade cinnamon buns or cake for our anniversary, no hesitation he picked the buns. He kept asking what step I was on and very excited but a bit guilty that they needed 14 steps.  I used what I had at home and Trader Joe’s almond butter thins seemed like a reasonable substitution and turned out great.

What I love is neither the buns or frosting are overly sweet. I’ll make again for another special occasion.

Not Simple, Not easy. I have made plenty of c buns and these are better. The tangzhong is interesting and the fish line trick was fun. I am sold on the cream cheese frosting, but by hand? Nope. If you have a mixer and and a microwave, use them. 
Add a pinch of salt to the frosting. Save time with the filling, (another commenter agreed) soften some (8 Tbsp?) butter, spread it on the dough, then sprinkle the filling on top and roll. Speculoos changed their name at Ellis Island to Biscoff.

I made these and they were wonderful. My pan was not large enough to hold all 15, though--so I ended up proofing three in a bread pan.  I also think that next time I'll experiment with spreading softened butter on the dough and then sprinkling the Biscoff mixture on top, as I think that may be easier than trying to spread the mixed in version.

These are so delicious! I followed the directions nearly exactly, and they turned out perfectly. I used half the icing to ice the rolls while they were still quite hot, then I let them cool some more (5-10 minutes as instructed in the recipe) before putting the rest of the frosting on. I wanted some of the icing goodness to melt and sink into the rolls a bit — and it was amazing!

I followed the recipe exactly as written and it was amazing. Most compliments I’ve ever received on anything I’ve ever baked. The cookies kind of melted in during the bake, so they didn’t give any sort of gritty texture — everyone was surprised that there were cookies in the rolls at all. I’d encourage everyone to try it as written first before making adjustments! They’re just perfect.
This was a great recipe. They are delicious as well. It was a lot of work, but worth it. I'd say read the recipe once or twice before you dig into it
and watch the video once or twice

I have made these 9 times. I can be that specific because the amazing taste is burned in my memory. Most recently, I made 57 cinnamon rolls and froze them for a family reunion. They froze well, though they did not rise as high. My family did not complain as the breakfast table was filled with oohs and ahhs and they jockeyed for more. I felt like a hero. Here are some of my tips: 
1. Double the filling. 
2. Find the biscoff cookies. They are worth it. When you find them buy many. They have a long shelf life and trust me, people will want you to make these repeatedly. I found them in a pharmacy of all places. 
3. When you cut the rolls, use one roll as a guide for the width. It will ensure all rolls are even. 
4. When you place the cut rolls into the prepared pan, do so neatly; with the rolled edges facing the same direction. The pan becomes the centerpiece from which people pull from. 
5. Subbing maple syrup for the vanilla is a nice touch for the icing. 
6. I am not sure what an over proofed roll looks like. I have had consistent results using this recipe. 
7. I have used high-quality and regular butter with similar results.

These are outstanding. I'm a baker but had never seen these steps in a recipe. It was strange to make, but totally worth it- I wanted them for an early morning birthday treat, so I made the dough a morning before, let it proof in the fridge for about 6 hours, then made the rolls, and let them proof overnight in the fridge to bake early in the morning.

Omygosh, these are fantastic.  The dough was a dream to work with and came out so tender and delicious.  Plan your schedule if you want to have them at a particular time: it took about 4 hours on the second day.

I have made these for family for two Christmas seasons now, and nothing compares! These will take your expectations for cinnamon rolls to a whole new level. The only change I have made to this recipe is doubling the cinnamon.

This January, I decided to see how they hold up if I baked them and froze for later. It works perfectly! I baked them fully and “vacuum sealed” them in pairs in ziploc bags using the straw trick. Then made the icing, split it up by weight and put that in bags to freeze. Come Sunday mornings, I take a pair out for my husband and I, microwave each separately for 1:15, warm the icing bag in hot water for 30 seconds, cut the tip off the bag, pipe and serve. Nothing beats a Sunday morning watching snow fall with a pot of hot coffee and the best cinnamon rolls 2 minutes away. Amazing!

Absolutely incredible. Took a bit of patience as each step was loooong... 100% worth it tho, and honestly, I think these are the best cinnamon rolls I have ever had. 

A couple notes: 1, I split the recipe in ½ (measuring by weight) and baked in a 9x9 glass dish. It worked great. 2, I added chai spices (ginger, allspice, clove, cardamom) to the filling and I think it elevated the flavor quite a bit. Maybe something fun to try! 3, I opted for a browned butter cream cheese frosting -  I think the rolls are better without it. They are so decadent, yet not too rich, and I think they would benefit most from a simple glaze instead of cream cheese frosting. 

Overall, I was ecstatic with this recipe. Not too difficult and yielded impressive results. Highly recommend.

Has anyone tried forming the rolls the night before? At what point would be the right time to do this? I want to make these for Christmas morning and would like them as hands-off as possible that morning.
Is there ANY way to make these with gluten-free flour? They look so amazing but my whole family is gluten-free.

This recipe is quite forgiving! I had tickets to a play, so I rushed making the dough, including the “slow mix step”. I think I mixed maybe 15 min total (including butter step) with speed 4 on my kitchen aid. The dough looked kind of sad, but the next day it looked so much better, more yellow and pliable. I had no issues rolling them up. Using the string to cut the rolls (which I thought was extra) actually made it super fast and easy. Turned out delicious!

The dough is fantastic, really perfect, but the filling and frosting ruin this recipe. The filling is anemic, almost undetectable, and the frosting overwhelms everything, and does not have a good flavor.

These are delicious. I thought that the most amazing part was the rolls themselves. They came out light and buttery, and greatly complimented the filling.  I found that the step of mixing and spreading the filling was a challenge, probably because my "room temperature" butter was too cold.  I ended up using latex gloves and mixing the filling by hand, and then I spread it around on the dough using a warmed metal spoon.
Biscoff is a brand of speculoos cookie

Delicious, with perfectly light and fluffy texture. 
1) some recommend spreading the butter first and following with the crumb.  Having done it both ways, mixing in the butter before spreading is def the way to go, it results in a very pretty and integrated filling.  Separating the butter and crumbs results in butter bubbling out during baking, an oily pan, and messy looking rolls
2) my timing required that I rise only 5 hrs
In fridge, followed by rolling out, and chilling the rolls overnight in fridge - the results were great 
3) I couldn’t make all the lumps go away in the roux, but it didn’t seem to matter
4) next time, I’d probably use less cream cheese and vanilla, and more confectioners sugar in the frosting to create an icing that dries, but this is personal preference
I cooked this in Denver, Colorado. I did not make any altitude adjustments and they came out beautifully.
Us too in Denver

We are complete novices and they turned out great.  We are thinking of adding a little more filling to make a little sweeter.  Would they be harder to roll if we added more brown sugar and pecans?  We were even considering Nutella -- would it be a mess?
César and Fred

Can someone explain the science between all these temperature requirements? Chilled over night, room temp, chilled again.... It must matter but I tried this according to the recipe, and also according to the basic timings and logic of a simple yeast dough with two warm rises. Not much difference. What have I missed?

I made these over the weekend.  1st, my kitchen aid didn't burn out, but it got plenty hot.  2nd used instant yeast if it matters.  3rd I used a bench scrapper to cut the rolls.  Some didn't puff perfectly.  But all were exceptionally  good and lasted 2 days nicely wrapped at room temp.  The icing was exceptional and very easy.  Also mixed in the kitchen aid.  It will be my go to for cream cheese icing.

Good not Great.I've been making sweet rolls and breads for over 50 years, lot of varieties. I was intrigued with this recipe as it was a bit different than the usual.I found it to be moderately more work and a bit more time consuming ever ignoring mixing the filling by hand, that is what mixers are for. Using the cookies was an interesting idea but I have to say I missed the gooey traditional filling. The dough did give my KA mixer a workout, if I make this again I will use my Bosch.

Good recipe! But pretty labor intensive & not equally rewarding. Am not sure if the biscoff cookies added anything. It was almost unrecognizable in the final product. The icing does seem to be less. So you’d want to double or at least try 1.5 times of the given amount.

I always like to make a recipe for the first time as written. The dough for these cinnamon rolls is fantastic. They are light, fluffy. I would call them plush. The filling is also really good, but there is just not enough of it in the final roll. I agree with everyone who said to double it, but my solution is to take my extra biscoff cookies, make more filling and top the cooked rolls with it and then put the cream cheese icing on. They will be extra decadent & have enhanced Biscoff flavor.
I will be making these for the holidays. Can you make the frosting the day before baking then bring to room temp to apply to baked/cooled buns?

Love Love Love this recipe! The rolls are so tender and tasty—very professional. My only point of divergence would be with the frosting. I ended up using an entire package (8 oz) of cream cheese with zero regrets. And I thought that an entire tablespoon of vanilla was too much. I would use no more than half that much vanilla next time. But those are minor quibbles. A masterpiece recipe!

This recipe is absolutely way more complicated than it needs to be. I have made very good cinnamon rolls in half the time. I wanted to try something different but like a lot of NYT recipes, not worth the effort.
Lacking flavor
The dough for this recipe is excellent, it’s light, fluffy, glutinous, and not too sweet. I recommend a different filling, just butter, brown sugar, and cinnamon (lots of it)

As much as I struggled with so many steps I have to admit Claire has done it again (not surprised)! I omitted the sugar in the filling and made just with cookies, cinnamon, and butter. Made cream cheese frosting and my family said they were way better than store bought, and I have to agree. I accidentally let the dough rise too long at RT but the rolls were still amazing!!!! Definitely worth the time and effort!
This recipe is a labor of love! The end result is absolutely delicious. I will still be on the lookout for quicker cinnamon roll recipe, but for holidays and special occasions, this is my new go-to!

Modified the following:
Added about half cup walnuts to the cinnamon mix. Dash of nutmeg
Added a TBL water to dough after adding sugar and salt since my dough was dry and it became much more pliable 
about 2 TBL of whole milk to the icing to make it a little thinner and didn’t wait for buns to be cool to add about half of it.
Spread maple syrup into hot buns as soon as they came out of the oven.

This is a wonderful recipe. I suggest cutting two (little) corners:
1. When making the filling, don’t bother with using a flexible spatula to mix in the butter: keep everything in the processor and process the butter with the rest. 
2. When making the icing, again don’t bother with the flexible spatula, just add everything in the mixer!
Very fun enriched dough, worth the extra effort. We added a good sprinkle of cayenne to the filling and Trader Joe’s spicy pecans on top, with bourbon vanilla icing.

I was kind of over it with the number of steps it took to create these.  But they are hands down the best cinnamon rolls I’ve ever had, let alone baked myself.  These are incredible.  Worth every bit of effort.  I added just the tiniest hint of clove to the frosting…. There’s room to play with the filling too, but the recipe as presented is excellent.
These were fantastic!

My new favorite cinnamon roll recipe! I made it with two small changes to the instructions:
1. Instead of individual rolls, I assembled it into one giant cinnamon roll cake and baked it in a 9" springform. 
2. I did the overnight in the fridge on the second rise instead of the first (step 10). I baked it for a breakfast event and would have had to get up way too early to do steps 6 - 13 in the morning.

I love biscoff but for me personally I don’t think it works in this. The filling is on the salty side and takes away from the whole experience. I’ve made cinnamon rolls a few times and I can’t say this is a favorite :(

This recipe was worth the time it took! The dough is soft soft & fluffy   had the bakery quality feel. I made mine individually in a large muffin tin & they baked up perfectly.

I tried the filling as it was written & I’m not a fan of it. This is personal preference - it didn’t have the nostalgic cinnamon roll/gooey cinnamon filling I personally prefer. It was drier & had more flavor from the cookies than cinnamon in my opinion. Only leaving that part in case someone prefers the oozy cinnamon!

I have successfully made these by hand three times and have had no issues! Definitely requires a bit of elbow grease, but doable if you are like me and have no mixer. 

These rolls are worth every single ounce of effort required. Highly recommend watching the accompanying youtube video a couple of times prior to starting.
What could I do to replace speculos (which I find gross) to make these have a classic cinnamon roll filling?

WOW. Unbelievably good. I even made them subbing vegan butter and cream cheese and they were fabulous. However, I had no luck freezing them as other readers suggested. They did not rise properly when I took them out to bake again and came out dry.

Worth every step. Freeze after separating, pull from freezer 12 hours before eating and pull from fridge two hours before eating. Can make only a few at a time. (15 was too many for my pan) Perfect for Christmas morning! Although used typical butter, brown sugar, cinnamon filling.

I was nervous and been feeling rusty and these came out gosh dang... GOOOOOOOOOOOD. Did King Arthur's cinnamon roll filling since it was a sandy texture like Claire's and I wanted that classic vibe. 10000000/10. Glad I made them. Glad the roux didn't scare me off.

This recipe works great as a King Cake. It stayed fluffy and moist the next day, not dry like most King Cake recipes I've tried. At step 9:  add the filling and away from you as directed until you get to the middle of the dough, then roll the other side towards you until it meets the other roll  in the center. It should look like the Dead Sea Scroll. Fold and crimp ends, shape into a U and bake or freeze.

I made these and took them to my office on a recent visit. So many people swarmed me and even now, two days later, I am hearing things like "that might have been the best cinnamon bun I've ever had in my life" and "your cinnamon rolls were better than [insert name of local beloved family bakery] makes".  

At the beginning of this year I made a pact with myself to stop being intimidated by yeasted dough. I'd say, so far, that has been a great success! Thank you, Claire and NYTC!

Modifications: Doubled sugar in filling , increased ground cinnamon to 1.5/2ish tbsp, subbed Punjabi cookies for biscoff, baked in round 9in aluminum pans, used 8 oz cream cheese

I found the rolls to be a bit dry and heavy at room temp and the filling leaked out possibly because I doubled the sugar amount. Not sure if I kneaded the dough enough because I felt it didn't rise as much overnight and it wasn't as smooth. However, the rolls weren't dry when warmed up in the microwave the next day.

I didn’t have the Biscoff (or any) wafers on hand, so added additional brown sugar (1/2 cup?) and 1/2 cup almond flour, to simulate the texture of the crumbs.  Instead of mixing with butter, I spread butter on first, then coated evenly. These were wonderful - best dough texture and structure I’ve ever had.  Almond flour did exactly what I wanted it to, gave the filling some heft.  10/10
A lot of work, but worth it. Per another reviewer, I filled and cut the rolls and let them rise (covered) in the refrigerator overnight. Let them warm up (about 2 hours) in the morning and baked.

Absolutely delightful recipe. I've made it a couple of times now and am getting more confident with it. Everyone we've shared it with love it as well. My only recommendation is to at the very least DOUBLE the frosting amount so that you can really cover these rolls. People can scrape off a little if it's too much (haven't seen anyone do that yet). Putting the frosting on while the dough is still warm also allows some of it to melt into the interior...delicious!
These are glorious buns. I am gonna try to make a lemon version of these with lemon cookies and zest in the filling.

I want to say everything about these are amazing!!! But I didn’t like the Biscoff filling! I thought I would because I really enjoy Biscoff, but these cinnamon rolls are so intensively homemade, it seems a shame to go through every laborious step only to have the filling taste like a mass produced cookie. Next time I’ll stick to the classic cinnamon-brown sugar-butter filling. If I used cookie crumbs again, maybe I’d try something like Anna’s ginger cookies. The Biscoff were just too distinctive.

This is easily the best cinnamon rolls I've ever made. Used the speculoos spread shortcut found here in comments. I only had a glass pan, so baked at 325 for 18min with foil on before removing, since glass conducts heat a bit diff.
How could one substitute yeast with sourdough starter?

Worth the effort! I had a crisis of confidence when I went to punch down the dough from the fridge and it was extremely firm and cold. The chilled dough rolled out beautifully and was easy to spread with filling. They didn’t truly puff until I removed the foil (15 min into baking) but then they were perfect, with tightly defined swirls, and done baking within 25 min.

Modifications for prepping on Christmas Eve and baking Christmas morning:
1) Did two full sticks of (salted Kerrygold) butter, more Biscoff cookies, and two tablespoons of cinnamon based on comments. I loved the crunch this filling gave the rolls but my kids prefer the plain old cinnamon + butter one.
2) Went with my trusty Sally’s Baking Addiction recipe for the rolling and filling step (18 x 12 inch rectangle, cut into 1.5 inch rolls) and the second rise (put back in the fridge overnight, let rise for 1.5 hours before baking). I’ve also switched from one 9 x 13 pan to two dedicated cinnamon roll pans - USA Pan Bakeware Nonstick Mini Round Cake Pans, Aluminized Steel - which ensure every roll is baked through, instead of the outside ones being underbaked and middle ones being tooo gooey.

This recipe is a classic perfected. You can customize it so easily, adding candied peel to the dough, orange zest or cocoa powder to the filling if the cookies aren't quite your thing. Another banger from our girl Claire. 💖🎀

Loved these and will be making them for years to come! Disagree w those who said they aren’t gooey. We ate them warm, I doubled the filling (double or the cookies and Demerara, and 12 oz of butter) and and there was plenty goo! They were pillowy soft and the perfect ratio of filling to dough when doubled. Perhaps it’s because we ate them warm? Also a tip for my fellow type b bakers - minty dental floss works too. I could not taste the mint haha

OMG.  I am a confident cook, but not much of a baker.   I watched the video 5 times :) These were SOOOO GOOD.  I made for Christmas and followed the great suggestion here to split into 3 days.  12/23 Steps 1-5, 12/24 Steps 6-10 and steps 11&12 on Christmas Morning.  Worked perfectly.
Based on some suggestions here I 1/Doubled the Filling and Used Trader Joes Speculoos cookies ) 2/ Doubled the Frosting (used 1/2 the sugar)

Thanks Clair Saffitz for your genius and teaching!
What are speculoos cookies?
They are thin, crispy, cinnamon-y cookies. You can buy them at TJs, WM, and many other stores.
Specaloos cookies are just like biscoff cookies

They’re the Biscoff brand crunchy brown sugar cookies that Delta airlines serves, or Lotus brand, like the recipe says. 

I’m curious about this recipe overall because it’s so different than a typical cinnamon bun recipe. I wonder why it was developed this way, as the potential grainy texture of the filling is throwing me off. 

Somebody make it and report back!

I like to cook from scratch wherever possible.
I wonder if a substitute for speculas cookies might be some ground almonds with a little bit of extra cinnamon? The almonds would certainly help with the moisture content and might add a subtle flavor.

So Step 5-- you're only chilling it overnight once? When I first read it, it seemed that you chilled it overnight twice, first at the beginning of Step 5, then again at the end. If it is indeed once, please amend this so that it only mentions chilling ONCE. Kinda confusing. Easy to miss a colon that a fast reader might see as a period.

Step 3 says to "whisk" ingredients together and then to turn on a stand-mixer and "mix." Do we use the dough hook on the stand mixer to mix? Or do we use the paddle attachment? And why not whisk with the stand mixer's whisk attachment to get started? And if we're mixing with the paddle attachment, wouldn't we switch to the dough hook for the long slow mix? Nothing beats kneading for gluten development. Would like a little clarification here!

Step 1 says to fit the mixer with the dough hook, so that's what you use for most of step 3 and step 4.  In the beginning of step 3 you can use the dough hook to whisk/mix the ingredients together, or even a fork or small hand whisk.  The point there is just to get the mixture slightly homogenized and cooled before adding the yeast, so you don't kill the yeast; it's not like making meringue or whipped cream where the whisking action itself is so important.  Hope you make them and report back!
A long process, but worth it! These are the best cinnamon rolls we've ever made. I couldn't find Biscoff cookies, so I used ShaSha ginger snaps.
I didn’t like it as much as other recipes I tried. Rather yeasty not sweet enough for my taste and not very pillowy. Sorry Claire I love your recipes normally

Question:  At what point could I separate the buns for freezing?  Would you do it after they're cut and put in the pan, but BEFORE the second rise?  That way, when you were ready to bake, you'd thaw them (overnight?) and do the second rise and bake as directed?  Otherwise, 15 buns is just too much for a 2-person household.
Any suggestions for those of us without a stand mixer? Has anyone tried mixing the dough in a bread machine?

I just made these for my family and they were hands down the best sweet treat I have ever made. I followed the recipe pretty much to the letter. Watching the video helped me understand the steps - and there are a lot of steps! 

Instead of cream cheese frosting , I made a simple glaze (preferred by all in my family) by whisking 3 T heavy cream, 1 t vanilla and 1 c of confectioner’s sugar. Poured a little over the buns when they were warm and then added the rest when they were cool.
I made this over the weekend, and they were fantastic.  Looking forward to making them again.
just made this and my son declared that it was hands down the best cinnamon roll he has ever had. Loved by all. I do not have a stand mixer so this was truly a labour of love.
I've made cinnamon rolls for 50 yrs this recipe is a revelation. Now I'd like the recipe for Gooey Cinnamon rolls, the ones that are baked in a sugary-syrup mixture & inverted to serve.
Is this recipe adaptable for gluten free flour?
If you're gentle, you can use sewing thread to cut the roll into buns

Let me start with some facts: 1-Claire Saffitz is amazing. 2-This recipe has a lot of active time. The baker benefits from some tunes. Can we get into the full Saffitz zone with a Claire Saffitz baking playlist on Spotify? Lets make this happen internet.

I have a kitchen aid mixer. For “slow”, is that the stir setting or 2?  And “medium-slow” is that 2 or 4?   4 seems fast compared to what I saw in the video.  But I’d assume using logic that 2 is slow.  4 is medium slow. 6 is medium. 8 is medium fast. And 10 is fast.  

But I have it on 2 for mixing in the salt and sugar and that seems to match the speed I saw on the video.  

Appreciate any input from kitchenaid owners. 

Thank you.
@Kevin F these are my exact thoughts as well. I’m treating “stir” as a separate setting and 2 as “slow” and then 4 as “medium slow.” Fingers crossed!

In response to Nathaniel, I did steps 1-5 day one, steps 6-10 day two and after they finished proofing refrigerated covered overnight. I served them in the morning on Christmas Day, completing steps 11-12. Everyone said they were the perfect cinnamon roll. This will be my new go to recipe.

The best cinnamon rolls I've ever made! Soft, gooey, and super flavorful. Only change I made was melting the butter and stirring into the filling mix, let cool to room temperature before spreading on the dough. It was easier for me than trying to mash in the butter.
Oh my gosh ! I sub’d cookie butter and brown sugar for the filling . But the dough is really the star & an absolute winner!

I made these this past weekend, and I echo the other comments when I say that this is it. This is THE recipe. I will never look for another. They are sweet and light and *enormous*. The only thing I would do differently would be to double the amount of filling, since it seems to absorb into the bread more than I expected. Otherwise, though, they are perfection.
@Rachelle, I did double the filling, thank you. It was divine!

I love Claire, and I'd never made cinnamon rolls from scratch, so I gave this a whirl Christmas Eve 2023. I DO NOT recommend trying to make cinnamon rolls from scratch for the 1st time the night before a major holiday, you will be EXHAUSTED the next day. As for the recipe, Claire made some pretty big claims with "the best cinnamon rolls you'll ever have." Did the recipe produce a product like pictured/featured? Absolutely yes. Was it a pretty mid cinnamon roll? Also yes. 4 Stars for Claire.

These are the softest, fluffiest cinnamon rolls I’ve ever made! For some reason, the icing came out a lot runnier than hers seemed to be (could be my confectioners sugar), so I found that putting too much icing made the rolls a bit soggy since they’re already so soft. I made this using an electric hand mixer and, although it was exhausting to hold, worked just fine. Will be making again soon!
What attachment should be used with the standing mixer when kneading/mixing the dough?
Dough hook

I’m sure there are short cuts but this recipe is fabulous.  I have frozen 1/2 of the risen  beauties for another winter morning!  I think next time I will add lemon juice and zest to the icing instead of vanilla.  The photos I sent the kids have come back with why I didn’t wait for them…hahaha

I made these for Thanksgiving morning and followed a few tips from the comments.  

1.  I scalded the milk and made the flour/milk mix in the microwave 

2.  I let the rolled buns rise in the fridge overnight.  They were maybe slightly overproofed but not enough for me to notice.  I’d plan to make them that way again for simplicity’s sake.  I froze half of them for Christmas morning.  Looking forward to them already!! 

They were great!!

It's a little involved, but it's very good. These cinnamon rolls are on the less sweet side. That can be a good thing, but if you're after the super sweet and gooey kind from Cinnabon, these are not like that.

Holy moly these are good!! My only recommendation is during the initial dough making stage, when you are incorporating the salt and sugar give that enough time make sure the dough is pulling away like the recipe calls for. Also, it's the winter here and our house is pretty cold when adding the butter make sure it's warmer like claire outlines, I think the dough itself will also benefit from being warmer instead of the 64 ish degree kitchen I was in. Otherwise simple, fluffy, and super delicious

Made this exactly as written (even with some bad influences encouraging me to cut corners and add the butter quicker - took closer to 30 min total for that step because my dough kept spitting the pads of butter out - perhaps they were a little too cool) - and every single person at Christmas said it was the best cinnamon roll they have ever eaten. Worth the time.
Any thoughts on how to make these mini? They are giant and would like them to be a smaller treat for my kids. Thanks!
I would make a longer, skinner roll and then slice them about same thickness. You'll yield more too :)
Not too difficult, just took a lot of time and prep- but such an incredible, delicious result.

The whole Specaloos thing did not sound good to me, so used filling from Butterscotch-Glazed Cinnamon Rolls; also from that recipe, added proportionate cardamom to dough.
Next time I would follow Lois' advice re: resting and rising so we might enjoy in the morning - our Christmas breakfast was at 2:30pm!
This came out fantastic.  Best cinnamon rolls. The only thing I changed is that i skipped the Biscoff. Frosting came out great, too.

I’ve never baked with yeasted dough but love cinnamon rolls and just got a stand mixer so I knew I had to try these. The mixing time made me a bit nervous as it caused my mixer to get quite hot, but with a few breaks (particularly when adding the butter), everything worked out great. These are easily the best thing I’ve ever baked and as far as cinnamon rolls go, the cookies add a lot to the final baked filling texture.

As other commenters have pointed out, the filling and icing in this recipe are quite subpar in comparison to the beautiful dough. Everything is quite bland and honestly overshadowed by the butter. I’d up the filling by at least 1.5x and leave some of the butter out; same for the frosting as well. The process in itself wasn’t that labor-intensive even without a stand mixer; most of the work is done overnight.

This was amazing! my friend and I had a amazing time making these rolls. They turned out delicious. The only thing I would do differently next time, is put less a bit less salt in the filling, it was a bit too salty. I highly recommend baking these!
Has anyone used this dough to make Swedish cardamom rolls (kardemummabullar)?

I doubled the filling and it left a really wonderful lacy bottom that was crispy and caramelized. Also— save your scraps and any extra filling and throw it into a small loaf pan to bake off. It was like monkey bread and sooo heavenly
I brought these to work and my coworkers said they were the best cinnamon rolls they had ever had! Will be making again and again and again!

100000/10. Made these for my husband’s 30th birthday. I gave him the choice between chocolate cake and homemade cinnamon rolls and he picked these and they were amazing. Followed the recipe to a t (did only 1/2 recipe and regretted not doing a full batch and then freezing the rest of the dough). I love cream cheese frosting and thought it was perfect.

I’ve made these twice now and both times they were delicious despite initial difficulties. Both times the dough seemed too dry initially and so never quite reached the same moist, stretchy and tacky texture as in Claire’s video. We didn’t have a dough hook so perhaps that’s why? This also seemed to strain the mixer and had to give it several breaks. Not sure if anyone has tips on how to get the dough more moist if it seems dry in the first few stages? Can’t imagine how the results get any better but more for the sake of making it easier for the mixer.
Mind the difference in salt types! I missed the differentiation and my first batch were incredibly salty. Next batch had my family fighting over seconds

I made the dough on Tuesday and put the dough overnight with the intention that the rolls would be baked on Wednesday. It turned out that I couldn't get back to the recipe until Saturday. I know I was taking my chances but some how we experienced the miracle of yeast and by Saturday afternoon we had 15 perfect, fluffy, cinnamon rolls cooling on the rack.
I love this recipe, but I times and a half the filling as I feel the original quantity produces a dryer bun then I personally like

Very good rolls and worth the work! Biscoff cookies have soy and my husband cannot eat soy. I swapped the biscoff filling for these ingredients and it turned out great!

Filling:
250g light brown sugar
2 T cinnamon 
1/2 t Morton’s kosher salt
12 T softened butter

An excellent Christmas morning treat. Froze them after proofing and then took them out a couple hours before baking. No need to let them fully cool on a rack; we just pulled one out of the pan at a time and iced it right before eating. Loved the filling (and leftover cookies).

revised filling with raisins and nuts
FOR THE FILLING:
¾ cup light brown sugar
1 tablespoon cinnamon
¼ teaspoon ground cloves
½ teaspoon ground nutmeg
¾ cup golden raisins
½ cup toasted walnuts or pecans chopped
¼ cup unsalted butter softened

A dairy-free attempt that went well. Still prefer the original dairy recipe. I also don’t have a benchmark to other dairy free cinnamon roll recipes.

In the end, my substitutions led to less tall, more sturdy rather than gooey rolls. Though I did refrigerate after putting the cut rolls in the pan to slow the rising time to 2 hours and letting sit only for 15mins at room temp before baking. I wouldn’t recommend doing this. 

Ingredient substitutions:
Butter -> Country Crock Avocado or Olive Oil sticks (note: these are salted)
Milk -> Silk unsweetened Soy (similar protein content)

When kneading the dough, it took longer with these subs and had a glossy look. After 20ish minutes, I knocked the speed up to medium-high for a bit until the glossiness was gone and it was pretty similar to my non-DF attempts, but less elastic when pulling the hook out. The rolling process was perfect and beautiful.

I tested two DF icings:
- this recipe with the CC sticks and Tofutti cream cheese (tasted a bit like play dough, could’ve overwhipped)
- a butter icing recipe of 1/2 cup CC sticks, 2 cups confectioners, 1 tsp vanilla, creaming room temp CC then whipping (this was sweet and thicker, which I preferred)

* Country crock avocado sticks have about a 1/3 teaspoon of salt in them, so I used just under a half teaspoon of Morton course kosher for the dough

I’ve made these twice - once completely by hand because my mixer overheated, and once using all the machinery - and both turned out great. Definitely make sure all your butter is actually at room temp and it’ll make the filling + icing easier.
These have good taste & texture but are not superior enough to justify the two-day process of making them

Time consuming? Yes. The best cinnamon rolls I ever had? Also yes!
The dough method was new to me but I was relieved to discover it is very forgiving. It's one of those doughs where everything resolves with more kneading. My oven turned off halfway through baking and I thought I was done for, but it turned out incredible! I did end up using a 10"x7" ceramic pan and a 9" metal cake pan. The metal pan is what I would use next time, but both came out great.
I will definitely be making these again and freezing them!

So soft! NEXT TIME--- 1) I'll half the icing since my sugar tolerance is pretty low. 2) I'll set out all the butter; the second day took longer cause I didn't do that. 3) Spreading the filling took a while. I guess the butter wasn't soft enough (kitchen was at 70 deg. F). OK--- 1) My glass dish seemed to do fine, but I'll invest in a metal sheet pan. 2) My blender seemed to do fine in place of a food processor.  3) My kitchen aid mixer definitely got warm, even at the lowest stir setting.

The video for this was detailed and helpful for the dough part. I could not find speculoos cookies, so I used honey graham crackers. I used raw sugar which gave it a sparkly look. The dough was really the star of the show. Using fishing line really made a nice swirl and a nice presentation. Line up the fishing line straight so that you have an even cut. I had a few that were off kilter. Still really delicious but not quite as pretty.
I am about to attempt these cinnamon rolls. I made Claire’s croissants and after many attempts, the croissants turned out perfectly delicious. Here I go with the cinnamon rolls:) .

Third time making this and it does not disappoint. However, I have never been able to get the Speculoos filling to spread easily. (Nothing like ball bearings, more like spreading the dry part of peanut butter that has separated on soft bread.) The mixture must be very sensitive to slight variations in “room temp” butter. My solve is to gently and briefly heat up the filling on the stove top and then spread on the dough, which I pre-chill in the fridge. Hope that helps someone!
Seems like adding raw flour to hot milk is a recipe for dumplings.  What am I missing?
watching the video helps - you have to vigourously stir/whisk to get the right consistency.

This recipe took me in all the wrong directions. The video is very helpful for visual cues, I believe I overmixed the dough initially which led a to a much more elastic, formed ball that did not easily incorporate sugar or butter in. The rest overnight in the fridge did not result in any rise and a skin formed over the dough as well.

However you make your cinnamon rolls, if you have leftovers (and can wait 3 days for them to properly stale and dry out) they can be recreated into the most heavenly bread pudding EVER. Mark Bittman's basic bread pudding is a fantastic starting point!
Is the stand mixer a must? I do not have room in my kitchen for a stand mixer. I only have a hand mixer.
Yes, a stand mixer is a must. Hand mixers cannot do full gluten development. You should watch the video! Claire addresses this right around the 3 minute mark: https://youtu.be/MrWMl1j4kmI?t=180

I’d like to make the full recipe but freeze half of the cut but unbaked rolls for another day.  What is the best way to wrap and freeze the unbaked rolls and what is the thaw and bake protocol for when I’m ready to bake the frozen batch?

When I make cinnamon rolls and want to freeze unbaked rolls for later, I prepare as directed up to the point of chilling, then rolling and cutting them into individual rolls, then wrap each roll in cling film and bag them in a zip-top bag for storage and pop into freezer immediately. I take out however many I want to bake off, defrost overnight in the fridge and chill until risen, then bake in a small loaf or other type pan that holds however many I want to bake.
What can you substitute if you can't find these cookies?
I think just any kind of spiced crunchy/crumbly cookie would work. Gingersnaps, spiced shortbread, a crunchy gingerbread, a seasonal pumpkin spice, etc.
Windmill cookies are another version of Dutch speculaas cookies.
I live in the mile high city.  Can this be adapted for high altitude?  Thx
I love my KitchenAid and am concerned about 15-20 minutes of mixing.  That's not going to overtax the motor?

I think the recipe time line is misleading. By my calculations this is a 3 day process. Step 5 says chill dough overnight. Then goes on to say chill for at least another 8 hours or as long as 24. Certainly worth it in the end but I’m glad I thoroughly read the time line before starting.
Marlene, if you watch the video, it’s clear that step 5 has two “rests”, the first for an hour to an hour and a half at room temp, then it goes into the fridge for the 8-24 hour long rise.
This looks delicious. For anyone interested in trying other approaches to cinnamon rolls/buns, the Flour bakery recipe (in the Flour cookbook but also online) is to me the platonic ideal!
Really great recipe! The dough is fluffy and moist. Used a mix of speculoos, cinnamon, butter, brown sugar, fleur de sel, and ground almonds for the filling.

Made these for a crowd coming for brunch and they were a huge hit. I followed the recipe exactly. Biscoff cookies in the filling work so well and it was a dream to spread on the dough (I have always found sprinkling cinnamon sugar to be annoying, messy, and hard to get even). I did have to refrigerate the rolled up log prior to cutting. I will never need another cinnamon roll recipe.
Hey Hannah, I’ve been toying with using a tzagon in my challah recipe but can’t seem to get the proportions right- any suggestions or tips?

Wow, this made some truly exceptions cinnamon rolls.  I have made MANY recipes over the years because my family is obsessed with cinnamon rolls and honestly, and among the good recipes the differences are minor.  This recipe makes picture perfect,  incredibly fluffy, not very sweet buns.  The frosting is a must because the filling is not very sweet at all-- the frosting balances the whole thing.  Even if you prefer a sweeter roll, I recommend using this dough.  It bakes up light and delicious.

I haven't even made these yet but I must say thank you to Claire Saffitz for this recipe. I tried the tangzhong method years ago and didn't really care for the texture, so went back to making brioche-based rolls; I've wondered for a long time now if a hybrid method would work. So THANK YOU Ms. Saffitz for developing this recipe. I can't wait to try it!
Anyone tried freezing these? Im thinking to divide in half to save for later.
You can definitely freeze cinnamon rolls (we typically freeze a bunch) and thaw out nicely for a great treat later. We wrap in plastic wrap and then zip lock freezer bag.

I found these heavy and not appetizing.  They definitely didn't keep any better than my old recipe and didn't taste as good.  I did follow all of the directions but they definitely weren't worth all the extra work.
I forgot to let it sit after mixing everything. I just threw it in the fridge. Does anyone think this will jeopardize my dough? Should I throw out?

Hands down the best cinnamon rolls! The video is helpful before preparing. The crumb is so tender. I did not use the cookies in the filling and did not miss them, just added another 1/2 cup of Demerara sugar. I had a hard time keeping my husband from eating them all!

I’ve made these twice and they are worth all the work. So good. The second time I made them I didn’t have the speculoos cookies so I made a double batch of the cinnamon butter sugar filling. I haven’t perfected the nice even spiral that Claire gets but no matter. They taste so amazing.
Should I refrigerate icing or will it keep on counter for 1-2 days?  I store icing separately from the buns, so that I can reheat the buns with that melting the icing.
Can you prepare up to step 10, stash in the fridge and complete these in the AM? Will they over-proof?

Disappointed; recommend 30 vs 60min proof for cut rolls. Followed recipe, was excited at how closely each step matched the video (love the videos!) I baked the end that I cut off and that was perfect. I put the tray of rolls in the oven 5 minutes after that end piece tester (45 minute proof time in a 68 degree Boston kitchen in winter). They seem to be overproofed — large gaps in the swirl, butter seeped out puddling at the bottom of the metal baking dish and the bread was dense. Bun bummer.

This was a revelation. After making various types of rolls for years for Christmas, I never knew about these extra steps to improve the outcome. These buns were pillowy soft and stretchy inside with a perfect crisp exterior. Thank you Chef Saffitz!

I did a long braided version instead of rolls and it just needed a few extra minutes of cooking time. Also skipped the speculoos and made a brown sugar, cinnamon, cardamom filling.
Can you freeze the rolled, filled and portioned dough for later use?
Can these be refrigerated overnight for the second rise instead of the first?

First time making cinnamon rolls, watched the video multiple times and it helped. Didn’t make the biscoff filling as I wanted something faster/simpler so did regular cinnamon sugar. THESE WERE AMAZING! Lots of steps yes but fairly straightforward.

These are the best cinnamon rolls I’ve ever had. The biscoff cookies are a great addition. I added more cinnamon to heighten the flavor, and doubled the frosting to account for a few people who like extra. Watching the video on YouTube was very helpful to understanding the process - I recommend doing that before starting the recipe.
Has anyone tried shaping these right after the first rise, and then doing overnight in the fridge?
Most delicious cinnamon rolls I have ever had (let alone made by myself)!! Perfect baking activity for the holidays and they turned out so light and fluffy! Definitely will be making again.
Love this!!

I made the dough and filling the day before Christmas Eve and was worried about it overproofing. But when I took it out Christmas morning and assembled, it all turned out as great as I hoped it would. I followed the recipe and let it proof for about 30 minutes. Then instead of covering up on the first half of the bake, I sprayed them with a good mist of water. Then baked for 30 minutes. They puffed up so much and were literally perfect! The best cinnamon rolls that I have eaten! Thanks!
I make cinnamon rolls for Christmas every year and these were the best ones yet! A labor of love but totally worth it!

Happy holidays to Claire Saffitz only! Another showstopper, perfectly tested recipe from the queen. SO FLUFFY. I lived in WI too long so I added 2 more oz of cream chz to the frosting. My partner, Taylor, says he'll eat 6 or 7 today - we won't be sharing! Cooked these in a half hotel pan (12) and a mini le crueset (4) and they turned out perfectly. Make sure your KitchenAid doesn't overheat- mine got close! Happy baking!
It’s never enough cinnamon in the recipe. 1.5x?
Wow! So pillowy and soft. I liked how the video made me more confident with each step as I still second guess myself with yeast doughs. Thanks NYTs!
these were great. don't own a mixer, so I was prepping the dough with my hands, and it turned out great (though took some physical effort). The preparation time is long, but really worth it.

I made this 2 times. Not because I wanted to, but I needed vengeance. Validation. Closure. The first time I made these, it was a mess and I ended up making sawdust crackers with proto-cookie butter. How? No idea since I followed everything as is! Then I made these again and made sure to use visual cues, and I'm so unbelievably happy I did. These were soul healing. My only gripe is that the cream cheese to butter ratio should be 2:1 in my ~personal opinion~ but I'm also a cream cheese goblin.
Like others have shared, worth the effort for perfect, puffy, rolls. This will be my usual recipe going forward.

I make cinnamon rolls every year for Christmas, and this is the best recipe I’ve come across. The texture of the dough is perfect. Swapped the cookies for pecans and added orange zest to the filling. Divine.

Very yummy. I subbed in 100 g each of bread and whole wheat flour and really liked the results. Had to up the liquid a bit to get the right dough texture.  The biscoff cookie filling was delightful!! With more than 1 T butter per roll (before frosting), these are definitely a once per year indulgence.

I tried this recipe and used fresh ground AP flour that was gifted to me for christmas purportedly especially for baking bread.  They were not pillowy, but almost eggy texture.  It most likely is the fresh ground flour.  The taste of the cookies was dominant and unpleasant. The cream cheese frosting had twice as much sugar as needed. It was a complete disaster.   If it was just the flour, I would consider making them again, but the center was very unappealing.

These are fabulous! I missed the first room temp rise of letting the dough proof before putting it in the fridge but they came out amazing anyway! Will definitely make these again to see what my dough missed out on lol these were not too sweet, a brioche style bread with a tender crumb. I also made the filling without the cookies, and while it was still good, I would add more sugar and cinnamon next time as I wanted more of that flavor.
Moist, light, delicious, delightful. The speculoos is a great touch.

Turned out fantastic! Made a couple changes based on my timeline and what I had in the house: used pecans instead of Biscoff cookies (didn't want to go to the store) & upped the sugar in the filling a little to compensate. Used a bit of buttermilk to make a full cup of milk for the dough. It separated and looked crazy in the pot but all came together once the flour was added. Didn't have time for a counter-then-fridge rise, so just did a long, cold fermentation in the fridge and it worked great!
Followed the recipe closely, and they turned out to be the best rolls I’ve made: moist, great texture.

Delicious! Very good recipe and I like the slow cooking vibes! The only change I made is to substitute filling from Claire Romans cinnamon rolls recipe. My children really like the way the butter and cinnamon sugar mix sticky caramelised bottom to the rolls. and no topping we don’t think it needs it.
Dutch ‘speculaas’ cookies are also great to use, or the ‘speculaas’ spice mix if you prefer less sweetness.

I just finished making these.  Love Claire’s desserts.  This was definitely a project, but so much fun.  i haven’t baked at altitude for 40 years  [5000 ft.]— so I was unsure of the rise and bake.  The texture is pillowy and melt in your mouth delicious.  I will say, they adding-in of the butter to the dough was really frustrating and time consuming.  One must do it properly though.  I added the sugar and salt to the dry ingredients early, to no discernable effect, thank goodness.  Great!

Made as described, had 4 rolls that didn’t fit in pan. Claire might be rolling buns tighter than me? 

Added the glaze from Allison Roman’s cinnamon bun recipe in addition to the frosting. For the frosting used vanilla extract with specs to add visual interest. 

Overall was a hit, people loved them. Will have to make again.

Just made these and they were delicious! Agreed with the comments on not being overly sweet which we loved. I had to stop at step 10 on the second day of making these and let them rise/bake on the third day and they still turned out amazing. Would recommend it for a holiday or larger family/friend gatherings.
The only thing I would adjust is to add more cream cheese and less powdered sugar. I like mine a bit less sweet. Besides this, it is a very good roll!
is this 350 degrees Celcius?? thank you!

WOW! So good! Baked it on Thanksgiving morning and I'm still smiling thinking about it:) See 
?! There I go again! :)
Amazing rolls - perfection! Not too sweet.
Could the kneading be done in a food processor?

I made this without a stand mixer, just my two hands for the dough, whew. Genius idea for the cookies in the filling (it's not gritty lol).  I didn't do the parchment, they came out of the pan easily.  Excellent Christmas morning breakfast, I made the dough on the 23rd, the filling and the icing on the 24th, assembled Christmas Eve, and baked Christmas morning.  I set the icing out while the rolls baked.  I added the zest of an orange to the filling and another to the icing.  Yum!

Not the biggest fan of the filling, but the dough was incredible!!! Like and fluffy, great rise, so soft and tasty. I’ll never make cinnamon roll dough another way! Next time I’ll probably just do a classic cinnamon sugar filling instead of the Lotus cookies.

The flavors are nothing short of world-shaking. (My family agreed.) However, the recipe is labor-intensive, so they're truly a labor of love. The dough's overnight rise was too vigorous. Was it the bread flour or the Red Star Platinum yeast? Still, the crumb was lovely

Out of necessity, I substituted mascarpone for the cream cheese (excellent!). I had to fit 6 buns into each of two 9-inch cake pans. I added a bit of lemon zest to the icing. Finally, per Kerry W's advice, I froze what was left.
The dough in this recipe is incredible.

These are incredible! I feel like cinnamon rolls are often disappointing because they are dry and crumbly, but these are pillowy and delicious! I was nervous about the non-traditional cookie filling, but I didn’t miss it at all. It tastes just like the cinnamon sugar mixture but without making them too sweet. PS- at least on the west coast the cookies are called Biscoff. Lots of work but worth the payoff. Total hit!

This recipe has been a Christmas staple for the last few years now. It is a LOT of commitment (and yes, based on the timeline, it's more reasonable to expect it to be an afternoon tea pastry than a breakfast pastry. But then again, I sleep in pretty late haha), and it is at its best the same day. So I usually call all my friends and neighbors to get some when I bake them. 
I have found the filling to sometimes overflow, so I would go easy on it.
I reduced the butter to 80g, upped the cookies to 180g

Fantastic rolls just as outlined.  I found the cookies softened so much in the filling it really didn’t add any texture (just as they do on Delta flights when we ever so quickly dip them in our coffee). So next time I will omit the cookies and just make a traditional c roll filling. Since the cookies add nothing special, why consume all those nasty processed ingredients?
These were instantly devoured with no leftovers. The best cinnamon rolls I’ve ever had. I enjoy the complex flavor of the filling from the Speculoos cookies

Sublime. I used brown sugar instead of demerara and skipped the cookies, which I did not have. I'm not a fan of cream cheese icing so I made the icing out of powdered sugar, butter, vanilla and fresh squeezed orange juice.
I have formed these the night before and put them in the frig overnight. It works well, but I would caution that sometimes they aren’t completely risen in the morning and need some time to proof.
Do you think you can use cinnamon graham crackers instead of cookies?
@B of course.
Love these! Our preference is to put the icing on while the buns are hot so that it melts in. Yum!
We didn’t have the cookies or the demarara so we just used brown sugar and cinnamon. Should have used a bit more for more filling

I tried baking this off in a much larger pan so the rolls would not be so crowded up against each other and the result were perfectly soft, fluffy and huge rolls. This recipe is a hit time and time again and I don’t think it’s gone wrong for me yet! And I’ve tried a lot of cinammon roll recipes. This one is my number one.
YUM!!! Different from traditional cinnamon rolls but I love the addition of biscoff cookies.

This recipe looks daunting, but is delicious. The verbose directions are actually very helpful. Made dough day before Christmas and baked Christmas morn. I used an envelope of dry yeast rather than a teaspoon and everything rose perfectly.

Definitely a trust the process sort of recipe, but they turned out great. If you want these same day, proof in the oven (on verrrry low heat - my oven lets me do 40 C/ 85ish F ) with a proofing bag/plastic wrap covering for 1 - 1 1/2 hours until the dough is large and in charge. I proofed the rolls once situation in the pan again for about 15 minutes and then baked as directed.
This recipe is WORTH IT. They’re so good. Do yourself a favor and add orange zest to the icing 🤤

I didn’t have those speculator cookies, and I couldn’t find them anywhere, so I just used equal weight of Cinnamon Toast Crunch cereal.  Turned out very very good.  Followed the rest of the recipe exactly.

A lot of steps but I found I can make them the night before, proof once, roll cut and fill and place them in the pan in the fridge overnight. Next morning I let them come to room temp 1-2 hours before baking. Perfect for holiday mornings or chill weekends. My theory is that the tangzhong and butter makes this a *mostly* foolproof recipe with a lot of flexibility. 
Bonus points for pouring a bit of cream (maybe 1/4 to 1/3 cup?) into the bottom of the pan as they bake! (gooey + caramel!)

Wow- I have always been intimidated by yeasted doughs ( I bake everything else under the sun) attempted these for super bowl weekend and they’re amazing! Wow! The filling is going to be my hack from now on for other treats.
In lieu of a food processor to crumb the lotus biscuits, I used a box grater and it worked nicely.

Insanely good, bakery quality rolls. The dough is a labour of love but is well worth the effort. If you’re not in the mood to freeze/want the rolls to be a one day effort, let the dough rise the first time, roll it out, spread the filling & make the rolls and let it rise in the pan for 45 minutes. And into the oven she goes! A+ recipe
Perfect dough. The recipe is easy to follow.  Always make this with traditional brown sugar and cinnamon and butter mix. No cookies.

Made as directed and they were wonderful. The directions make it seem like a lot of work but they weren’t any harder than other cinnamon roll recipes I’ve made. The dough came out delicious: soft and fluffy (other recipes I’ve made always turn out a bit tough, which may be my doing, but still). I might try next time with a more traditional filling, as I was hoping for a more cinnamon-y aroma. Still, amazing rolls!

putting this here for my OWN benefit lol but feel free to try it
Earl Grey Macarons: 
- sub freezedried raspberries in the flour for 10g loose leaf earl grey
- no raspberries in the dough, but boil 1/2 cup of cream, stir in 1 tbsp earl grey, steep for 30 min, then strain w/cheesecloth and reheat before mixing with chocolate

I think 275 might be too low a temperature. This was my first semi-fail in a while, and I think it was the temperature because the centers didn't quite finish, and they stuck to the silicon mats I typically use. Usually, 300 works great, per the Melissa Clark recipe. The flavor of the cookies was great, though, so I'll definitely try again soon!
I noticed mine stuck too and I was wondering why. Next time I’ll do 300°
My macarons came out fantastically! I would recommend not necessarily sticking to the suggested times for whipping the meringue, mine took maybe 6-7 minutes to get to stiff peaks.
Yeah, same here. Definitely look for it to start collecting inside the whisk

I would love to make these! But after reading through the recipe, I think it calls for a video, especially to demonstrate the parts that call for a “rapid, tight circular motion” or “fill in the pockmarks”. How?
There is a video on the NYT youtube cooking channel. If you read the attached article, the link can be found there.
For those asking for a video, luckily there is one on YouTube: https://youtu.be/j1QImGKOd5U

This recipe worked beautifully for me on a very cold and dry winter day in Chicago. After baking these off, I was crazed with power and decided to make a batch of vanilla macarons as per the Melissa Clark recipe, which had worked well for me last summer. Despite first forming a nice skin, the vanilla macarons immediately domed and cracked in the oven. Looking back, I noticed that this CS raspberry recipe has a much greater ratio of powdered sugar to almond flour than the MC vanilla recipe does. Long story short - after this experience plus some research, I concluded that high-sugar recipes like this one likely work best in dry climates whereas recipes more balanced between almond flour and powdered sugar will be less prone to cracking when it’s humid.

If you're like me and don't have all the equipment, I used parchment, inverted rimmed baking trays (watch out with the countertop tapping), the whisk attachment of an immersion blender (hold a huge bowl at an angle to avoid splashing, and stop to stir), and didn't sift. They weren't as pretty as Claire's, but they developed feet and tasted great! Great video & great instructions for someone new to macarons. If you're not into identical/round, give it a whirl even if you don't have all the stuff.

Came out great, but I might use a higher temp next time and bake for less time.

Also, no reason not to use a pinch of cream of tartar when whipping the egg whites before adding sugar. Will be easier to achieve stiff peaks and a stable meringue than without it. With this planned tweak, I added the half teaspoon of salt to the dry ingredients before blitzing in the food processor.

I am also inclined to go with parchment paper over silicone liners for macarons. Macarons tend to adhere a bit too much to silicone, in my experience.

I'm excited to try this as macarons are one of my favorite things!  Thanks for the great tips!
Do you have any experience substituting with coconut sugar?
@Julia.  There is a video for the whole process in the article associated with this recipe.
There is a YouTube video from Claire, posted yesterday.
I love a little challenge in the kitchen, but there are limits. I'm sure these are delicious, much better than store bought. I'll never know.

Adaptations: used store bought egg whites and frozen raspberries.

First batch of meringue either wasn't room temp or there was grease, but I never got to the stiff peak stage. Started over and gave it a solid 45 mins out and they worked out a whole lot better.

Hygrometer is showing 40% humidity and they took 50mins to get to skin stage.

Raspberry ganache was a lot darker than the photos and video. I did use some frozen berries, but I didn't think they'd make that big a difference!

Like another commenter noted, 275 is too low of a temperature to bake these at. I had already made the chocolate ones a week prior, and wanted to try these next. Could not think of why this time they came out totally split from the top of the shell and soft almost raw underneath, despite giving lots of extra time between “wiggle tests”. Then I made the chocolate and they turned out perfect, like the first time I tried them. They bake at 300, so that must be the answer. Delicious anyways!

Updating my Earl Grey recipe:
Actually heat 3/4 cup of heavy cream and steep in 1.5 tbsp of loose leaf earl grey. Strain and shoot for about a tablespoon shy of 1/2 cup. It’s up to you really.

Shells turned out fabulous. Sifted twice and skipped the gel food coloring because I didn’t want to take any chances. The ganache was a bit runny because I washed the raspberries and didn’t let them fully dry. So it took a while to be firm enough to pipe, a few hours. If I make these again I’ll probably just use the rest of the freeze dried raspberries for the ganache because it would be easier and save time.

The shells turned out quite good, tried a higher temp but they browned too much. Ultimately I did 275 for almost 30 mins and they worked!

The ganache is where I’m having trouble, are the proportions right? The video calls for 6oz choc and the written instructions say 4 oz. Tried both and it was so runny! Any tips?
I recently took a cooking class to learn how to make macarons. The shells are quite fiddly to get just right. I highly recommend taking a class if you haven’t! I am now obsessed with macaron making.

Yes I agree with notes below …after reading this recipe

First - take a class
Second - practice a lot 
Goal - make macarons for holiday presents

I use to love macaroons and then I developed a severe allergic reaction to almonds after my second pregnancy. Does anyone have any suggestions for substitutions for the almond flour? Any considerations while baking?
Honestly I have always needed to add powdered egg whites to make the macrons set. Even with aged egg whites I could never get the tops to set correctly in the over. Any tips would be great!!
The best substitute for almond flour is Sunflower seed flour. Hope this is helpful!
Made this and they came out perfect! Thank you!
Made it exactly as she called for, baking at 275, and they turned out perfectly! Claire’s recipes never fail.
First time baking macarons. Cooked all three ! Thanks Claire :)

After reading the comments, i tried baking one tray at 275 and one at 290 - 275 was better. However, they did brown a bit, so i’ll be trying a super small batch at 270 next. I ended up baking for about 20 minutes.

These worked beautifully, once I got the hang of piping the shells. Per others’ comments about the oven temperature, I baked my first sheet at 300 and they were delicious but a little bit brown in color. The second sheet I put in at 300 and immediately reduced the heat to 285. They took a couple of minutes longer but retained a nice pink color.

The filling was a little sweet for my taste—I think next time I would use a raspberry buttercream.

If you're a lowly peasant like me and your oven temperature is unreliable, THIS RECIPE WILL NOT WORK. I have, however, been able to turn out perfect batch after perfect batch using my toaster oven! I cut silicone mats in half and load them on an upside-down half-sheet tray, turn my Ninja Flip toaster oven to 260 degrees, and bake one half-sheet at a time on the lowest notch for 23 minutes.
