comments.commentBody: Watching Claire Saffitz make these leads one to think that precisely following her instructions will yield approximately similar results. But Claire is a super-experienced chef and a great presenter. Mine have never come close to the her excellence standard.Though I can watch her making these all day long.

I have had great success with Will Cotton's Parisian style lemon macaroons(NYT - 12/2005). Technique is slightly different but works amazingly well - yummy! I look forward to trying the chocolate macarons as it looks delicious!

I made these chocolate macarons as a test drive before taking on pistachio.  I watched Claire's video and followed her procedures exactly.  I ended up with 45 wafers instead of the 46 I needed because my test wafer was too big.  I didn't have bittersweet chocolate for the ganache so used semisweet.   I used Trader Joe's cocoa powder for the wafers. 

It was magic!   My macarons came out exactly like the picture with the little ruffled feet.  They tasted dreamy after aging.  Thanks Claire!

What are the dimensions of a "Medium circle pastry tip"? Also, can one assume that freeze- dried raspberry powder is an acceptable alternative to whole, freeze-dried berries?

Thank you, Claire, for the excellent tutorial video demonstrating each step in making macarons. You are a brilliant teacher!

I followed the recipe very closely and also referred to the YouTube video and they turned out almost perfectly! I only got 15 macarons though - maybe I made them a little bigger than Claire’s? The tops also turned out a little uneven. But otherwise, excellent. Great recipe.

It too me two tries but on the second try Glorious Success! (The first try was delicious but the tops wrinkled like a good brownie. They tasted like the perfect chewy brownie edges though- I'm not mad about it). For me the big difference was quitting the macaronage a smidge earlier than I had the first time- judging the batter falling off the spatula was a little tricky. Both times were in very dry conditions. One of the best things I've ever made. Thanks!

I taught myself to make macarons in 2022 and I've gotten quite good at them after about 3-4 months of mixed results.  I've never made chocolate ones that look accurate though. The cocoa powder can make the shells wrinkly,  I think it's the oil in the powder. They've been delicious but just don't look accurate, and a macaron is a very precise in looks cookie. I often make unflavored shells and chocolate ganache as the filling.  I'm tempted to give this recipe a chance though.

Malika, I too have taken on Macaron making over the last 5 yrs. Chocolate shells are finicky. often leading to wrinkly top or sticky bottoms. I've learned to check the fat content of the cocoa powder I use: the less fat, the better in this case. I find Hershey's works the best. Also, I err on the side of less cocoa and add brown food coloring. I get pretty standard results, and if I use a strong, chocolate-flavored filling, I get consistent, tasty results with the look I want.

The other issue most relevant this summer is humidity. Meringue is finicky too.  Even if you have the desire to bake on a rainy day, the odds in your favor go down unless you have  a controlled humidity environment.  Just something to think about.
@Lynn Thank you for your insight… first time making chocolate and have wrinkly tops. I will try Hershey’s next time!

These are really delicious. I am not a great baker of cakes or cookies but followed the instructions to the letter. Much simpler than I thought they would be given the rather long and detailed instruction. Only problem I had was trying to stuff the mixture into a bag for piping, I ended up looking like a muddy mess, and lost a good amount of the batter! Wound up using a couple of teaspoons to drop the mixture onto the baking sheet. Worked perfectly although less perfect-looking than the picture!

I look forward to trying these. Have had great success with Will Cotton's Parisian style lemon macaroons(NYT - 2005)- really luscious! The bittersweet chocolate filling sounds delicious. Will report back.
This is a wonderful recipe for chocolate macarons. Just do exactly what Claire says, and you’ll get amazing results. Currently on my third chocolate macaron of the night—they’re delicious!!

Also, I must be stingy on filling, because I had a bunch of ganache left over. I stirred in some absinthe & popped them in the freezer to make truffles, then used the egg yolks to make absinthe custard ice cream.

These turned out beautifully! I’d never made macarons before, as they always seemed a bit intimidating. I’ve seen plenty of people have trouble with macarons turning out correctly, but I decided to give it a go after watching Claire make them. And I’m glad I did, because they’re delicious. I was able to get them to bake up with a foot and the ganache is the perfect filling. I got about 30 sandwiches out of the recipe, so I obviously couldn’t eat them all but they freeze incredibly well.

I’ve been making macarons for about 10 years now, but I moved to high altitude about 5 years ago and have not been able to get them right since then. These turned out SO perfect! I wish I could add pictures because they are just beautiful! I think the key changes were WAY stiffer egg whites than I have ever used and food processing the dry ingredients. Welcome back to the rotation beauties!

Claire makes it look so easy, but it isn't.  This was my first attempt at baking macarons and I followed all the instructions, processed the dry ingredients, beat the egg whites to a still peak, turned the bowl over , then macronage, which took some time to get right.  I tested a teaspoon to see if they had enough drying time, everything checked out.  Except, they turned out rather flat and crackly on top with a tiny foot and seemed done but was undercooked after 18 mins.  Very disappointed.

Would be nice to have some consistency with the measurements listed. The chocolate for the ganache is the only thing in oz. I made the ganache and it was way too liquidy, never set enough to be used as a filling.

My first batch had cracked tops - i think it’s because of air bubbles.  Got them right on my second try - i whacked the tray firmly on the counter (with no kitchen towel, as she does in the video) and that did the trick.

I always thought that macarons were these elusive impossible dessert that could only be made by pros with stand mixers. Gave these a try after watching the video and they came out so perfectly- I was shocked!! Thank you as always Claire for making the impossible possible :)

My husband and I have been planning on trying an ambitious baking project lately and this is the one we settled on. Claire’s video was super helpful. We accidentally bought 70% Cocoa for the filling. The filling was bitter by itself, but it ended up tasting great in the finished product. We were so pleased with our very first attempt and it made a great date activity. Thanks Claire and NYT Cooking!
They are delicious very time consuming but worth it .   I'm not a bot

My macarons had a tiny foot and were quite wrinkly on top. I think it’s because my meringue wouldn’t move past medium because (I think because I used an immersion blender with a whisk attachment to whip them) and I used brown sugar with a 1:1 ratio. Any advice?

First run at macarons! I made a batch of these and a batch of the raspberry. I didn't have crinkly tops but did get some top cracking & doming -- second batch with longer resting/drying time had fewer cracked shells. I used King Arthur black cocoa. Also, macaronage finished almost immediately, much faster than the raspberry batch.

I've been making macarons since my first visit to Paris, 22 years ago. I prefer the results I get when using the Italian meringue method with its boiling sugar syrup. You can easily find a recipe online if you'd like to try it.

160°C fan forced oven
To get skins, dry in fridge or room with dehumidifier

Mine had tiny feet and domed (but not cracked) heads. I bought some King Arthur espresso powder and I will add it to future chocolate confections. It improved the ganache. I may try this again and beat the eggs whites longer and see if I get taller feet.

Macarons have been on my “to conquer” list for years. I finally gathered the courage to have a go at them today. I was a little hung up on wanting to do an Italian meringue for added stability, but in the end decided to do Claire’s recipe verbatim as she has never steered me wrong and every recipe I’ve ever made of hers has ended in success. I am thrilled to say that they turned out (nearly) perfect! My two issues, easily correctable, are that I made them too big, and two have a tiny bubble.

I made this recipe working in grams and the ganache ended up much too thin to pipe. I used ml=g as a default for liquid ingredients, but double checked and it turns out 1/2 cup heavy cream is 112g not 120g. Apparently a big difference!

I also made my own almond flour in a food processor, sieving a few times and using only the finest fraction. Worked great and is much cheaper/more available where I am.

I can't believe I successfully made macroons! Followed this recipe to a T and they came out great. Not as perfect as Claire's obviously but good enough that I was able to share a plate of these with family. Everyone loved them and were impressed at my first time result. This is my bible recipe for macroons from now on! Trust Claire, her recipes have never lead me astray.
AMAZING I’m litterally a kid and made them! My first time and soooo easy

Help! Any advice for someone who has added ALL the dry in and sifted it only to realize the granulated sugar and salt was supposed to be reserved for whipping the egg whites to stiff peaks?? Asking for a friend… Considering adding half the granulated sugar that the recipe asks for but will the peaks get stiff enough? And what’s going to happen to the final product with the extra sugar in it? Hoping the solution isn’t to throw it away and start again because that almond flour was pricey. Blaaaahh

I did the same thing - despite reading the recipe through, I still accidentally added the granulated sugar in the dry ingredients! I ended up starting over with a new bowl of dry ingredients because I was worried the end result would not work and be too sweet, and will just try to turn my original bowl of dry ingredients into a marble cake or brownies.
these macarons are delicious, and they’re coming out almost perfectly, but the tops are a bit wrinkled.  Any advice?
i'm excited to try these. Serve chilled and keep in the refrigerator? Has anybody tried keeping them at room temperature? What happens?

I give it a 4 out of 5 stars because fat content of the cocoa powder is not mentioned nor given as a tip in the recipe. My macarons while delicious came out wrinkly. And I think it’s because my cocoa powder has 1% fat per serving which caused me to make another batch only to have the same issue. Research suggested it was the fat content in the powder I chose. Not a big deal but really messed with my perfectionism a bit.
Feet were gorgeous, ganache was fabulous.

I followed the recipe exactly using weight measures and attempted two batches this evening. I think I underwhipped the meringue, overmixed the batter, baked at too high a temp, and didn’t rest them long enough (only 1 hour) for the first batch. They didn’t get any feet and were wrinkled/cracked. The next batch, I think I properly whipped the whites and rested them for two hours, but maybe my macaronage is still off? I dropped the oven temp to 270, which seemed to reduce the cracking a bit. I did get little feet, which is progress, but they still look like the cracked/wrinkled top of a brownie? One site said that using darker pans may be contributing (I used dark, rimless insulated pans), so maybe I’ll try lighter pans next time. Other commenters mentioned the fat in the cocoa; mine was 1%. I appreciate the recipe and guidance. This is my most successful attempt so far, and I’ll keep working at it. There are so many factors that can change these things.

In step two you advise to mix and grind all dry ingredients. Which I did.
In step three you advise to whisk the egg whites and add the sugar.

Can you advise me how I should get the sugar back out of the dry mix? Tweezers are not working.
Two types of sugar are required for the shells: confectioner and granulated. You mix the confectioner with the almond flour, and use the granulated with the egg wites.

EEK yay mine got feet! They’re a bit wrinkly but they have feet! YAY! I had to whip for very long and my macaronage was…excruciating (I got a giant painful callus on my finger) but i think it might all be worth it! The moments of pure glee when I saw the feet rise and the skins form are why I bake.

This is certainly an intense project! I played my meringue some inspirational Hamilton music when it wouldn’t whip, and it totally helped. The shells definitely got wrinkly - maybe lower fat cocoa powder would prevent that? They still look photogenic with wrinkles, though. My skins formed very quickly (about 20 mins on a cold day), which was very relieving knowing the first time I tried this it took hours and even then didn’t work well. I haven’t finished aged them yet, but before aging they tasted very…fudgy. Like delicious brownies! Good luck to everyone trying this!

I need to know why my strawberry macaroons came out like a Hersey kiss , please help me perfect them next time ! They tasted great and had the perfect texture but I couldn’t stand them when sandwiched together ! Had to cut off the tip of the ones on the bottom to balance them!

The tip means that you need to mix the mixture more up to a ribbon like consistency (when you drop it from a height, it should fold/incorporate into itself). The tip means that your mixture was still stiff, and needs more mixing.

Miraculous! Made these following video & recipe as closely as I could, and I gasped when I saw the feet appear in the oven after a few minutes! I weighed ingredients, followed the stiff peaks, used KA Triple Cocoa powder and no wrinkling, beautiful feet, perfectly smooth on the bottom. They are crispy and slightly hollow, but hoping that the refrigeration helps. I am at altitude and will try again with slightly softer peaks, perhaps more almond flour, less baking time. But soooo good!!

comments.commentBody: I think you ought to be more specific about the pistachio spread.  There are a variety of commercial brands and a number of homemade recipes and the fluidity of each is variable, which might disappoint the final product between runny and stiff.
There is a YouTube video of her making these on NYT cooking channel.  I don’t know why they didn’t link it to the recipe.
Pistachio spread is pistachio butter similar to almond butter or peanut butter. You can get it online or at Whole Foods.

In the description above the recipe, it says pistachio spread is sweetened.  There is no other sweetener in the filling recipe, so I think if you use pistachio butter, you'd need to add some powdered sugar.
This would be an awesome video. I love Claire's videos and macaronage is something so visual.

The batter for the pistachio macaron is too runny.  What could I have done wrong? Is there a fix or simply start over? One possibility I can think of is the grinding of the dry ingredients. I seemed to have a lot more than a tablespoon of dry ingredients after sifting. The mesh I use could be too fine although the chocolate ones came out just fine.
@Dilkash It’s possible you beat up the macaronage too much and knocked the air out of it. This is just a guess though; macarons are finicky.
Why's the bowl gotta be heatproof for the pistachio cream
I think they did a copy/paste from the chocolate macaron recipe, which actually does require a heatproof bowl for the ganache filling.

Great teaching video, thanks.  Is there a Claire Saffitz bomb proof ingredients list for a plain macaron (no pistachio, no freeze dry fruit, no chocolate)?  I have tried the chocolate and rasberry but would like to expand my horizon with lemon or orange.

@Jim I wondered the same thing and didn’t find one. But I did some math to work out the relative percentages of the ingredients in the shells for each of her macaron recipes, and found that if you sub almond flour for the pistachios in this recipe it works great. 85 total grams almond flour, skip the pistachios, and I’ve had awesome results for a plain shell!

also a tip--claire doesn't really mention in the video anything about overdoing the macaronage and making a thin batter. in my first attempt, i was really pressing the batter against the bowl as she described to knock the air out, but it was too much apparently, and my batter went really thin (spreads like a pancake when you pipe it). some other causes of this might be stopping at firm peaks rather than achieving stiff peaks in the meringue.
What is pistachio spread?
I thought I followed this recipe to a T… But my cookies turned out incredibly flat.
Can coconut cream be substituted for heavy cream in this recipe?
The video says to bake at 300, but the written recipe says 275 — which is it?

I wish the exact colors she used were given.  I'm ordering a kit of the brand she used, but there are so many colors available and they are expensive when you don't often need them.  It's not easy to make a correction once the color has been added, so I'd have preferred more guidance.  Anyone have thoughts??
I totally agree. I really, really mucked up the macarons when I put in too much dye. I remade them a second time today and opted to not use any dye and fortunately they are (so far) doing fine!
Does anyone know if we can freeze these? I love the idea of having a variety of flavors, but I don't think I would have the fortitude to make them all on the same day.
You can! The shells freeze really well.
Recommend the sweetened pistachio spread vs the un sweetened.

Divine! Great instructions (video really helps). My piping skills left much to be desired, but my macarons had feet and great texture. I found the filling a bit too sweet, so I added the finely grated zest of a lime and another pinch of salt -- perfect! You don't taste the lime so much as sense some freshness. Now I'll tackle the raspberry ones .  . .

I made these and they turned out well. I skipped the food coloring because the pistachio flour added a touch of pastel green to the batter. I baked them at 275F at first, but since the feet were slow to form in the first 4 minutes, I increased the heat to 300F, which helped get a rise but I couldn't stop the macarons from browning slightly. 

The dry ingredients call got 147g of sugar which is a LOT, so the shells are super sweet, so it's good that the filing doesn't call for any sugar.

Also, I couldn't find pistachio spread at my local supermarket, so I made my own by blanching and removing the pistachio skin, and then blending it to a paste. The texture it creates in the filing is fantastic!

This did not go well.  After 2 hours of resting - no skin - so cracked, hollow and no feet.  We had success with a different recipe with twice as much almond flour and half as much confectioner's sugar. Had to make my own pistachio spread which is easy but I refrigerated prior to using in recipe so filling broke. Flavors were great but structure a fail.

agreed with those who said this temp is too low--i had to bake around 30mins at least, and for my final batch i increased temp to 280. maybe would try 290 or 300 next time as mine were quite chewy and threatened to stick to the parchment (though i actually really like them this way). filling was also a bit thin and spread a bit when bit into, but people LOVED it--they said they normally don't like macarons bc they find them too dry. cant wait to try these again and improve my technique!

I tried several other recipes without success.  I followed this recipe to the letter and the macarons came out perfect.  I did not use food coloring, and like the texture and color without.  I used a purchased pistachio cream for the filling, which worked great.  Next time I might make pistachio butter and see how that works for the filling.  If I were to add food coloring, I would use a small amount of avocado.

The macarons Claire made in the video looked so good, and I wanted to try the recipe myself. Unfortunately, it didn’t work out so well for me. Idk if the recipe is wrong or could be an error on my part. Some cracked, some have feet, others didn’t, and it was incredibly sweet because of the 147 grams of confectioner sugar. I’ve tried another recipe and those came out great.

Hi Claire

I just want shout out to your amazingly clear instructions on this recipe. I made the pistacio macarons for our Christmas day dessert and it turned out really well. So thank you so much for sharing your expertise! Happy Holidays!
Can you recommend a recipe for high altitude macarons? Thank you.

I made this and I used the exact measurements. I found that how long you bake them is very important and in the video she mentions not taking them out of the oven until you can wiggle the shells and not feel them move. This is important, because the ones I slightly overbaked did turn out a lot better. The ones that were slightly underbaked wouldnt even come off the baking sheet. So definitely check the shells before taking them out

My shells came out perfect… the second time (practice makes perfect).  For the filling, I used a very expensive Italian pistachio cream bought from an Italian market and sadly, as beautiful as they looked after being assembled, the filling (unless refrigerated) turns into a gooey mess.  A delicious gooey mess. The fulling never set at room temp.  I will experiment with different filling recipes next time. Also, my successful attempt at the shells was when I made the pistachio flour separately in a small cuisinart and sifted all dry ingredients separately and then whisked them together and sifted them all together before the macaronage stage.   Cooked on silicone baking sheets on inverted cookie trays at 300 degrees for 18 minutes.

Former professional baker here. The cast iron pan steaming method does not work in domestic gas ovens. Domestic gas ovens are designed to circulate and vent the air inside, which they must do to safely burn gas. They'll efficiently vent the steam you create in the pan before it has a chance to raise the humidity in the oven. It does work in electric ovens, which are usually sealed. To do this in a gas oven, you need a vessel like the Challenger Bread Pan or the Emile Henry baguette baker.

@Jonathan I’m sure those bread pans work great, but I personally like to avoid buying pricey, limited-use kitchen gadgets. I’ve made these baguettes in a gas oven following Claire’s instructions and they’ve always turned out great. So for those with gas ovens who can’t afford (or who are disinclined to buy) bread pans, no worries! Your baguettes will be amazing.

@David like an idiot I bought one, found it on sale 40% off but still $100. I couldn’t even figure out how to transfer the dough into it gracefully… 

I placed 1 baguette in the pan and 2 on top of my cast iron griddle (as I don’t have a baking stone). Baguette stuck to the lid as it expanded while baking and would not cook through. $100 and a good baguette wasted. The 2 on my $50 cast iron griddle looks perfect.
@Jonathan. Inverted foil roasting pans trap moisture, as per ATM suggests.

Too complicated! 500g any flour, 1t yeast, ~380ml warm water, handful of starter.Mix until moistened. Rest 15 m. Sprinkle 1t salt. *Pull dough over from edge to center, all the way around, 15-20 pulls. Let rest 30 min, then pull again.* Repeat from * 3-4x. [Optional: Can refrigerate now.] When ready to bake: Rest 1hr.Shape into rough baguettes on floured surface, don’t overwork. Let rest 15 min, slash. Bake 20 min at 400°, tossing in 1/2c water on oven floor at 0 and 10 m. Interior 205°=done.
@Ann but really, really good! Worth the complicated directions
@Ann. I think some, of all skill levels, can always find a little something to learn from any recipe.  I enjoy species, myself.
I made a peel from the side of a cardboard box. It works just fine and costs nothing.
Emile Henry makes a ceramic baguette baker that is worth every penny if you plan on making baguettes with any frequency.
I have a metal tripartite baguette baker. Hoping this will work well, too.
I just gave mine away. Kind of useless and the baguettes are tiny...easier to bake as in this recipe.
There’s a video she made with the NYT explaining rationale for unit measurements and providing more tips, explainers, and ratios! https://youtu.be/bxXZVNc4SVw
And 10 grams of fine sea salt is going to measure 1 1/2 teaspoons (1/2 tablespoon).
No need to dissolve yeast in water. Instant dry yeast can be added to the dry ingredients. (Maybe second time is a charm with this comment.)

Three recommendations I have for this technique. 1. Just add the yeast to the dry flour and skip mixing it with the water. 2. Both the transfer peel and oven peel can be cut from corrugated cardboard. Should be without bends. Excellent peels for 1 to 4 uses. 3. Rice flour is far better than semolina and is magic on couche. But be careful not to use when shaping. Wait, one more. 4. Unglazed tiles, such as 6" "quarry" tile make a great oven hearth.

Before you add the boiling water to the heated cast iron pan I would HIGHLY RECOMMEND covering the window on the oven door with a double layer of kitchen towel to protect it from the drops of water that invariably bounce out of the pan when the water is added. The flour is key to the authentic taste of a baguette.  I have been making baguettes for several months using Francine (French) T55 flour.  Almost as good as the best baguette I ate in Paris.  I don't use the Poolish method.  I mix my full flour, instant yeast, water and salt in a bowl and perform 3-4 stretch and folds with 30 minute rests.  Then I ferment the dough in the fridge for at least 12 hours.  The following day it's divide, shape rest and then roll rest in a couche and then bake.

Notes on high altitude, dry climate…more water helps with airier loaves, usually around 3-4 T more water, proof times are shorter, sometimes as much as 30 minutes shorter… do the press test earlier than you think. I’m at 6900 ft.

Saltiness refers to the amount of salt per volume. The more “salty” salts have more salt in the same volume. When you use weight as your measure (as in 10 grams) it does not matter how salty the salt is. You are measuring the same amount of salt, regardless of the volume and therefore regardless of the degree of saltiness of the product

Sea salt & kosher salt have VERY different levels of "saltiness" so having them both at 10 grams is probably an error, especially as it's "fine sea salt" as opposed to flaky sea salt (which is closer to kosher salt). Morton salt says 1 tbsp of fine sea salt is equivalent to 1 tbsp   3/4 tsp of kosher salt.  I'm sure weight equivalents are available somewhere online...
Salt by weight is the same "saltiness" regardless of format. 10 grams of sea salt or 10 grams of kosher salt will work. (If you measure by volume, then you get much more saltiness from finer salt.)

Constructive critique:

The recipe could be greatly improved as follows:

- triple the poolish (i.e. 180g each of flour and water)
- use far less yeast in the poolish (a tiny pinch) and ferment it longer (16-24h at 75F)

The main point of a poolish is to improve the flavor of the final product. The recipe's 6-7h ferment does not allow enough time to develop significant flavor.

These changes imply other mods to the recipe (e.g. omitting the refrigeration step).

I have tried this recipe three different times. The bread is wonderfully delicious, but at least one of the loaves looks like a snake that has eaten a  rat with a bulge (or two) along the loaf. What step am I not following accurately ?

This is a wonderful recipe and directions! It’s no more work than a good sourdough. I put about 25% whole grain flour in mine, which enhances the flavor, imo. I bought a yard of linen, & have pizza stones in my oven, and my DH made an oak transfer peel. For steam, I’ve dedicated a heavy 9x13 pan to the bottom of my oven with lava rocks in it-preheat the oven & (carefully!) throw cool water on them. I don’t like to use my good cast iron for that.

Paris-Brest-Paris as it is called, is a ride(or time race if you prefer) and is non-stop 1200 kilometers, 762 miles of grueling bike riding spanning nights and days. A rider has 90 hours, or less, to complete it to win a medal and be dubbed an "ancien". Think Le Mans' 24 hours of racing cars is tough? Forget it. PBP is it!
This dessert prep reminds me of that ride. Long, involved with bad weather and easy to get lost along the way. None the less, I think I will give it a try this weekend!

Easier way to skin hazelnuts, set up a waterbath, bring 2 cups water to boil, add 3 TBSP baking soda, then hazelnuts.  Boil for 3 minutes, remove one, drop in water bath, If the skin pops off easily they're done, drain them and put them in the water bath.  If the skin doesn't come off easily give them another minute, preheat oven to 350 degrees.  When the skins are removed, dry them on a dishtowel.  Transfer to a sheet pan and bake for 15 minutes shake 1/2 way through. takes off all the skin.

I had this many years ago in St. Louis at the extraordinary restaurant "Tony's".  Their version added profiteroles around the top covered with a sugar glaze which added a display of pure decadence.  For all the effort that goes into this desert, added decadence would not be out of place and would not add all that much more effort.
Thanks for this Claire! In the video, for the mousseline, you use 2 sticks of butter. In the recipe, you call for 1/2 cup (1 stick). Please advise which one is correct. Thanks. Richard

Echoing Richard's question. Recipe says to whip one stick of butter with the praline paste and pastry cream but Claire puts two in the mixer in the video. NYT/Claire, can you please clarify whether it should be one or two sticks when preparing the mousseline?

Thank you for noticing the discrepancy between the video and the printed recipe. I was getting ready to make the mousseline and went into a panic. I consulted several recipes. Some had a full cup and some had 2/3 cup. I decided to go with 1/2 cup as that is all I had at room temperature. The mousseline came out very well. I think more butter would have been to much.

Woke up early this am. Multi step project, perfect. Praline before work, great. Spent 30 minutes trying to rub off hazelnut skin. Several flew out onto the floor. Threw in the towel, blanched a fresh cup and just now toasting. Taking the skin on, toasted ones to work for snacking. Will complete the praline after work. I will not be deterred!
Wow, I respect your determination! Bravo! I'd rather do the bike ride; sounds much easier.

So, I've tried to make this three times now, with the pate a choux being the failing point. Each time, the pate a choux comes out spread out and burnt, rather than puffed and golden. I'm trying to troubleshoot as I give it another go, paying attention to the visual indicators, weighing every ingredient, drying out the dough etc. Is it possible the oven temps/times are wrong, or is there something else that I could be overlooking?
I also found the pate a choux came out too dark. I think 425 for 15 minutes is too high/too long but not sure how to modify.
I had the same problem, carefully weighing ingredients and following the directions to the letter. Please advise.

could be too many eggs. my tried and tested recipe is 1 cup water, 1 stick butter, 1 cup flour, 4 large eggs. 450 is a fine starting temperature, I would drop it down to 375 once the pastry gets golden and cook the rest of the time on 375. poke holes in the cooked pastry once you take it out and put back for 10 minutes to finish drying (this allows steam to escape without collapsing the pastry)

This is a rubbish recipe.  Weights and measures are *all* over the place.  In one place 6 tablespoons is 90 ml (it is), and in another place 6 tablespoons of water is 76 ml (sorry, no). But the crowning glory is in the assembly step.  The list of ingredients say 1/2 cup /113 g of butter (fine)...and the instructions say 1 cup/225 grams of butter.
don't make. Or at least find another recipe that someone, somewhere, may have edited for consistency.
Just a note: bags of skinless, roasted, hazelnuts at Costco.
Also at Amazon. Three pounds, $27. Easy peasy.

I haven't made this recipe, but have made Claire's choux from her book. For those having trouble with the dough: use temperatures instead of the visual indicators for when to proceed! The dough should register 175ºF (79.4ºC) before being removed from heat and be cooled to below 145ºF (62.8ºC) before the eggs are added (to prevent curdling). It's also possible for the choux to collapse if not given proper time to dry in the open oven or if steam is not allowed to escape (poke those holes!)
Made the praline paste one day, pastry cream the next, then the rest on serving day. Very do-able! Followed the instructions and everything came out perfectly!
Definitely cook the choux pastry until pretty thick. Piping should focus on creating height not width in the ring. Watch the bake time on the pastry. Really delicious.

And of course, if all this seems like too much effort, you can always fill the Paris-Brest full of thickly whipped cream, flavoured with your favourite liqueur, and then replace the top and drizzle with melted chocolate with a tad of yummy butter to help it spread/set!  The pastry is very easy to make and bake, the rest of it is a work of art conjured up from your imagination.

I just made this today and I am no pastry chef, and it still turned out wonderfully! Not one problem throughout, but I followed her video instructions closely- not so much the written recipe. Also the cook time for the pastry was 35 mins before drying out for an hour in the oven.

I signed up with NYTimes cooking specifically for the recipes. I watch a lot of Claire and I've always got.to write the recipes down. So I signed up. However, in this recipe alone, there are at least 2 (perhaps 3) errors between what she's doing and the recipe. For instance, the pate a choux, she calls for milk and water. Your recipe only mentions milk, no water. Same for making the praline. Water absent in the ingredients list (though it is mentioned in the method). It's very frustrating.
Can peanut butter be substituted for the almond butter? I hate to buy a whole jar for a quarter cup.
I think it would really change the flavor in a negative direction. I suggest buying the almond butter and enjoying it on toast and in smoothies. Almond butter is delicious.
I wouldn’t use peanut butter. Almond butter is delicious, nutritious, and keeps well. A good investment.

You can put the rest of the almond butter in the freezer for another time that you find yourself facing a recipe that calls for it. You will extend its shelf life and not feel that you have purchased something that will only become wasted.
Can this recipe be doubled? Might as well make two with the effort involved.
An interesting journey and I learned a lot. I especially like the video that gives great advice. But, with this amount of work, I had hoped for something more spectacular. Won’t make it again.

Fait accompli! Did it in stages over the last three days. Came out beautifully. These Claire projects are like meditation for me and enjoy the challenge. Keeping the pastry cream and pate recipes for future use. I discovered that I can live without hazelnuts. C’est la vie!
Like others, my Choux did not rise. I tried the recipe multiple times with the same results.

Thanks Vicki for the note on getting the skins off the nuts. Mine were a dogfight. Everything else went great except for the pastry. No rise at all on 2 attempts. Made the second one smaller and drier. It failed but the 3 eclairs I made with the excess were perfect. Interesting. Was it the aluminum pizza pan I had to use in my tiny oven? No worries. I took the slightly nicer of the two flat tires and made a giant, ring-shaped hazelnut praline buttercream sandwich. Husband and friend devoured it.

This is indeed a "show-stopper."  If making again, I would make the following adjustments:

1.  In Step #3, be careful not to burn your sugar.  
2.  In Step #4, do NOT include all of the candied sugar when you begin to make the praline paste.  Use ONLY the candied hazelnuts.  Using all the candied sugar makes the whole thing ultimately too sweet.  It also adds grittiness -- like you're chewing very fine glass.
3.  A smaller choux ring is better.  Also, the bake time is shorter than given.

I'd give this recipe a 4 out of 5. Total time was about 9 hours, including the chilling time. I followed the instructions exactly, but I had the same issue that other commentors had in that the pate a choux only rose a bit. So, instead of a wheel shape, I cut it in half and made a rainbow. It was delicious and reminiscent of peanut butter pie. (Lol, despite having no peanuts) A project for sure and used more bowls than I thought necessary, but I shall dream of the pastry cream filling.
In the recipe, it asks 1 stick of butter for assembly. Yet in the detailed steps, 2 sticks of butter is asked. In the video Claire did use 2 sticks…
I just couldn't bring myself to add two whole sticks of butter to the pastry cream. So I added only one stick. The resulting desert was a major success at a dinner party. I will be making this again.

Recipe calks for 1/2 C butter for mousseline but written instructions say 1 cup and video shows 2 sticks….   frustrating b/c I followed written and only brought 1/2 C to room temp!   also video she says 10 mins at 425 but instructions say 15 mins… i did 15 b/c i was following instructions… saw later she said 10…. feeling like 15 maybe got it too brown….  some better editing eyes might be good!  Surprised to not see corrections anywhere.

I have had excessive cracking with making pate a choux at these varied temperatures. I baked mine at 375 for 40 minutes and it was perfect. I also spray the dough before baking with water instead of brushing with egg. I do think the mousseline could have used a touch more salt. A chocolate sauce or creme sauce would have brought even more decadence.
I used cupito gf bread flour as a 1:1 substitution for the wheat flour and it turned out perfectly as a gf dessert.

I made this last new years after falling in love with Paris Brest on my last visit to France. It turned out exceptional, it was devoured by all my friends, I make a double recipe of the praline because that paste in the Choux pastry is my favorite part. Chefs kiss

This was delicious when I made a few modifications: 
First, I had a hard time getting the hazelnuts to blend into a paste (probably b/c of my cheap food processor). I ended up blending with 2-3 tablespoons of canola oil to get a smoother paste. In the end, consistency didn't matter too much b/c it blended into buttercream nicely. 
Second, I followed Eric T's suggestion of using the Serious Eat's pâte à choux. The rise was beautiful!
Did anyone try this with just almonds? I randomly have an allergy specifically just hazelnuts…
I haven't however almonds would likely be fine to sub for hazelnuts. And when I hear "praline" I think pecans, those might work also.

A family member is not too fond of hazelnuts.  Could pistachios or another nut be used? This will be a Birthday treat for same family member.  Any suggestion would be most welcome. 
 TY so much
If your friend dislikes hazelnuts, use almonds. They have the same texture and work well with the brittle idea of caramelized sugar. It will be different, but still quite similar.

Do not scramble the eggs. Add them one at a time until incorporated and the dough is satiny. 
You don't have to do this by hand. Dump the hot dough into a stand or hand mixer, let cool a bit and beat in the eggs. Much easier.

I really want to make this, but the praline paste from scratch is very time consuming and praline paste is only available online with out of sight shipping.  Any suggestions that are less expensive or time consuming?
I hate to shill for Amazon, but they have several vendors selling blanched hazelnuts in the neighborhood of $11 - $15 per pound. Buying them already blanched takes a lot of work out of it.
Can the mousseline filling be mixed in advance and stored in he refrigerator?
I did, but only for about an hour. My concern is that it get too cold and hard to pipe. So I re-beat it for a couple of minutes before I loaded it in the piping bag. Your mileage may vary.
If you use premade praline paste, how much do you use?
For the almond butter should it be salted or unsalted? I bought raw unsalted almond butter and am now second guessing it.
This is actually quite easy to make! Such a treat for special occasions

I made the choux twice and both times the egg addition was way too much. I used three eggs the first time and two the second. Claire needs to review the recipe as written in the NYTimes. Maybe more flour or just one egg?

After two attempts at this Choux, I gave up and tried Serious Eats’ version, which came out perfect with no problems. I don’t think there is enough flour in Claire’s version. Everything else was clear and the crème mousseline came out amazing.
My choux turned out perfectly.  Didn't "rise" much when baked but expanded laterally a lot.
It would be nice to have directions for cooking the sugar that involve a thermometer. You're going to burn the caramelized hazelnuts ever time if you follow these directions.

Beyond about 310 F the magical transformation begins, and the compounds that made up melted sugar become wholly different compounds with a new range of tastes, smells, and colors. Once that process begins, do not take your eyes off of it for a second. Your eyes and your nose will tell you everything you need to know. Oh, and if you burn your caramel, don't put the hazelnuts in it.
Mine went from amber to burnt at 240 so I didn't put in the hazel nuts. The next time I just used my eyes and nose and it worked perfectly.

Wow. I made my first Paris-Brest today, from this recipe and my friends loved it. For me, the revelation was making this ethereal and slightly complicated pastry, using a series of techniques that have become familiar to me over a lifetime of baking, and combining them in a new way. I really appreciate Claire's clear and efficient directions - and her great video - which kept me nicely on track.  The only embellishment I made was a squiggle of ganache all over the top.

Came out beautifully and was absolutely delicious! The video tutorial was very helpful to have a visual of the various stages and techniques. My praline spread wasn’t perfectly smooth and the end result was still very silky, decadent mousseline.

Made this for New Year's Eve and it was a big hit. I would make two modifications next time.  First, I'd just spread the mousseline rather than piping it, since most of the piping handiwork can't be seen once you put the top on. Second, I would not spread the extra hazelnut praline on the bottom during assembly, but just fill with the mousseline and keep the excess praline for snacking. It's absolutely delicious, but made the dessert too...unctuous. Was a fun, challenging and delicious dessert!

Made this yesterday and it turned out pretty well.  Tastes delicious!  Got awfully brown baking so I cut the time short a bit.  The only thing that didn't turn out well was that, once baked, the bottom part had holes/tears in it so couldn't put the filling in evenly on all parts of the base.  Don't know what caused that.  My inclination next time would be to either cut short a bit the higher heat baking and maybe the lower one too or cut the temperature of one or both a little.  Thoughts Claire?
If you watch Claire's video, she also has a rather large hole that she patches with a piece of interior choux that she tears from another area. A smart repair that I will use in the future.

I made it, and it worked! I found Claire's video accompaniment to the recipe to be enormously helpful; she gives a lot of little tips that the recipe doesn't go into. Very happy to have made/conquered it, but I have to say... it was a little too rich for me. Maybe the mousseline needed to be whipped more, or too much praline paste, but it needed something lighter in it. Thinking of deconstructing a bit and using the pastry cream with less praline and using the praline as an accent.

Chaotic, looooong, re-reading and re-reading the directions, panicking that the eggs were scrambling, that the pastry cream was breaking, and where did the right size pipping tip go?   And then! Ahhh! beautiful and delicious and any left over, no matter how small, is great with coffee, or in stuffed french toast! Happy New Year!
Fantastic recipe! It took some patience, but was doable! A perfect showstopper dessert for New Year’s Eve.
These were the most specific instructions I've yet seen for making pastry cream, always a stressful undertaking for this amateur! It turned out beautifully, thank you!
This took way longer than 3 hours. Had to try this once. I am now five hours in and still waiting for the rings to cool. Off to make the mousseline.

Spent most of the day making it, and we had multiple issues. First- the caramel got burnt and we had to redo. Then the prailine paste became too hard and only got harder with time. We tried hard to fix it but to no avail. Eventually had to ditch it and go for store bought hazelnut paste. Then the choux didn't rise as much as we expected. Overall the dessert was fine but totally not worth the effort it took us.

Proud of my effort—first choux ever attempted! But I made one fatal mistake.. I processed the nuts (subbed macadamia) with salted caramel leftover from another recipe. Tragically the macadamia nuts were also salted (didn’t read ingredients) and so was the almond butter. Without thinking I added salted butter to the mousseline. It was almost unbearably salty. Dumb mistake when every other element was delicious. Still edible. Live and learn. Love Claire Saffitz! A fun baking adventure nonetheless.

I made this yesterday (in 7 hrs) and while it took way longer than I thought it came out perfect and was delicious. I halved the whole recipe and pipped 3 circles instead of one large one. I probably should have made 5-6 smaller ones because they puffed to the size of large bagels. 

While it was excellent in the future though, I'll make the pastry but fill it with something else - this was almost too rich. Maybe just vanilla whipped cream, whipped sweetened mascarpone with crushed hazelnuts

Just could not make this recipe work - despite the incredible precise instructions. The praline mousseline was too runny for piping and the pate de choux didn’t turn out as it should. 
I waisted a lot of work for a mediocre result - will try another Paris-Brest recipe

Recipe was detailed, and easy to follow. Just delicious! We loved it. However, my pate a choux spread out and did not puff up, as another reader described. I noticed in the video you add water to the choux, yet the recipe does not mention it. Could that be the issue? After beating the dough, it fell in a V shape and seemed the right consistency. I'd like to try this again. Thanks!

This is an incredible dessert and fun as an endurance bake! I, however, forgot to change the salt measurement for using Morton’s kosher and not Diamond Crystal and it came out almost inedibly salty. Learn from my mistake, future bakers!
Everything went perfectly as planned until the puffed choux pastry deflated while cooling in the oven. Perplexed, but it was still delicious. I’ll try again.

I too had issues with the choux.  I think the recipe proportions must be off. My first attempt was a hard burnt cracker. Second was a little better with less egg and shorter baking time but still not enough of a rise. If you make this, I'd use a different choux recipe. For some reason, even after really blending it, my hazelnut praline had little sticky bits of caramel but they disappeared when mixed into the mousseline.  All in all - this is a delicious project but still needs a few tweaks.
https://www.nytimes.com/2022/12/27/dining/paris-brest-recipe-french-pastry.html
This choux collapsed on me - might be worth reviewing some other choux pastry recipes to see what’s different…

Time saving tip — after making the pastry cream, scrape into a ziploc bag, seal, and place in a bowl of ice water. From time to time squish that pastry cream around in the bag. Because heat travels so much more quickly in water, your pastry cream will be cool in minutes instead of hours.
The recipe ingredients call for 1/2 cup butter for the mousseline but the instructions say 1 cup of butter.   Which is correct?
I just watched the video, and she is using 2 sticks of butter for the mousseline.

My praline paste never became a smooth fluid, but rather a sticky clump even after two tries - processing 5-7 minutes - any suggestions? My dear friend and I are earnestly attempting to perfect this fabulous pastry.
I made this twice and the PÂTE À CHOUX did not puff as shown. I have made pate a choux millions of times but not in this shape. I think I will it try one more time with another recipe.
I switched the praline paste for pumpkin purée and the Paris-Breast tasted so light and fluffy

I made a 9 inch circle and it spread laterally rather than puffing up but the mousseline is so delicious that it didn't matter. I might bake it on a cookie sheet rather than an upside down sheet pan because it spread so much. 15 minutes at 425 and 35 at 350 was plenty.
I am very excited to make this for my spouse’s birthday. I want to do a test run since I’ve never baked choux (only fried). Has anyone tried halving the recipe? Thanks in advance!
Has anyone made this with hazelnut butter instead of almond?
Has anyone tried making the Pate a Choux using the food processor - one egg at a time? I've done this for cream puffs and it worked beautifully.

I followed the PÂTE À CHOUX prep and baking instructions exactly, and it burnt to a crisp. I hope that someone can reexamine this recipe, as the final results look great. I would love to be able to make this!

I made this for New Year's Eve in 2022.   It was INDEED a showstopper and completely and utterly DELICIOUS.   Claire's video and step-by-step instruction were so incredibly helpful.
I am going to attempt it for Christmas dinner this year!  Per many comments, I am going to start a few days ahead and then pipe and bake the pate a choux on Christmas day.   I might be crazy....
It felt very rich and dense… is it 1/2 cup of butter or a whole cup for the mousseline?
It felt very rich and dense… is it 1/2 cup of butter or a whole cup for the mousseline?

Everything was going swimmingly until I added just a tad too much egg to the choux. Not knowing what to do since adding raw flour seemed like a major no no, I tried to bake it anyway. Alas…. 

Saved the delightful praline mix and mousseline by baking puff pastry and putting in the middle. Still a good pastry that lessened the pain of the choux fail. Next time I’ll get it!

The praline mousseline was very tasty, but the recipe made way too much. The dessert was very good if you got a bite with the pate a choux and praline paste, but otherwise the filling was too rich and sweet to eat it all.
The pastry cream tasted very savoury rather than sweet. Reminded me of dark chocolate or espresso without sugar. Maybe something went wrong in execution but did not enjoy.
Has anyone scaled this down? I’d like to make it for a friend’s birthday, but this would be huge for 4 people!
I'd suggest larger-sized profiteroles instead! They're awfully cute and can be scaled to any number.
Instructions and video say 1 cup butter with the praline to make the mousseline. I think the instructions are correct for the amount of pastry cream.

Made this dairy free using soy milk (Bonsoy) and vegan butter (Naturli block) and the filling turned out wonderfully. I had trouble with the choux ring- it collapsed and spread outward after I reduced the heat in the oven- but that could be due to my oven. Still tasted delish but didn’t look as nice as Claire’s ring. Will definitely be remaking the creme mousseline filling but might have to troubleshoot the pate a choux.

This dessert was extremely fun to make, and I felt the instructions were mostly very clear. It took me 6 hours. One part I was a bit unsure about was the consistency of the dough after cooking it. The pastry didn't puff up the way I expected to and then flattened during the hour it was cooling inside the oven. The two bottom rings separated while baking and therefore didn't make a solid base, which I think was the issue. Joining them with water or making them thicker than the top ring may help.

I have made this twice. Both times it was a big hit at a dinner party. Unfortunately the recipe and the video do not agree on some issues. With respect to the butter added to the mousseline, I have stuck with adding only 1/2 cup (as per the ingredient list, but not the instructions nor the video both of which say 1 whole cup). The result was plenty rich enough. Remember, there is already 6 tablespoons of butter in the pastry cream, fat in the milk, and oil in the praline paste.

Definitely one of the harder and more time consuming baked goods I've tried. I've failed my choux twice because it came out really flat. I figured out that it means either the dough is too wet or under cooked. I used slightly less water and more flour, and cooked the dough in the pan longer. It worked very well and the choux came out beautiful. As hard as this recipe is, the outcome really is a show stopper. I am glad I have this under my belt. Thanks!
Choux is not hard and as a twelve year old I did all by myself with no problems and the end product was impeccable. Wonderful recipe by the way.

I have made this recipe 4 times now and have made some small modifications.  I never use all 4 eggs in the patê chou as it makes it too runny and does not get enough life.  Typically 3 - 3.5 is all I need.  I also use a star tip to pipe it so I done have to run a fork across the entire surface.  The dough should be stiff enough to easily hold the ridges of the star tip.  I use untoasted nuts to top the ring since they will get too dark if I use toasted.  I only bake for 10 min at 425 to avoid it getting too dark too quickly (this is what she does in her video.  Also, I had issues with my mousseline developing hardened pieces of praline when I whipped the praline with butter first.  Instead, I whisk together the pastry cream and praline paste first then add it slowly to the whipped room temperature butter.  
Also, Claire is the reason I bake at all!  Thank you for teaching me so much.
@Hamish Water is almost never listed in an ingredient list, that is not an "error", it's convention.

The recipe has problems as written. the choux was a flat disaster. Luckily, the comments came to the rescue. I used the Serious Eats choux recipe and saved Easter dessert! The other components are fabulous.

stats.Total Time: 1 hour 15 minutes, plus several hours’ cooling

comments.commentBody: The instructions are mostly great, and I can tell that the flavor will be fabulous. A few notes: 1/3 cup of water is 71 grams, not 142; 1/2 tbsp extract in place of vanilla bean is financially less risky; and I’d recommend straining infused cream into measuring cup before even making caramel. I had to do this anyway, as my cream, set over lowest heat, bubbled over when I went to re-watch video (thanks 142!); I made up the difference with crème fraîche, as I was out of cream and feeling plucky.

My grandmother’s caramel recipe, that has been my staple at Christmas for years, calls for boiling the caramel to 245 degrees for firm candy. Since 250 degrees resulted in a toffee-like consistency for some folks, consider shooting for a lower temperature.

I cooked this to 255, Claire’s preferred temperature per the video. After cooling Icouldn’t cut them they were so hard. Otherwise they are very tasty, but terrible on teeth and dental work. I’m going to try again today cooking to firm ball instead of hard ball and used my instant read thermometer to double check my candy thermometer. I used vanilla paste instead of a bean because it’s cheaper and what I had. Simmering the cream, coffee, and vanilla paste was worth the hassle, it smelled so good!

I gave this a go last night, and ended up w/some lovely toffee, rather than a caramel.  I have made candy since I was a girl in the 50's.  Both my candy thermometers are off, so I will blame that, and try again tonight.  I tried to re-calibrate to no avail...Loved the coffee infusion, but may go a bit less as a bit strong for my taste and add more vanilla, which I adore.  And, yes that grams weight of the water was off, please advise!

Hard caramels? Altitude matters! It’s a crucial consideration in any candy making, one I learned the hard way. Water boils at 212 (Fahrenheit) at sea level, and that number drops by about 1 degree for every 500 feet above sea level. That means you need to lower the target temperature according to your elevation. (I wish we knew the altitude where this recipe was tested.) I’m at about 5,300 feet, where water boils at about 202F, and I lower everything by 10 degrees. It has always worked.

I've never made candy or caramel, but I was really inspired by the detail in Claire's video. I made these exactly as described following along with the video, with one exception, I infused the cream with 4 tbsp of loose leaf earl grey tea as I didn't think my children would like the coffee flavor. They came out *perfectly* and the only problem is that I can't stop eating them. Super smooth and chewy but still quite soft and melt-in-mouth. Amazing texture.

To avoid buying whole coffee beans for just this recipe and vanilla bean I used 11g of ground coffee and about a tsp of vanilla extract.

I used a coffee filter to drain out the grounds after it simmered but before starting the caramel. After straining the grounds out I put it back on low heat to maintain the temperature. The coffee flavor still comes through nicely. Also overdid the temp on the caramel and it was rock hard. A few bursts in the microwave got it back to a lovely texture.

Though I always keep whole beans for making coffee daily, I wondered about that myself: if you're supposed to smash them up anyway, why not a) just use ground coffee, and a coffee filter for straining, or b) pulse them briefly in your coffee grinder (which you certainly have if you buy whole beans regularly)? Either is less messy and wasteful than crushing them in a bag with a rolling pin. Thanks for confirming that I was right.
Confirming that these are luscious little darlings, oh!

I made these with ground coffee (about 1/3 of the called for amount) since I didn’t have any whole beans. I had to strain it through a coffee filter and even that required two goes. I finally gave up and added extra cream to what I had strained and then just went on. Anyway, I would do what she says for ease of preparation. They look lovely and the bits I scraped out of the pan were very tasty. Hopefully cutting and wrapping will go smoothly.
Where is the video link? I’ve looked multiple times and can’t find it!

Below the photo there is a link: 

"Featured in: 3 Fun, Festive Candy Recipes That Are Worth the Effort"
If caramel is too hard when it sets, melt it again and add more cream.
Most folks who live at altitude already know this but I learned the hard way when I moved to Denver from sea level. Reduce your targeted cooking temp 2 degrees for every 1000 feet over sea level.

Made many times and yet to have a failure!

 I’ve used the metric water amount (142 grams) and the imperial measurements, both to relatively the same level of success. More water slows down the caramelization which makes the watching and waiting a little longer but ensures the sugar dissolves entirely, making a lot silky smooth caramel. On my stove, the higher water content takes around 20-25 minutes to get to amber depending on how aggressive I am with the heat. Less water is faster but runs the risk of a grittier caramel. Recommend heating to a lower temperature initially to ensure all the sugar dissolves before starting to caramelize. More water: slower, easier, more likely to turn out. Less water: faster, harder, more likely to be gritty. 

I don’t use a sauce pan for the sugar. I use a high-walled, heavy bottom fry pan with a 10.5 inch diameter. 

I also take the cream off heat and just leave it on the stove top. The lowest setting on my stove still causes the cream to simmer. I usually start the milk later. 

I heat to 245-ish using a thermoworks dot thermometer rather than old school candy thermometer after adding butter and milk. I’ve used the old school style of candy thermometer that clips to the side. For me and the pan I use, it’s too far from the heat center to be accurate. 

I also like a softer caramel and find going to 250 is just on the edge of too firm. 

If skipping the coffee, add more vanilla for more flavor. Get the good flaky salt and be generous.
Giant hit with the family! This has been a request since I first made them a few years ago.

Pretty sure I burnt the batch my first go around. Bubbling subsiding and deep amber color was hard to discern and the timing suggested was way off. Alas I’ll have to try again. Excited to see Claire at NYT Cooking!
Second batch was successful! If you watch the video in the article link above (bottom of description), she takes it off the heat at a medium amber. For me, this meant the first hint of amber color.
NO CORN SYRUP? What should I use instead please?
Corn syrup is an essential ingredient. It's one of those "if I don't have it, I can't make it" ingredients. Sorry.
Lyle’s golden syrup works for me. All you need is an “invert sugar.”

Cooked these to 253 and they were perfectly chewy! The candy making process was pretty simple if you have experience making caramel from scratch.  However individually rolling them took forever, I think I cut mine smaller I had about 50 candies in an 8x8 pan.  If you can get ahold of candy foils I would recommend it! Everyone is raving though they are a great adult style candy, the bitterness from the coffee is a nice addition.
Cooking to 250 degrees was perfect- soft and chewy but still good structure!

This was my first go at making caramels, and found the instructions easy to follow. I used the volume measurements without problems. I infused both vanilla bean and pod and crushed Seattle Strong coffee beans in the cream. The flavor is fabulous! Now to learn how to cut the squares evenly next time to make them pretty...and do a better job wrapping the pieces. This recipe is a keeper!

For making sea turtles: 
Increase salt
Cook first to lighter color than dark amber 
Second cooking heat to 255
Has anyone tried to make these further ahead than 1 week? Do they freeze well for longer?
These were UNREAL good and turned out so well. Don’t skip the coffee infusion - the ratio is just enough that it’s a suggestion of coffee while the caramel and salt still take center stage.

These are positively sinful and turned out brilliantly. I added some cocoa to the cream mixture with extra vanilla bean paste in place of a bean. Cut the cooled caramels into squares as the rectangle suggestions seemed too big.

I thought this recipe turned out great!  I made the recipe exactly as written EXCEPT I did not use the coffee and I did add toasted pecans, because yum.  I had to account for our altitude difference (almost 3000 ft here) but the caramels were perfect.  Chewy and soft at room temp but didn't lose their shape when you held them.  Another time I would cut smaller pieces, and dip them in tempered chocolate... *swoon*.

Spicy Margarita Caramels
For the full recipe (8x8 pan)
- no coffee or vanilla
- replace 4tsp of the cream with 2tsp each fresh lime juice and juice from pickled jalapenos
- once final mixture reaches 250-255F, remove from heat and quickly stir in 2tbsp mezcal or tequila mixed with the zest of 2 limes and ½ tsp of cayenne (can substitute ⅛ tsp of the cayenne for chipotle powder if using mezcal)
- after setting, sprinkle with flaky salt, tajin, and candied jalapeno (optional)
Can you use whole milk instead of heavy cream?

For sweet vs bitter caramel:

USE A THERMOMETER 
add butter to sugar, cornstarch, salt, and water. 
bring to a boil (i used low-medium heat)
trickle by trickle add infused milk (i pre strained) stirring constantly until completely incorporated (add to quickly and it will boil over)

heat this mixture without stirring to 255°F

this will get you a traditionally american softer sweeter caramel to the dark caramel in this recipe which is just not for me. i’m a sweetie

I added 1.5 Tbsp of brewed coffee from my morning pot to the cream mixture on the stove top. Gave a nice, faint coffee flavor to them. My family raved about them. I cooked these to soft ball temp which lended a firm but pliable texture.

title: Nougat With Honey and Pistachios Recipe
author: Claire Saffitz

stats.Total Time: 45 minutes, plus at least 6 hours’ cooling

comments.commentBody:
I have now made this 5 times.  Some thoughts:

1. Edible paper on the bottom.  When I was a kid, nougat came on edible paper. You can eat it or peel it off. It can even be decorated with food safe colors.
2. Spray oil tastes bad.  I buttered the sides, used edible paper on the bottom and top.
3. Boil the heck out of the honey and sugar mixtures. Don't miss the temps by even a little. Use an instant read thermometer, and don't touch the bottom of the pan.

I tried this (solo) last night. It's possible to coordinate everything, but takes a lot of concentration. I found that mine was delicious, but not as firm as I would have liked. I didn't quite have enough pistachios, but figured it would be ok with a higher nougat: pistachio recipe. I don't think that's where I went wrong.
Almonds are a good substitute for pistachios when making nougat candy.
I’d love to see a recipe for the traditional Spanish nougat, known as Turrón, which has almonds in it. Surely it is somewhat similar to this, but with almond meal?
The times has a recipe for Almond Nougat Parfait With Orange Coulis, which uses crushed almonds.

These turned out perfect. The texture of the nougat is wonderful. I did use more like a cup of pistachios. Have everything measured out and warn the people in your home to not speak to you while this is happening, and then just focus and follow the directions and all will work out. I watched the video for tricky parts and that was helpful as well. Rave reviews all around.

This recipe was so delicious! PSA: watch the YouTube video before making this. I think it just depends on your kitchen temperature, but my egg white mixture took much longer to reach medium peaks. When my thermometer in the honey hit 248, the eggs were still frothy. Could be why some people ended up with a goopy end product? Keep mixing those egg whites until you get medium peaks. Can't wait to make again!
This looks very similar to Iranian Gaz. https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Gaz_(candy)

I made one batch last night. Mixture was too gooey so I saved the pistachios for a new batch today.
I’m pretty sure my sugar mixture did not get hot enough last night, hence it the nougat didn’t set. Tonight I made sure the sugar came up to the right 310 temp and it’s much much thicker tonight. Todays batch will def. set firmer due to better (thicker and hotter) sugar.

And yes, this is exactly like the Iranian candy I grew up with called GAZ! Very happy to find this recipe.

This was sensational. Followed directions, prepped all ingredients, focused like a zen master and viola! Amazing result. Not too gooey at all, used an oiled knife to cut and wrapped in small pieces of parchment which came off easily. Not sure where others went wrong, I will be making this again, and again, and again….

Long time reader, first comment. I was able to complete this recipe completely solo on my first attempt with very good results. I advise you prep everything in advance, and think through your plan a couple times before executing it. I am lucky to have a lot of open counter space to be able to concurrently whip eggs and swirl hot sugar, etc which made the chaos a little easier to manage. The final product is worth the effort.

In the end, this was absolutely delicious, but mine also came out too gooey. What salvaged these is rolling them into balls while dusting my hands with powered sugar and then dipping into tempered chocolate. I used the lightly salted shelled pistachios for lack of a better option, worked out great!

Delicious!  I have never made nougat before, but this recipe came out beautifully for me.  It helped to watch Claire’s video on the NYTimes Cooking YouTube channel before staging my ingredients and setting up my space.  My candy thermometer was just a tiny bit off, so I’m glad I tested it in boiling water before getting started and adjusted the readings I was looking for by a couple of degrees.  Thanks so much for the wonderful video and detail-oriented instructions!
What's a good substitute for pistachios? I love them, but I break out in hives when I eat them (or cashews or peanuts).
Almonds are a possibility for you?
almonds?
hazelnuts, pinenuts even better
This delicious-sounding  candy recipe looks tricky with the various independent step’s happening simultaneously-can anyone who made it lmk their thoughts? Thank you!
It's great to know how it's made. There are some really good nougats available from Italy and Spain! ;-)))

This delicious-sounding candy recipe looks tricky w the various independent steps happening simultaneously. Can anyone who made this lmk their thoughts? I know (or think I know!) candy making to be quite time & temperature sensitive. Thank you!
Do you need two people to do this or just one very coordinated cook? Is there any chance you could make a video? I adore nougat and have never seen a recipe for it.

right? how does one watch a candy thermometer on the stove (twice!) while whipping egg whites AND making sure the bowl is warm not hot AND making sure the candy gets to the right temp? Times giving me major anxiety over here lol. I really want to make this and would love to see some comments/tips from people who have tried it, rather than arguments about thirds and quarters
There is a video on NYT Cooking youtube channel! Search "Make Holiday Candy with Clair Saffitz".
“Cut the slab into quarters in one direction, then slice each third crosswise…”. Once sliced into quarters, one has fourths, not thirds.  What thirds are you referring to?

Probably the issue that got missed was that it takes three cuts to make four quarters. I imagine that the division of the nougat slab is not a huge deal and that the final quantity/size of the pieces will end up more a personal preference.

I imagine that they altered the recommended cuts in the recipe from fourths to thirds, and then failed to update "quarters" when editing, because 8" / .5" = 16. 16*3=48, "to make about 50 pieces." But, as L mentions, you can probably get away with whatever width cut you want.

It’s nice to imagine myself making this. Maybe one day I will. I love nougat but usually the firmer kind, with glace pieces in it as well as nuts, and especially when it’s wrapped in rice paper, as the best nougat is. I wonder how available that paper is.
I feel like I'm listening to the thoughts inside the head of someone who is standing outside a candy shop and staring at the nougat display in the window
Why turn the oven down to 200 if you're going to turn it off 5 minutes later?
Made this with a glass bowl mixer and it came out too soft. There were large amounts of hardened syrup on the glass bowl. A warm bowl might have worked better.

Made this with toasted almonds.  Sensational.

I have made tons of candy and cookies in my life, but never nougat.  

Tip: Traditional nougat (at least in my childhood) is wrapped in edible paper.  It still works, and you might even be able to get it in color.  With uncertain weather, nougat can get very sticky, very fast.

I followed this recipe to a ‘T’ and the flavor was perfect but texture is just so gooey and sticky. I let it set overnight, then cut into logs and wrapped in wax paper, and back in the fridge to set further. Alas, still too gooey for gifting. I hope the NYT recipe monitors could amend with different temperatures for the various syrups to lead to a more formed end product!
Never solidified. I think where I messed up was letting the honey begin to crystallize in the mixer while waiting for the sugar to reach 310° F.
Rather gooey. Had to freeze to make a firmer texture

Flavour is spot on. Texture, however, is not quite there. Even when the sugar and honey are cooked multiple degrees higher than the recipe calls for. I ended up baking the nougat for approximately two hours at 180F and then let the slab to set again overnight. Texture problem solved.
Are the nuts salted or unsalted? I can't find this info anywhere (in any terrone/nougat recipe). Thank you!
@Heidi I just made it with salted pistachios and I really like the salty contrast it brings!
Maybe you could substitute almonds if you need to?
What is recommended for wrapping individually cut candies?
Wax or parchment paper, cut into smaller pieces, are ideal for wrapping sugary confections. And they look cute, too!
Click on the link to the main article at the top of the recipe.
Can I substitute corn syrup with liquid glucose? Would the ratio be 1:1?  I live in Europe and I can't find corn syrup here.
I assume that the temperatures are in degrees F? Odd when the weights are  in grams
How do you make the spongy coating you find on the boats around the Greek islands?
has anyone used yellow corn syrup vs white, how’s the colour difference? can only find regular!

Made this the other night, solo.  It is SO delicious, a bit sticky so perhaps my egg whites were not quite right or temps not spot on.  I had unrefined coconut oil which may have added a bit more coconut flavor than optimal, afterwards realizing regular butter would have been a fine substitute.  While challenging to test the bowl for retained heat and watch the sugar syrup, not impossible.  Sugar syrup does take a while.  I live at in Denver so altitude may also be factor in softness of candy.

Well of course I misread the recipe and added the honey to the sugar and corn syrup mixture. Still worked. Still tastes delicious. I really like the coconut oil instead of butter.. my friends make it with butter, it makes it too rich. I used roasted almonds. I took it off the heat too soon so it's a little soft in a warm house. Good thing it's winter.

I made this yesterday and used a candy thermometer to make sure I hit all the right temperatures. Unfortunately mine did not set even after 24 hours and it was a gooey mess that was impossible to cut. I ended up throwing the whole batch out as it was just a viscous mess that I couldn’t use in my Christmas cookie box. I don’t know where I went wrong :(
I’m thrilled (truly) to find this recipe. This version with pistachios is Iranian and is called Gaz. I grew up eating it and it’s so delicious. Can’t wait to make it.
Made these, and though the flavor is delicious, mine  came out a little goopy. I left them to cool overnight, but wondering what I might adjust to have them settle more firmly.

After setting overnight, its too soft and doesn’t seem particularly “sliceable”. I put it in the fridge but i doubt that will firm it up much more. Im seeing a lot of people had the same problem- and i saw some people had the same problem as i did with a large portion of the sugar syrup hardening up in a big clump on the side of the bowl so im guessing that was the problem. I dont want to waste it so im thinking of tempering some dark chocolate and dipping so the soft texture seems intentional.

Why use coconut oil instead of butter?

Italian nugat recipies will usually have some sort of solid fat to go with the egg (it seems as though they 
either have the fat or instead have an additional egg-white for the same volume of sugar).

Why did you choose to use coconut oil instead of butter (or leave out the oil and add an egg white)?

This was a delightful recipe to make and it creates a candy unlike anything I've ever made before! There are multiple sweet flavors that come together harmoniously but aren't overpowering: honey, coconut, vanilla, pistachio. These flavors plus the texture from the pistachio really is special.

I didn't have a stand mixer so I used a handheld electric mixer which worked well; though pouring the the honey and sugar into the egg whites while you whisk them can be challenging, but doable.

I’m also having the issue of the nougat being too gooey. Both the honey and sugar mix hit the right temps using a thermometer (actually two thermometers to make sure). Any idea what the issue could be? I absolutely love the flavor but can’t gift any because it’s too gooey.
As a child, I got nougat wrapped in edible paper.  It still works - especially when the weather is humid.

I just went to buy pistachios and was stunned to see that bulk shelled pistachios at my local food co-op were $38 a pound! I dispensed about a quarter of a pound from the bin before I came to my senses. So I will make this with mostly almonds and the small handful of pistachios I purchased to give it some color.

Yes, mine turned out gooey too! A total waste of expensive ingredients. I think the temp of the sugar should be 303 degrees- hard crack. Taking it up to 310 turns out to make syrup which only makes it gooey.
I’ve successfully made nougat before, but this version is a disaster
Has anyone tried this at high altitude? I'm at 8,000 feet and just want to make sure my temps are right. Thanks!!
Lower temps by about 16°F
Ken is right, you need to lower the temp 2 degrees for every 1000 ft of altitude above sea level.
Nougat was a big hit!  Came out a little softer which made eating tricky.  Definitely follow the directions for the best results!

I had trouble getting the sugar hot enough without it burning. It burnt twice and on the third try I heated it only until it started to catch. The nougat still turned out but I think it was softer than it should have been.
Any idea if this would work out if I left out the corn syrup and replaced it with the same amount of honey?
It's been 6 months since you posted this but I tried subbing honey for the corn syrup tonight and it worked!

This is delicious however a bit too soft to handle well, a slightly harder set would be preferable especially when trying to wrap/pack individual pieces. Would try again possibly with increasing the sugar temp to a bit over what is recommended.
any tips on how to make it harder please?
Has anyone tried to keep these longer than just a week like the recipe says?

I love nougat but have never attempted to make it.  But this recipe is so well written — clear, concise, anticipating my questions — that I have to congratulate Ms. Saffitz on her technical-writing skills.  She’s inspired me to make nougat at last!

Somehow I lost the volume in the middle stage after adding in the honey. End result was delicious but not the super soft night I was hoping for. I did make sure my temperatures were spot on.
