Is over whipping the egg whites a possibility even though it looked ok before adding in the sugar mixtures? Any idea where I went wrong?

Watched the video and read the recipe through several times. I also tested my thermometer with boiling water as someone recommended in the comments above (turns out mine is 6 degrees off). The recipe requires some good coordination but it turned out perfectly and I’m so happy with it. It’s definitely meant to be a soft style nougat, you can see this in the video when Claire is cutting it up. 10/10 would make again.
Did anyone make this with butter instead of coconut oil? As others have suggested, it seems like it *should* be ok...

I made this using a hand mixer, which complicated things only a little (be sure to hang on to your bowl, since the mixture is so dense and sticky, if you let go you may launch it into orbit.)  Watched the video twice and didn't have too much trouble with the recipe. I was hoping it would compare to some nougat I bought many years ago along the roadside in Provence.  Sadly, it didn't, but it was still good.  Might use almonds or reduce the pistachios next time.

Definitely needed an instant read thermometer here. This was my first time making nougat and it turned out fantastic. I used almonds and pecans instead of pistachios because I had so many almonds and pecans. Other than the nuts I followed the recipe exactly and the only trouble I had was that I leaned the hard way that my candy thermometer wasn’t actually on target and had to remake the second stove mixture when the first burnt. I grabbed my instant read to remake it and had no further issues.

If you like it harder, simply add the almond mixture into a large pot and continue to cook on low for another 20 - 30 minutes, stirring constantly.  This will remove additional moisture and get you to hard candy structure.

stats.Total Time: 1 hour 15 minutes, plus at least 3 hours’ resting

comments.commentBody: If you are going to provide volumetric salt measurements for a hyper specific brand that is both expensive and hard to find, at least do subscribers a solid and provide a weight measurement for salt such that they can make recipes without having to do the mathematical heavy lifting for you.

@ncphelps: Are you concerned about the Diamond Crystal salt? Yes, volumetric measurements are problematic for something as variable as salt. But in the US, Diamond Crystal is one of the standard kosher salts. Cook’s Illustrated says, “Generally, 1 teaspoon of table salt equals 1½ teaspoons of Morton kosher salt, which is 2 teaspoons of Diamond Crystal kosher salt.”

Ok so after 2 failed attempts I finally made this successfully! Here’s what I learned: DO THE SOFT BALL TEST! For my first two attempts I relied on my thermometer and took the sugar mixture off the heat when it reached 238 or whatever and both times my fudge never solidified. I beat the mixtures for probably 20 minutes before giving up. The third time, I actually did the old school test and didn’t get to soft ball stage until my thermometer read 242. So, save your shoulder/mixer and do the test!

Another great recipe from Claire. Used a stand mixer instead of hand mixer and made it even easier. Used paddle attachment and mixed for about the same time indicated by recipe. Will be a welcome addition to my holiday cookie boxes!

Most of the pecans in grocery stores are old,flat, and dark.  I live in Georgia, which is the nation's largest pecan producer. Fresh pecans are light cold in color, and plump. Recipes tell you to roast them 8-10 minutes ( I have seen 30 miunutes!) That is nuts! Ovens differ of course,  but I roast pecans for SIX minutes at 350.   Pecans are rich in oil and burn easily. Pecans are good for you too! Look it up! Therefore it is worth the time and money to get pecans from Georgia, or from New Mexico

Sugar forms crystals as it cooks and this is what determines texture. Graininess occurs when sugar crystals get too big. Avoided by: Washing down sides of the pan with cold water. Making sure mixture doesn't overcook by using accurate thermometer. Incorporating corn syrup into recipe. Beating mixture after cooling to 115 degrees. If too hot, stays soft; if too cold, will be too firm.
Texture is heavenly. But use a big pot! My medium sauce pan boiled over. Both heating to soft ball stage and cooling took considerably longer than suggested in the recipe.

Easy-peasy, came out great.  After reading the notes, a couple of comments:  1)  Test your thermometer!  Put it in a pot of hard boiling water - it should read 212.  Mine read 218, so I adjusted -6 degrees.  2)  Follow the recipe!  Baking and candy making aren't cooking - they're science.  If you're told to perform a soft ball test, perform the soft ball test.  It seemed to take forever to get to temperature, but it did eventually get there.

There are so many issues with this recipe. It would have been helpful if you had said that the boiled mixture would expand as much as it did. I had to switch pots halfway through. Also, why didn't you suggest a stand mixer? The fudge shorted out my hand mixer. A stand mixer would have been so much better. And lastly, the fudge took way more than 30 to 40 minutes to cool down to 115. Disappointed.
Fannie Farmer recipe is outstanding. It does not involve a mixer, or , shudder, corn syrup-or nuts for that matter

@Debbie
The corn syrup reduces crystallization or slows down the crystallization

12.23.25 Thanks, but in searching for the Fanny Farmer Fudge recipe the use of a mixer is recommended.   "Beat well until thoroughly combined. This will be easiest with a hand mixer or Kitchen Aid Stand mixer." 
One commenter mentioned their 'fudge shorted out their hand mixer.'
Think I'll stick with NYT recipe.
Finally! A real fudge recipe and not junk!  I have an old recipe like this that I've made for years. Other fudges don't compare in flavor and texture.
This broke my hand mixer after about 4 minutes. I never got fudge - I tried to mix by hand but it stayed ropy and shiny. I have no idea what the issue was!

Don't let the detailed recipe intimidate you -- this fudge comes together fairly quickly. Thank you to other commenters for the suggestions to (a) cut the amount of nuts in half, (b) roast the nuts lightly or not at all, and (c) test the fudge for soft ball stage rather than going off temperature alone. I only had 70% chocolate around and the fudge is on the sweet side but the flavor of buckwheat honey and walnuts balances it out.

This took me 3+ hours, including waiting for the mixture to cool. After about 80 minutes, I finally just put this in the fridge, which the instructions should have stated. (Not having made fudge before and not having been instructed to do this, I assumed it needed to cool more slowly at room temperature, which I did on a cool NYC March day.) It also damaged my expensive Breville hand mixer, which kept stopping due to overheating, adding on time. The results were good but not worth the trouble.

Attempted this recipe twice;  the second time with the assistance of a professional pastry chef in the family.  Both times the mixture failed to lose its shine and become creamy when beaten in step 7.  The end result was a thin and sticky taffy rather than fudge.

We followed the directions exactly and upon cooling the mixture to 115 degrees the edges completely hardened. There was. I way to mix it all together. We tried to heat it up a bit and the chocolate and liquid separated. So disappointing. We could sure use some help or advice - what did we do wrong!?

This was A LOT OF nuts for the amount of fudge—we had at least a half cup left over after the fudge seemed completely saturated with nuts. Otherwise everything went according to plan—note to the person who had their fudge harden at 115 F, it softens again when you beat it, but it’s definitely easiest if it’s cooling in the bowl you plan to beat it in because it’s so stiff before that beating—if you’re using a stand mixer, cool the fudge in that bowl.
These cookies are wonderful but there is a good bit of prep work involved especially when you have to make your own candied orange peels.

This is a beautiful recipe. But it's also a bit complicated. I don't want to take anything away from it but very decent  old fashioned fudge (not the marshmallow/evap. milk kind) can be made more simply  I just made a very good batch using a recipe from James Beards "American Cookery", 1972. You dump everything except the vanilla in at once, boil to soft ball stage, a bit more if its humid out. remove from heat, let cool and then stir until losing gloss. Then add vanilla. Not grainy.

The fudge was best just after folding in the nuts.  It was creamy and delicious.  However, it dried out really fast cooling to room temperature.  If I make it again, I'll probably avoid cutting it into pieces (more surface area to dry out) and I'll wrap it immediately in wax paper and plastic wrap.  Once it dried out, it wasn't at all good.

Attempt #1 went great—soft ball test was perfect at 238, I let the whole mix cool to exactly 115—until the beating. It’s was gorgeous and creamy and shiny, and then it lost its shine and seized almost instantly. The taste is lovely, but the texture (obviously) is a mess. Definitely something I goofed up along the way. If anyone has thoughts, I’d love to hear them. Trying again…

@Melissa
I think it is the crystallization. I have watched a bunch of recipes on the you tube. Some use marshmallows to “inject” more corn syrup into the mixture. Corn syrup slows the crystallization. Although I am not a food scientist. Just an observation. As someone else said in the comments. It’s science not cooking. Will attempt this recipe soon. I have a marble slab that I will use with a bench scraper and offset spatulas for my attempt.
@Melissas same thing happened to me four times. I followed the recipe to the letter. Any suggestions anyone?
I agree about the salt. I live in Germany and there is no such salt here. It’s always a guess. Why do all the recipes call for it instead of universally available table salt?

There's a big difference between table salt and Diamond Crystal! You can substitute but you need to make the proper conversion which you can learn online. Convert 1 1/2 tsp Diamond Crystal salt to table salt measurement.

We don't have Diamond Kosher salt in our area grocery stores so I use Morton's and adjust my amount. Diamond is softer tasting. It doesn't have the same punch that sea salt has, and it's a bit less punchy than Morton's Kosher. I have ordered Diamond's in the past from Amazon but my palate is used to Morton's now and my cooking suffered with Diamond's because of that. I know many chefs prefer it but if you're a salter by touch and taste, which I am, it takes some getting used to.

Diamond Crystal salt is used by just about everyone that writes for this site and most chefs. Yes it's a bit more expensive than Mortons Kosher. You can find it for sale online and it is worth it! Ask yourself, why do they use it and then learn more about the different kinds of salt.
Double boiler too small for boiling the chocolate mixture.

Too many nuts for my taste. With the toasting, the flavor is overwhelmed, and the texture doesn’t get a chance to shine. YMMV. 
Other than that, good recipe and wonderful technique! I’ve never heard of melting the chocolate into a sort of ganache at the beginning, to jumpstart the cooling process later. Other recipes just withhold the butter until the end, and that’s not nearly as effective.
If your mixer is weak or old, use a wooden spoon at the end. It works just as well. Thanks for this one!
Would agave work as a sub for corn syrup?

Recipe was fairly easy to follow provided steps were followed precisely.  It did get a lot of stuff dirty in the kitchen, but then again this isn't something I'm going to make all the time.  It smells heavenly though!
I’ve never made fudge before. I’m going to try this tomorrow.  It looks sooo good.  Thanks for showing us!
Just like in the video, my hand mixer started to smoke after a minute or two. I think a stand mixer is likely the way to go!

I also had to switch pots when the mixture started to boil. I waited at least 45 minutes for everything to cool. Beating after cooling made my mixer start to smell and I was unable to make the fudge creamy and matte with just arm strength. I assume I'll have chocolate caramel with walnuts after it's completely set. Very disappointed!! What a waste of expensive chocolate and nuts :(

For everyone raising concerns about the hand beaters…watch the video demonstration of the recipe. Claire burns out the NYT Breville hand beaters. 

Doesn’t change the validity of your concerns, but you can’t say you weren’t warned. It’s also an opportunity to enjoy a little schadenfreude.

@Edward yeah, it’s kinda whacked. I’ve made a delicious traditional boiled fudge recipe for decades. Far less futzsy. No separate bowl or brushing down the pan with water, everything is boiled, cooled and mixed in the same pan. Never had it blow out a hand mixer.

Claire is Queen of pastry. Her recipes are virtually flawless. And yet…the times here are so so wacky. I was prepared for coming up to temp to take forever (which it did). I wasn’t prepared for cooling to also take forever. And my house is cool! (was this recipe tested inside a fridge?) At least double the estimated time.

Claire is Queen of pastry. Her recipes are virtually flawless. And yet…the times here are so so wacky. I was prepared for coming up to temp to take forever (which it did). I wasn’t prepared for cooling to also take forever. And my house is cool! (was this recipe tested inside a fridge?) Easily took at least double the estimated time.  Also unclear as to why using a fridge isn’t written into the recipe. My best guess is that’s it’s harder to easily monitor.  All-in-all, a solid fudge recipe. But a VERY long affair.
For baking, I read removing the skins of walnuts is highly highly recommended. The skins contain tannins that are bitter and astringent.

Question for Claire: I love this recipe and video. If I want to use Glucose Syrup instead of Honey in this recipe then how much glucose syrup would I use? The same amount? (2T) or less? 
Thank you!

stats.Total Time: 2½ hours plus overnight soak, time to cool and time to set

comments.commentBody: Made hundreds of fruitcakes since 1979, all wrapped in cheesecloth and placed in plastic zip-lock bags before I pour brandy on them. Made 32 this year alone. My best advice: use more brandy or rum on the cake and give it time to soak into the fruit/nut mix. I pour enough brandy on mine so they are well soaked with a tablespoon or so left on the bottom of the bag. I flip the cakes for a few days so the brandy is absorbed before they are put away for a month of aging. Oh, it is all well worth it!
Thanks for this tip! I am going to make my own fruitcake for the first time this year. I've got all my ingredients ready. Guess I better get going with this!

I'm Irish. Grew up with rich, brandy soaked fruit cake and loved it - still have my grandmother's hand written recipe 70 years later.  BUT this cake is a different bird and rather than make suggestions for changing it why not give it a go as written. That's what I plan on doing . And hey, I'll have the extra brandy on the side.
32! You must have happy customers, then! I want YOUR recipe!

Hi, Kathleen, Instead of alcohol, Bread Ahead Bakery in London suggests subbing an equal measure of a really strong brew of black tea, such as an Earl Grey. Bread Ahead uses this tip so they can offer their mince pies and Christmas Cakes to those who can’t or prefer not to consume alcohol. Also makes the taste of your list expensive ingredients (the dried fruits and nuts) really shine without the alcohol, which does tend to overpower things for me. Happy holidays!

This is a perfectly lovely cake, but if you are seeking a simpler alternative, do a search for the "Tu Bi'Shvat Cake" posted by food blogger David Lebovitz. It is a loaf sized cakes made with dried fruits and nuts, warm spices, 3 eggs, and just enough sugar and flour to hold it together. It takes about 10 minutes to put together, and 90 minutes to bake in a 300° oven. It is the easiest cake I know, and everyone I've made it for, loves it.
(Tu Bi'Shvat is a lesser known midwinter Jewish holiday.)
I made this yesterday in small bundtlette pans. I tested after 40 minutes. Total baking time was approximately 50 minutes.
I found the substitution of almond butter for the almond paste led to a perfect cake.
I haven't made this fruitcake yet, but in my experience with other recipes, fruitcake is rich enough without icing.  I would consider skipping the icing.
I'm not a fan of almond paste. Can it be left out? If not, what would be a good substitute?

I often want to bake something that requires soaking dried fruit overnight. I put the fruit in a Pyrex measuring cup, cover it with rum or brandy and heat it in the microwave until it starts to bubble. Cover it for a half hour and it's good to go.

To the issue of shelf life, my yearly batch of 10 Guiness Stout fruit cakes wrapped in cheesecloth soaked & topped up from time to time in brandy last until all eaten up - about a year.  The cake Robert Scott took with him on his ill-fated expedition to the South Pole was found in excellent condition over 100 years after he left it there - "almost edible".

Fruitcake gets a bad name - undeserved in my opinion.  The delight is there is no end of dried and candied fruits which can be added.

Having been steeped in the traditional English fruitcake recipe, this cake is a revelation.  I love the addition of almond paste (homemade of course) and the non- traditional fruits steeped in rum and orange make for  an enriched flavour profile. Made as per recipe, but suggest not cutting the cake til the following day when it is fully set.  Delicious with a cup of tea.

Note: This cake slices poorly on day one, but will slice beautifully after resting for 24 hours. The flavor also improves with the overnight rest. I subbed out 200g of the fruit and added back 100g of high quality candied orange peel and 100g of semisweet chocolate chunks. We are on day 4 now and the cake is incredibly moist and delicious. This is a keeper. Everyone who sampled wanted the recipe. There was rum flavor throughout, but this was not "boozy."

I have made cakes like this, with dried fruits and nuts--they are good. But real, traditional fruitcake must use candied fruits, too. To get around the tasteless supermarket candied fruits, I make my own, which takes weeks but is well worth it. Candied pineapple, orange, & citron (see David Leibowitz's recipes) and apricots, can be glaceed in season when fresh, refrigerated until baking time before the holidays. It is possible to find candied cherries without artificial colors (from Britain).

Hi,
I would like to substitute the alcohol for something else, any suggestions?

I will try this cake this weekend. I make Jehane Benoit’s cake (my mum’s recipe) which is molasses and clove based and great with a good barbadian rum for resting. The molasses mellows up after a while.

According to Mme. Benoit’s instructions, butter makes the cake last longer, as do the fruit, sugar and alcohol. The wrapping in cheesecloth soaked in rum would presumably work to age this cake.

I shall follow the recipe and bake in 2 loaf pans, and age one to see how well that works.
@andree do you have an update for us? I’m very curious how wrapping in cheese cloth worked out for you.
@andree  Thank you! I loved seeing Mme. Benoit in print. She was awesome and I still refer to her cookbooks. A Canadian icon.
OMG this looks wonderful. Can you advise if part of this could be frozen? I doubt my small family could eat this inside of a week.

Fruitcakes are made to last for years - literally.  Hence the jokes about families regifting the same fruitcakes to one another from year to year.

The infused alcohol acts as a preservative.  I'm sure the amount of rum or brandy called for in this cake will easily see you through a few weeks to the last crumbs.

Made the recipe exactly as written with only one exception: I used mini Bundt pans. (I didn’t think anyone would want that much fruitcake!) For those who want to go this route just be careful as it cooks a lot more quickly. Super fun to make although a lot of work.

What Gerry said! I love fruit cake of all kinds, but I am going to make THIS fruit cake from Claire Saffitz and enjoy it for what it is. I look forward to it, because it looks like a gorgeous cake. And Claire's recipes are always delicious.

My cake was a bit on the dry side, but I think I may have over cooked the cake by about 10min.  I have enough ingredients to give this a second attempt, I may adjust my personal cook time to come out a bit earlier.  However, overall this cake tastes good, and is somewhat simple to make.

I love fruit cake and wanted to try this recipe, but have been told that I need to get my cholesterol level down. I decided to substitute the 8 oz of butter with 8 oz of applesauce and the result still tastes like fruit cake! Lovely!
Any hints on what could replace the alcohol for those of us that don't use any sort of alcohol (even if it's cooked out)?

Gluten free: Just sub equal weight of Bob’s Red Mill 1-to-1 GF flour blend (light blue label) and proceed exactly as written.  Make sure to bake through, which always takes a bit longer w GF flour.  Absolutely delicious.

Simply the best fruit cake I have ever eaten. It's one of the few recipes I have followed as written, with the exception of using more dried cranberries instead of dried cherries, as I couldn't find them. Making homemade almond paste was surprisingly easy and satisfying.  It turned out exactly as shown in the picture.
I’m wondering if this can be made in smaller pans for sharing/gifting.  I don’t want to eat this giant cake myself and I don’t think my family will eat it.
I inquired about this for a different recipe (not owning a bundt pan) and was told that 2 loaf pans would work. You can then give one 'fruit loaf' away!
Made a 2/3 version in a wreath Bundt pan. AWESOME. My only substitution was dates instead of prunes. My mom used to make a non-alcoholic version with dates.

This cake is delicious and not difficult-you just have to plan ahead.  Made a half recipe in a 6-cup pan.  No prunes, used dried blueberries, cherries, apricots & currants.  Used bourbon not brandy. Don't skip soaking the fruit.  Just a tiny drizzle of the glaze.  I think most people would like it if you could get them to try it, but that's okay-more for me! Thank you, Claire.

Changed my mind about fruitcake! This is absolutely beautiful and delicious. Definitely a project - I enjoyed every minute! I love that Claire said if you don’t like a particular fruit, use something else. I did! Subbed in chopped dates for the prunes. So good!

I do have a question - my icing wasn’t as snowy white as pictured. You can see through it. Did I ,are it too thin maybe? Just wondering if the wonderful NYT community can weigh in. Thanks!

Made this with strong tea. Did not want the alcohol, but we love the dried fruits and nuts. Kept well and easy to make. We never got around to offering it to others, so we’re not sure it would be a hit!

If you want to make this all in a single day, cover the dried fruit / liquid mixture and stick it in the microwave for about a minute, and then let rest until cool.  It won't be quite the same as if you let it sit out 12-24 hours, but not far off!

Substituted earl grey tea for the rum.  Putting the dried fruits to marinate I was reminded of the mince I made last season which included diced Granny Smith and included those here. Delicious, my favorite cake ever!!

@janis made this in four 3 cup bundt pans. 45 minutes at 325. Used earl grey instead of rum, left out the apple I put in last year, added Trader Joe’s candied orange slices with the walnuts. Perfect fruitcake!
@janis To be clear, I used earl grey lavender this year because it was all I had, and I feel it enhanced the flavor even more.

Made my third "Saffits" Fruitcake cake of the season and all have come out great and eaten quickly by three different parties.  Didn't have apricot jam the third time so used plum jam, which darkened the cake even more.  Also used Walnut & Fig Rum: a winner!  My daughter divided her recipe into small loaf pans wanting individual portions but came out a bit drier than mine.  Also, in my most recent one I used green raisins that gave it an even juicier texture.  Added to my yearly tradition.

I’ve made this cake as written two years in a row and love it! The only thing that irks me is I can’t seem to get clean slices, possibly due to the chunky nuts and fruit, but each slice comes out very crumbly. Next year I’ll try adding a couple of tablespoons of sour cream to moisten things up a bit or maybe a spot of neutral oil

What type of flour is recommended in this recipe? The flour quantity in the ingredient list is listed as 2 cups or 270 g. This is inconsistent with the standard weight of a cup of AP flour referenced by both NYT Cooking (128 g) and King Arthur (120 g).Choosing flour type is always challenging when I use American recipes, since Canadian AP flour has higher protein (13%) than American ( 9-12%). Some commentators reported a crumbly cake.  Perhaps they used a lower protein/gluten AP flour?

This cake lasted much longer than the recommended five days.  And what a fabulous fruit cake!  I soaked the fruit in orange juice and brandy for a couple of weeks because I didn’t have time to make it right away.
Thanks for having Claire back to share this recipe. I’m allergic to nuts. Is it possible to substitute sunflower seeds for the walnuts?
Can I leave off the almond paste?
The almond paste is integral to the cake batter. It is not placed upon the baked cake as marzipan is.
I think substituting cognac instead of the rum would also be fantastic if you're not a rum fan
I’d like to adjust this recipe for mini loaf or mini Bundt pans.  Can someone suggest a cooking time?

Depending on how small, start checking at 30 minutes or so.  As always though, the toothpick test is your best friend!  A toothpick inserted into the middle of the cake should emerge clean, or with just the barest hint of crumb clinging to it.  Once it does that, the cake is done.

Any thoughts on how this could be made gluten-free?  My husband is a huge fan of fruitcake (any and all!) and for years I made them from scratch.  But he's recently been diagnosed with celiacs, so traditional fruitcake has been off the menu.  I'd love to reintroduce it, made with a g-f flour mix, but these mixes behave very differently than regular flour.  Does anyone have any experience with g-f fruitcakes, or suggestions for what I might try?
King Arthur make an excellent gluten free all-purpose flour. I've substituted it in many recipes and it has performed just like normal flour.
Can anyone advise whether the dried cherries and cranberries are sweetened or unsweetened?

They should be unsweetened.  Dried cranberries and cherries are plenty sweet enough as-is!

Source: many years of baking fruitcakes

I made this yesterday and my apartment smelled amazing! I soaked the fruit for 3 days as soon as I saw Claire's video on Youtube. I did not ice the cake and stopped at the apricot glaze. Soo delicious, I will never spend a fortune on expensive fruitcakes again! A  new Holiday tradition. Very easy for follow recipe too. Thanks Claire!
I love fruitcake and this looks good. Made exactly as the recipe said. Next time I will probably make my own almond paste rather than buying it ($8.69 for a 7 ounce package!).
Any tips on how to adapt this for muffin-sized minis?
I love fruitcake!

I made this this weekend for a family tree trimming get together. 
  I did skip the icing (gilding the lily) and used a double loaf pan, but otherwise followed the recipe exactly. It's a really great recipe.
  I did it as a kind of trial, never having made a fruitcake. I will be using it from now on, thank you, Claire.
This cake is terrific and very easy to make. It's sweet enough without the icing, but the icing would certainly dress it up for a special occasion.

I can't stand "candied" fruit, citron and peel so, years ago I took a standard fruitcake recipe and switched out all that ick for dates, golden raisins, dried apricots, currants, marschino cherries and I forget what else. It was the BOMB and quite similar to Claire's.
Nut allergies in my home.  Can this cake be made without almond paste or nuts?  What would happen if I just left them out?

I am SOOOOO happy to see this article and recipe.  I have always thought that fruitcake was an item that SHOULD be wonderful if only the neon candied fruit was ditched and really good ingredients were used instead.  I have now successfully converted everyone I know with a version I cobbled together from old reference books (which includes sugared orange rinds,  crystalized ginger, a variety of unsweetened/unsulfered dried fruit, pecans, and port.  I am 100% sure this recipe is a REAL winner.

This is delicious, but it's more European Christmas cake than a traditional American fruitcake. I used both walnuts and pecans and toasted them in the oven for eight minutes while getting the cake together. Next time, I will use golden raisins instead of cherries and a 1/2 C of brandy instead of 1/3 after baking. Goes perfectly with a cup of Irish tea.

My Mum (and her mum before her) made fruit cakes every year forthe family and neighbours.  At 81, my mother has already made 6 this year.  She uses Grand Marnier to soak the fruit and then douses the hot cake with a few tablesspoons of Conganc immediately after it comes out of the oven.  I dont generally like fruit cake, bu i happily steal a few slices of hers every year.  I will make this one but plan to steal her idea and use Grand Marnier rather than brandy or rum.

I made this gluten-free subbing almond flour for the wheat flour. It turned out delicious but more fragile.  I also used almond extract instead of vanilla. 
TIPS:  Slater on the butter thickly on the pan to keep it from sticking. Use GF flour, GF breadcrumbs or almond flour in the pan to minimize sticking. Better yet, use a spring form pan and line with greased parchment paper. When you flip it out of the pan flip it onto the serving platter, don’ try to flip onto a rack then move to a plate.

I followed the recipe exactly and made 15 mini bundt cakes (bigger than bundtlettes). They were perfect after 40 minutes at 350ºF. Turned right out of the pan perfectly thanks to Claire's excellent how-to video. The difference is surface area. You'll need an additional 1 C of rum to soak them, 2 to 3 times as much apricot glaze, and twice as much icing. Suggest chopping all the fruit to the size of the currants. After gifting, we had 2 left to taste. YUM YUM YUM and a BOTTLE OF RUM!

Dear Simpler Alternative, 

In 2009 we bought The book of New Israeli Food at a Golan Heights Winery.  Up until now we had only made the Chreime North African Fish Stew (p. 132) which is amazing.  After reading your note, I made the Tu B’Shevat cake (p. 246), using date, prunes, dried cherries, golden raisins, prunes, figs, walnuts & almonds.  It was wonderful!! Thank you so much for mentioning it.  It will now be a part of our standard rotation.

I tasted every type of fruit before I started - if you wouldn’t eat dried fruit out of hand it has no business going into a cake. 

Mine is in the oven right now and smells heavenly. I subbed out raisins and cranberries and added chopped dried mango and pluots.

Made exactly as written. Family ate the whole thing in a week, and I made it again. So easy and delicious—my favorite fruitcake ever. The dried cherries are the best, but love how each type of fruit adds flavor.

Complete winner!!  Everyone loved this cake and two people asked to take some home!  Even when it was a week old!  I followed the recipe exactly except using raisins instead of currants.  I will make this an annual event.
Followed recipe exactly. Perfectly moist, not overly sweet.

I made this for yesterday's feast and it could not have been better. I changed up the dried fruit a little, using 170g each of dried cherries, peaches, and pears; and 113g each of finely chopped crystalized ginger and currants. I also used Irish whiskey in place of the brandy. The substance of the recipe is just perfect though, and I can't wait to make it again next year.
Recipe calls for toasted walnuts or pecans. I tried a combination of toasted hazelnuts, walnuts and pecans totaling 1 cup and that turned out great. Still can’t believe how good this recipe is!

This felt like a lot of work at the time but worth it once I got a bite. I ended up making a 9x5 and two smallish 8x4 loaves which worked out well. I reduced the baking time as needed. The icing is almost too sweet, but here’s hoping the health benefits of the dried fruit, nuts and rum make up for it!
It's OK.  In my opinion it needs lots more booze and aging to taste like a proper fruitcake.
What sub for almond paste for those with nut allergy??

Ok so I had the wrong bundt pan -- one with lots of curves and a great design -- but I made it anyway. The result: totally sticking! So I turned bad into good by making it into a pudding and it was delicious! Guests loved it and I will make it again -- this time getting the right bundt pan!!!

Why the emphasis on only just incorporating the eggs before stirring in the flour after beating the butter/sugar for a full 3 minutes.  I understand about not over-beating flour but not the caution about eggs.
Delicious and moist.  Dried fruit was much better than candied.
This will become my go-to fruitcake recipe. I love the addition of the almond paste. Absolutely delicious and keeps well. I left off the frosting.

I was thrilled with the fruitcake recipe results last year. Yes, it freezes beautifully. I make loaves and cut them, and keep them in a zip lock freezer bag. I am not fond of the strong alcohol flavor, so I use orange juice and rum for both soaking the fruit and the loaves. One of my recipients soaks his loaf in rum when he receives it and is delighted with the flavor.

This recipe makes the best fruitcake we've ever had.  Last year, we used two glass loaf pans; this year we used an antique cast iron bundt pan.  We found it helpful to use a serrated bread knife, washed before each slice, and lightly 'skated' through the cake, to slice it.  The cake made in the bundt pan was harder to slice without crumbling - we think due to less cake surrounding the fruit on the "thin end" of the slice.  Got dried fruit from Nuts.com.
Does anyone know if this could be made with a gluten free flour? Seems like you could almost just swap out the flour as levening isn't important here.....

I try to make a fruitcake for my mom every year, as she loves them. The past cakes have all been ones that I needed to feed or age for weeks but this one was way easier and the family favorite so far. In order to modernize it a bit I used a mixture of dried strawberries, cherries, cranberries and dates instead of currents etc and I glazed it with a cream cheese glaze. The cake was so moist and even some of the fruitcake haters liked it!

This was delicious and held at room temperature for about 10 days. Started to look a bit sloppy and crumbly but the taste remained.
Thanks Claire for the very explanatory video. I watched it several times to get the hang of it. It helped a lot.
This is a rather expensive cake to make but so worth it! 
Mine turned out like the picture but more drips of icing. I will definitely make it again.
A long laborious recipe, but well worth the time and effort. The best fruitcake I've ever tasted. Boozy, fruity and moist.
I made this 2 years ago aand still remember how outstanding it was. Excellent recipe, kudos to CS.

I've never had fruit cake before but love Claire Saffitz so I made this. She has converted me into a believer. I'd like a slightly boozier version. I wonder if upping the amount for the post bake rum "feeding" would work. Tip: Use a good serrated knife to slowly saw away the slices or it will fall apart on you.

I'm not great at telling when cakes are done with a toothpick. 
Anyone know when it's done by testing with a probe thermometer in ⁰F?
Love this Fruitcake!  Please read their ingredients list carefully to include 8 OUNCES / 2 Bars /16 Tablespoons of Butter, and not just 8 Tablespoons of Butter (as I misread the first time I made it)

@Bill from SF

I made the same mistake and wouldn’t have ever realized if I hadn’t seen your comment.  Thank you!  Turned out surprisingly well for being 1 stick short on the butter.

It may not seem like a traditional fruitcake, but it's a fantastic recipe.  My only issue following the instructions was the consistency of the icing that turned out too be way too dilute.  I had to add 50% more sugar (by volume) to get an icing that had the consistency of what you see in the video and photographs.

Absolutely delicious! I typically don't like fruit cakes, but this one caught my eye due to it being so quick to make. Figured I'd give it a chance, especially because of the almond paste, of which I love. Cake was a delight to everyone who tried it. And now, I'm making my second one...this time with brandy (first one was with rum)!!
Is it possible to make a non-alcoholic version? How? I hate the taste of rum, brandy, and whiskey.
Try tea.  Coffee might be too strong.  The key is softening dried fruit with an added flavor.  Skip dairy products and enjoy!

You could use an equal amount of apple juice or apple cider, or perhaps a fortified wine like a semi-dry marsala or madeira if those flavors are more to your liking. If it's the taste of raw alcohol you object to, you could boil the rum for a few minutes to cook off the alcohol. In that case, I'd start with twice the amount called for and boil until it's reduced by half.
Curious about the almond paste?  What does it add, is there a substitute?
The almond paste adds flavor, moisture, and richness to the cake. If you can't locate some in your local supermarket, it is very easy to make it yourself. Simply google "homemade almond paste".
“Brush” the pan with butter? Does that mean it’s melted?
Watch the video of Claire making the cake and brushing the pan with butter. It's very helpful. https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=RG3d5JlL3xs
Would this be horrible if flour and butter were substituted for those with gluten and dairy intolerance?

Has anyone substituted olive oil (perhaps a light olive oil so that the flavor would not be overpowering) for the butter? I know butter is better, but I’m looking to take the cake in a more Mediterranean connection, not least because the person I’d like to make the cake for loves fruitcake but can’t eat butter.
I think I'd use vegan butter such as Miyoko's rather than olive oil.
Do you actually taste the almond paste? unfortunately, I do not like the taste of it. Is there a viable substitute?
I would like to make two smaller Bundt cakes in two 6-cup Bundt pans, but don't know how to adjust the bake times. Thank you!
My mother made a "simple" fruit cake with cherries and brazil nuts- it was exquisite!
I am very allergic to almonds. There must be some substitute for almond paste? I would love to try this recipe.
You can leave it out, but consider adding another 1/4 cup of brown sugar and 2-3 tablespoons of butter to compensate for the missing fat and sugar.
Here's a video of Claire making this fruitcake. I can't wait to make it myself! https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=RG3d5JlL3xs

I doubt this has to be eaten within a week. Stored in an airtight container in the fridge or in another cool place, it will last longer, just like German stollen. And Deb B, to answer your question, I would not freeze it, it will get crumbly. - I am still on the fence about adding the icing, will decide after baking it tomorrow.

The fruitcake from Collin Street Bakery in Corsicana, Texas, are delicious. If you don't want to bake one yourself, theirs is a great substitute. Same cakes for multiple decades sent all over the world. They come in a tin and last a long time if refrigerated.
It’s delicious!  However, it was impossible to cut neatly. It just crumbled. I’ll definitely make it again, and maybe freeze it a bit behind slicing.
For me, it cut perfectly using a long, sharp, chef's knife. A serrated knife will tear it.

IMO, leaving out the alcohol is OK only if you plan to have the cake eaten before it molds. Moistened dried fruits do mold easily. The flavor of rum is an easy fix, the preservative calls for short storage periods. Also consider the sweetness via the booze and adjust that factor.
I personally like dates and figs which are missing here. I'd toss the prunes and add one or both. I have never liked "candied fruits" other than a few cherries for color. My last cake I used maraschino cherries.
Has anyone tried this with gluten-free flour? The ingredients are pretty expensive to experiment with, but I'd like to send a smaller loaf to my sister who does no gluten.
I'm going to try it with King Arthur 1:1 flour. I made a pumpkin bundt cake with it for Thanksgiving, and it worked perfectly.
Can the cakes be frozen.  Only one week of shelf life means can’t make them too early in December.
See the video here... https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=RG3d5JlL3xs
Can the cakes be frozen?
Wow, out-of-this-world delicious! A real winner.

Candied peel might not be easy (or cheap) to buy, but it's very simple to make your own. And it's easy. My favorite way to to soak the peel for 24-48 in salted water, then bring the syrup to a boil every morning (without the peel) & then returning the peel, then putting the lid on the pan. Do this every morning until the peel is translucent. 

As for pre-soaking the fruit, that's what most people do. That being said, this recipe is appealing and I will be trying it.
Very good. More rum needed at all steps, but especially after the cake is cooked.
I did one step accidentally out of order, added the flour mixture after the fruit and nuts. Will this change the density of the cake?
I did the same! I think the recipe should have been written a bit differently in that section. Anyway, it turned out fantastic despite adding the flour after the fruit.
I’m not a fan of prunes what other fruit could I substitute? Thanks
Add pineapple, oranges, maybe Other tropical or citrus fruit, leave out currents, try sweeter liquor not brandy
How long to bake if using two loaf pans? Also, can this be frozen?

Oh my Lord. I just love this recipe. Added a bit more run and orange juice to soak the fruit - 1 cup didn't seem like enough. Soaked it all right up. Seemed to make more than I expected: had enough for a Bundt and 6 muffins. Don't like to use the rum without cooking off the alcohol, so blended it with the fig jam and the juice of 1/2 of an orange - perfect clear glaze. So delicious - can really taste the almond paste. Thanks!!
Does this cake really have no sugar in the batter??? I don’t want to make it until I know for sure.
Dark brown sugar

Made this in two 5x8 loaf pans. The cake, though delicious, is dry and just a bear to cut.  Falls apart under the knife.  I tested them with a cake tester at the minimum time and had some crumb, so I left them in for the maximum time.  That was a mistake.  Any others have a similar experience?
Disregard, I read the recipe too quickly!
I made this and it's great! However, the icing was grainy. I sifted the powdered sugar and whisked it a lot. Any suggestions?
Just finished glazing cake.  It looks scrumptious.  Be sure to glaze while while apricot mixture and cake are still a bit warm.  Can't wait to sample.

I made two of these cakes exactly per the recipe with no substitutions and neither held its form once sliced. The cakes baked for about 1 hour 15 minutes and the cake tester came out clean. They cooled for 15 minutes before being lightly fed with alcohol. But as soon as you slice into it and try to serve it, it completely crumbles. I’ll also be adding nutmeg and cinnamon to future versions as the spice level could be better.

No Bundt pan, so used an angel food tube cake pan.  Got my wires crossed with the gram ingredient list, thought it called for 700 grams of almond paste instead of 200 grams (home made from whole almonds). 700 seemed a bit much, probably used 600 grams.  Was difficult to remove cake from tube pan and major cracks developed.  I used the melted jam to repair cracks, hid them with icing, kept it cool while copiously adding more Lemon Hart 151 Demerara rum for a week.  Delicious and sliced well!
The best fruit cake I have tried so far. Thanks for sharing.
How important is it put on the apricot jam glaze while the cake’s still warm?
What is the baking temperature?
I baked on a low oven (300-325) for an hour and five minutes and it came out perfectly.

I am making this fruitcake on Christmas Eve in honor of my father, who recently passed away. My family likes the taste of nuts but not the texture. What would happen if I ground the nuts up so that they were very fine? Could I use them that way? Or should I just leave them out and add more fruit?

I just made this cake as directed and there is almost no perceptual texture of the walnuts, just the flavor. I roasted them till gold brown before adding them to the batter (as directed), and I they became very soft in the final cake. I don't think your family will even notice them.

I followed this recipe more or less exactly as written with a few changes: I made my own almond paste out of ground (blanched almonds) + confectioner's sugar + egg whites; and I substituted dried currants with dried blueberries. The cake requires a bit of work, but it's well worth it. It doesn't quite have the texture of a traditional fruitcake, something more like a moist carrot cake, but with ALL the kick and flavor of a fruitcake that's been aged for a few days. So good!

I used a mix of golden raisins, cranberries, dates and dried banana and mangos and soaked it in brandy/oj for two days.   I bought those disposable paper loaf pans bc I was planning on gifting fruit cakes but that turned out to be a disaster- the cake broke into pieces when I tried to remove it. I lost two small loaf cakes this way. The third attempt which used up the rest of my batter was in an 8 inch metal loaf pan and it came out fine. The cake was done at about an hour and 10 minutes.

I just made the cake and if the cake tastes as good as the finished batter than it is a hit!  Waiting for it to cool completely before icing. I was a little reluctant to add all the rum, so for the rum you put on the outside, I only used a splash of rum and the rest fresh squeezed OJ.
I made this with almond butter rather than almond paste, as I just couldn't find the latter in Australia. It turned out great, so delicious (almost) as written.
Has anyone tried this with cream sherry?

Broke coming out of the pan. Maybe not buttered and floured enough. Maybe underbaked (went 70 minutes, maybe go 80). Maybe not cooled enough (waited fifteen minutes, but maybe wait longer for better setting).  Maybe it’s a cheap pan. Will make a bread pudding out of it.

Has anyone frozen this cake?  I’ve had the fruit soaking 24 hours to bake today to serve on Xmas day but now have to postpone the party (Covid positive person in the household).  If I bake, should I glaze and ice before or after freezing?

Last year I let the cake sit at room temperature for about 5 days with the apricot preserves.  I then iced it with the glaze to serve at Christmas. After this I froze the remaining cake in small segments. We ate the cake all winter and it was delicious.  The icing was fine after freezing  but if you are serving to guests, I might ice after it has thawed to make sure it looks perfect.

Check the oven after 50 min or so because my cake was a little overbaked at 55min.
I don't understand how in the instructions they say 1 hour 10 min to 1 hour 20 minutes, such a discrepancy .
Hmm, interesting - mine took 1h15 exactly!

I used a double loaf pan from target, as I didn't have a bundt pan, and it was just the right size.
I skipped the apricot jam and extra sugar. I thought it unnecessary. Other than that I followed the recipe and it was really GREAT!!! This will be a "go to" recipe from now on.

Add hazelnuts, toasted & chopped
Increase cognac for marinating fruit
Try including crystalized ginger in fruit mix

Cheated with canned roasted red pepper, substituted castelvetrano olives for the lemon, simmered for several hours… (and admittedly didn’t really measure all the spices once I got into the spirit) - this is a keeper. The kefta themselves are amazing.
Glorious cake. Not a “traditional” fruitcake, but every bit as satisfying. This will become my Christmas cake, as I’ve lost my mother’s dark fruitcake recipe.
Cooking time too long; at 70 min the tester wasn't clean, so I let it go to 80. The cake was very dry. Delicious flavor but very disappointing texture.

We loved this cake.  We made it exactly as specified, but used twice the amount of rum: a third of a cup for each of the top and bottom.  The only problem was the icing, which we ended up not doing.  I don't understand how this amount of confectioners sugar can be combined with such a small amount of liquid.  I am wondering why a hand mixer was specified instead of a stand mixture for the initial steps.

I just watched the video.  For the icing, it looked as if she had more than the 2 tablespoons of milk that the recipe called for.  But anyway, we loved the cake without the icing and are afraid that the sweetness of the icing would detract from the great flavor.

This was a fun cake that I substituted for my usual Stollen using homemade marzipan and candied peel along with the other recommended ingredients. However it seems to be missing something like sour cream to moisten the cake and the proportions for the frosting seem off. I needed to add a lot more powdered sugar. If I make it again I will try including solid ring of marzipan in the middle of the cake instead of crumbling it into the cake.
Consensus among eaters was that there was too much fruit, not enough cake. The almond paste was lost in this.
Also it didn’t slice. Just crumbled.

Most fruitcake-haters are thinking of those awful bricks of congealed candied fruit they'd get from some great aunt when they were young. This is not that fruitcake. I made it exactly as instructed. Even the skeptics at our get-together were won over. And don't be afraid of the booze. It's really quite subtle and compliments the fruit and spices wonderfully.

I made this fruitcake. Instead of marzipan, I used  almond butter. Everything else was exactly as was written. It was not overly sweet like the cakes usually are. There was a strong smell of booze as soon as I uncovered it, but it was not boozy. It was delicious. Next time i will make it with crystallized ginger instead of dried prunes.
This cake turned out great. Excellent recipe.

I made this cake the day after I read about it - followed the recipe to the letter, using a delicious 12 yr old Demerara Rum (Eldorado is the brand), which I believe enhanced the final product. Needless to say, the final product was D-Licious!  We ate the whole thing way before it had a chance to get old.  Thank you Claire for sharing this recipe!!  I've already restocked all the dried fruit needed for the soon to be made sequel.
This was SO good.  And the recipe is wonderfully precise—esp if you watch the video too.  Gratifying to make, and delicious! Thank you!

What leeway is there add a bit more with the fruit and nuts before risk of it not holding together or some baking issue with additional sugar content?  I liked it enough to make a second one, with pieces of candied ginger and high-quality candied orange peel but cut back on that much weight of cranberries and currants in abundance of caution.

I was not a big fruitcake fan before making this cake, but now I am a fan.  It was absolutely delicious and stores well. I made it in smaller loaves and froze before adding the lemon glaze. Everyone loved it!
This cake captured the taste of old fashioned fruitcake. This may become a holiday staple at our house.
Your video demo is fantastic.  Thank you.

I made this cake, as written, in mid December for Christmas.  I made 3 loafs from the recipe. One was eaten at Christmas. It was delicious. One was a gift. The third I wrapped in freezer paper, put in a plastic bag in my fridge and forgot. I opened it today. Oh my goodness!!
It is wonderful! Next time I will put it away for over a month before I serve it.

This fruitcake is DELICIOUS! I typically make the traditional cake that takes forever but wasn't able to this past Xmas. So I decided to try this recipe & was pleasantly surprised. It's different than the typical fruitcake in a good way. The fresh dried fruit is a game changer & I will be making this one going forward as it's a much better version in my opinion. Also, I didn't ice it, just coated it with the apricot preserve. The cake is so good it doesn't need it. Try it, you'll love it!

This was great with lots of flavour and my kids loved it. I just realised last week that US cup and spoon measures are different to the rest of the world (?!) so I made this using my new US cups and spoons. I used homemade marmalade jam instead of the Apricot and it worked well add a nice note also only used half the brandy after baking as it was for kids.

This was delicious lots of flavour and recipe worked well. I made this using my new US cups and spoons as only realised last week that the US has different sizes cups and spoons sizes than the rest of the world (?!?!!). Beware American bakers of overseas recipes. I subbed homemade marmalade jam for apricot which worked well and only used half the brandy post baking as it was for the kids to eat after school.

Made this cake last Christmas, following the recipe except used Bourbon (Kentucky native) instead of rum and cut the currants. It was lovely! Perfect texture and flavors. Been craving it again and can't wait to make it this weekend.
How many eggs? I do ot see eggs in the ingredient list.

4 large eggs, at room temperature.
It is listed near the bottom of the ingredient list for the batter.

This will irritate some. I love the flavours of fruitcake but cannot stand the texture fruits in cake. I baked this cake and it was lovely. My sister and mum are beyond thrilled by it. I want a fruitless version: a dense boozy spice cake, with nuts and maybe some chocolate chunks, or maybe no chocolate.

I made this and it was enjoyed by many! I tried making it in advance so that it wouldn’t turn out crumbly like many commenters noted, however, mine did still turn out quite crumbly. I might leave out just a little dried fruit next time to see if that helps, or chop the fruit even smaller.
I really enjoyed the flavor the almond paste brought! The cake didn’t taste boozy at all, which I also appreciated, however, I really didn’t taste the rum at all, so maybe a little more would be ok :)
Loved the recipe. It reminded me of the fruit cake I grew up eating. However, it’s way more glamorous.

Ah, the memories. My Mother was also a Department 56 queenie with the entire LR dedicated year round to her numerous villages. The collection is something my brother got all of, and to this day I am a little little miffed; I wanted the North Pole collection.
Another tradition was her fruitcakes, which had the rare quality of being moist due to copious amounts of orange marmalade and brandy. I have her original handwritten recipe and made it for several years, until the dried fruit became so dear

Please update the  ingredient list to include TOASTED nuts. I know it mentions toasted nuts in the instructions, but I think it’s an extra step to stop and toast nuts when you are in the middle of making the cake. If I know I need toasted nuts up front, I can have all ingredients measured and ready to use.

This year, I made fruitcake with black coffee, molasses, three tablespoons of cocoa and--my own idea while looking for the cloves--a tsp of desert hibiscus (tart). Best one ever. Oh, of course, lots more booze and water the cake with it at least a few times before serving. I made mine last Monday, had it last night. Yum. Will eat the rest over the next couple of weeks.
so much dried fruit caused such digestive crises in our family that no one would have a second piece...ever. it was tasty, though.

I made the cake in a Bundt pan, reduced the sugar by 2/3rds and it was plenty sweet.  I baked it for 50 minutes and it was done. Even though I followed  recommendations to wait a day before slicing it, it was still very crumbly, although delicious.  I’m still looking for a go to Christmas fruit cake recipe. This one is not it.

This recipe is quite similar to Rose Naftalin’s Holiday Fruit Cake from her “Grandma Rose’s Sinfully Delicious…” cookbook. I’ve long substituted dried fruits of many varieties soaked in brandy and fruit juice for the dyed candied cherries and pineapple called for in that recipe, which makes it a lot like this one. I highly recommend that cake—and that fabulous cookbook if you can find a copy. It’s been my go-to for celebratory cakes etc. for three decades.
… and I prefer that recipe. It’s slightly more effort, but I’ve always had wonderful results with it.

I second this suggestion. I have been making this cake since it was first published in 2012. It is the easiest recipe I know, but is endlessly adaptable, and everyone that I've made it for loves it. 
If you can't make this cake, throw out your oven!
Can a part of the cake without the icing be frozen so it keeps longer?

I did not figure out was pomegranate molasses was until I was knee deep in the recipe.  I substituted saskatoon jam.  It is pretty good!  I used a whole pomegranate which was a little more than specified; the directions did say to taste!  Also I didn’t have the grains so made one batch with farro and another with buckwheat.  Both were good.
I checked mine at one hour, done to perfection.  Now let’s see what happens when I try to turn it out.  Wish me luck!!

Followed the recipe exactly with the exception of reducing the sugar amount to 1/4 cup and cutting it the next day add some suggested. The result was an amazingly rich, moist and flavorful cake. My new go to for Xmas now. Thank you for sharing the recipe! Happy holidays!

Cooked this yesterday exactly to recipe save for a little variation in the dried fruits. Turned out pretty nicely, but there are a couple of things I would change.
- Prune flavour is too prominent. Would use less.
- A bit too moist (!). Slices crumbled. Maybe a little less rum/brandy after baking? I know, hard to imagine. Perhaps this will correct itself over the next few days.
The cake is easy to cut cleanly! Just not with a cake server. Use a good sharp chef's knife on this one, otherwise the fruit can be difficult.

I've made this three times now and it turns out really well each time.  This year I managed to forget to add the almond paste, and it's still turned out well. I have a two-oven Aga and this year I cooked it for about 45 minutes in the roasting oven, under a cold sheet, and then about another 45 minutes in the simmering oven.
This was a miss for me. It was dry and too sweet. The flavor was medicinal.

Update: Two or so days of ageing improve this cake a lot. Unsurprisingly, the flavours mellow out and meld a bit, and the texture improves. My wife, who’s not been a big fan of my utterly fabulous Christmas cake made in past years using Rose Naftalin’s fabulous recipe, slightly modified, loves this cake. I’m going to make another one today. I’ve cut the fruit a little finer this time; I am pretty sure that will make the cake less apt to crumble when sliced thinly.

I’m a strong cook, but a so-so baker… Made this exactly as written. I broke it up into a couple of days work, which made it quite approachable. This cake is soooooooo delish, my whole family has insisted that I make it a Christmas tradition! I think I’ll make a double batch and get some mini Bundt pans - so I can give it as gifts of love!!

This was one of 3 cakes I made for Christmas. I followed the recipe and it was a perfect cake. I chose to use a good quality rum, but I’m sure it would be just as good with brandy. It was moist and delicious. The apricot glaze finished the cake beautifully and it could be served just like that. I made the icing following the recipe and adjusting viscosity with temperature and sugar- beautiful! I prepared and soaked the fruit one day - made cake day 2 - served day 3, 4 (Christmas Eve and Day).

Instead of a 12-cup Bundt pan, I used 4 3-cup Bundt pans. I placed the cakes on one cookie sheet and baked for an hour at 325 degrees; the toothpick came out clean. Some of the dried fruit was very dry, so I kept an eye on the soaking and ended up needing almost twice as much liquid. I used very strong Earl Grey tea instead of alcohol. This cake is my favorite of the holiday season.
This was remarkably good-and worth the time soaking the fruit. Family loved it- rich and moist.  Kept well for about 5 days on the counter too.

I made this during the holidays for King’s Day for my gardening group - many of whom are British and accustomed to traditional fruit cake. They loved it and some had seconds which almost never happens. My husband who is Irish also appreciated it despite not being  a ”true” Irish Christmas cake. And the frosting makes it look spectacular and is a nice addition of sweetness.

This is a wonderful recipe! I used good rum when making it and followed the recipe closely. I can’t get over how good this was without weeks of investment. Next time when I make it (and there will be many more times that I do), I will make it a couple of days in advance. The cake can handle the delay and the flavor just gets better with the wait. Not a crumb went to waste and we continued to eat pieces with coffee until gone. Of the 4 cakes I made for Christmas, this was my favorite.

To those of you who love this fruitcake, great. I would hesitate to call this fruitcake, it's more like cake with fruit. I'm used to a ( much) greater depth of flavor. I doubled the spices and peel and it's still just a shade of what I was expecting. Honestly, I'm perplexed. Usually NYTimes comments are reliable; how can there be so many raves over this?
If you want a tea cake with dried fruit, make this one, sure. But if you want real fruitcake, look further.
Induction? Cast Iron.  Non-stick? Cast Iron.  Perfect crunchy bottom? Cast Iron.  Hubby gets back from the bar too late? Cast Iron.

You rock, BettyJo! I laughed so hard, I cried!
Plus, I agree with you 100% on all points!

Mixing up the potatoes, garlic, butter, and sour cream inside the skillet was a headache. Do yourself a favor and mix it up in a separate bowl. Yes, it's one more dish to clean, but saves you getting potato mash all over the stove, and everything gets mixed up much more smoothly.

My husband who is Swiss has been making this for years. It’s a Swiss classic called rosti. He always uses Yukon golds which he boils the day before and the next days he uses a box grater. No crème fraîche. He cooks it all in one big potato cake in the pan using butter and olive oil. It’s light as a feather. In Switzerland it’s often served with a fried egg and a slice of ham on top. It’s a very special treat.
@saundra he grates the boiled potato pieces? Or does he boil them whole and then grate them?
I would think a cast iron skillet would be good. It's not considered "nonstick," but I think it would give the bottom of the cake a nice crunchy texture. I'd love opinions on that idea.
I don't entirely trust my cast iron to be non stick, but I'm willing to try it for this recipe. Maybe some extra ghee.
The intro suggests a " very well-seasoned" cast iron skillet as an alternative.  I agree that cast iron would be the way to go here!
It's one of the options that she gives at the beginning of the recipe.
Save energy. Microwave the potatoes until soft, probably 10 minutes.
I made this with olive oil and omitted butter.  They were tasty and very good.
like that idea!
Safety note: Teflon pans are generally not oven-safe above 350 degrees. If  you haven't put your non-stick pan in the oven before, check to make sure its coating can withstand the temperature.

We made this last night to go with beef short ribs. It was delicious -- but four of us ate the whole thing with no leftovers. I don't know if we had especially big appetites, but this seems a little skimpy for 8 people...
Made a halved recipe tonight. Really good even thou mine didn't have the same even brown crust as shown in the picture. I was using an 8"cast iron pan for about 1.75lb russets.
Do you think I could make it with sweet potatoes and leave out the garlic, replacing it with cinnamon, brown sugar and butter?  Perhaps pecans and bourbon as well.
For dairy-free and vegan, Tofutti makes a tasty vegan sour cream.

A Suggestion: perhaps the cooks who don’t want to make a recipe because they think there is too much sugar or fat could just move on to their fav low fat or low sugar recipe and spare the rest of us their negative comments. If you’re not making the recipe anyway, why comment? The rest of us like real food and if you practice moderation and exercise some real butter and sugar is really ok.
Never microwave a potato. Inconsistent results. Instant Pot pressure cooked "baked" potato at 15 minutes. Perfect everytime.
Great recipe!!!! It's a keeper! No changes, we loved it. Served 3 of us, no leftovers.
70000/10 would make again. No notes.
You can also steam whole, unpeeled potatoes in the Instant Pot for about 11 minutes. Put them on the trivet with water underneath.
Six tablespoons of butter? I get heartburn thinking about it.

That’s only 1T butter per spud. 
If you go out to eat you’re being served a lot more than that.
The recipe yields 8 servings, so 6 Tbsp seems like a temperate amount of butter to me.

I’ve made the recipe before and it’s good. However can get similar results using leftover mashed potatoes or boiling potatoes and smashing them up putting them into a buttered skillet. Place in hot oven for 15-20 minutes
I have an induction stove. What kind of pan will go on the stove is non-stick and we’ll go in an oven at 400°?
If your cast iron is well-seasoned, it should work.  You might have to pry it out a bit.  If you don't have cast iron, I'd probably put a light layer of oil on a stainless steel pan.
My ALL-CLAD D3s work. Induction friendly and will handle up to 500°F in the oven. Pricey though
I have the Wirecutter recommended Tramontino non-stick skillet, which Wirecutter says is ove. safe to 400 degrees, so I'll try it here.

My husband doesn't like garlic in his potatoes so I used onions and scallions instead. About the same amount as the garlic. Came out nice and crunchy on both sides. I did not use a cast iron pan just a non-stick oven proof skillet.
garlic in mashed potatoes is probably the best thing ever
Think keeping most of the skin would good add to the texture.
We love the skins…. Any reason against chopping them up and including them?

I doubled the garlic, left the skins in, halved the butter, did not fold in any cream, only lightly folded in shaved Parmesean, and baked for 15 minutes at 450, after lightly buttering the top.  Crunchy and delicious. (And looked like the picture)
I don't have a "nonstick, oven-safe skillet" or a cast iron pan (too heavy). Any suggestions for substitutions?
Properly tempered cast iron IS the ultimate nonstick pan.

I don't think there is a substitution for an oven-safe skillet, whether nonstick or cast iron.

Maybe try a different recipe?

@Naima, how about preparing the potatoes through step 4 in a skillet and then carefully transfer potato mixture to oven safe (oiled) casserole dish that's a similar size to your skillet before baking in step 5? Another option: prepare through adding sour cream (half-way step 4) and put mixture in casserole dish and adjust baking if needed to brown bottom.  That should work and allow you to enjoy this recipe, which sounds amazing. Will update when I try.

This was delicious exactly as is! I followed instructions completely,  minus the chives because I didn't have them.  When it first came out of the oven, my cake did not appear to hold together well,  but after sitting out to cool it solidified perfectly. It didn't look very pretty to me, but all of the guests at my Thanksgiving meal asked about it and there was very little leftover. I would make this again for sure!

My mom made this with left over mashed potatoes- added a beaten egg or two, omitted garlic.  Cast iron pan, plenty of butter and a bit of neutral oil. Sprinkled with kosher salt. She served the delicious, crunchy on the outside, creamy on the inside, potatoes with sour cream.  Ultimate comfort food.
I made this and it was delicious. I used a cast iron pan and sour cream. Next time I’d rather try it with the crème fraîche. But I could not make the crunchy bottom come out like the photo!

I always make mashers the same as in her step 1. For just 2, I scrub 2 large russets, then bake on the lowest rack of my large toaster oven at 350° for 1 1/2 hrs. I just scoop out the flesh from skin into a large bowl and hand mash (we like it with lumps remaining). I always stir in grated cheese to melt (often sharp cheddar), then I add butter, sour cream, chopped green onion and fresh garlic. I'll definitely try sautéing my add-ins in the butter, then frying and roasting. Looks amazing.

Tried this recipe twice, following instruction to a tee. Both times were failures--the results were not-bad tasting (better with creme fraiche than sour cream) but neither time did the cake adhere when I turned it out. And the texture wasn't anywhere close to the tater-tot crust shown in the image. Disappointed given that it's a fair amount of work for what ended up being a pan of chunky mashed potatoes.

It was tasty. A nice change from regular mash but I had a few issues. 
The mix was never liquidy. The idea that it would "lose it's liquid" had me confused. I had no idea how to tell when the bottom was brown. I ended up flipping it onto a plate and it was getting too brown in some spots and not brown enough in others. 
Once in the oven. I could not get an even browning. I stuck it under the broiler to brown and that worked out. Next time I think I'll cook it all in the oven.

I used russets as instructed, but I think a more flavorful and creamier potato would make this better. It was fun to make, but not a huge step above garlic mashed potatoes, which I love to fry as leftovers. Would likely do that instead next time: make the potatoes the day before and just fry patties.
Cast iron worked great, and the recipe was a hit.
I wanted to like it and maybe I messed up following the recipe, but not worth it overall. Still edible of course, it’s still potatoes! But too much work for not a worthwhile payoff.
Poke your potatoes first so they won’t explode in the oven like mine did.

It never browned, didn't get crunchy, and eventually just burned black in the pan on the stove (was waiting for it to lose it's moisture). In the end it was still soggy (from the oil) and also burned. Not sure how I completely messed this entire thing up, it seemed pretty simple, but it was awful.
Made with leftover mashed. Added one egg and heaping spoonful of flour. Added parm and cheddar. Used dried garlic instead of fresh.

This recipe is a good “base,” but like many of the other reviewers I put my own spin on the dish using some ingredients I had on hand. I included an entire yellow onion, fully caramelized, and a large heap of shredded sharp cheddar cheese. I also gave it 5-10 additional minutes in the oven   a minute under the broiler to get extra crispy edges all around.

Chopped parsley or cilantro are a nice, but simple addition to this recipe. Sauteing finely diced red bell pepper with the garlic, before adding the potato works well, too. Yes, it changes the flavor profile, but as it is, the recipe is a somewhat blank canvas.
Garlic goes in essentially whole? And stays that way in the finished dish?
Says peel and smash? I would imagine minced would work as well.

A solution to the problem of making mashed potatoes at the last minute to serve hot.  This seems to me to be a cook's little helper.  I am trying now, but using potatoes I had already boiled, and with peel still on.

As written, this recipe is a mashed potato kugel. My mother made something similar but smaller with leftover mashed. If you are looking to go dairy free you can make a kugel or giant latke. Shredded or grated potatoes, 1 egg per lb of potatoes, garlic powder, salt, pepper and either flour or matzo meal to bind (I prefer matzo meal). Add fried onions if you dare. I cook the same way. Fry on the stovetop till brown then flip. Get it a bit more brown then into the oven in a pie dish.
Has anyone tried this approach using cauliflower instead of potatoes? Cauliflower does so well as a substitute for potatoes when mashed, so I wonder.
KK I think mascarpone cheese could be lovely instead. The texture is thicker though; more like cream cheese. I like the Trader Joe’s brand a lot. I wonder if anyone else has tried this.

4TBLS of Butter is enough
Mash garlic in comel with 1/2 tsp of salt and a tsp of olive oil
Fry garlic medium heat in butter 2 to 3 mins.  NO LONGER.  it will continue to cook to a nice golden brown.
Cook potato mixture 5-8 mins - NO LONGER.  AVOID BURNING.
Has anyone tried substituting Greek yogurt for sour cream?
Would it be terrible to simply microwave those potatoes and save and hour?
I don't think so I actually just cut and boiled my potatoes and roughly mashed them
Calphalon nonstick is oven safe to 450. Also this is like a mashed potato kugel.
Has anyone added cheese, like gruyere or cheddar?

Sooooo good just as written.  Served 4. Two questions:
1. how might you scale this up for a crowd?
2. would it work with leftover mashed potatoes the day after thanksgiving?

I don't have an oven safe skillet. Instead I used a light weight metal cake pan, melted half the butter and garlic in it in the hot oven, took it out and put the potatoes and sour cream (mixed in a bowl) into the hot pan, and brushed the rest of the butter and garlic over the top. Baked until browned and crispy on top. It was not crispy on the bottom but still tasted great!
I'd reduce the garlic a bit.

This increasingly little old lady's carpal tunnel syndrome-affected hands can't flip a heavy cast iron pan.I opt for caution over chaos and make individual patties approx 90mm in diameter (OK - 3.5 inches for you US folk). Cook them in the cast iron skillet and flip individually using and egg slice.
I certainly don't try and do mixing of ingredients in the skillet. I mix everything in a bowl, form palm-sized balls and flatten them with an egg lifter when they're in the skillet.
Could I do this in a glass pie dish??
Great tast if don’t have a iron skillet pan use a normal pan with half a teaspoon of butter

This recipe is comfort food. I made it vegan-style with olive oil instead of butter and vegan yogurt instead of sour cream. And I boiled the potatoes before mashing, because that is the type of potatoes they were. The cast iron gave a crunchy base, which was probably the best part of all. I will certainly make this again.
Better texture, healthier and more interesting with skins on.
With onions added and skins left on was absolutely fantastic, I scaled up a bit for more/hearty eaters; concur that mixing in a bowl was waaaay easier, not in the yes, cast iron, skillet.
A hit at my Thanksgiving. Did it in a cast iron and baked it for much, much longer than the recipe stated. Also made vegan. Huge hit! Would make again for a holiday feast.

Definitely use cast iron. Tried baking this in a cake pan and the crunch was missing.

Doubled recipe, and added a package of garlic Boursin. Not a crumb left.
It completely fell apart, although still delicious. Perhaps I should add a beaten egg or a bit of flour?

I've not made this yet but have a few comments of a more general nature on the recipe:
1) Microwave the potatoes! - Easy and quick.
2) Fold the creme fraiche or whatever into the potatoes in a large bowl.  Scrape the mixture into the skillet and continue with the recipe.  This will help maintain the non-stick aspect of the skillet.  The creme fraiche will not be all up on the sides of the skillet or in lumps and streaks on the bottom.  I use this technique all the time.

It was disappointing ... as others mentioned, it does not come out as crispy as pictured.  It takes considerably more time than just getting a baked potato...and the result isn't a lot better than a baked potato as it is.  Would love to hear from the recipe developer on how to improve it.  I had hoped it would be good enough to add to holiday meals...but it doesn't make the cut.

Outstanding! Two changes for me next time. 
1. Reduce butter by 2 Tbsp
2. Use top shelf of the oven to better brown those potatoes
In theory, this seemed great but it was SOOOO greasy!! Used a cast iron skillet all the way which works beautifully. The flavors were good but I would cut the butter by half.

3lbs of russet potatoes made an obscene amount. I boiled the potatoes and mashed them with seasoning and milk and sour cream. I fried the mash in butter and oil then flipped it and put it in the oven. Good but not great

I made this yesterday in a fairly standard Lodge 10" cast iron skillet (very well-seasoned). Absolutely delicious. But, with 3 lb of russets, the potato cake was fairly thin (short?) at full diameter. Next time, I'll probably try doubling the recipe to get more tasty innards to go with the crispy outards. Adjust oven time accordingly.
I used natural yogurt instead of sour cream. Less calories.
Fewer calories generally means less flavor. Yogurt is not sour cream!
1/2 onion half olive oil half butter.  Some olive oil In mix.
