Lisa,
Your suggestion about adding gruyere will turn this recipe into Tarteflette, a sublime winter dish popular with skiers in the Haute Savoie of France. It is layers of cooked and sliced potatoes, sauted lardons, and Reblouchon cheese. Reblouchon is expensive and hard to find (outside of NYC) in most of the US. Raclette, Comte, and Gruyere make fine 
substitutes. Some people add garlic, sauted onions, broth, creme fraich. With a green salad and wine this is a great meal.

I made this a few night ago with tiny creamer potatoes and managed to come away cut free from the mandolin. I used a boxed stock enriched with better than bouillon base.  It was delicious the first night but even better reheated the second. Will definitely keep this recipe in the arsenal.  I bet it would be delicious with some gruyere intermingled in the layers.

If you ever buy a rotisserie chicken, freeze the carcass!  Simmering the leftover carcass in store-bought stock for an hour or so is a great way to get both the gelatin and a taste that is closer to homemade stock.  After straining, the resulting broth can be refrozen or stored in the fridge for a few days before using.  Dedicated cooks will make homemade stock, but for novice cooks and/or those short on time and money, this is a cheap and easy way to a better-tasting dish.

Please do not try to slice the fingerling potatoes bare-handed on the mandolin as is shown in the video.  They are small and slipppery and uneven and hard to palm towards the end. A cut resistant glove is essential for this recipe.
totally! I can guarantee you one thing, if you ever cut your finger on a mandoline, you’ll never use one again without a glove or a plastic guard. Everyone please do NOT use a mandoline this way!

I find it un-helpful when recipes use in-exact descriptions like "two large yellow onions," and "3 garlic cloves." Onions and garlic cloves vary widely in size. Better to use weights in ounces or grams, and volumes like cups, teaspoons, and tablespoons.

Have a friend make this recipe with two medium yellow onions and two large ones. Do a blind taste test to see whether you can tell the difference. Odds are good that you can't. And that 1/4-tsp of thyme leaves? How recent are they? In New England summer homes I've encountered spices so old that you couldn't tell tarragon from oregano. Some recipes don't have exact amounts. Maybe because the author is lazy; maybe because it doesn't matter; maybe because other variations dominate.
@Jim Brown: Criticize less and cook more and the dimensional mysteries of garlic cloves and onions will be revealed to you.

Can you make 1 big souffle without changing the time?  No, you will need more like 25 - 35 min. for a large souffle to bake through.  The author specified small ramekins for a reason though - your chances of success are greater.

Jack - Claire suggests that the reason for adding a little flour is to provide structure, not simply thickening, for the souffle. I take this to mean that the stretchy membranes that gluten produces trap air and provide a little resistance, giving the souffle an airy texture with just a bit of stiffness, i.e., structure.

To the people wondering if this can be made ahead: short answer is no. The beaten egg whites are the leavener, once you add them, you need to get it into the oven fairly quickly to get the desired loft. Otherwise you'll get a fairly dense, but I'd wager a still delicious, dessert. You could possibly make everything up to the point of beating and adding the egg whites, but if you're refrigerating the chocolate mixture, it will harden. It needs to be slightly warm to incorporate well.

Served to PERFECTION with champagne just 30 minutes after our meal (sprinkled with powdered sugar) was so much fun to bring such a wow dessert to the table. I will most definitely do this again!
Would it be helpful to use a collar when baking these? I used to do that with large souffles, but was wondering whether it would be a good idea to use them with these smaller ones.
Could you substitute a liqueur for the coffee?

Just feeling nostalgic. When I was a teen I had an intense interest in baking. I even have a part time  job at a bakery. On holidays and summer vacations I dove into every difficult and mysterious recipe out there. I still laugh at the memories of my dad being served soufflé after a hard days work. This looks fantastic. Will make it for my partners birthday.

So making a dessert souffle *IS* every bit as difficult as I thought. Especially fiddly individual souffles. I wouldn't dream of making this for a dinner party. Sounds delicious, though. Might make a batch for the 2 of us & call it dinner!

Not difficult at all! So much easier than baking, and was a HUGE dinner party hit! You just have to prepare your miss en place before you start - and follow the recipe to the letter - I didn't and found myself with soufflé ready to pour and no prepared ramekins, there's a reason that is step #1!! 
If you follow this recipe exactly you'll be done and cleaned up in less than 30 minutes and your guests will be delighted!
What would be the best gluten free flour to use?
I recommend using Cup4Cup as a good gluten free flour substitute. I used 2 tbsps of C4C and the souffles rose beautifully.

I wanted a vanilla soufflé recipe and reached out online. Liz A very thoughtfully and thoroughly replied with modification to this chocolate soufflé recipe. 

Ultimately, I used both recipes — both the chocolate and the vanilla were delicious!  Never the easiest to get egg whites just so but this straightforward recipe was a big success. 

Huge thanks to Liz and to NYT Cooking!!
Note that in the recipe it says to use a saucepan to simmer the water. Don’t! Use a pot. Just watch the video, Claire shares some great tips.
@mam She used a saucepan in the video…
I was thinking of using caster sugar but the grams are not the same.  So I wasn’t sure if I should go by weight or the number of tablespoons.
Can I easily double this?

I doubled it for a dinner party of 6. Had a ridiculous amount leftover - if time had permitted I would have prepared my large single soufflé dish and had an entirely separate soufflé! Instead I just had to throw it all out. I think she is either using very large single ramekins in the video or nobody actually checked the amounts... but doubled would have easily made 12 or 14 individual ramekins, so I think the recipe saying it serves 4 is super conservative...
I’ve been trying to get up the nerve to make this for a month. Watched the video several times. Read the recipe at least ten times. I shouldn’t have worried. It was perfect.
Dumb question: But can any part of this recipe be made in advance? Sadly this doesn't seem do-able for a dinner party, unless one has an actual cook in the kitchen!

I made this for New Years Eve. Huge hit! Easy!;
1.I made everything complete and in ramekins 30 minutes before my guests arrived, sat on a baking sheet on a side counter for approx two hours prior to putting in the oven, rose perfectly, so no issues with making ahead.
2. The recipe is for four, my party was for 6. I doubled it and had SO MUCH LEFTOVER. I think the recipe as written can probably serve 6.
3. It would have been really nice to have a creme anglaise over the top. Next time!

I made this as directed but I think the video that Claire did made me follow the directions a bit better than just the written instructions. also, I wasn't able to quite make four full souffles. I made about three and three quarters. next time I make this, I will have to ensure that I don't fully incorporate the egg whites quite so much.
Is this supposed to be salty?  Ours was really salty for some reason. We used the correct measurements.

Give the video editors/directors a bonus. Unlike so many NYT Cooking videos, this one focuses on the technique -- both the chef explaining it and the director making sure to show the whole process. No stupid jump-cuts that leave the viewer unsure how the chef began and how she finished.

As always, Clarie’s recipe is fail proof. 
Since there were only me and my partner, I used half the ingredient called for. Worked well  ♡

stats.Total Time: 1 hour 20 minutes, plus proofing and cooling

comments.commentBody:
The timing listed is deceptive to someone not used to making bread. Clock time is in the range of 4 1/2 - 7 hours. Maybe this one calls for listing active time and clock time.

1-2 hours for preferment. 
Maybe 1/2 hour for mixing and kneading. 
1-2 hours for rise. 
1/2 hour to braid and add wash. 
1 1/2 -2 hours to proof. 
35-40 minutes to bake.

@Deborah Kaplan.  Are you serious ! your not a bread baker by your own admission and your 
critiquing  and tweaking the recipe !
Practice
practice and then more practice !

@Deborah Kaplan, I agree with your comment, as a new bread maker I trusted the time listed… didn’t read the whole thing. Now that im actually doing it I’m realizing this is taking lots of time…. And this bread was supposed to be my breakfast hahaha!

@Deborah Kaplan 100% it should include the entirety of time. I AM a regular bread baker (sourdough), and when people ask me about the process, I always mention that it is at least a half day process, even though the hands-on part of it is maybe just an hour or so. The resting and rising times are integral to the recipe, so why the author didn’t include them in the overall timeframe, I am at a loss.

Claire this challah is superior to any recipe I have ever made or tasted. I have been making challah at home for over 55 years. The flavor is excellent as well as the crumb and the smell of the bread baking brought my family and dogs into the kitchen. I was a little hesitant using olive oil, but the flavor was perfect. This loaf was gone before bedtime! I am sure my grandfather, who was a baker in Manhattan in the 1920's to the 1940's would approve. Thanks for the YouTube video as well.

I often like to prep the challah the night before, put it in the fridge, then proof it and braid it and bake it day of.  Do you have any recommendations on how to do that?  Would the best way be to put it in the fridge after Step 7 then pull it out of the fridge for a couple of hours before step 8?  How to know when the dough is back at an appropriate temperature/state to divide and braid?  Thank you!

This is addressed in Step 13: "Alternatively, before proofing, you can refrigerate the dough overnight, but omit the egg wash and make sure it’s covered (plastic should cover it loosely but be sealed around the pan so the dough doesn't dry out)."

@AKCuisine when it says “Alternatively, before proofing,” does that mean you can refrigerate overnight instead of proofing, or that you can refrigerate it overnight, then shape it, then do the egg wash and proceed with proofing as written. I can’t tell if the refrigerating takes the place of the proofing or just gives you a break point before proceeding with proofing. Thx!

There is a crucial step omitted! What makes bread “challah” is not the shape, the ingredients, or the taste (it can be any kind of bread, even matzo!). It’s “taking challah”: pinching off a tiny bit of the dough to be set aside as commanded in the Torah (Numbers 15:18-21). That piece is really the “challah.” Observant and many non-observant Jews do this today. https://www.myjewishlearning.com/article/ask-the-expert-taking-challah/
The quantity of flour in this recipe does not reach the minimum necessary for the taking of challah to be required with or without a blessing, so it’s moot unless you’re doubling it.

I'm famous for my challah, which takes 3 hours, start to finish, so I was curious to try this one. It is indeed quite wonderful, and next time I will probably do some of the rising in the fridge overnight so I'm not stuck in the kitchen. The only thing I question is timing for the final proving of braided loaf -- after only 75 minutes, mine was over-proofed and I lost the definition in the braids. So next time I will check after 45 minutes to avoid this problem.

My daughter bake’s for challah for hunger at her Hillel.  She preps for 90 loaves and then the helpers come in to braid - it is cinematic to watch her prepare the dough.  

She has two secrets- 

Mixins- sautéed onions or chopped olives - she splits the skanes and buries them in the Dough

The other secret is a little honey mixed into the egg wash

I’ve made plenty of breads with preferments and no issue, yet had an unexpected failure with this recipe.  To my delight, I figured out why and learned something new.

The sponge rose beautifully as expected, but the dough never did. Not a millimeter after 2 hours. After scratching my head as to what had killed the yeast (as seemed happened), a line in Rose Levy Berenbaum’s The Bread Bible clued me in… the honey! Apparently some honeys are so antimicrobial they can kill your yeast. I had used raw honey, which more internet-ing tells me is no good in yeast breads for this reason. 

So use your mild, pasteurized clover honey for this one… I look forward to trying this again after I get back from the store! Well, after I make a bunch of enriched flatbreads first— can’t waste all this dough!
@Sue make onion pletzel with your “failed” dough, https://share.google/V4UIPHjxycyxraybd
@Sue. I made this today for the first time and didn’t see your comment until after I started the 2nd proof.  Mine came out beautifully even though I used raw wildflower honey.
I brought this to a brunch and two guys asked me out. I give all the credit to this recipe

I prefer to proof and shape the loaves then cover and refrigerate on the night before baking. Take the loaves out the fridge a few hours before baking to rise, uncover, egg and bake. It's just easier to shape the dough when it's not cold and I've seen this technique recommended in many baking books.

@mhj  that interesting. i find the opposite to be my experience. the cold dough easier to handle, and an extra benefit, is that handling the cold dough helps warm it up faster to room temp, giving a boost to the rising time after being slowed down with the cold.
How essential is bread flour vs. all-purpose flour?

If you look at King Arthur's Challah recipe, they use KA all purpose so I think you will be okay. The protein difference between the two is 1% so not huge. Just make sure to build gluten while kneading either by hand or by machine. If you have a thermometer, when stuck in dough, it's ready/built up gluten when at 78-80 after kneading and it's ready to prove.

I wrote to King Arthur about AP vs bread flour - here is the answer Thanks for contacting us here at King Arthur Baking Company regarding using bread flour instead of all-purpose flour in our bread recipes.

You can absolutely use our bread flour instead of our all-purpose flour in our the challah recipe. You may find you need to increase the liquid to obtain a workable dough—start by adding 1 tablespoon additional water for a recipe made with 3 to 4 cups (12 ¾ ounces to 17 ounces) flour. Or app

Or approach it from the opposite direction: reduce the amount of bread flour by 1 tablespoon for each cup used. If the dough seems very sticky, add some of that bread flour back during the kneading process.

There are two main reasons we call for King Arthur Unbleached All-Purpose Flour for most of our yeast bread recipes. Our all-purpose flour is higher in gluten than other all-purpose flours on the market, so it makes a great loaf of bread. Also, it’s carried in more markets around the country

This is a phenomenal recipe and the video is incredibly helpful! The braiding really benefits from visual aid. The only thing I deviated from the recipe is I added more water to the preferment because the consistency was more dough-y than paste-y.

All that said, I was successful on my first attempt! Claire is a baking god. Thank you!
Add a little honey to the egg wash

@Toni if you want a nice dark crust, add an extra yolk to the egg wash and then egg wash twice, once after braiding and again after final proof. Contrary to expectations, the first application of egg wash does not inhibit the proof.

I made this vegan by using Just Egg replacer. It worked perfectly and even gave a gorgeous brown crust when brushed on top. I also divided the dough into two challahs for our small family. It was a perfect size for us for Shabbat. I’m so happy with this recipe!
@Bubby a very late thank you so much for this! I’m afraid life circumstances have deviated me from my vegan life, but I’m so glad to know this is a good option! I’ll try it next time.

I refrigerated preferment after an hour on the counter and took out about 4 hours later.  I warmed the preferment bowl in a larger bowl of hot tap water for about 15 minutes, resumed with making the dough and followed recipe as written.  Mixed dough in my Kitchen Aid (speed 2, for 8 minutes, needed about an extra half cup of flour).  First rise was 75 minutes, second (after shaping) was 45.  Challah was delicious, and dough was really easy to work with.

I made this for the first time and followed the recipe exactly as was written. I even watched the YouTube video which was VERY helpful. I had a problem though. The preferment rose during the first proofing but the other 2 times I proofed it, it never rose. I still baked it and it was delicious, it was just very dense. Any idea why that might have happened?

The same thing happened to me! My guess is that the yeast was pretty old or the ambient temperature when rising wasn’t high enough! For the first ride I heated up some water in a cup and placed with with the dough in the microwave (off) to heat the air up, and for the 2nd rise I boiled water and put it in a baking pan in the oven with the dough!
This is happening to me right now.  I am thinking it's too cold in the kitchen so I put the dough ball in the microwave with 2 cups of water that I boiled...it may be slowly rising...

Using this recipe I made the best challah of my baking life.  I agree that one tsp of yeast is a small amount for such a large amount of flour.  What astonished me was the amount of oven spring achieved.  I let the loaf proof for 5 hours at which time it was barely twice the size but the dough was springy and seemed ready to bake.  It really blossomed in the oven and not just at first but during most of the bake.  Excellent recipe and worth all the effort.

Proofing the sponge (ie the preferment) overnight in the fridge helps to build the yeast flavor up. Doesn't work nearly as well if the entire dough is prepared and refrigerated (it gets oddly wet and the yeast doesn't grow nearly as well in the final proof). Also want to note that you can taste the olive oil and honey, which taste lovely and is somewhat untraditional, but may not be for everyone. I'll definitely be playing around with this recipe, it's very easy.
It would be nice if the ingredients section had the water portions along with the other ingredients instead of having to pick it out of the text.

1-2 hours for preferment
Maybe 1/2 hour for mixing and kneading
1-2 hours for rise
1/2 hour to braid and add wash
1 1/2 -2 hours to proof
35-40 minutes to bake

Used this recipe to make two smaller loaves, rather than a single loaf, a spiral round and very simple plait. One had added fresh rosemary (1tbs) whilst the plait was plain, topped with sesame/rock salt. Taste & texture were lovely.

First rise, I left the dough in the fridge over night. The final proof took around 1hr15mns

Although there's a lot of time spent waiting for the yeast to do its thing, this is really very straightforward & the video v helpful.

This was my first time making challah and even trying it as there’s no existing Jewish community where I am from and I knew I could trust Claire because this is without hesitation the best bread I’ve ever made (and I’ve tried to do lots of different breads over the last years)

It’s almost just like the one from her video for the look and texture. I guess I’ll be the one introducing a piece of Jewish culture in my family because I can’t not make this for holidays and gatherings.

I made this recipe last week, and I'm planning to make it again this week. The bread is delicious, the perfect texture, perfectly sweet and yeasty - everyone loved it. The only thing I changed, is that I made the preferment and let it rise, added the dough and refrigerated it overnight. That way, in the morning - I made the shapes, let them rise for about an hour with the egg wash and baked them. I recommend doing the same... Hope it was helpful!

Challah  bread. 
Tuna fish. 
Stop with the redundancies.

I made a test batch of this challah before baking it for my family Rosh Hashanah meal. I’m glad I did because I found it profoundly disappointing. Very little taste, not enough salt, and two heavy. It’s back to Joan Nathan for me!
I really think there just isn’t enough yeast. The flavor is wonderful, but the crumb is very tight, even with the longer rising times.
It came out beautifully! I am so happy with this and the lack of sugar and bad oils and margarine. Highly recommend this recipe!
Challah bread? To differentiate it from challah meat loaf and challah fried dumplings?
This loaf looks absolutely beautiful! Warm water to start is very helpful! Great recipe here.

Using a different kind of yeast may be the solution for slow or incomplete proofing. 
Instant yeast (e.g., SAF red/instant) rises somewhat more quickly than active dry yeast and results in a higher rise (less dense). It also avoids the initial step of proofing the yeast.

However, a better yeast for sweet breads is SAF GOLD. it's osmotolerant & works esp well in recipes where sugar weight = or > 10-12% of flour weight, as in this recipe. Go to King Arthur, "learn", for more info about this.

I used this lovely recipe for the third time on Sunday.  I used jumbo eggs instead of large.  I think the preferment appreciated the zaftig eggs.  I also used a smidgen more than 1/3 cup olive oil.  That made for a tender, slightly tacky dough.  
For a sweet egg wash, I use 1 jumbo egg, 2 tsps. good quality vanilla extract, 1 tsp. honey.  We like sparkling sugar on our challah so I shake it liberally on the braided loaf before baking.
Thank you, Claire!

when in the kneading phase, I cannot get the dough to become smooth or pass the windowpane test after 10, 15, or any number of minutes. it stays bumpy and easily tearable forever. I'm following the recipe exactly. what can I try?

If you are like me and are baking for a household with divisive opinions on raisins, you can use this recipe to make 2 smaller challahs so that you can make one with and one without raisins. Divide the dough in half and add 36g soaked and dried raisins to one half of the dough. Proof each dough ball in a separate bowl - otherwise follow the recipe as written. Bake time will be slightly shorter (around 30 minutes) but use the same doneness indicators. Love this recipe!!

I have been making, and trying, challah recipes for decades. This is the absolutely best recipe and challah I have ever made!
The steps and multiple rises make this recipe foolproof. 
My husband, who has always crinkled his nose whenever I make challah because he is a self described disdainer of challah, declared this the best he has ever had - and proceeded to eat two huge slices! 
This will be my only challah recipe from now on.

Such an absolutely delicious and beautiful loaf! The video helps tremendously. Agree with the other comments - even with using the proof setting in my oven this was an all day Sunday project (I think 8 hours from start to eating)

You don’t egg wash your dough before you proof it. That’s just plain wrong!! When your challah is ready to go in the oven is when you put on your egg wash. I almost never comment but couldn’t let this slide. I noticed this in by someone but thought this should not be buried at the bottom of a long comment.

I am not a baker but this bread may make me one. I loved making it. I live in a cooler climate and it's winter here so the total proofing time was about 7 hours or a whole day. The bread is slightly sweet but not overpoweringly so. Wonderful with butter. Would be good with soup or chili. Definitely going to make more and double the recipe so I can share with my friends.
Adding a tiny bit of honey to the egg wash and using good quality olive oil (like Graza) amps this up to a 10/10

I have made the same challah for 20  years and have varied sometimes out of ease or curiosity. I watched the video -super helpful! I made this for Rosh Hashanah - a hit! Tonight I’ll braid it. Timeline I used (since now I work a FT job):  preferment at 5 pm; add ingredients at 6:45; knead and leave for 2 hours; shape and put into fridge overnight w/o egg but covered well. Pulled out for 2 hours before baking (at 5:30 am; slid onto room temp baking sheet) - turned out perfect! Done before work!

I didn’t have enough honey, so I Supplemented with sugar and malt barley - might’ve been the reason why my dough was way too sticky to knead. I ended up having to add nearly a cup worth more of flour, but the bread came out very well! 
I was surprised at how much olive oil it was once I started pouring. Unfortunately, I find the olive oil flavor too strong for the bread so I might use a more neutral flavored oil next time. It tasted great as french toast the next day!

I have been making this challah for the past 10 months and every time I make it I bless Claire Saffitz. Even though it takes about 6 hours of various rising times, the result is spectacular in terms of crumb texture and taste. Once again, thank you Claire!
Great recipe. Why did the video editor speed up the braiding demonstration? That’s the most important and difficult part when the process becomes confusing, especially for new bakers.
Can one use a sourdough starter instead of the yeast sponge?

The easiest way to do this using the fridge is to let it rise,  gently deflate, and then stick it in the fridge over night.   Next day, take it out,   braid it,   and let it proof and then bake.  this is much easier than refrigerating an already braided loaf - especially in a crowded fridge or if making multiple loaves.

After four years of making Melissa Clark olive oil challah every Friday I tried this recipe toda and I’m blown away. I followed it to T and got a gorgeous giant challah with the silky crumb, just like Claire describes it… and the flavor is sublime. I can’t believe it’s only one teaspoon of yeast. May use a bit less honey next time, I find it a bit sweet.

I borrowed a tip from Adeena Sussman's challah recipe and added a little less than 1/8 tsp turmeric to give the bread a richer yellow color.  After years of store-bought LarOmme, my family is used to that color.  The turmeric does not affect the flavor in that tiny amount.

@Theresa Levy
About that amped up yellow color - at what stage did you add the turmeric?

I made mine babka style and stuffed it with Texas bbq chopped brisket, fried onions and spicy bbq sauce. It was amazing! Great bread, great texture and taste. The slight sweetness of the bread worked really well with the spicy meat mixture. This was a lot of work but provided a truly unique taste experience.
as a long time NYT baker, this is the worst challa recipe especially for 1st time bakers..I have been making calls for 40 yrs and this is by far the most confusing recipe I have ever seen.

This recipe is time consuming but so worth it.  Came out fabulous.  Only thing I’d say is to make sure you get enough tension on your twists/braid.  I did the round twist and it was a little too loose but still super!  Just a note for next time.

After never having my challah turn out correctly, with dough that was too wet or yeast that made it too big, finally a recipe that resembles my sourdough bread making with ingredients in weight. I made it and it turned out wonderful. Perfect texture, gluten, flavor, crumb. My only problem is it’s too good and I don’t want to share it. My other challahs I always left as a gift for the hostess, now I want to bring it home with me. Thank you so much for the video & recipe!!
The challah wouldn’t roll. Not sure what about several hours of work makes it good for beginners. My dough wouldn’t roll out and just sprung right back into a ball.
If you notice in the video, she squeezes the dough into a long shape before starting to roll it. And even hers wasn't a smooth roll. But the texture smoothed out as it rose in the proofing.

Genuinely divine. The best bread I have ever made myself - and the best bread I have ever eaten. The texture is totally unique and the flavour so complex owing to the preferment (I let it rise slowly overnight in the fridge). I also used raisins and this was the crowning ingredient. Thank you Claire!!

78 degree ambient temperature and the dough didn't rise as expected even at the maximum time suggestion. The loaf rose a ton in the oven but was slightly dense. Issue with the yeast? Honey? Not sure, but it still tastes good. Great explanation of the braiding, too -- ended up a beauty.

Bread came out beautifully golden, with a tight crumb and very mild, non-eggy flavor. But boy, it was a lot of work. So much proving! I started it at 10 am and we ate it at 8:30 pm. It was delicious fresh and even better as toast the next day.

I agree  with  Deborah 
Times are way out at least 7 hours 
For all stages. 
But worth  the effort

Question about an initial fridge/overnight rise (before shaping): In a similar challah recipe from Claire's book she recommends allowing the dough to complete its first rise and then refrigerating it up to overnight. I have always mixed my dough and simply thrown it into the fridge to rise slowly and overnight. Would this work here, or is this dough too enriched for a proper fridge rise?

Thank you!

Thank you for weighing all your ingredients including the water and for explaining the difference between the two popular Kosher salts and table salt. These are two common areas where home bakers can get confused.

This challah is superb, though the risings took longer than 2 hours. I made round challah following Claire's shaping instructions, but instead of raisins, spread the dough with a thin layer Trader Joe's fig butter. The bread was delicious!

For a novice challah baker who would love to make my own every week vs buy it (especially because, after moving from NY/DC to The Woodlands, TX, it is difficult to find challah to buy here - and you must ask for it by its local name - CHA-LUH), is this recipe one where you can make extra dough and then freeze at some step (perhaps after braiding?) to ensure home baked challah every Friday without having to make it each week? As a busy lawyer, doing this process every Fri is just out of the Q!
I make a double recipe (a family one that is close to this but includes no oil) every 6 weeks or so, make 6-8 small braided loaves, and freeze them.

David. I made it up to Step 8. Formed it into a round loaf (you could also braid it) and put formed loaf covered (lightly oiled plastic wrap) in the fridge overnight. I took it out and let it proof probably 2.5 hours.
Can this be adapted for whole wheat? If so, how?

Instead of raisins, I used chopped up cheese and charcuterie board leftovers, chopped parsley, olives and green onions, tossed with a bit of the flour meant for dough and good grind of pepper. Mixed in per raisins instructions, was worried that the extra 1.25 cups of add ins would screw it up, as I really kneaded it thoroughly. Holy cow, it turned out incredible! HUGE, fluffy, flavourful, with perfect texture, just like the video! Hubby now thinks I’m a baking goddess, lol. Thank you!

I love the use of a preferment, but don't understand the discrepancy between the printed recip &the video regarding the amount of water.  The ingredients list for the preferment calls 1 1/4 cups water yet the directions only call for 3/4 cup water for the preferment.  What happens to the other 1/2 cup?  In the outstanding Video Clair adds a total of 1 cup of water to the preferment and says that this is the total amount of water for all the dough.  What am I missing?
the 1 1/4 cups refers to the bread flour, not the water - the water isn't listed at all in the ingredients, which threw me too a bit!

I made both the Challah and the Cinnamon Babka and both turned out beautifully as well as delicious.  I really appreciated the video tutorial as this was the first time I had tried a six braided bread.  Two tricks I learned which were very valuable were: #1) soaking raisins before adding to bread and #2) using the windowpane testing method to judge if the dough was properly kneaded.  I liked this recipe so much that I made a second batch of babka to give away as a gift!

I tried making this yesterday - my preferment was gorgeous, bubbly, fluffy, but my loaf never rose despite proofing for four hours :'(
NO idea what went wrong.
I sent my husband out for more yeast, and he came back with "Rapid Rise Instant Yeast Fast Acting" that says it doesn't need water and can be added to dry ingredients - OY! Will this work?

If you are used to the Forkish no-knead method, the process here can be something of a shock.

My advice: be patient. The dough didn't rise much, the loaf didn't get much bigger in proofing, and the braiding was next to impossible. I couldn't make those long strands; they kept bouncing back. But somehow I managed to come up with something that sort of resembled a braided loaf, and the result out of the oven was magnificent. I still can't believe how it came out.

Claire Saffitz recipes are great with their detailed instructions and multiple checkpoints. The preferment as mixed obviously had too little water, but her description of what the texture should be enabled me to correct. After a night in the refrigerator the loaf proofed for almost 6 hours. But it came out well.

Well, I did the best I could.  First attempt at challah (or bread, for that matter).  The braid was a little uneven (but that made it look very homemade), but it was fairly dense.  The dough felt pretty sticky on the last proofing, even though I let it go for 2 1/2 hours.  I'm not sure if it was proofed enough at the other passes either.  I would like to try again but it's hard when you're not sure how to adjust.  Perseverance!  And happy new year :-)

I have made challah for years. Never have I made one that took such an incredible amount of time nor had a recipe that didn’t specify that this would take an entire day to make. No way is this for a first-time bread maker! While it smelled delicious coming out of the oven and looked beautiful, all at my holiday dinner tonight agreed that it was totally devoid of flavor. It was as bland and blah as could be. I won’t go to the trouble this took to make ever again.

My Grandma Tobye's directions see the link below. 7. Add the egg mixture to the flour and yeast and stir to mix. When mixture forms a soft, cohesive ball, Mrs. Hollander said, “then comes the revolution.” Dig in with both hands and “knead away for dear life, up and around, down and outwards. Pat it gently and say a prayer.” Mrs. Hollander does this in the bowl. https://timesmachine.nytimes.com/timesmachine/1971/05/12/82006988.pdf?pdf_redirect=true&ip=0
Excellent recipe.  I’ve made many challah and this truly is the best. Needs no tweaks at all. Only issue is that it is huge. Next time I will halve it but that doesn’t really count as a tweak
Under proofing is real! Great recipe but am gonna try again and double the proofing times for each step.

This is, by far, the best challah I’ve ever made! I was skeptical about the olive oil, but not any more! The dough is the perfect consistency for shaping, and the taste and crumb are fantastic. I made 2 decent sized loaves with each recipe. The only thing I warn is that it takes a very long time to rise. Only 1 teaspoon of yeast for an enriched dough is not a lot, so be patient. It was probably 6 - 7 hours from start to finish. Also, it gets brown fast, so watch and tent with foil if needed.

I made two batches of challah - this one and another that is my standard challah go-to recipe.  This was AWFUL.  Heavy, didn't raise enough and will never make again.  I would have attributed this to a problem with the yeast, but I used the same yeast for my other challah and it came out perfectly.  This was just too complex and not worth the effort.  I did think that using honey was a great idea which is why I tried this recipe, but I'm unsaving it!
I had instant yeast but it worked out well, everyone loved it!

I discovered if your round loaf is high enough it requires about 5 - 7 more minutes (beyond 40) of bake time than the six strand form.  The first loaf I made was underbaked even though the crust color was dark golden.  I checked the temperature of the second loaf just to make sure and covered it with foil to prevent over-darkening in that last few minutes.

I’m an experienced baker, usually use sourdough, but didn’t have the time needed (takes an extra day), so I made my Rosh Hashanah challah using Claire’s recipe (no raisins). I followed it to the letter and it came out perfectly. I watched her video, which was very useful to see what she is observing at different stages. I put the preferment in my oven with the light on and door slightly ajar to speed the time for that. I used a different braiding technique (Jake Cohen’s 8 strand round weave).

As a beginner this was an excellent recipe. Clear instructions with tests at each step of the way. The only problem was that I baked it for 35 minutes before checking and the crust was too dark.  But it was perfect inside. Next time I’ll check at 20 or 25 minutes.

An outlier here. Although a huge fan of CS, this wasn’t my favorite Challah. I thought I could taste the olive oil, which was off putting. I will concede that perhaps it was the oil, I’m at our vacation home & I don’t have access to my “good” olive oil. I missed the richness of butter, and since I don’t have any dietary restrictions, I’ll go back to my old standby. I did like all the technical advice & techniques, and love the community comments.

Excellent recipe, watch the video so you can follow along and it’s more detailed. Make sure you weigh out everything that can be weighed. I have a convection oven so I did 325 degrees at 35 mins. Came out PERFECT, it’s my first time making bread ever and my 6 braid challah was impressive to say the least. Excited to try more bread recipes now!

I've been baking challah for many years, and this is the bet recipe I've tried!  The extra step of the preferment sponge was worth the extra time it took.  The dough was luxurious to work with, and the crumb of the baked bread was perfect.  I'd like to try this recipe using whole wheat flour, but I know I'll have to increase the amount of yeast - I just don't know by how much.  Any suggestions?

This was my first time making challah, and it was delicious! I would warn to be careful about proofing and to go with visual cues instead of times - my final proof took 3+ hours and probably could have gone longer, but I'm pretty new to bread so I was nervous. Can't wait to make again but try the overnight fridge rise to break up the time commitment

I made this recipe last week. I actually split the recipe and made a small round raisin Challah following Claire’s instructions. It was beautiful and tasted wonderful. With the other half I made the Cinnamon Babka. I didn’t have orange .. and it made no difference. If you make the dough the Babka is a must. I almost ate the whole loaf !!!! I highly recommend

The tip about soaking the raisins is wonderful! I’ve made lots of breads with dried fruit, and they are always on the dry side. Delicious, but dry. None of the recipes I have recommend soaking the raisins or currants, etc. I’ve done it in a couple of breads now, and it’s amazing! The breads are so wonderfully moist and light now!!
Any suggestions on how to successfully convert this to a gluten free challah recipe?
Great bread. At altitude, I would check at least 10” early to see if it’s done.

I just pulled this braided challah out of the oven.  I can’t believe I made it!  Thanks so much Claire!  I braided mine in real time with your instructional video.  I could never have done it otherwise.  Now I’ll be ready to do it again next week.  Thank you so much for taking the time to demonstrate so clearly how to think braid this beautiful bread! I can’t wait to make the twist and babka!

Lovely bread! As with all yeast breads. letting the dough 'rest' in between steps helps make the dough easier to handle, allowing the flour to hydrate and the gluten to relax so they aren't fighting you.

Agree with all the other bakers about the proofing times (it took about 8 1/2 hours in total). I eventually put the rising loaf in the laundry room for the final rise. The texture of the crumb and crust were superb but I, too, found the taste of the olive oil overpowering. However, this was my first attempt at Challah and I am so proud that I mastered the six strand braid - thank you, Claire!

I'm a beginner at-home baker, and this came out so good! The pictures make me look like a pro, and the challah tasted terrific! I wondered if the amount of dough was enough to make one each, challah and babka. So, I mixed both. I did a five-strand challah. Three plain and 2 I rolled with the same mixture but substituted raisins with walnuts and a bit of water to sub for the raisins. YUM!
For the people losing their minds over “challah bread” - it’s to help when searching for the recipe. Notice how it’s only referred to as challah bread in the title.

I’ve made this three times, and consistently hear that it’s the best challah anyone has had. It’s so, so good!

I found the best results when I let it rise overnight in the fridge and took it out 5-6 hours before baking. When I let it rise for only 2 hours, it came out very dense (but still tasty!).
Used butter instead of olive oil first time I made it, came out great both ways! Also used Ap flour rather bread flour.

I made this on back to back weekends for Easter dinner with two sides of my family. Weekend 1 I followed the instructions and baked to 190 degrees and was disappointed to find the bread was on the hard/dry side. Weekend 2 I decided to pull it out of the oven at 170 degrees. Night and day! Soft and moist and so much better. Not sure if the style at 190 is something specific to traditional challah, but I personally preferred it greatly when baked to 170.

Very delicious loaf, with caveats. Watch the video WITHOUT sound if you need help braiding (her verbal explanation will just confuse you). After an overnight proof in the fridge after plaiting, my loaf barely rose at all. It took hours in a proofer set to 90F to get a decent rise. Next time I'll try the suggestion to refrigerate the preferment instead and do everything else the next day. Despite using a strong honey, the sweetness is pleasant and the olive oil isn't overpowering.

The challah appearance is too dark. Using an oven temp of 350 for 35 minutes yields a perfect golden-brown color. Also, the proofing time is too long for a classic challah. The cross-section should not look perfect like Wonder bread but should have a few holes here and there, suggesting hominess ("haemishe" , imperfect in appearance).

I make this recipe twice a week for a family I work for. They are very healthy and requested I use half wholewheat flour so the children have some benefit from it. I chose spelt for the light sweetness and nutty flavour, I use it in the preferment and one cup in the dough. The pre-ferment is not as wobbly as with white flour and the bubbles don't rise to the top so much but stay in the centre/bottom, the texture is the same and it works the same way.
Several comments ask about how to make this recipe gluten free. I don't see any answers. That would be very helpful with celiac disease in our families.

I have made this multiple times and it is by far the best challah recipe I've tried. It is time consuming but worth it (If i don't refrigerate over night, it takes about 6-7 hours). It makes a very large challah - for Rosh Hashanah this year I decide to split the dough and make two smaller round challahs. Baked for about 25 minutes and they are perfect. This recipe hit severy time!
I actually weigh the ball of dough and then do the math dividing by 6. i do the same when I make bagels.

I brought this gorgeous challah to a Rosh Hashanah dinner. The raves didn't stop and I was even asked to give challah baking lessons! (I gave the credit to Claire.)

I made a six-braid long loaf with poppy seeds. After an experimental loaf where I skimped on the final proofing and it showed, I proofed the next loaf as directed. I made the following adjustments: 1 heaping tsp yeast; 150 g honey; 2 egg yolks & 2 eggs. It baked up high and proud, and the crumb and flavor were perfect.
the time for the recipe was much closer to six hours but otherwise loved this!

I make challah weekly. A few tricks: If you proof in a gas oven with a proof setting, it is not necessary to cover the loaf (I have no idea why, but when I had a gas oven it was  did not dry out). Adding gluten to all-purpose flour (start with about 2 Tbsp; the package wants you to use too much) will make the AP flour into bread flour—it’s gluten content that is the difference. Roll strands in a little flour before braiding to keep them from growing together while they rise and bake.
This has nothing to do with the recipe but, calling it Challah Bread is redundant. It is just, well, Challah.

Made this last night and put it in the fridge for the last proofing. Took it out this morning and it took about 2 hours to warm up and rise. Looks beautiful. I was a bit concerned because I sometimes have trouble with things over proofing overnight  in the fridge. Probably because I live at 7k ft. This is my first attempt at challah. Claire's video was great and I had no problem following her braiding instructions. Can't wait to taste it.

I’m an experienced baker, usually making sourdough bread. But needing a challah for Friday and not having time Thursday to begin a challah from starter, I used this recipe-so I could do it all on Friday. It makes a gorgeous and large challah, just the right size, wish I could add a photo. But I found it flavorless. I will add 1/4 cup sugar or honey next time with the preferment, I’m used to making a bread with a high hydration dough, so the additional honey should be ok.

I weighed the flour- this preferment was too dry
I went back and airated measured 1 1/4 cup and lo and behold   after 4 different measurements I averaged 149-150 g  with King Arthur bread flour.

finding that I held back 1 cup of flour in the final mix  and put 3/4 of that back in the flour bin
This is a beautiful bread. Weight measurements are off.

I followed all the directions exactly & would like to issue a warning that this is too little yeast for the volume of flour! i assumed this had been carefully tested but now there’s a sad braided brick of a loaf sitting in my kitchen (no rise at all!)
Excellent recipe. Added 1/3 cup white sugar in addition to the honey, and sprinkled with cinnamon and brown sugar on top before baking because my family likes their challah on the sweeter side.
Best recipe for challah I have ever tried and I've tried many. I can now end my search.

This looks amazing.  The video is the best cooking demo I've ever seen and finally solved the mystery of the six braided challah that only the late mother of a late friend ever solved.  Thank you thank you.  Will do the two braids challah this weekend. And get that perfected before tackling the six braids! It is really that inspiring.

In the oven, it looked golden and knocked like it was hollow but the bottom was burnt and the center wasn’t risen.
Beautiful recipe, Claire! Thank you for the video instructions. This will be a great edition to our first night of Chanukah this week!
Came out picture perfect. The texture, color, taste. Thank you for sharing, Claire.

This recipe yielded the largest and most perfect challah loaf I have ever seen. It was my first time ever making bread and it came out so beautifully I could hardly believe I had made it myself.
I followed the recipe exactly. No changes necessary. It’s perfect!
Oops. Never mind. I found the two different bread flour divisions.
Can I use fleischmann’s rapidrise instant yeast?

@Kerry I think rapid rise yeast would work fine. As a long time baker I recommend you use 2 teaspoons of this yeast in the preferment. Every thing will take less time if you do. Using 1 teaspoon of yeast in a recipe like this guarantees much longer rising times without an equivalent increase in quality.

Made this tonight. Used my KitchenAid, as I didn’t want to knead forever. Claire’s video on braiding was very helpful. Very pretty and tasty, making another loaf tomorrow for a friend for Rosh Hashana!
REALLY good!  Video was very helpful.

My six-strand bread came out great!  I absolutely love the texture and taste of this challah.  I will surely make it again.

One thing though, I found it difficult to roll out the 'snakes' after the first rise.  Some other recipes suggest letting the six pieces rest (20-25ish minutes) after dividing and de-gassing.   The rest relaxes the gluten, so the snakes are more pliable and shrink back is much reduced.

Classic challah is a straight-dough, mixed all at once, enriched with eggs, oil, and a touch of sugar, then given one or two rises. That approach goes back to home and bakery practice long before preferments became fashionable in modern artisan baking. Here's why: Eggs, sugar, and fat already slow fermentation. Challah’s character comes from richness and softness, not fermentation complexity. A preferment can muddy the clean, slightly sweet flavor people expect.

I'm wondering if this challah's texture is cakey vs bready. I've used Joan Nathan's recipe every week for the past year and have been happy with the resulting bready texture. I'm hoping this texture is similar.

I hope Claire realizes that she is becoming a baking legend!
I over-proofed my shaped loaf slightly as I lost some definition while baking. Next time I will keep a closer eye on it and proof it closer to 1 hour. So flavorful, moist and airy regardless. I will be baking this recipe over and over again.
This is the biggest lesson i have learned too over the yrs, to not over prove. You will get oven spring when you put your proved dough in oven for baking.
How do you know it is over proofed? (What do I look for?)
How would you adjust the liquid if one were to use half whole wheat flour?
Typically whole wheat flour requires more liquid. Look at your texture and feeling to decide.

The first day I kneaded by hand, the second day by machine. I preferred my results using the machine for 10 minutes. Thank you so much, this is my most successful Challah ever.
This Challah is delicious and worth every step. Highly recommend, and will be making it again for Rosh Hashanah.

This was my first time making challah.  My 9-year-old and I did it together.  The recipe was easy to follow, and the video tips about how to know when you were ready for the next steps were super helpful.  The challah came out beautiful and delicious!

I've made challah without measuring for 45+ yrs. My technique is very similar - making a sponge (preferment is a fancy name), then adding the other ingredients. I've always used olive oil and honey (I often use 4 eggs in the dough) But I was interested in trying this recipe and it was very good. My one criticism is on the measurements. I poured EVOO into measuring cup on the scale (tare at 0). At 1/3 c. the scale was at 36 gms. 75 gms. would have been over 2/3 c.

This is interesting to me because I used the grams for all measurements and found the bread very dense. I think the weights are off for the flour as well—1 1/4 cup of KA bread flour, according to their charts, would be 150 grams, not 167. 3 cups would be 360 grams, not 405.

AZ: To answer your question about the bread machine. Had one and sold it… I would make the preferment. Then use your machine to mix preferment and all dough ingredients, let it knead, do your windowpane test and then rise in machine for first rise. Take it out, shape, proof then oven bake if you want it braided or some shape. You can also do your shaping and refrigerate overnight as previously discussed, then bake.

Easy for new bakers?  The first line says, Make the preferment.  What is that???
@Barb. Add a dash so this reads pre-ferment

pre-ferment!  Or sponge is the more common term.  Gets the yaast going so you know it's going to work.  If it doesn't bubble here, it won't work for the rest of the steps.  If that happens, check water temp and also maybe get new yeast.  This is your fail safe step.  Hope this helps.

Hi Emily!  I'm sorry to hear that your Challah didn't turn out.  If your preferment never bubbled, your water may have been too hot or to cold (I always aim for about 110°F), OR your yeast may have expired.  I would give it another go and, if your preferment doesn't bubble, get some new yeast!  Happy baking!

I’m in no position to judge a good challah, but this loaf came out beautifully and tears off into fluffy shreds, just like the video. I’ll use this recipe again and again, playing with different flavor combinations. 

Claire gives us permission to try different types of challah: with raisins, with cinnamon, etc…in the spirit of “cooking notes” and not a review, I was preoccupied with Spanish flavors this week. I steeped saffron and a bay leaf in the water. I sautéed garlic and onion granules in the olive oil, then sieved it over 1 tablespoon of a homemade Spanish spice blend to bloom the spices. I added 4oz of shredded Manchego to the stand mixer towards the end kneading. I didn’t have any issues with the rise. I followed Claire’s directions for a six-strand braid, which I’ve never done before. I’m very proud; it’s *almost* too pretty to eat! I impatiently waited a couple hours before cutting it. I’m interested to see how the flavors develop overnight. Right now, I can’t taste the Manchego, but the other spices are coming through. The inside is bright gold, and the saffron is the most prominent. I used 1.5tsp Morton’s coarse salt; I feel like I could bump that up a tad, but we’ll see what happens overnight - if the cheese and other spices develop more.

stats.Total Time: 1 hour 30 minutes, plus proofing and cooling

comments.commentBody: I beg your pardon? Cinnamon takes a back seat to no Bobka. People love cinnamon. It should be on tables at restaurants along with salt and pepper. Anytime anyone says, "Oh This is so good. What's in it?" The answer invariably comes back, Cinnamon. Cinnamon. Again and again. Lesser Bobka - I think not
I am in full agreement.  Cinnamon is what a babka is all about.

THANK YOU THANK YOU for this most perfect babka.  Very reminiscent of my own grandmother's (and she died in 1967).  The current commercial ones have too many striations/layers and way too much cinnamon fill.  

This is what our ancestors baked.  I know there's a school of thought about chocolate Babkas -- but chocolate was nonexistent in Russia, et al, when our families came here, so it's less than authentic.  This one is BRILLIANT.  

Zei gezundt.
What size loaf pan? 9x5”? 8.5x4.5”? Recipe doesn’t specify. Loaf pan size can make a difference in the rise of the finished product.

Great recipe! I noticed the written recipe calls to form then chill the babkas for at least 4 hours, whereas in the video, Claire goes straight from forming to proofing. Since I wasn’t going to have 4 hours the following morning to proof them after chilling, I went straight from forming to proofing like the video. Came out great! Thanks!

Babka is a Polish Easter bread in shape of an overturned flower pot with deep grooves (like grandmother's skirt). The name means "old woman" or "grandmother" in Polish. It is very rich and sweet, made with eggs, milk and butter, usually studded with raisins and candied orange peel, like Italian panettone. Yiddish appropriated the name for other yeast breads. Polish babka never had cinnamon or chocolate.
https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Babka

every group in Eastern Europe had a version.  We can't guarantee that Poland invented it, there was such cultural overlap.  I agree about the chocolate -- it was never a go-to flavor in that part of the world. But to say that there's not a legitimate version in Jewish cuisine is just wrong.

This turned out wonderfully. The flavour and texture of this babka was excellent. I had to leave out the almond flour to make this school lunch friendly, and made a filling of cinnamon, brown and white sugar, cocoa and silan (date paste) and it was delicious. Orange zest would have been a great addition if I had any! I followed the instructions 4 baking time and found the babka a little overdone- but I think that was because of my dark pans. Next time I might pull them out at 30-35 minutes.

Absolutely love! I brushed nutella under the cinnamon mixture instead of olive oil, and reduced the brown sugar, and it came out like a delicious caramelized churro. Will absolutely be making this again and again!

It has a cinnamon swirl & usually raisins. Also has a cinnamon sugar crust. AND it's round!  Cheryl Ann's in Brookline had been making them, but there was a fire & I don't think they've reopened.

This sounds so much like the only "morning bun" I've ever liked, sold at a coffee shop called Pasqua in Berkeley (taken over by Starbucks years ago).  It was light and fluffy, not flaky like a croissant.  Not too sweet, but filled with a swirl of cinnamon and chopped hazelnuts.  I really miss it -- I wonder if I could riff on this recipe to make some?

This recipe is easy to follow  — just be sure to note the three rises and allow enough time for them). 
A few notes:
1) I made one in a 9x5inch loaf pan and another in an 8x4.5”.  In the smaller pan, I had to fold the dough (ie curve it around the pan. It looks very pretty when sliced as there are two sets of swirls.
2) the written recipe says dough should triple in size in the third rise. In the video it does not triple, nor did mine — rather it billows up and becomes soft, perhaps double.

Holy. Wow. Made this for Rosh Hashanah and never had a babka recipe go so well and taste so good. The dough was easy to work with and the instructions were very  clear about how to handle it. For once, no tearing while rolling!!! For the first time, I put it in the oven with OPTIMISM. 

My only regret: I didn't let it sit for at least 2 days before cutting into it. It just gets better after a day, and then EVEN better after two. I can't stop eating Loaf 2. Claire Saffitz, you're my babka hero.
If you've got a nut allergy, can you just use additional regular flour instead?

Fantastic! I adjusted rising times — the pre-ferment went longer than recommended, and the final rise was shorter. I did not put the babka in the fridge for 4-12 hours prior to the final rise. That’s just the kind of day I was having. The results were still great with a wonderful crumb. I do recommend kneading for a full 8-10 minutes, watching the video, and paying attention to the bounce/spring of the dough to decide when it’s risen enough.

Or am I missing something?
top notch savannah

Biscuiteer, that is exactly my question! Why does the photo show the short sides with the parchment? I’ll no doubt do it the way the recipe says instead of the way it shows to get the best chance of it coming out of the pan cleanly. But why the discrepancy?
We have a family member with a nut allergy. Okay to sub other flour?
Can you exchange all purpose flour for the almond flour?
I am allergic to nuts. What is a good sun for almond flour in this recipe?
So from challah bread to babka this could take about a day to make? Need to be prepared! Looks delicious.

Has anyone had success making this recipe gluten free? I have some friends coming to stay next weekend and would like to make a treat like this for my gf pals! I’ve been successful with gf cake in the past but have struggled gf bread — always in the rise or the texture!

I think it would be challenging for the home baker to make this, or any bread, gluten free since gluten is what works with yeast to make a risen loaf. Maybe use the topping with a gf cake recipe to make a coffee cake type treat?

Babka came to E Europe from Italy. Butter was never used in the dough - scooped soured milk was used (mixture of cream and soured milk on top). That was Baba... Baba then was picked up by Jewish bakers, oil and water replaced soured creamThe difference between the recipe here - the filling was always moist - honey was used as a sweetener, there were eggs in the filling and bread crumbs used as a thickener (rather than flour). It was the rich filling that was giving granny’s Jewish Babka an edge.
Is there a substitute ofr almond flour? I don't always have that on hand, it is expensive and has a fairly short shelf life.
I’m going to attempt this this weekend, but I’m worried about getting the timing right with having to wait anywhere from 1-4 hours four different times along the way! Anyone have advice for this?

Probably too late for this, but I am making one recipe of dough and making a half size round challah and one babka.  Spent several hours now, making the pre-ferment, mixing and kneading dough, letting it rise, blanching and processing almonds for almond flour, mixing cinnamon filling, forming loaves, and wrapping for refrigerator where they will sit unbaked overnight.  Remove to proof in the morning and bake before noon is my plan.  Cooled and ready for sundown.
You could finish step 4 of this recipe by the evening, and then chill the babkas overnight and do the final proofing & baking in the morning
This took me all told like 8 hours (I made the challah, not the babka). The preferment took an hour longer to be ready than the recipe said it would.

Excellent - but really triple check the timing on proofing and read through FULLY this and the Challah recipe. Might be oblivious to more tenured bakers but for a novice the proofing schedule  might shock

I made this this weekend and it is to die for!  The orange, honey, olive oil and cinnamon blend together to make a delectable babka.  My dough did not rise well after being refrigerated.  I let it sit out for 5 hours.  Not sure why, but wow, this is absolutely delicious!!!  I will definitely make this again.

I refrigerated this overnight as written (vs skipping it as in the video), and was convinced I'd messed up when after proofing 4 hrs this AM there was a sugary oily puddle in the bottom of the pan. Drained it off (a tablespoon ) and baked it anyway, 45 min in an 8.5x4.5" pan. Happily turned out very good. Next time I think I'll skip the refrigeration as others noted. For what it's worth I split the dough into a half sized challah and one babka, nice to get both!

I’ve made this recipe three times; the first time I didn’t realize that it should go in the fridge overnight (I watched the video before I read the recipe through) so I just proofed and baked the same day. It was really good albeit I didn’t finish until early evening. The second and third times, I did proof overnight and while both were good, my first attempt was better. any thoughts on this? I’m used to making sourdough and the overnight in the fridge is pretty much a must.
Not my favorite Babka recipe ever. They were bready and not enough filling. Still looking for the perfect cinnamon Babka recipe.
I added raisins to the babka and it was SO GOOD
Is the almond flour essential? Looking for a replacement ingredient because of a nut allergy. Any suggestions?
Would it be ok to leave in the refrigerator in Step 4 for more that 12 hours? I'd like to make the babka closer to 24 hours later.
I thougnt I wasn't going to be able to make it, since I have never baked bread before. But it turned out fantastic. I will make this Babka again! Thank you.

Made as written without the refrigeration after shaping.  It came out great, rose well, and had a delicious flavor.  I had never used almond flour in a cinnamon bread before, but it worked well and made the filling less sweet in a good way.  The dough was really easy to work with.  I actually made the dough ahead of time, stored it in the fridge for 3 days before shaping and baking, so that's an easy way to make in advance.

Wonderful recipe. Have made her braided challah as well & also exceptionally delicious. Added some chopped bittersweet chocolate to filling. Next time will make extra filling so there is more to sprinkle on top before baking

This is a keeper, and a great way to begin 2025!!
Thank you Claire, for this wonderful recipe.
The recipe calls for olive oil. Did anyone use extra virgin?
Love the way this came together easily.  Definitely a little dry for my taste but very tasty.

So delicious! As per the video, I skipped the refrigeration step. Proved for about 2 hours until doubled in size and then baked for 45 minutes. The loaf was light and not too sweet. My partner begged me to make it again ASAP

Can someone help me figure out what I'm doing wrong?  First try didn't get much rise.  Tried again with newly purchased yeast.  Preferment was improved, but I didn't get much rise during the resting or proofing.  Did close to 2.5 hours resting.  Again, final product tasted great but was small and probably denser than it should be.  Of note, final weight was less than expected (per directions) despite weighing all ingredients.  (I also had a hard time rolling it out - perhaps overkneaded?)
This is a wonderful recipe. Instead of 2 babkas I made a small round challah & one babka giving me the best of both worlds - bread and dessert
I use passion fruit from my garden on Saba, Dutch Caribbean, for the curd. It is a success.
@Ellen Lockhart you only use the passion fruit or also a not of lemon? You use the whole juice and seeds? I have some fresh pulp and thought of using it as you did. Any help will be appreciated

delicious and extra sour break from your more traditional christmas flavors! 

after reading the notes, i was careful to make sure my curd mixture was thickening at each point and was not disappointed in the final texture. I let the mixture bubble for more than 1 minute during the first boil for the juice/sugar/cornstarch mixture and the curd had a very thick consistency even before going into the oven. 

the flavor was very limey and tart, with minor hints of mint

I’ve been cooking NYT recipes for years, and never been disappointed until making this one. I followed the recipe to a “T”, but they never properly set (even though I added cooking time, and eventually upped the temp) - it was liquid the whole time in the oven! Even after fully chilling, they have a sticky jelly-like texture rather than creamy and luscious as hoped. The oil from the butter split and was pooling on top…the mint and lime flavors didn’t go together…and more. Do not recommend.

These turned out very sour with just a hint of mint. I love them! But they are certainly not a traditional Christmas cookie. 

Agreed with other commenters that the curd needs to be thickened longer than directed. As I told my 10 year old budding baker daughter who made these with me, it’s all about the consistency, the time they write is just an estimate. Thankfully I’ve made curd previously (a long time ago) so I knew to ignore the time and look for it to actually thicken at both points.

The flavors were very tart, which was perfect, but there wasn't enough mint. I was hoping for the flavor of the sort of limeade/lemonade crammed with fresh mint leaves you can get at some beaches. Also, sophia is spot on with allowing more time for thickening at the two points - that seems crucial. I don't think the balance of the shortcrust and curd is perfect - too much crust for me. Next time, I'll cut that to 3/4. Made meringue cookies with the extra egg whites - nice sweet balance.

THIS is the curd method my mother used to make TRUE lemon and/or key lime meringue pie in the era before popularization of today’s faux short-cut and disappointing sweetened condensed milk version that proliferates every contemporary recipe source (sorry, including the current NYT).  Can’t wait to try these bars.  Thank you, Claire!

Key Lime Pie BY DEFINITION uses sweetened condensed milk.  One (of many) sources says: "No fresh milk, no refrigeration, and no ice was available in the Keys until the arrival of tank trucks with the opening of the Overseas Highway in 1930.  Because of this lack of milk, local cooks had to rely on canned sweetened condensed milk... Key lime may be the star ingredient of the key lime pie, but it is the sweetened condensed milk that makes it so smooth and delicious."

the lime bars were super delish but got stuck in the pan! 

make sure there is no way the curd can seep through the corners of the foil and don’t be shy about buttering the foil—I wasn’t able to pull the bars out of the pan and lost a lot of them while trying to get them out.

it also developed an eggy film on the top of the curd after baking but I was able to peel it off really easily.

These are delicious. My only advice is not to use aluminum foil to line the baking dish. Even when properly buttered, it sticks to the pan. Instead, use parchment paper. It is already nonstick and works much better.

Agree with pretty much every other baker here - the curd doesn’t set even if you follow directions verbatim.  In what alternate universe does a cup of liquid (lime + lemon juice) thicken with a mere teaspoon of cornstarch?

Unlike any other curd recipe I’ve seen. Now I’m reluctant to try any other of the author’s recipes…

I made these last night and have eaten half of them they are so good.  I followed the recipe as close as I could.  But the curd did not set well, even after refrigeration.  So I think if I make them again (haha, I will), I will be sure to cool the crust more and thicken the curd more.  Finally, I need to make sure more people around to help me eat them fast!  Wow are these good.

One of the more technical key lime bar recipes I've tried. For the shortbread crust though I added the zest+sugar to room temp butter and fluffed it together, then added the rest into that. Otherwise followed the instructions, and everything came out right. This is at least 1/2 cup key lime juice more than what I'm used to, and it was way too tart from the typical key lime bar. Also uses a lot more eggs resulting in a darker bar. Next time I'll try adding vanilla and mint to key lime curd.
Can you use gluten free flour?

Rats. It's delicious but it didn't set in the oven, even though my son and I made sure to thicken things at each step. The curd was quite thick when we put it onto the crust, too! I baked for 30 minutes- still very wet. 15 more mins in hopes to stop it from jiggling all over, but it never did. By the time the top was really brown, the thing was still more liquid than solid. Now that it's been chilled, it's goopy yet very tasty. Maybe we'll eat it from out of the freezer.

The other half of the citrus mixture stays in the saucepan! You’re adding the egg   citrus mixture back into the saucepan. The reason for adding some of the citrus mixture bit by bit while whisking is to raise the temperature of the eggs without cooking them into scrambled eggs.

Be generous with the mint. The recipe says packed tablespoons so either really pack it in or do more than 2. There was plenty of mint in mine and I think it's what makes this recipe interesting. The curd will probably take a little longer than the recipe suggests and I would definitely use a thermometer. 170 is the magic number and they set in the oven after 30 minutes. These are definitely for people who enjoy sour.

This is exactly what I hoped it would be! Tart but not too tart at all. My whole family loved it and the shortbread cookie base was a nice surprise. I think I’ll chill them longer before serving to firm up the top even more. 

I echo the comments that highlight the need to take more time when boiling the curd. Take your time!

I had to cook mine in one of those foil single-use pans you can find in grocery stores because I was traveling, but it turned out great! Just buttered the pan directly and did not use extra tinfoil. Put the whole thing in the fridge to chill, and cut them after an hour. The nice thing about those pans is you can put right through the bottom of the pan and just remove that part, leaving a nice square lime bar! I also had to whisk and cook the curd for closer to 10 mins to get it to thicken.
I used a tablespoon of cornstarch rather than a teaspoon and then all the thickening directions worked and the consistency at the end was correct.

Fail. Same results as above. Curd never gelled and separated in the oven, creating pools of butter. Huge waste of six eggs. This recipe is in her book and I baked before I realized the recipe and comments were online. Hugely disappointing.

This recipe didn't work. I followed it exactly as written, including temping the curd as I heated it. The result was soupy liquid curd after 30 minutes in the oven. By the time the curd had set, after almost an hour in the oven, the crust was burnt.

I've made this recipe as written a few times and it is A    . 

A tropical variation I tried with great success:

For the crust: omit zest and mint. add 1/2 cup sweetened dried coconut flakes and reduce sugar by half 

For the curd: swap pineapple juice (I used Dole brand) for the lime juice

The shortbread base was a bit crunchier but I enjoyed that. Will be making  both versions again!!!
Just tried this variation per your recommendation - fantastic!!!!! My boyfriend said it was the best desssert I've ever made.

These were way more work than all other lemon bar recipes I’ve made, and the result was not worth it. The crust was too thick and not nearly as delicious and flaky as shortbread crusts I’ve made for other lemon/lime bars. I couldn’t taste the mint at all. As others have said, the bars are very sour. I used slightly less than 3/4 cup of lime juice (which was 11 limes for me, not 7) but it was still very sour. Overall they came out edible and not bad, but I won’t be making this recipe again.
This recipe turned out horribly. It never set and was way too limey.
I made this recipe a few months ago. I was not impressed. Not much flavor or "crunch". Won't make it again.

Step 4 says: " until you’ve added about half the citrus mixture to the eggs."

When do you add the rest of the citrus mixture? Is it blindingly obvious to everyone but me?
Claire's recipes are ALWAYS on point.  Make it! it's good. It will work, even if you suck at baking.
Follow up question as Bella - what happens to the other half of the citrus mixture? Where does it go?
The other half stays in the saucepan. When you “whisk the egg mixture back into the saucepan,” you are whisking it into the remaining half that you left in the pan while tempering the eggs.

I made this exactly as written and it came out perfectly. My whole family was wowed. If you like lemon bars, this is a great twist - the mint shortbread is really special. Just take your time and follow each step carefully.

I had the same issue with the curd not setting in the oven even with greatly increased cooking time. The curd was fairly firm when it went into the oven too. After cooling in the fridge it's still sticky and goopy. Tastes fine but I'm disappointed they turned out so messy.

After reading the comments about having to cook the curd a little longer to ensure it set, I fear I may have gone too far and increased the acidity of the curd by overcooking it.  These bars went beyond "tart" and verged on being inedible.  The mint was completely drowned out by the lime.  I'm chalking this up to operator error but am curious to check out other curd recipes to see if the ingredient proportions are the same as in this recipe.  Hope you have better luck than I had!

They weren't lying, these bars are extremely tart. HOWEVER, they are also refreshing and fun as an alternative to traditional holiday flavors which can overwhelm the season. I was tempted to make another shortbread and make a sandwich cookie out of the bars because it's that tasty. I followed the recipe almost perfectly, and even though my lime/ sugar mixture almost boiled over, had no issues with consistency.
I was told by many of my official tasters, eg my kids, that these were too tart. I liked them and found them fun to make, but not sure I'll do them again anytime soon.

This is the first Claire Saffitz recipe that hasn't worked for me, and I think it comes down to the juice to yolk ratio being way off? I cooked my curd for a lot longer than directed, and it still did not set in the oven. Additionally it was oddly a little bitter rather than the tangy I was hoping for? 

Separately I used gluten free flour for the shortbread, and if you do that add at least 6-10 minutes to the blind bake time since the gf flour needs more time.
I had the same results as Jessie. followed the recipe to the letter and ended with soup. the taste was quite bitter as well not just tart.
Each lime seemed to one tablespoon zest, so I added 3, rather than two
omg yes her citrus are huge in this video. when I went to the store I specifically planned for this lol!

My bars were TRASH! UTTER TRASH! And let’s just say I’m not willing to take full responsibility! something is surely amiss- took so long to get it to thicken even a tiny bit and by then i had reduced it by so much it was doomed to be sickeningly tart.
Same problem with the bars not setting. I made sure to let each step thicken as other reviewers commented, but alas they remained liquid after adding 20 mins to the cook time!

Glad I checked the notes. I actually dumped the liquidy syrup out of the pan and back onto the stove to cook for 5 minutes on high heat until the filling was pudding like. Then baked. Ended up with nice, tart bars. I mixed lemon, lime and orange juice and used salted butter. Recipe is forgiving and flexible— just get the filling to the right consistency!

I read everyone's notes before making this really delicious recipe. The bars are a great alternative to the recipes for much sweeter lemon desserts out there. 

To resolve the issues everyone has had with the curd thickening-- increasing the heat slowly at the second stage, and continuing to whisk, enormously helped.

Funny, my problem was the opposite to Jessie's - my curd was super thick. I cooked it for much less time (15 minutes), but the curd started boiling around the edges?! (Yes, I did lower the temperature) Also, the crust cooked really unevenly. There are better lemon/lime bar recipes elsewhere.

I've made these twice and love them! As the description promises, they are very tart—I omitted the lemon juice and subbed in more lime, which made the balance perfect for me. As other commenters have suggested, I let the curd heat at both stages for longer than recommended to make sure it set. Both times the curd has been perfect, the shortbread is slightly herbaceous from the mint, and they've gotten great reviews from my family and friends. I'll definitely be making them again!
A little tart for the whole family, but I loved the flavor. Did require an additional 10+ minutes in the oven, and even then it didn't really set very well.

I’ve made many citrus and Claire Saffitz desserts from both NYT and her book, but this was my least favorite by far. First, I see no reason to make a shortbread crust with cold butter.
