It’s way easier with melted butter for the same result. Second, the filling was too eggy and imparted an unpleasant flavor. Third, the cornstarch never thickened and the filling remained goopy. I’m a pretty experienced baker and I read about the issues others faced beforehand. I will not make these again.

Unfortunately these were not a hit for me. I've made lime and lemon curd many times before and this curd just never set. I absolutely love sour and even this was pushing the limit for me. I lined the entire dish and it was still a bit difficult to remove and forget about cutting them into the perfect squares shown by Claire. The were runny and sticky and glued your teeth together. I was hoping to bring these to a holiday get together but couldn't as I didn't want to embarrass myself.

Darn. I overcooked it. I wish I had trusted the recipe rather than the notes. I added 13 min to the cooking time because it was *too wobbly*.  But if I trusted the butter would firm as it cooled, etc. I didn’t have the trouble thickening that others had. It puffed beautifully. But it just seemed too loose in the oven, so I left it in. Now the top is brown and the shortbread is tough.

The final result was SORT OF tasty. 

The lime flavor was wonderful but was overshadowed by an eggy tang… Since baking this recipe I’ve read that the egg white is what gives that sulfurous tang. I think I will try this again but use the egg yolk/cornstarch ratios from the NYT vanilla creme brûlée recipe.

Also the mint in the short bread just made the crust taste like a pot cookie. No mint flavor - just earthy.
Made these in a pan 9x9 square springform pan. Brilliant. Baked a little too long but and therefore are super crunchy. Bake slightly less and will be perfect next time. Followed directions exactly.
These are quite tart and absolutely fantastically delicious. Made no changes. A keeper and repeater!

I did not follow her curd recipe. I use one that is way easier and has fewer ingredients. I would go with a basic curd recipe, ie, one that does not require corn starch nor egg whites. I love the "limeyness" of this recipe!
Like Jessie, I followed the recipe very carefully but the curd broke and was a melted butter mess.  So disappointed!

Fail. Followed recipe and curd never set; butter pooled and the lemon curd remained jiggly. Huge disappointment and a waste of six eggs and butter. I had much better results with Tanis’s cranberry curd tart.

it sounds like your curd was not set enough before you added in the butter. you need to cook out the eggs during the middle step while continuously whisking like a lady gone mad. it takes quite some time and I don’t think that was evident from the video. you really want it to be thick and coating the spoon like in the video, before you add butter. also add the butter SLOWLY and whisk from top to bottom of the pot making sure all of the butter is incorporated.

This is a complicated recipe and although the flavors and textures are good, the final product was not a great success.  I cooked the final step for 35 min because of the comments about a runny curd.  Curd texture was fine, but the top had a brown crust, and the shortbread was overdone.  It is a very rich, tart dessert.  If I try again, I will cook for less time.

So sour and very delicious. I failed to read the comments before and didn’t allow as much extra time for thickening as I should have. They set but were softer than should have been even with a few minutes longer at each step. Would make again for a sour loving someone.
This turned out amazingly well. I was a bit concerned after reading the notes but I had no issues with separation and the flavor was, for me, perfect.
These did not set well for me and the taste was ok - we were disappointed. I will stick w traditional lemon bars (and key lime pie bars) from now on.
So do you dump the other half of the citrus mixture?  Did I miss a step?

I’ve made a lot of curd in my day - my advice would be to make sure it is the proper consistency before you stop the stovetop cooking. It should be like pudding. Also, many readers say it’s too tart, which always makes me laugh as it’s easy to adjust that to your taste. Start by putting less lime in and then add as you see fit.

Overall if you LOVE lime I think these are perfect - definitely very tart! This is included in Claire's "Dessert Person" and she mentions that the hardest part is making the curd - which is correct. The shortbread was super easy and delicious on its own, I wanted more of it. I also feel like the recipe would benefit from a meringue on top, but I understand it is meant to be mixer-less! Ends up being a bit messy - would recommend watching her video to get the curd right.

Very important: make sure you reduce the oven temperature to 300F after baking the crust. 

I have made curd several times, and was happy with how mine turned out. So  when the bars didn’t set like so many others had mentioned in the comments, I went back to read through the entire recipe, the ingredients quantity, and the comments. 

It wasn’t until I watched the video, every step looking identical to my experience, and then she said “Oh, and this is important, reduce the oven temperature to 300… It’s easy to miss this in recipes.”  Yep, I sure did miss it on multiple read throughs. I think it would be very helpful to repeat the 300F temperature in the baking step. I am convinced this is why so many folks haven’t had their bars turn out!
A waste of 5 eggs and 2 sticks of butter. The filling never set completely even after baking an extra 15 minutes and chilling over night. Disappointing because  usually love Claire’s baking recipes.

The bars were edible but I wish I’d looked at the comments before baking as I also missed the extremely critical instruction to turn down the oven after baking the crust! The top got very brown by the time I took it out (it was boiling) and it did set, but the texture was very weird.

wow! these were a real flavor bomb! I found the juice, sugar, and cornstarch mixture needed a lot more cooking than 1 minute, I used parchment paper instead of foil, and baked the crust a few days before the curd, but otherwise pretty much followed the recipe.
Add two whole eggs instead of one to the curd

After reading the comments about the curd not setting, I tried this recipe but added an extra 1/2 tsp of cornstarch and whisked the virus over the stovetop for a very long time. This still wasn’t enough to get the curd to set, and I wound up with an overcooked crust and runny curd. I’ll opt for a simpler recipe next time, as the texture and taste from this one didn’t make the extra effort worth it.

title: Strawberry and Cream Layer Cake Recipe
author: Claire Saffitz

comments.commentBody: After an unsuccessful attempt at splitting my sponge, just wanted to say this makes a very lovely trifle.

Absolutely delicious. I halved the entire recipe (incl. that of the sponge cake) and only made one tier since we aren’t big on desserts. I reduced the sugar in both the cake and the syrup since most American desserts are far too sweet. The cake deflated a bit, but it was moist, light, and delicate. The syrup took a little bit longer to produce given the reduced sugar—but it was beautifully intense in flavor! Beautiful result, only wish the icing was lighter. Clearly inspired by Asian bakeries.

It’s delicious. But steaming the strawberries and sugar to extract clear juice is a bridge too far. I did it. Never again. Just hit the berries with sugar and a few pushes with a potato masher.  Let them sit on the counter while the cake cools. You’ll have plenty of sauce and no spent unusable berries.
@Nancy Thanks for this tip! So much easier and far less time.

It turned out wonderful! I had sponge fails before but this one turned out great. Used Mascarpone (love) and added a tbsp sugar to the cream-mix. Cake is not overly sweet, so I wouldn't cut sugar. To the syrup i added 2 tsp balsamic vinegar and 2 grinds of pepper. I have no ambitions for the clarity of the syrup, so i just boiled the sugar and strawberries for a couple of minutes and didn't strain it. I make a variation of this cake every strawberry season, and am saving this recipe for the next
Success! Besides my cake deflating a bit, even after resting it upside down, this turned out great. Highly recommend watching the accompanying video before starting.

Hi Jackie!

Quick question… did you refrigerate this at any point? Wondering if it’s possible to make the glaze or cream the day before or if the fridge causes the glaze to loose it’s beautiful translucency or if it causes the cream to fall? Just hoping I can pre make some things to make my life easier. 

Thanks!
Here's the video: https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=N4nvGZTC9RE

Skip cooking the strawberries, and brushing on syrup. Add sugar to whipped topping instead.
Split cake. Place first half, cut side up, on a serving platter. Apply a thin layer of whipped topping, a single layer of sliced fresh strawberries, and another skim of whipped topping. Place other half, cut side up, on top. Spread a generous layer of whipped topping, and cover with sliced strawberries, lightly fanned. Served at a party and was gone in minutes.
My mother made this for my birthday cake when I was growing up but she put banana slices in the middle... strawberries, bananas, & cream yum, yum.

Sharing a tip: When making the mascarpone whipped cream, you'll get the smoothest/best result if you first combine all the mascarpone with about 1/4  of the cream on medium-low speed. Continue mixing the mascarpone and cream until the mixture becomes looser and more liquid, then stream in the rest of the cream gradually. The first time I made this, I combined everything at once and got a lumpy result. The second time, I used this method and got a smooth, fluffy result!

This dessert is incredibly delicious. First off I would like to point out: this is not an overly sweet cake, so don't reduce the amount of sugar. It could throw everything off and you won't be happy with the results. Secondly, this is not a strawberry shortcake so don't try and replace the whipped cream with whipped topping. Huge mistake, in my opinion. I used sour cream and heavy cream. With the strawberry syrup, you don't notice the tanginess at all, it has just the right balance.

Lovely cake--delicious, light, and simple! I added a tablespoon and half to the cream mixture for a little extra added sweetness. I originally saw this recipe and the video on how to make it on Instagram--I recommend watching the video first if you've never made a genoise sponge cake. https://youtu.be/N4nvGZTC9RE

Instead of making the syrup, I thinned some strawberry jam and spread that on the layers. It worked perfectly. I added 2 tablespoons of powdered sugar and 2 teaspoons vanilla to my cream/sour cream filling. It was delicious but if you’re serving on a warm day, make sure the assembled cake can stay refrigerated. The cream layers get loose quickly.
Omg! This is probably the most delicious cake I've ever made, and I bake a lot. Just follow the directions, and watch the video. Thank you Claire! It's a very elegant cake. I will make this forever.

I must have done something wrong because my cake was SO deflated in the middle (after cooling it upside down) that halving it would have been impossible. The cream was also pretty sour (I used creme fraiche), which I thought may be fine considering the sweetness of the strawberries but it ended up just kinda gross. Granted maybe it was a lot of “me problems”... I LOVE Claire but probably wouldn’t make this again.

Those used to an overly sweet taste are not going to appreciate this recipe. If the frosting was sweet, the cake wouldn’t have the subtle taste that it does and it would be close to inedible, especially given the sweet syrup.

We halved the flour and it had less volume/deflated, but we made up for it with extra strawberries in the middle layer to create height.  Our now 17 year old said this is the best birthday cake he's had in his entire life and our 14 year old has already requested this cake for his birthday next December.

Second time I made this sponge, I accidentally added all 1/2 cup of sugar to the egg yolks and whipped the whites with just tartar and kosher salt. At 4 minutes, the whites were foamy and soft. I folded them in just the same and the cake turned out similarly spongey and equally as tasty.
I actually did that too, and I think it still turned out

I have made this cake twice as a birthday cake for my mother in law.  It’s fantastic for summer, light, chilled and not too sweet. The first time I made it with strawberries and it was amazing.  The second time, I made the sauce with red currants and decorated the top with raspberries - also amazing

For those of you who are unhappy with this recipe, I recommend one in "Simply Sensational Desserts" by Francois Payard.   And for those of you having trouble splitting the cake into two layers, visit your kitchen supply store.  What you're looking for is a tool shaped like a harp with a wire that can be adjusted to various heights.   You set the height of the wire and then saw through the cake.  Both Sur La Table and Williams Sonoma carry this product.

We have two family members with celiac disease, so I made the sponge cake gluten free. I used Namaste brand all-purpose flour. Loved the result, but I made two sponge cakes because GF cakes don't rise like non-GF cakes. I served it at a bday party, and I didn't tell anyone it was GF. Everyone raved! Thanks for such a great recipe and making me look like a pro! (I used mascarpone, and everyone loved it.)

Made a mango version of this cake by simply swapping out the same weight of strawberries for defrosted mangoes which still worked really well! Would love to make it again but maybe try some other fruit to see what else works!

Made this with mascarpone and ripe strawberries, delicious! The strawberry syrup should be sweet enough for the whole dessert, but if you want you could also macerate the fresh strawberries for additional sweetness.
The unsweetened cream made the dessert very balanced.
I put the strawberry/sugar mixture in my oven, over the pilot light to the same effect. A lovely summer recipe.

Using mascarpone will stabilize the cream & cut back on the sour taste. It can be made a day ahead, but the texture will not be as nice. A touch of sugar in the cream helps if you prefer a little sweeter taste.

A wonderful recipe! Uncommonly amazing that it is not too sweet! I used mascarpone along with cream and it was perfect (not sweet but creamy :)! I will definitely make this again! I also recommend the You Tube accompanying video! Another great recipe Claire!!

Wonderful recipe!!  Next time I think I will use almond extract in the cake and slightly sweeten the whipped cream.  I do love the addition of sour cream to make the cream a bit more dense.  I also didn't bother with the double boiler method of heating the strawberries, and just put them on a very low burner, which worked perfectly.

This recipe is dynamite. A worthy use for in-season, farmers' market berries. I agree that the video is worth watching. I personally used caster sugar AND added the sugar gradually when I was whipping the egg whites - a trick I know from making meringues. Everything else I followed to the letter, Only addition was a little chopped mint to the strawberries. I can see myself making this again and again.

I and my family loved this, and will definitely make again. Used mascarpone and added about a tablespoon of sugar and some vanilla to the whipped cream mixture, because I thought my family would like it better that way. Still not overly sweet, and was a great dessert. I think I’d like to try it again this summer, subtituting peaches or nectarines.

Abolin's tip above about adding balsamic vinegar to the strawberry syrup is spot-on. I added blackberry ginger balsamic and it was the perfect counterpoint to the syrup's sweetness. Trying not to eat it all before I use it in the recipe.

cake is delicious, strawberry soak is a must! I used sour cream/ heavy cream option, since this is what I had on hand. I don't love or understand this combination, I think just plain heavy cream would be much better, lighter choice. i will be omitting heavier dairy addition in the future.

Made this for my birthday! I have a terrible sweet tooth, but I resisted the temptation to add sugar to the cream mixture (I used mascarpone rather than crème fraîche or the other; it was lovely), and the cake was better for it. Perfect balance of flavors, and just stunning when sliced and plated. This is my new favorite cake.

This cake was amazing - the sponge is delicate and light and it’s the perfect summer dessert.  My only word of caution is to make sure you have enough of the strawberry syrup. This cake is a delicate balance of flavors, and the syrup is key. Note that the whipped cream doesn’t have sugar, so it needs to derive sweetness from the strawberries and the syrup. I ran out of syrup half way through the top layer, and while the cake was still delicious, it was not sweet enough in some areas.

The sponge is great and easy. I didn't care for the cream mixed with sour cream. This did not evoke the delicous creamy sweet strawberry cake I was hoping for, it was too heavy and it lost volume over time. Next time I would just make whipped cream from scratch and serve it with the sponge and strawberries.

This was such a success!  I followed the video and did not change a thing.  The cake is perfect. The best sponge I've ever made.  I have access to beautiful Alabama peaches, so I made a peach syrup and used sliced juicy peaches instead of strawberries.  Fantastic!

This was incredibly delicious. I split the batter on two 7in cake pans and it came out perfectly baked in 30 mins. So didn't have to cut the cake. I reduced the sugar only while making the syrup and used the specified amount while baking the cake.

Fantastic recipe! Made it for keto friends, using extra fine almond flour and monkfruit/erythritol sweetener and mascarpone.  One friend said it was the best dessert she'd ever had, keto or not.  Followed directions exactly. Only problem was that the sponge fell as soon as I removed the springform.  I took another reader's suggestion and made a half-layer cake, which turned out great.  Next time will try the non-keto version to see if the almond flour was the problem.

Followed the recipe and the recipe for the sponge to a t with the minor exception of adding a dash of vanilla to the whipped cream. This recipe is perfectly balanced and not too sweet. If you prefer less sweet, i suggest a very light coating of the syrup to reduce sweetness. The sponge is delicate and did deflate more than anticipated even following the upside down cooling technique but was still thick enough to slice in half. I will make this again!

Used Mascarpone (love) and added a tbsp sugar to the cream-mix. Cake is not overly sweet, so I wouldn't cut sugar. To the syrup i added 2 tsp balsamic vinegar and 2 grinds of pepper. I have no ambitions for the clarity of the syrup, so i just boiled the sugar and strawberries for a couple of minutes and didn't strain it.

Perfect for fresh sweet summer berries-- the sponge came out perfectly and the 1/2 C sugar yields a very lightly sweet cake. Since the only other sweet complement is the syrup (which is wonderful- must do!) I would add a Tbsp. or so sugar to the heavy cream/marscapone next time. This cake also slices like a dream (with serrated knife) and looks just like the pic! My only deviation from the recipe was to use a 9" nonstick round pan (2"sides) bc I wasn't sure ab my springform. Thx NYT!!!
I made this last weekend with the mascarpone and it was divine. Thanks so much.

I do the sponge cake in a jelly roll pan and stack it in 4 layers. It’s delicious. Also, make double the strawberry syrup. It’s delicious to drizzle when serving. Also be careful with the salt in the cream. I added just a tad too much and it really throws off the balance of flavors. I’m going to try it without salt just to get a bench mark.
Lovely recipe. Used the sponge cake recipe provided.  Used 9” round pan not non stick.  A little di Serrano in the syrup and subbed vanilla for almond extract.

Delicious cake. Go-to sponge. Go-to berry cake. Simple, but multiple steps. Did all steps in standing mixer. Have to watch each for color, consistency. Used 2 lbs strawberries. Maybe use all/most syrup. Add 2-3 spoonfuls of sugar (or 1 tub maple syrup) to mascarpone cream.
The best layered cake I have ever made. Keeps well in the fridge. I made a strawberry jelly instead of the syrup because I did not want it to get soggy.
Has anyone tried making the sponge & syrup in advance and then assembling, together with the whipped topping, the next day? pros/cons?

Ab fab! I made to celebrate my son's June birthday, when strawberries are in season here in ON. Gorgeous with just-picked, sun ripened strawberries. Could probably serve 8 but there were no leftovers with six of us at the table. Will revisit this recipe again in June for that birthday and throughout strawberry season, just because.

DELICIOUS. Hate idea of wasting strawberries so I puréed cooked ones into the syrup - was a jam consistency so I spread on cake. Next time I would strain it, brush the cake with syrup, then puree cooked strawberries to mix into fresh ones for filling (not top). Used 1c heavy cream, 1c mascarpone added 3T crème fraiche. I would increase cream mixture to 1.5X. I like unsweetened whipped cream but knew my family would not, so I added sugar to cream and to the fresh strawberries. HUGE HIT!!!!

Was excited to try Claire’s recipe because I have had sponge failures with some of the British recipes for Victoria Sponge.  My sponge came out perfectly, and I thought the strawberry syrup really added to the overall flavor of the cake (vs just using strawberries).  However- the 50:50 premix of cream and sour cream never reached any degree of “stiffness” In my stand mixer, nor did it set up after an hour in the fridge!  I think the should have been whipped separately a then combined

Loved this!!! I made the cake a day ahead and underrated but was still delicious. I might try a 6 " cake pan next time. I added a little confectioners sugar to the cream and it was perfect. It is great the day after as well. A keeper!
sour cream is a not a good 1:1 substitution for crème fraiche

I made this in the middle of winter so I kind of went off the rails and used poached pears instead of strawberries and infused my cream with honey and thyme, but it added such beautiful depth to the cake. Highly recommend if anyone wants to try different variations.

Heads up- if you make the recipe as written the cream will not be very sweet and taste like butter. Added about 1/2 cup of powdered sugar and 1/8 tsp of vanilla to the mascarpone and heavy whipping cream mixture and it was great!

Used sour cream substitute option (not even the full amount)and it resulted in a more watery cream filling that didn’t really hold up. Strawberry syrup is delicious and easy to make. But I added a few tbs of powdered sugar to the cream, in part to try and bulk it up a bit. But also for sweetness. Without it, it’s basically just plain cream with some tang.

Our favorite go to cake recipe. It’s simple, light and delicious! When I followed the steps in the video, the sponge cake turned out perfectly. The strawberry syrup is amazing, as well as the left over strawberries over yogurt. Five stars!!
I have made this 3-4 times and every time is a hit. Great when strawberries are at their peak!

Delicious! I doubled the cream recipe to put more around the edges since I like the look of a completed cake. The mascarpone gave it the right amount of tang with the strawberries. 

Next time I would just mash the strawberry/sugar syrup instead of straining it. 

OH! And HAPPY ACCIDENT! The only heat proof item I had that would fit a pot for the steaming was a teapot. If you're going to do to the straining, having it already be in an item meant for pouring was handy.
This is my third year in a row of making this cake on strawberry harvest day. What a gem. Save yourself a step by using strawberry jam instead of making the sauce in the recipe.

Made this with a store bought sponge cake - it was meh. The sour cream whipped cream mixture was too sour - total waste of calories- why not just use whipped cream? There was not enough sugar syrup. This is a bit like a trifle but missing the moisture.

I made this today. I felt the sour cream taste isn’t up my alley so I also thought of doing just whipped cream next time as well. I think this recipe is great still- but the sour taste is more suitable for others. I recommend her sponge cake recipe though; it turned out great and this was my first time making a cake from scratch. Thanks.
Delicious and light. Not too sweet. Very simple flavors. Do NOT make with bad strawberries.

This cake got raves along with the sponge cake. I was concerned about the taste of the crème fraîche with the heavy cream instead of straight heavy cream but with the strawberry syrup and fresh strawberries, it tasted amazing.

Watched the video (excellent) but I don’t have wide, flat mixing bowls or a not non-stick springform pan but dove in anyway. Then forgot to add the sugar to the egg yolks until I had already added the flour. Yikes, expecting a real disaster but the cake is beautiful and the top is flat. I love Cooking recipes- always the best.
So the strawberries to be cooked are about 183 g. and then you'll have 274 g. for layer 1 and 274 g. for layer 2.
This is how my Alabama mother always made strawberry shortcake, except that when she could not get fresh strawberries, she used frozen ones.  Her cake was always delicious.
In my family, we call this strawberry shortcake. Anyone else?

This recipe is super simple and gives you a cake that looks amazing. Made this for a friend and they loved it. I used Nigella Lawson’s sponge cake recipe from How to be a Domestic Goddess and it worked perfectly!
Could someone suggest a non-gluten version of this cake?

I made this for my mother’s birthday! It was so delicious with the sponge cake. Everyone was thoroughly impressed. I changed a few things however; I simply added the strawberries and sugar to a small saucepan with a bit of water and allowed the syrup to develop-I cooked it probably for 20 mins total, and it came out perfectly. I also used marscapone with the cream. I found that this mixture actually turned out a bit salty so I did end up adding probably 2 tbsp powered sugar and 1 tsp vanilla.
I haven't made this yet, but the photo looks like this cake is a takeoff on the British Victoria Sandwich. Has anyone made both and can compare?
This was great! I cooked as directed and it was delicious. We were worried the cream wouldn't be sweet enough, but with the strawberry syrup it was perfect. I used marscapone.

I made the cream with mascarpone and needed more sugar. Not really crazy about the flavor of the cream and might try creame fraiche next Sunday. This was a practice run for father's day. Glad I did it as the sponge was only a bit over an inch tall and there was no way it would cut in half cleanly, so I ended up making 1/2 a layer cake by cutting the 9 inch down the middle and using one half as the top. Turned out beautifully!

Made this exactly as written and it was amazing.  This will replace any version of strawberry shortcake I have ever made.  Not too sweet at all, but really ripe fresh strawberries are key.  The cream had just enough body to hold up to the cake and berries. Another win for Claire.....and us.

Just made this today and family LOVED it!  I used the self rising cake flour (Presto) with baking powder and salt added because I searched high and low for Swan’s Down cake flour in four markets and couldn’t find it. Sponge cake came out beautifully.  Perfect summer dessert!

I had the bounty of fresh local strawberries from the farmers’ market. I made this cake exactly as the recipe details and paid close attention to the prep details. It appears complicated but wasn’t. The cake was amazing - my guests loved it. I used Creme Fraiche with the whipped cream.
For non gluten: I have done sponge cakes with ground almond and teff flour or ground almond and potato starch. It works well.

Made the sponge as instructed and substituted fresh nectarines for the strawberries. Liked the whipped cream and mascarpone as they were. Made my own syrup from very ripe nectarines directly in a sauce pan, instead of a double boiler. It turned out really well and I got a lot of positive feedback.

Loved this cake. Made the sponge cake, strawberries and cream with creme fraiche a day ahead - assembled it in the morning - and then refrigerated it until mid-afternoon. Really like the taste of the cream - sweetness from strawberries and cake were enough.

We loved this, but the contrast between the sweet cake and the totally-not-sweet cream was a little much. On the other hand, you don't need 1/2 cup of  sugar for that amount of berries. When I make this again I'm going to cut the sugar with the berries to 1/3 cup and add 2 Tbsp confectioner's sugar to the cream mixture--enough to add just a hint of sweetness and balance it out a bit.

This recipe is extremely well-written. If you follow it exactly you will have no problems whatsoever. It’s a master class in chemistry!  If only chemistry had been taught this way! That said, for our Father’s Day dinner for 3, I realized two layers would be too much for us, so I served it just with one layer and will freeze the 2nd for another time. It was so light and so delicious! I used sour cream and whipping cream in my topping and it was just fine.

Made this yesterday for Father's Day.  I did add a bit of sugar and vanilla to the cream mixture because my husband like it sweet. It was exquisite and beautiful.  Cooking the syrup was a bit over the top, but I made the cake early and cooked the syrup while preparing and eating brunch and it was s pretty!! The strain strawberries will get used for something I'm sure.  We plan to make this as a sheet cake for 4th of July with the addition of blueberries as part of the decoration.

All Claire’s recipes are delicious and this is no exception. The sponge was so light and fluffy. The cake as a whole was a beautiful summer cake and will be a cake I make often at my family’s constant request. Thank you so much for this recipe!!!

Gorgeous recipe. Made it twice; once with mascarpone and whipping cream and again, with sour cream and whipping cream. Added 2 tablespoons of sugar to each combination for a hint of sweetness which contrasted nicely with the simple sponge and unsweetened berries. Was concerned the layers might slip apart but they were very stable on the cream and berries. Beautiful presentation.

Stunning, delicious and dreamy cake.  Split batter into 2 8" pans to create separate layers.  Used monk fruit sugar substitute for strawberries to cut back on sugar consumption (highly recommend).  No one complained about the non-sweetened whipped cream.
I’ll admit, I tossed a splash of caster sugar into the cream. Had to please a younger audience!

This is a lot of steps, if you're starting with making the sponge. It was definitely tasty and impressive to serve, but I'm not sure I wouldn't prefer to serve the sponge with just chantilly whipped cream and some sugared berries.
Somewhat involved recipe and not worth the effort. It is not particularly sweet and those who ate it (including me) generally prefer a sweeter dessert.

Took suggestions and watched video for delicious results: Expected it to fall (only had non-stick springforms) so doubled sponge cake recipe (used 2 9"round pans). Don't loosen edges before inverting to cool! Next time I'll try parchment round on bottom; mine needed offset spatula to loosen. Evened out sunken sides (then cut-sides up to absorb syrup). Syrup was easier than expected, and amazing (needed only 1/4c sugar, added tbsp balsamic too). Mascarpone   1 tbsp confectioners sugar in cream.

So delicious, I used creme fraiche because I was unable to get marscapone and it was great.  I did add a few TB of sugar and some vanilla because we like it a bit sweeter.  The strawberry syrup was super easy and came out perfectly.  The only thing I will change next time is I would use my kitchen aid stand mixer rather than the hand mixture used in the video.  I just don't think it put enough air into the eggs, in my case anyway. Will definitely make again and again!
I used the sugary strawberry mush as a middle layer for the cake( unstained). I did not use salt in the whipping cream/ marscapone. It was very yummy and light

Made this yesterday.  Fantastic!  I used creme fraise and a couple tablespoons of sour cream.  I added a little sugar to the fresh strawberries. I made the cake, creme, syrup the day before, then assembled just prior to eating.  I left the sponge in the pan overnight.  It didn’t deflate at all.  I’ll make this again with peaches!

This was quite good.  My first time making the sponge, came out perfectly.  I was very careful with measurements, weighed everything.  The sour cream/whipped cream mixture felt too droopy, wouldn't firm up, but the taste was nice against the sweet fruit.  Like others, just mascerated with a few tablespoons of sugar while baking, strained out the juice and proceeded.  Delish.  Incredibly light and not too sweet.

My parents had a strawberries and cream wedding cake some 60 years so. I asked if this sounded like what they ate, and the verdict is YES. So I'm going to try to make it for my mother's birthday, which is the next family event. It will be a small gathering, but I'll bet every bite gets eaten over the course of a couple of days.
This is light and heavenly, easy and a pleasure to make. I baked at 7000’ elevation with no adjustments or problems.

Such an absolute beautiful cake! I made this for fathers day, and completely forgot the syrup, and it was still amazing!  The next two times, I added the syrup and it was still really good, and not overly sweet.  By the 4th time I made it, the sponge was perfect and did not have any areas of deflation.  I'm not sure what I did differently, but it came out perfect.  I will be making this again tomorrow for my parents who are visiting in town.  Also, my 4 and 7 year old loved it!!

absolutely delicious. I made the entire recipe from the sponge to the final cake, and it turned out delicious. not too sweet at all and the cream was so refreshing. all my guests loved it and raved about how nice of a finish it was.

I made this dessert exactly as written in the recipe. My family loved it and I wouldn’t change a thing. The only “shortcut” I took was purchasing strawberries that were already hulled, which cost about $4 more. The syrup makes itself while you make the sponge. Think GBBO!
Made it as is (including sponge cake) and added fresh figs on top. Very good!

Oh phooey. I'm so disappointed. The cake tasted good but it didn't rise enough. I tried cream/sour cream because Whole Foods was out of marscapone. It thickened nicely but I didn't like the tartness. It was also unappetizing without a bit of sugar. There is no need to heat the strawberries. They macerated fine in sugar at room temperature and I got plenty of strawberry syrup. This cake wasn't worth the effort.
Amazing! The cake didn’t deflate at all. I really wish I had doubled the whole recipe because my family at the whole thing in two days and we all definitely wanted more! So, so good.

Stunning recipe - was super easy to make even for a novice baker, the flavors are subtle and not too sweet. I am not a regular baker but I will definitely be making this regularly. It really looks amazing when finished!! Worth watching the video as well

My grandmother made this cake for our birthdays, with the difference being that she made it in three (possibly thinner) layers. The top layer was cream with just a garnish of strawberries, so you could put candles in. It tasted like a cloud of happy flavors. 

I've been looking for this recipe for soooo long, and only finding strawberry shortcake instead of sponge--a very different thing.
Great sponge cake recipe. We used creme fraiche for the cream but thought the flavor was too pungent with the other components. Next time I will try marscapone and add a Tbsp of sugar with the cream.
Same a jelly roll just in a spring foam pan
My girlfriend was hoping for a cherry cake for her birthday and loves the look of this cake. Do you think I could substitute fresh cherries for the strawberries in a 1:1 ratio?
Do you think this could be doubled (4 layers instead of 2) or would it make it too heavy and crush the layers?

Made this recipe for a friend’s birthday & got rave reviews. I like sweeter desserts than my friend so probably will add 1 T of sugar (and maybe vanilla extract) to cream if I make this recipe again. Do be sure to use every last bit of that strawberry syrup to maximize the deliciousness of the recipe. And watch Claire’s video on YouTube! It’s really helpful to actually see what to look for when whipping the eggs or cream.

DELICIOUS.  First time making a sponge and it was perfect, worth the effort. Slipped out of the pan despite following instruction not to use a non-stick springform. Not at all too sweet. Recommend watching accompanying videos, very helpful details esp for the cake & syrup. Sour cream filling was not a hit (too tangy), subbed regular whipped cream with a little sugar and vanilla. Make sure the whipped cream is firm enough not to slide off/smoosh out sides when 2nd tier is put on top.
Can the components of this be prepared ahead of time and assembled the day of serving?

I had a lot of trouble with the cream—1 cup cream to 1 cup mascarpone just gave me soup. I managed to add almost 1 additional cup of cream cheese to salvage it, but it was still really runny. Not sure what I did wrong? I looked at another recipe (Melissa Clark’s Strawberry Moscato Torte), and the ratio is 1/2 cup cream to 2 cups mascarpone ( 1/3 c powdered sugar). Curious if this was a problem for anyone else?

Hi Jessica,

The heavy cream and the creme fraiche, or mascarpone, or sour cream, are mixed together and then whipped until you get medium stiff peaks. No sugar is added. If you make it again, which I hope you do, check to make sure you're using "heavy cream" which has more fat, than regular whipping cream and continue to beat it until gets thick like whipped cream. Hope this helps :)
Steaming berries will give clear syrup.   Do it.
I like to use Confectioners/Icing sugar instead of granulated in whipped cream. It dissolves much better
To me, this cake is the perfect example of the sum being greater than its parts. What a beautiful and delicate summer treat. I would expect nothing less from Claire
Check out ATK’s version of this. Heavier, denser, luscious, scrumptious.

I made this twice.  Once as described for Father’s Day and the second time I doubled it for a large 4th of July party (very end of our strawberry season here) and decorated it with Stars and Stripes using blueberries also.  It was a huge hit!

When we have fresh strawberries, usually I macerate the strawberries with some sugar, because they are not always sweet enough.  In that case, eliminating sugar from the whipped cream might be necessary. Any comments?

Hi Miriam,

Actually you don't add any sugar to the whipped cream mixture. The sugar is for making the strawberry syrup and it adds all the sweetness you need for this dessert. Don't hesitate to try making this. I was really happy with how it turned out. All the different components together really balances everything out in the end. Hope this helps :)
A sponge fail when I made this with my niece yesterday, even after watching the video. Ours was too dense. Grrrr.

The cake is lovely and light. I used marscapone with the cream. I thought it needed a little sweetness or maybe I need to use less of the cream in the middle. It overpowered some bites. I’m not sure why some think the strawberry syrup is omittable. It really made the cake and I wish I had even more. I think this cake is best when it sits for a few hours before serving. Each component is well highlighted.
Delicious cake. The cream is not sweet, so consider combining some of the strawberry syrup with it to prevent it from getting boring. This also prevents the syrup from making the cake soggy.

What was curious (and serendipitous) was when I removed the springform bottom, the cake had formed a 1/2 inch deep uniform depression, though the sides were fully intact.  It resembled the oft-maligned store-bought shortcake cups but made the perfect vessel for the syrup, strawberries and cream topping.  A lucky/happy “mistake” somewhere in my prep or baking!

halved the entire recipe (incl. that of the sponge cake, only made one tier . I reduced the sugar in both the cake and the syrup since most American desserts are far too sweet. The cake deflated a bit, but was moist, light, and delicate. The syrup took a little bit longer to produce given the reduced sugar—but it was beautifully intense in flavor! Beautiful result, -recommend watching the accompanying video before starting.- Used Mascarpone (love) and added a tbsp sugar to the cream-mix. Cake is

A lovely and delicious cake. The sponge turned out nicely although a tiny bit shorter than what was pictured—but I think that’s because my 9 in pan was nonstick and the cake couldn’t cling to the sides and also my egg whites were still a bit chilled. 

I substituted sour cream for crème fraiche and the sour taste was strong at first but it seemed to balance out the next day. Next time I’d spring for the crème fraiche and probably use more heavy cream and add a pinch of sugar.

Loved this so much! The texture of the cake was wonderful, especially with the syrup soak. I used Greek yogurt in the frosting instead of sour cream because I forgot the correct ingredient at the store. All together a very tasty, light, summery cake. I served it at a dinner party and multiple people had seconds!

Delicious! Shortcake rose to about 1.5 inches (looked shorter than in pictures), but cut well and tasted light and spongy. Used sour cream in cream which balanced nicely with the sweetness of the strawberry syrup.
It was so good but the recipe didn't make enough so I doubled the ingredients

Fantastic recipe.  I actually made a keto version of this, using Lakanto sweetener, for some keto-dieting friends.  They said it was the best dessert they'd ever eaten.  And I tend to agree (though I'm sure I would have preferred the real sugar version because those sugar replacements tend to have a cooling mouthfeel).  But the sponge baked up perfectly and the strawberry syrup was divine. It's worth the extra labor to make it.  Plus I now know how to make stable whipped cream, which is great!
Okay, somebody tell me why my heavy cream and sour cream together didn't whip properly. I followed directions and had not one but TWO fails to get it to stiffen up. It was a delicious but soupy cake.

I’m not a sweet tooth, but I think the whipped cream needed sugar. 
I also had to adjust the amount of strawberries. I think mine were not as juicy, so I needed more to get the same amount of syrup. 
My sponge cake did not rise as much as hers, but that was probably my fault. I didn’t fold enough to get an even mix.
I thought this was amazing with no alterations. I love sweet desserts and found this to be perfectly sweet even with no sugar in the cream. It was delicate and delicious! My whole family loved it!

I am a big fan of Claire's, and this recipe did not disappoint, I have made it twice in one week already. 
Why I love it:
Minimal ingredients, and you can do all of the components ahead of time, and put it all together right before you want to serve it. Eating it day-old is even better! It doesn't get soggy at all

Notes:
- If you are using mascarpone then it is an expensive cake to make
- I make 1.5 the amount of cream, I think this is a ratio
This is the best cake I’ve ever tasted. Thank you so much for these amazing recipes!

This came out really well. I echo the recommendation to watch the video, especially if you've had previous "sponge fails." I was better able to recognize the correct stage of egg white whipping, plus how the yolk mixture should look. And I wasn't anxious about removing the cake from the ungreased 9 inch springform because I'd seen how hers came out. We used mascarpone in the whipped topping, and it was not particularly sweet, but really good with the berries and syrup.

Amazing! Made it for my husband birthday! It’s his favorite combination! 
To add a touch of crunch to the recipe, I added coarsely crushed meringue between the layers. And also smashed the cooked strawberries and put it together, making a layer of sponge cake, syrup, whipped cream, fresh strawberries, smash cooked strawberries and crunch meringues. This pie was the hit of the birthday lunch!
Has anyone ever made this successfully with cherries? ( maybe even frozen ones?)

This cake was delicious! Tasted great - and was very moist. The video was super helpful and made it much less daunting for me as a novice baker. I used non dairy heavy cream and lactaid sour cream - I bet it would have tasted even better without these substitutes, but it was still great.

I made this as is (or as close as I could) but made double the strawberry syrup. We didn't use it all, but it was nice to know we had enough for the whole cake. It was a big hit at my (adult) daughter's birthday party.

Superb and easy! Made the sponge with day before, then assembled the cake the morning of our holiday dinner. One tiny change: added 2 tablespoons of powdered sugar to the cream-creme fraiche mixture to slightly reduce the tanginess. 
Don't be intimidated by comments that say making the strawberry syrup is a lot of work: it's not.This delicious dessert was a huge hit!

Made this for Easter. Sponge cake was great, but would use dental floss to cut into two layers. The serrated knife did not yield attractive results. Biggest issue was using combo of sour cream and whipped cream. Despite it being very well whipped, the topping sagged a lot, leading to a less than pretty cake. Would use crème fraiche next time and hope for better results.

Beautiful and delicious cake. Everyone loved it.
I baked the cake as directed and with all the steps. Turned out great.

This is a delicious cake. I have made it twice. The second time, I totally goofed and added the sugar to the cream/mascarpone. (It belonged in the strawberries.) When I realized my mistake, I took out as much of the sugar as I could but couldn’t retrieve all my f it. Rather than pitching the cream mixture, I went with it. It turned out to be a happy accident. The sweetened cream mixture was very good.

This cake took some concentration to get exactly right, but was worth the effort. The sponge cake was dreamy , the strawberry syrup worth the long simmer, and it looked impressive. (Using fresh summer berries was important.) I made it for dinner party hosting friends visiting from Sweden and they were thrilled to be served what they all immediately exclaimed was a Midsommartårta - Midsummer cake.
I love Claire so much, but this sponge recipe almost broke me! Maybe it's me, but I tried twice with no success. I followed Sheldo's chiffon cake recipe instead, which came out amazing.

My family declared this the best cake ever. I made everything as described by the recipe. Used sour cream. I was concerned the whipped cream would be too sour, but that taste was offset by the sweetness of the syrup, strawberries, and the cake itself. I used the NYT sponge cake recipe.

Just finished this beauty and it is stellar. Perfect to make when you’ve got tons of strawberries. Read many comments here about cream not thickening (I did sweeten mine btw). Used a hand mixer on high and spent some time on it…it thickened into lovely peaks. Will use the extra cream to pipe around the bottom before serving. Reminds me of my childhood bakery windows!
So basic. If this is the best cake you ever made that’s sad.
Thinking of making this for Memorial Day gathering. Wondering how to make a chocolate version of this sponge - any ideas?
Curious if anyone has tried adding powdered dried strawberries to the cake batter? Seems like it would up the strawberry flavor.

I made this with the linked sponge cake (didn't care for my results with that recipe).  I struggled to get the cream to stiffen up.  I think sour cream was just too heavy with it (couldn't get mascarpone or creme fraiche easily).  I also didn't really like the taste without sugar, so we added sugar to the whipped cream mixture.  Ended up having to use some canned whip cream to make a dam/edge to hold the whipped cream.  The strawberry syrup was good and not that much extra work.

Subbed Greek yogurt for sour cream and went through the faddle of cooking the strawberries down for syrup ... there's got to be an easier way! Still, delicious and very popular with both adults and children.
Has anyone tried making this (at least the sponge part) the night before serving?
Yes. It deflated but was still tasty. It was thin the second day so a bit hard to split but I used the iffy half on the bottom and pieced it together. Looked perfect when assembled.

I made the sponge layers three days beforehand and then wrapped them well in plastic wrap then foil and popped them in the freezer. They freeze beautifully. I took them out the morning of the day I wanted to serve the cake and finished it up just prior to serving.

Loved this cake! The syrup was fantastic, easy enough to make, and it added a lovely depth of flavor to the cake layers. To simplify, I might use only whipped heavy cream the next time. Definitely get the best strawberries you can! Yum.
Question, here: has anyone tried making a ganache and spreading it on the bottom layer after the strawberry syrup? I love chocolate and strawberries…

Wonderful cake! Made a few changes - I added 2 fresh basil leaves from my garden to the warm strawberry syrup; when the cake was assembled, I cut up some fresh basil and added it to the top for some contrast color and to promote the subtle basil flavor in the syrup. I will use a liner next time in the springform pan for the sponge -- it was hard to remove the cake from the base even though the cake was completely cooled. Next time I plan to use 1.5C cream and only .5C mascarpone.

Made this with the Victoria Sponge cake from this website (because it is so easy and makes two perfect layers without having to cut), which I made ahead and froze. I cooked down the strawberries with sugar briefly for the syrup and did not strain. Added 1 T of confectioners sugar to the cream mixed with mascarpone, and then my cream kind of chunked up (overmixed?), but it was nonetheless delicious and lovely. I did all the assembly the morning of the party and chilled. Well-received!

Made this as per the recipe (using crème frâiche) for a family birthday celebration last weekend with native strawberries and it was a huge hit with everyone. It's not too sweet, which is a big plus around here. I will certainly make it again and will probably try the mascarpone variation next time around.

The strawberry syrup was delicious and the ratio of strawberry and filling was perfect.  I made with sour cream and think it would have been more to my and my company’s taste to either sweeten the heavy cream or use mascarpone instead.

Syrup: combine 1 c each sugar/water, 1 lb frozen strawberries in saucepan, bring to rolling boil & boil for 10 min, then simmer for another 10 with handful of basil leaves. Strain syrup, reserve berries/basil. Return syrup to pan, bring to rolling boil, then add slurry of 1T cornstarch w/3T water; boil til slightly thickened, add a few grinds of fresh pepper. Compote: Puree berries/basil, add several grinds of fresh pepper. Brush cake w/syrup and spread layer of compote before whipped cream. Mmm

I simplified the berry syrup by making it directly in a saucepan (and accidentally boiled it – it was still fine) and letting it sit while I made the cake. Rather than straining the syrup, I just dipped a pastry brush into liquid with the berries still in it. I used the sponge cake recipe and divided it between two 8-inch round cake pans. The finished cake is light, creamy and fruity – a delicate strawberry shortcake. Thank you Claire Saffitz!

I love the syrup and the overall taste, but the whipped cream mixture needs some powdered sugar, in my opinion. The cake is not sweet at all, so it balances out well. I failed at torteing the cake but it was still delicious!!
I baked this for a friend’s birthday cake two years ago and they still talk about it!!

Made for Shabbat with another couple and we thought we’d have half the cake for leftovers - we were happily wrong and the four of us polished it off with no regrets! I made this with whole wheat flour (2 tbsp replaced w cornstarch) and half sugar.
You can make the jellyroll then just cut it in half and stack so that you don't need to cut a 9". Much easier.

It seems that the transparency of the sauce is absorbed into the sponge and it doesn’t pop in the video when drizzled over the top. So I put the chopped berries and sugar in a double boiler and it rendered a lovely translucent sauce that wowed everyone just the same. I will make this a syrup another time and use it to make strawberry sodas for the grands.
I've made this a couple times but never bother to do the strawberry thing, just serve with the whipped cream and some macerated strawberries haha

What a heavenly cake, we were fighting over that strawberry simple syrup which takes it to next level. Made it for an outdoor 4th of July party and the cream got a little wilted in the heat but it was eaten up anyway. Difficult to cut into and requires some skill to extract the slice intact but the taste is amazing. Next time will try as pinwheel cake roll

I’d actually say that this recipe was really not sweet at all. I followed the recipe for sugar amounts. Not sure of comments that say they halved the sugar even further!? It was fine, but my husband and I were left wanting the strawberries to be in the syrup too, just to add some more moisture.

I made this now a few times, and I skipped the sour cream/ mascarpone each time, but whipped the heavy cream with 4.oz white chocolate, after melting and cooling the chocolate.  I baked it in two round pans. Comes out great, family favorite!

1.5x’d the cake which was perfect for two 9-inch round cake pans (parchment paper, no oil) which fit perfectly the amounts here. Added a bit of powdered sugar to the cream since I used sour cream which was a bit tart otherwise. A big hit!
I’m a notoriously horrible baker so my cake deflated a bit but it was still extremely delicious! hoping to get better soon but if I can make it work so can you
Can you use a cheesecloth instead of a metal strainer?

Really good.  I was nervous baking the layer cake, but it came out beautifully.  I valued greatly the video's description of how the whipped whites and whipped yolks should look, as it took me several minutes longer for both to get to the desired consistency.  After that, all was a breeze.  At our dinner, there was a happy disagreement about whether the cream was just right or a bit too 'tangy' with the sour cream.  But with or without a bit of added confectioner's sugar, you can't go wrong.

I made two sponge cakes because I wanted the cake to be bigger - to serve more people. The cake was very thin, so hard to cut, but it was beautiful at the end. The flour I used was old -best by date was 2/25 - so maybe that was the reason the cake was thin. The strawberry syrup was a great addition to the recipe. The cake was enjoyed by all.

Not sure why this recipe has a 4 of 5 rating. My partner and I concluded it was the best cake we ever tasted, even though we made this in the dead of winter, with strawberries well out of season. I would highly recommend watching the video while making the recipe, it made it much easier to follow along!
Does anyone know how tall the cake should end up being? Mine was only about an inch. It was still amazing, but Claire’s looked taller.
Mine too, I had to make a second one! Where did I go wrong?

I don't understand this recipe at all. The best part, strawberries soaked in syrup, is left out of the cake. I'm not a fan of overly sweet desserts but the fact that the whipped cream/mascarpone mixture was completely sugar free made the whole cake taste weird. I like Claire's desserts in general but I will not be making this one again.

I was surprised to see there was no sugar in the cream recipe but with the strawberry syrup soaking the sponges, I thought it worked really well.  I didn't even sprinkle the sliced pieces with the syrup because it didn't seem to need it.  People loved it and I thought it was a real gem of a cake that is nothing as elaborate like the fraisier and yet delicious.
The cream wasn’t very sweet so maybe add a little sugar to it.

I will make this over and over again because it's far less labor intensive than a fraisier and almost as delicious.   I love that it's a casual and yet delightful strawberry and cream cake.  I like that the sides don't need to be frosted.  The chiffon cake has extra steps but you can make it the day before and assembly is quick and easy.  My dream strawberry cake.

I don't have a hand mixer so I used my stand mixer and transferred my meringue into a bowl while I whipped up the egg yolks and the meringue didn't deflate.  I also don't have a nine inch springform pan so I used a regular 9 inch pan and placed parchment paper just on the bottom and it worked really well.  Also for cooking the strawberries for syrup, which many seem to think is too much work, I used a double boiler and straining only about 6 ounces of strawberries was no big deal.

this is a great recipe but I would strongly advise trimming the browned or crisped edges of the sponge cake off before you put on the filling and try to roll it. The stiff edges ended up causing the cake to tear when I tried to roll it up. Otherwise the recipe worked wonderfully.

Rave reviews from everyone! Cake is light and airy. The fresh raspberries and sweet jam pair well with the unsweet cream and creme fraiche. I was tempted to add sugar to the cream but glad I didn’t—the berries sweeten it perfectly. Surprised to roll the roulade from the long end but this worked well. I wouldn’t change a thing about the recipe and will definitely make again. Huge benefit to “have to” trim the ends off the cake before serving because I got to taste it before sharing with others.
With the vast array of similar pans out there, it might be a good idea to provide the dimensions of a “jelly roll pan,”

A jelly roll pan measures 15"L x 10"W x 1"H. The size is critical to produce a layer which will successfully roll without cracking.

Re: rolling the cake while still a bit warm; YES. It works better.

Re: trimming edges before rolling; YES. Premium bakeries use the trim for cake crumbs to decorate the sides of layer cakes.

Re: seedless vs, seeded berry filling; it is a personal preference, but straining the seeds out produces a much more elegant result.

Use a neutral flavored vegetable oil.

Is a traditional cake in Dominican Republic,  we fill it with Guava jam home made, and use for the topping English cream, home made to. We call this Brazo Gitano. Really deligthful.
Margarita Auffant Najri

I just made this recipe for the first time, but made two cakes. The first one rolled just fine. The second one completely split apart while rolling. After looking around online, I see some people suggest you do the first “training” roll while the cake is still warm. Maybe I’ll try that next time.

Excellent cake. I used strawberry jam and added sliced strawberries on the whipped cream.  Needed about 2T more jam and 1/4 c. More whipping cream.  
One note, roll the cake while still warm or it will crack.  I knew that, but followed the instructions.  I should have listened to myself.

Excellent recipe and very easy BUT you must roll the cake while warm! Simply drag a knife around the edges to loosen the cake then invert onto a kitchen towel generously sprinkled with powdered sugar, roll up with the towel and leave the whole thing (towel included) to cool completely on a wire rack, seam side down. Makes filling and re-rolling as easy as can be. I’ve done it twice with Claire’s recipe and didn’t suffer a single crack.
Followed the advice to oil the cooling rack and to trim the edges before rolling. Came out perfectly looking exactly like the photo. Going to try with blackberries/blackberry jam.
Any reason this couldn’t be done with fresh and strawberry jam?
You could definitely use fresh strawberries and jam!

I've always been an avid cook but intimidated by baking. Trying to branch out this year.  I really want to try this with blackberries, but I'm a little worried about the bitter seed throwing off the flavor of the filling.  Anyone have experience with using fresh blackberries in applications like this?  Am I too worried here?

I bet you are too worried.  If you like eating the blackberries out of hand, you'll like them this way.  Or you could use extra berries and push the mashed mixture through a sieve with a silicon spatula, then you'd have no seeds to worry about.
To CBR, you could try mixing the blackberries with blackberry preserves or jam, like what the recipe does with raspberries.
Buy a pianono and unroll and fill. Easy, delicious.
a nod to the Austrian origins of this dessert would have been nice
With either oil or butter, a sponge cake becomes a chiffon. Sponge is angel plus yolks. Using a chiffon for a roulade is genius, either way!

I highly recommend lightly oiling the rack on which the cake cools upside-down. My cake sank slightly and stuck to the rack. It wasn't presentable with just powdered sugar, so I had to whip more cream (purely for cosmetic reasons, you understand). Added some blueberries inside between the raspberries and cream an a few on top for the 4th of July.

Now that I think of it, my cake stuck to the rack too. I was very lucky that it came off in one piece. A strange thing to happen, as it has never happened to me before in all my baking...thank you for the reminder.

So Delicious! I adore all of Claire’s recipes, she never disappoints. 

The balance of the cake was amazing I usually always reduce the amount of sugar in a recipe but 1/2 a cup in total isn’t crazy so I didn’t change it. 

Only changes made were due to lack of ingredients: 

- instead of the raspberry jam situation I just macerated approx 2 punnets of strawberries with a touch of sugar and a squeeze of lemon and left until juicy (the juice acted as a syrup on the sponge!) Yum.
Nearly flawless recipe. The other notes on here make it flawless: roll sponge when warm, grease cooling rack, trim edges.
Simple and delicious! Roll the sponge cake while still warm; invert the pan directly on to the towel dusted with powdered sugar - no need to put it on a cooling rack.
The roulade is even better the next day because the cake becomes more moist and has absorbed  the jam a bit more. Next time I will make it the day before serving.

I recommend d watching the video on YouTube before making this cake. Also the recipe for the sponge cake gives the dimensions of a jelly roll pan you could also Google this 10X15 in.

The directions also describe ‘training’ the rolled cake while it’s hot and setting it aside. 

I followed all the directions a the cake came out perfectly.

Planning to serve this whole, to be cut and served at table, I transferred the finished roll to a platter rather than chilling it in the towel on the board. I decorated with fresh raspberries and powdered sugar. Because the sponge cracked during the rolling, next time I think I’ll underbake by 5 minutes so it’s more pliable.

This came out well--the second time. The first time I couldn't get the cake to look as dark as Claire's in the video so I went with the longest recommended cooking time. The cake cracked pretty badly on rolling. Made it again, went with the shorter recommended time, and it came out well. She mentions in the video that overbaking the cake will make it more prone to cracking. We also had to bump up the amounts of filling (and whip the cream filling VERY stiff) to support the roll.

I tried this recipe in search of a perfect sponge cake for my bûche de Noël. At 27 minutes of baking, in a 10" x 15" pan, the cake split when I rolled it for the « training » part. The dessert turned into a light, delicious raspberry sandwich. I will try it again, perhaps cutting the baking time. I'd make a little more of the raspberry filling.

In our Berkeley kitchen: 25 minutes. Damp kitchen towel with hot water. Next time I will direct transfer from the jelly roll pan to the cooling rack with the damp towel on top and the powdered sugar on the cake itself.

I don't bother with the towel and powdered sugar. I use parchment paper in the shhet pan. I place cut freezer bags (to lay flat) over the cake when it comes out of the oven.While the cake is cooling I make the filling.    Slide the cake out of the pan with the parchment paper.  Spread the filling and start rolling off the parchment.  No cracking ever!!!
Here is a video for making the sponge cake for this and her strawberry and cream layer cake: https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=N4nvGZTC9RE.

Baked it and it was OK-ish. The sponge was ‘meh’’, didn’t like the olive oil in it, There are better sponge recipes out there. Also - this recipe seems to miss the trick - you will get much better cream consistency if you whip cold cream first, before adding chilled mascarpone. Anyone aware of other sponge recipes in nyt cooking application?
Should the jam be seedless?

This is my second time making a roll cake and both times I’ve had issues with splitting. I followed the advice of one reviewer and rolled into the towel while warm but when I had to unroll for the filling it instantly split. Any helpful tips from veteran bakers?

Sponge came out beautiful.  I struggled a little getting a tight roll. The filling started to come out from the sides. I will definitely make this one again. Anyone else struggled with the filling coming out. Any tips?
carole2z: the size of the jelly roll pan is given in the recipe linked for the sponge cake.

This sponge cake was so easy to work with that I am replacing my classic sponge cake recipe with it. The oil (instead of butter) makes such a difference. The finished cake is simple, elegant, and not too sweet at all, just perfect.

Great recipe, made the cake one day, left it rolled up in the towel and then filled it the next day. Held its shape nicely. Used strawberry jam and fresh strawberries instead of raspberries.  Watching the video helped me with any questions about the recipe/ directions.

I agree that the size of the jelly roll pan should be specified. Mine is pretty old, and my cake came out only one-quarter inch thick. The recipe came out well, and the filling is excellent. I never thought to mash jam with berries, and it made all the difference. I will make this again, but in a smaller pan. Definitely a keeper.

This came out great! I turned the sponge out on a cooling rack for about a minute before moving it to a towel, but because it stuck just a little bit to the cooling rack it left these perfectly spaced lines on the sponge for cutting, what a happy accident!

My kids loved this! First time I found myself without raspberry jam, but made filling with fresh raspberries + sour cherry jam (what we had).  Was amazing.  Making again to follow recipe after procuring raspberry jam.  Kids asked for it for their lunches! Kept well two days in fridge.  We used sour cream as it was what we had.

Lovely to look at and better to eat! Rolled it the short side because the genois tore removing it from the pan (no parchment). Served thinner slices. my whites didn’t whip up to to nice peaks but the cake still ended up sooooo light.

I followed Claire’s very helpful video and the roulade came out perfectly. It had a beautiful crumb, the fillings complemented the delicate cake, and everyone loved it! Since raspberries are not in season, I used frozen ones.

Everyone loved this recipe -- we made it for NYE dinner and it served up beautifully without being overly fussy/time-consuming. Since we only had four people, I used a 9X13 inch (quarter sheet) baking sheet with 3/4 the original volume of all ingredients. I still rolled it up along the long edge - it worked mostly fine, just had one fewer whirls in the middle for the visual effect. Will definitely make again!

My sponge cake didn't rise nearly as much as Claire's did in her video.  I was very careful to do whip the egg white and the egg yolks the exact amount of time mentioned in the recipe. Anyways, I highly recommend everyone to watch the video as she makes her own syrup from the berries that she dabs unto the sponge that really allows the sponge to absorb it. Also, make sure if you are making a roll you use the right dimensions as the first time I did it, I made a sponge cracker lol

I didn’t have a jelly roll pan so I increased the entire recipe by one-half and used a regular sheet pan.  I added a tablespoon of powdered sugar to the cream mixture.  Turned out perfectly!  Thank you Claire for this birthday stunner.  :)
This is a great recipe, and one of our family favorites! The same baking tricks works well with a sheet pan. Just smooth the batter out 2/3 way of a 16-17inch sheet pan and trim the crispy ends.
I used to eat a variation of this with strawberries in Bogota, Colombia, where it was called Brazo de Reina (Queen's Arm).

This is FANTASTIC. My first time ever making sponge cake, I think I slightly underbaked it because it stuck to the cooling rack and the dish towel. BUT it rolled wonderfully and tastes great-- nothing a little powdered sugar can't cover :) I used strawberries and strawberry jam because it's what I had.

I’ve made this version once with mascarpone, and the strawberries and cream cake version a few times as well with no issues and to rave reviews. However! This time I could only find sour cream and I could NOT get the whipped cream to form any kind of peaks. Made for a very oozy roulade!!!!

This was delicious. I did add just a tbls or so superfine sugar to the cream and used sour cream (lower fat) and was happy with the results. I rolled it the short way and that worked well too. Topped with chocolate shavings.
Has anyone tried this with chocolate sponge? Interested to see if it could also turn out well

Made this yesterday with great trepidation at the rolling up part.
It worked as described, rolling first in a cloth. My family were delighted with the end product that looked very good decorated with icing sugar and berries. Also it is a generous amount - the roulade was quite long - had to hunt for a long enough platter or board! I can’t believe it did not break up and crack as I expected. Thanks for the recipe and video. It really is a versatile sponge recipe.

This was a nice little cake, just didn't wow me with flavor. I followed it exactly except I rolled from the short end and ended up with more of a swirl. I ended up serving with extra berries and ice cream.
For a slightly more decadent version, add finely chopped walnuts to the sponge cake for less flour and add cocoa powder to the whipped cream. It is sublime.

Perfect sponge! I’ve tried many times to make Genoise with zero success. I was half way into this when I realized that basically what this recipe is. So maybe going into it without the stress or trepidation of attempting a genoise resulted in perfect sponge or the 4 eggs and oil. Don’t know don’t care! So happy to have found a recipe that is the perfect vessel for summer fruits and cream. Yay!
Delicious! We tried the cream with sour cream and sadly could not get it whipped into peaks. Still tasted great :)
Given the small amount of cake flour required, would using all-purpose flour be okay? Add a bit of cornstarch to accommodate the substitution? Thanks.
This one is similar to Japanese style cheesecakes that require cake flour, and they often let you substitute with all purpose flour and corn starch.

I would try 2/3 cup flour minus 1 tablespoon and 1 teaspoon. My mom had an old cookbook that had substitutions and recommends this (they show 1 cup minus 2 tablespoons all-purpose flour for 1 cup cake flour. I don't think you need cornstarch. I plan on trying this cake today or tomorrow and will let you know.
@Beckie, I used cake flour and the result was great! It was worth it. Like a restaurant. But I think you’d still get a good cake with AP.

I've made many sponge cakes over the years and I can tell you that this is a terrific recipe. The family versions involve huge quantities of eggs, tube pans and a lengthy cooking time. This one is so simple, especially because of the manageable quantity plus the ungreased spring form pan. My cake came out prettier than the picture! I subbed some almond extract for some of the vanilla - a taste we prefer. I served it with rhubarb - ginger - raspberry sauce and whipped cream!

For those who like to bake with a scale (what baker doesn’t?)

1/4 oil - 57g
1/2 cup sugar - 100 g
2/3 cup cake flour (KA brand) - 85g

I made this in a non spring form pan and it turned out great! There was a big bubble at the bottom of the pan but I still was able to slice the cake in half to assemble. It’s deliciously light and reminds me of the sponge cakes I got at Asian bakeries as a kid.

I didn't have cake flour. I didn't have a 9" springform, only a 10". I decided to give it a go regardless and if it didn't work, then I would only have myself to blame! But there was also the possibility of cake. To make the "cake flour" followed the interweb's advice and substituted all purpose flour by removing 1T+1t and replacing it with corn starch. As for baking time, I went for 30 minutes for a golden color (could have gone for another 2 maybe), and it was perfect (if I do say so myself).

I'm curious as to why in baking recipes the authors do not provide a temperature for the inside of the cake when done.  

Oven temperatures vary; 325 or 350 degrees doesn't mean the oven is constantly at the set temperature; any oven thermometer will tell you that as will any technician. 

Moreover, pre-heating the oven to a set temperature is not sufficient.  The oven needs to be pre-heated to a given temperature for at least 45 to 60 minutes to allow the walls to heat up and retain heat.

In my professional experience a loaf of bread should temp at 210F, enriched doughs should temp at 180F. I've never temped a cake or muffin or scone because what you are looking for (in most circumstances) is a set crumb and in every bakery I've worked in we gauge this by sight and touch. You can tell a cake is done by lightly pressing the center point. It should feel set or on the softer side of firm. The clean toothpick test is also effective.
209 temp for cake

I have never made a sponge cake before because they typically call for so many egg whites but I love how this recipe utilized the whole egg. I used cake flour and the entire recipe was pretty easy. It was the perfect cake for Ontario strawberries and sweet cream ice cream.

In case there's anyone else with the same limited supply of baking pans as I have, wondering about substitutes: I made this cake in an 8-inch anodized aluminum cake pan (Fat Daddio's) with a parchment round on the bottom but still with an ungreased side, baked it for about 5 minutes extra to compensate for the additional height (40 mins), and it came out perfectly. I did still invert it to cool. After running an offset spatula around the side it slid right out once cooled! No springform needed!

so much easier than expected. I only had a non stick springform pan and was worried it wouldn't work, but it did all the same!! no one had commented that yet so thought I would confirm that it worked out pretty well!

Yummy and easy recipe.  I made twice to test - first with cake flour and then regular.  Slightly coarser but otherwise it works.  Perfect for summer and fruit with a bit of whipped cream or ice cream if you need that consistency alongside it.  A keeper
I tried this with cup4cup gluten-free flour and it came out well—it didn’t rise high enough to slice in two but it was delicious piled up with strawberries and whipped cream.

I made this as written, except I used a stand mixer. I moved the egg white mixture to another bowl, then beat the yolks without cleaning the mixer bowl or beaters. It came out fine. My results for the 9" springform were lighter and higher than the image here -- no dip in the center.

Next time I'd use less salt and maybe add some lemon or orange zest.

I very accidentally left the oil out of mine the second time I made this recipe and it was…better? It rose higher, tasted lighter, and rolled into a roulade just as well as the version with oil. Honestly not sure why, but thought I’d pass along for folks who are looking for a lighter cake.

Because I live at a higher elevation, cake making is almost never successful. Well, enter Claire Saffitz and her wonderful sponge cake recipe.  I watched her video and then followed the directions almost completely, except I didn’t have cake flour on hand and used bread flour. It turned not as high as hers, but perfect for my planned dessert and very delicious.  Good news for us cake bakers in the mountains. Next time I’ll have cake flour on hand.

Thank you Randy for the link! By the way in Germany we don’t use fat for a sponge cake either. We mix the egg yolks with a tablespoon of warm water per egg   sugar until very frothy. Than fold in beaten egg whites and flour ( baking powder) in small portions. A bit tricky and I reach the level of my mums cake:-)
This recipe turned out perfect. I used parchment paper in a 9 inch round pan and the cake came off easily. As others said, it was easy to make.

My  cake is currently in the oven and  it seems to be working out well. I just have a question though: why not add the sugar to the  egg whites  gradually like you do when making meringues? Also, and I dropped a note to Sam Sifton on this topic, where are the weight measurements? I think serious American bakers are  moving to weight measurements more and more; I know  I prefer them both for accuracy and for convenience.

Made this twice. Doubled the recipe second time. Both times, I baked in a quarter sheet pan w/parchment. After it cooled, I sliced the cake in half to create two halves that were each 6.5" x 9". This was easier to do than baking a round to slice. The directions for inverting and cooling made no sense to me. Is it cooled inverted with the pan still on top? I did this, and the weight of the pan made the cake flatten. Second time, just left the cakes in the pan. KA cake flour, 80g. Everyone raved.
I also found this part confusing!

I tried it with gluten-free flour and it worked out really well — not as high as Claire’s but that was probably my error when beating the eggs. Next time I’ll make sure to use room temperature eggs and I expect it will be perfect. The video for this recipe is well worth a watch because she explains her reasoning and I learned from it.
Thanks for this note. Cup 4 Cup or a different GF flour? Would almond work?

Cake flour substitute:
