For full recipe as written (two layer cake), use 2/3 C all purpose flour less 4 t.  Add 4 t cornstarch; mix thoroughly.

For half recipe (one layer cake), use 1/3 C all purpose flour less 2 t.  Add 2 t cornstarch; mix thoroughly.
Interesting. In England, a true sponge cake never contains any kind of fat. My mother made perfect sponge cakes; usually served with raspberries and whipped cream.

FYI, you cannot substitute coconut sugar when beating the whites. It will not form stiff peaks. I thought it was a bit of yolk in the first batch, and added the sugar last in the second. The eggs were foamy and well on their way to stiff peaks. The coconut sugar made them runny. :-(

as she says in the video, separate the eggs when they are cold, then bring to room temp. This was so good, even though I accidentally whipped the egg whites in 1/2 cup of sugar instead of 1/4. Might be good as a foundation for tiramisu...

Made this yesterday.  Didn’t have a springform pan so used 9” cake pan. Forgot to put parchment in bottom. Everything else according to recipe. Nice cake, looks great. Allowed it to cool completely in pan upside down on rack. Removed from pan easily…loosened by sliding knife along edge and a few taps of edges on countertop. This is one tough cake. Stands up to lots of manipulation. Not crumbly. Perfect for petit fours, etc.  some may not like the Uncake like texture.
I would definitely recommend lining the cake pan.  Also, the time for 9” pans should be 20-25 minutes (30-35 is too long).

This came out perfect! I did not have pastry flour and used all purpose.  I also did not have cream of tartar and used the same measurement of vinegar.  My daughter loved it!  Ours also looked better than pictured.  We enjoyed it with macerated whole blueberries, cut up strawberries & raspberries, and whiskey infused whipped cream.  Highly recommend.

Tripled this recipe to make two 9 inch cakes and a jelly roll.  All were a success despite the fact that I accidentally omitted the oil.  Remembered it the moment all three were in the oven. Some notes:
-I baked this in two 8 by 8 aluminum brownie pans. Then I sliced each cake in half to make a 4-layer small rectangular cake, which turned out to be the perfect size for a dinner of four. Cute too.
-This needed close to 40 minutes in my oven. It's best to follow the "golden and springy" guidance .
-It's a very light cake; in my opinion, it benefits from being paired with something rich.

I’ve used other recipes before but this was by far one of the best in its simplicity (and also detail). I found the video to be very helpful for knowing when the egg whites mix and yolk mix were ready. We layered it with peaches and whipped cream. Perfect for summer.

How long should this cool off for before I release it from the spring-form pan? 20 minutes? Hours? Should I "help" it cool off, before using it to make a strawberry cake, by putting it in the refrigerator for a while??? 

Help!!

You need longer than 20 minutes. Wait till the bottom and sides are cool to the touch--at least an hour. Putting it in the fridge won't help much, and I'm not sure what effect it might have on the texture of the sponge.  *It will not release easily if it isn't cool*!

I mistakenly added all the sugar to the egg whites.  Conundrum:  start over, or add more sugar to make the yolks form the ribbon.  I added 25G more sugar. I split it, slathered it with unsweetened whipped cream and a layer of strawberries macerated in 25-year-old balsamic.  Delicious.

For the record US cups can be a dry measure OR a liquid measure and they are not the same. One is weight and the other is volume. For all those asking- yes you can bake ahead and freeze. I froze it for a couple of days and then thawed it on the day I wanted to use it. it turned out awesome and not at all dry. Just make sure to tightly wrap the cake in plastic wrap (whether freezing or simply storing) or else it will very easily dry out.

Hi! Question from a German user: how big are the „cups“ in this recipe? (And all the others?) I am only used to recipes with grams. „Cup“ can be small or big, so I am a bit lost....how many grams of flower? Please help!

King Arthur Baking may be the best American resource for your question. Here's a link to their conversion chart: https://www.kingarthurbaking.com/learn/ingredient-weight-chart A lot depends on how the baker scooped the flour, so it's a bit of a guessing game. I've stopped baking recipes that don't give measurements by weight.

Hello, Ute. The "Cups" measurement used in American recipes is 8 fluid ounces. Flour measures out to about 120gm per cup, both all purpose (regular) flour, and baking flour (lower protein, used for more delicate cakes). Some recipes will vary, telling you 140gm or 128gm per cup - there's a bit of wiggle room if that's the case. 
And you didn't ask, but sugar weighs 200gm per 8oz cup.

As written, mostly a bland, unsweet dessert.  IF, however, you wish to use this sponge cake and make an extraordinary dessert, spike the sponge with something…more sugar, a dash of rum, maybe even some strawberry jam between the two layers so as to hold the second sponge in place (it tends fall off).  The same goes for the heavy cream/macarpone filling….you need to add some flavoring…almond extract, powdered sugar.  You’ll need 1/2# strawberries to get adequate syrup plus 1.5# fresh for layers.

I tried to follow the recipe, just replacing cream of tartar with white vinegar. The cake didn’t turn out fluffy, just an inch high using 9 inch round. What could have gone wrong? Any suggestions would be appreciated!

I’ve made this cake five times so far — so versatile! Today I went with cupcakes, frosted with chocolate whipped cream. Light and sweet and a big hit with little kids (and a heck of a lot less sugar than most cupcake recipes, to boot).
My sponge didn't rise. Maybe because I left the egg whites sitting for so long? It was very humid and foggy out, I think it affected it.

I made this for a friends birthday party and it worked great. I only have a 10” springform pan so I used that, to be honest it looked really flat and wide because of it being 10” in diameter. I would recommend using the suggested pan size.
The recipe does not include adding the egg mixtures to the flour batter. I assume that one does the folding into the flour batter. What did I miss?

Excellent, light and tasty sponge cake. Will probably use neutral oil rather than olive oil to serve this with fruit. I thought the description that the mixture looks "like mayonnaise" or "a bit like cake batter" was good fun.

Not exciting but a good cake nonetheless, especially since my wife likes it better than any of the other cakes I’ve made. And somehow I managed to pull it off on the first try, thanks to Claire’s recipe and instructions.
Used all purpose flour with cornstarch instead, worked perfectly

I made this after watching Claire’s video. I had a bit larger pan. So I added an extra egg and a bit more flour. I paid special attention to the whipping of the whites and yolk mixture and it came out perfectly. Very delicate and tolled easily.
Mine didn’t rise for some reason. Still tastes delicious. I made another one to put it on top.

I used the substitution method for the cake flour using cornstarch.  Just took it out of the oven and it looks good.  Rose nicely.  I've never made a sponge cake before so this was a first for me!  I felt like I was in the Great British Baking Show technical challenge part.
Works PERFECTLY gluten free!!! So pleasantly surprised by the rise and retained sponginess. I used Bob’s Red Mill 1 for 1 GF flour and had no issues. Five stars!!!

I made this last night for the first time. It was a success. A bit dry - I’ll check on the baking time in the future. I cut the cake in half & spread a mixture of lemon curd and whipped cream between layers & on top. Fresh sweet strawberries topped it off. Easy, yummy & beautiful.
Although I agree that it’s helpful to have baking ingredients listed by weight it’s certainly easy enough to convert cups to grams.

This is very good and was a hit for our small gathering.  I made two-thirds of the recipe, using two extra-large eggs and a 6-inch springform pan.  Combining homemade jam with some smashed fresh berries worked well for a filling, especially with a mound or two of whipped cream on each plate.

The instructions were clear and reassuring, so much so that I'm going to make a roulade, a cake that has been a near-disaster for me in the past.
Made this few times. Even good when I overcooked it. great with cream and Louisiana strawberries
2/3 cup of cake flour (King Arthur) is definitely not 85 grams. It is much more - 104 grams, big difference.
I used AP flour and I think this was my downfall, try again.
When I made this I didn’t know I had cake flour. So I made it with regular flour, it was kinda disappointing. It was as fluffy as I hoped, hopefully do to the absence of cake flour.

Great recipe! Only issue was following recipe for cooling. You don’t want to overcook so it rolls well, however the cake stuck to the cooling rack. Might try oiling cake rack or using parchment on it next time. Other ideas?
This sponge cake came out perfectly! I will add it to my repertoire :)
Can you make ahead and freeze

I used a nonstick springform and otherwise followed the recipe exactly. The texture was good, with a light, fine crumb, but I thought the taste was slightly too eggy and too salty. I used Morton salt, but apparently Claire Saffitz prefers Diamond Crystal, which is around half the salt for the same volume.

I’ve baked this twice and while it came out pretty well on both occasions, broth cake had the rise pictured in Claire’s rendition.

Wondering what I may have done wrong to interfere with the rise? Followed recipe strictly and used 2 un greased  springform pans.

Thank you!
Excellent sponge. It made a fantastic base for a Finnish berry cream cake. This will be my new go-to recipe.
I live at 6500 ft above sea level. What modifications do I need to make to this recipe ?

I have made this recipe exactly as is this for several years and many people have loved it. 
But inspired by another reader, I just made a gluten free version with a cup-for-cup commercial GF flour substitution. I used an additional egg, though mainly as mine were already on the smallish side, and an 8 inch pan to help with the rise. 
It was excellent! If you need a gluten free cake, this one really does tolerate the substitution. 
My kids now say they prefer the gluten free version.
I’m going to attempt making this chocolate, replacing half the flour with cocoa powder. Tbd!
Delicious. Easy. Perfect for a sponge roll.

Doubled this recipe and used for an Asian bakery style sponge cake with whipped cream and fruit. Didn’t have cream of tartar on hand and unsurprisingly needed much more time for the 8 egg whites. It was a big hit!

This is an excellent recipe for a jelly roll sponge. Lined a rimmed baking sheet with parchment paper (crumple and uncrumple it hard in your hands to make it easier to form into the pan) and sprayed it with baking spray. Baked about 20 minutes and let cool. Used raspberry preserves and Greek yogurt for the filling. Wrapped in cellophane wrap and refrigerated before serving for easy cutting.

This made a spectacular fifth birthday cake for my son. The batter stood up to being portioned into six, deeply dyed a rainbow, and swirled into a springform. I torted the baked cake, filled it with 1.5 quarts of softened vanilla bean ice cream, and iced liberally with whipped cream. Empty plates!

Fabulous, easy, and delicious! 
No significant issues with sinking - in fact, it had a slight dome. Definitely a keeper!
Add almond extract to the cake batter and use all raspberry jam & fruit
It says in the recipe that you need cake flour, is there any way to substitute it?

Turned out fantastically! I made it at high altitude (4270 ft) and ended up adding one extra egg white, an extra teaspoon of vanilla (you could also use rum) and then baked it at 340 degrees. It was done in about 30 minutes in my oven. Was amazing!

Delicious cake but it didn’t rise as much as I was hoping.  
Was scratching my head about why but I realized when cleaning up that my springform pan is NON-STICK.   
Didn’t think about that earlier. 
Big mistake!  

Will definitely make again

For people wondering about internal temp, since ovens and cook time vary so much (I used convection bake, for example, and it was about 24 minutes in the oven), I pulled out when it had just barely reached 200 degrees Fahrenheit. Because it stays in the pan to cool, albeit upside down, it continues to cook slightly. Between 200 and 2010 degrees is the desired internal temp for a sponge cake.
Does this cake freeze well ?
What are you suppose to add half the flour to?   Later  the recipe calls out adding the told and egg white mixtures so I'm not sure what step 3 wants me to add the flour to.
Baked exactly as directed and had no issues with deflating but the sponge is just a bit heavy for my taste.  But great recipe overall, easy to follow and loved not having to prep the pan!!

I accidentally added all of the sugar to the egg whites; turned out to be a happy accident since it produced a super stable meringue, and made no difference as far as I could tell to the end product. I beated the yolks, gradually added oil (basically made mayonnaise), and folded in the flour. I aerated it by adding in a scoop of the meringue to the yolks, and then folded the aerated yolk/oil/flour mixture into the rest of the meringue. The result was DIVINE, super light and melts in your mouth.

First time trying this and used what I had on hand to make cake flour and subbed 1/2 tsp lemon juice for the cream of tartar, and I only had a nonstick springform pan. Voila! Came out perfectly!! Except I cooked using convection and needed a bit longer. Convection or regular for this cake? Anyway, fun to make, very light. However, in the end, not what I was looking for in nastiness and texture in an efforto try to replicate a local fruit basket cake. So, not for me. Bummer.
This recipe is an amazing foundation for so many desserts. Minimal ingredients, cake is not too sweet but has perfect texture. I already want to remake it with different fruit combinations
I made this four times this summer- I use the springform pan- and it never gets fluffy/thick enough to be able to slice horizontally, but it tastes great. What is going wrong?

Made this for my birthday cake in June with the strawberry shortcake recipe.  I loved it, but the family, especially my grandson, did not like the whipped cream; it was not sweet enough.  My daughter made it the following week and added sugar to make the cream sweeter.

"but don’t use a nonstick pan, as the cake will collapse"

Excuse me?  Why, how, is that?
For GF: I have made this many times using Bob’s Red Mill 1:1 gluten free baking flour and it’s great!

The great thing about this recipe is its simplicity and adaptability. On my umpteenth time baking it, I accidentally used APF 11.7% protein and the cake still turned out great - lovely and light, cooling to a full 3" height (baked 30 minutes in an 8" pan) with a glorious color, flavour and texture.

Delicious and forgiving. I didn’t have a 9” springform pan. Split the batter between two greased and floured 8” nonstick cake pans. Even forgot one of them in the oven for a couple extra minutes. Left them cooling upside down for several hours while I was out running errands. They came right out of the pans and were delicious.
All instructions are spot on. Came out perfect. I made this at a lake house without an electric hand mixer and made it all by hand (fork and whisk) - perfection.

stats.Total Time: 2 hours, plus 6 to 26 hours’ resting and proofing

comments.commentBody: Laborious but delicious.  The butter/honey mixture is better 1:1 - otherwise they aren't sweet enough. (Made the 1st batch as described, 2nd batch with this mod was much better.)
Can this dough be frozen at any point? Signed Cooking For One.
Highly suggest adding chocolate bittersweet chocolate to the filling. I also replaced the lemon zest with orange zest and added orange blossom water to the honey and sugar syrup. Divine.

Thanks so much for this suggestion—had made these a number of times and they were even more special with a few ounces of chopped bittersweet chocolate! I subbed orange zest for the lemon because I like it better with chocolate but followed the recipe exactly otherwise.

I did a short initial rise of 4 hrs (dough), then shaped buns and let rise overnight on terrace (cold where I live, maybe a bit warmer than fridge, but not much), and they were perfect. Did rise a little, just enough, not too much, definitely still individual buns with the sides touching.
I used almonds bc pistachios are REALLY expensive and everyone loved them. I make a lot of yeasted bread and rolls and these were def different and richer in taste.
Very good recipe

I halved the dough recipe and it made a full pie pan of 7 buns. I also made the dough ahead of time... after 4 hours of proofing the dough in the fridge I assembled the buns and put them in the fridge overnight, took them out the next morning 2 hours before baking and they rose perfectly and baked perfectly.
I use no flavor, no wax dental floss to cut the bun slices.  Cuts through the dough perfectly and you never have to worry about flattening or changing the shape of your bun/roll.

Wonderful! I used the referenced Enriched Bread Dough with buttermilk.  I really liked the finished bread.  What I would do differently next time: 
  - Use roasted unsalted pistachios.  I think I overcooked mine, so those on top burned. 
 - I had way more butter/honey mixture than I used.  I would try 3/4 stick butter next time. 
 - Leave out the salt in the sugar mixture; tasted salty.
 - total timing is unclear.  Seems that it should be an ADDITIONAL 20-25 minutes after the 15 minute turn.
Anything that take 3 hours to make is not a "morning" bun.

I didn't have raw pistachios so made this using pecans, 1/4 tsp cardamom and 1/2 tsp cinnamon, more like a traditional cinnamon roll filling. They were super yum and the roll itself is quite nice. I'm excited to make it again with Claire's actual filling recipe!
A tip I like it to make one loaf of the bread, then make a half recipe if these rolls. Just remember to use half the ingredients and roll it out to 11x12. You get 8 rolls.

I've made these twice without modifications turned out great! Worth the effort. I made the first batch as described, second batch I sprinkled the filling instead of pouring down the center and spreading. Sprinkling the filling gave a better coverage for me.

I added some chopped fennel seed and took it up a notch. Yummm!

These are better not tight in a pan, but baked separate on a tray.
I’ve made these many times and they are delicious as written in the recipe. The dough is delightful and the flavors are spot on. I don’t mind the work involved, it is therapeutic. Thanks for sharing!

This recipe (minus the cardamom) reminds me of James Beard’s Pistachio Bread, which he serves in the shape of a wreath. At our house we make it every Christmas morning. The overnight rise in the fridge makes it perfect for a holiday breakfast.
I'm thinking of doing this with puff pastry to make pinwheels.  Brush it with the honey butter, sprinkle it with the "filling", roll it, cut it into pinwheels, lay them flat side by side and bake.
Please try out Irish butter for this recipe. This is on repeat here. They are so moist. Most comment on how it has just the right amount of sweetness.

Making the enriched dough today for buns tomorrow. I usually shape breakfast rolls the night before and let them rise in the fridge overnight to bake in the morning, but am hesitant to take a chance with a more involved dough. Would love to hear what people think!

My gut reaction is to follow the proofing / rising sequence as described: if the shaped buns rise overnight all together, I think you’d risk losing definition of roll within buns and separation between them. Excited to try this!
The overnight proof is slow down by the cold so you end up with the same result.

Definitely a two day process, followed recipe exactly. Results good, in my opinion they were better the second day when the cardamom flavor resolved somewhat. On the fence about whether the final product was worth the work.
Mine looked gorgeous after proofing but shrank back a lot with the baking leaving large gaps between layers. Strange.
Maybe over-proofed?

I thought these were OK. I have been baking a lot lately and I enjoyed the multi-day process with these, However, the finished product didn't blow me away. They were not very sweet, even though I increased the honey per an earlier comment. Overall, I expected the filling to be a flavor bomb but I just tasted the dough. Might try a traditional cinnamon filling next time.

Cardamom scented is generous - I found the total amount for the recipe overwhelming. If I make this a second time, I'd significantly reduce it to 1/4 teaspoon or replace it with cinnamon. The recipe is otherwise pretty good.  Slicing the buns as evenly as possible is key. Love the dough, will make it with different flavored fillings in the future.
These are amazing! One small modification: try halfing the cardamom sugar, as this recipe makes a ton extra. Otherwise, fantastic!!!

This was fun, and I really really enjoyed making these buns. 
The one thing I'd revisit: the pistachio flavor. I love pistachios, and expected there would be more of a nut "oomph." Not sure what happened there - mediocre nuts, didn't toast enough, 1 cup <> 128 g, something else.

I'm thinking of hunting down pistachio extract (or, for that matter, using a little pistachio oil, maybe swapping a tsp in the enriched dough). Anyone else try this?

These are really fantastic - the layer of browned honey butter on the inside with the filling is a serious step up from other recipes I’ve made. I’ve pulled that trick into every version of buns I make now!

If you can follow directions, excellent by the way, you will be very pleased with this recipe. The first time I did not have a lemon on hand. The second time I did and it made a wonderful addition to the subtle sweet/ savory balance. Just had a day old one and it was just as moist as the first day! These have become a wonderful addition to my baking rotation!

Sheer perfection! Followed recipe exactly as written, however, substituted orange zest for the lemon. Absolutely delicious! 

P.S. Claire Saffitz is a genius!!!

thank you Claire,
love this recipe.  with the same recipe, but don't rest overnight, it dose not crate this suppleness, and airy quality, which is so wonderful.
I do like that it is very precise, it helps to know, the 70 g X 16 makes  tow loaf, 8 little balls in one loaf.
love that it has 10 steps.
Pistachios are water hogs in California and worsening the drought. We can bake without them and help save water

Morning buns
Morning buns
Zest another lemon and
Releeeease me

Can I just get as far in the recipe as creating the roll, and then chill the roll overnight, and then put them in a pan in the morning to rise? Given that this is a multi day recipe I’m wondering how to create the least amount of work on the day they go into the oven.
Used 1:1 honey butter ratio. Came out great.

Delicious! Reminds me of the famous “Ocean Roll” from Sparrow Bakery in Bend, OR. I did 1:1 of honey and butter and added a bit of vanilla bean paste. Time consuming, but not difficult. Fabulous. My husband said “better than a cinnamon roll”.
Let the dough hook work it’s magic. Used orange zest in lieu of the lemon. Double downed on the pistachios. Already thinking about alternative fillings. Fascinated with the milk roux. More to come!

Used half this recipe for morning buns. (Other half went to slider buns). First off, ain't nobody got time for "austere" morning buns. Noted a bunch of  comments in here then made walnut, orange peel, and chopped chocolate excessively fun buns in a 9" springform pan. Also used a lovely floral Manuka honey with a splash of orange blossom water for the topping. I've baked Claire's BA + DP recipes. Her recipes here seem more foolproof and reliable as vetted through the NYT Cooking edit manufactory.

Used sourdough for dough. Wow 1 stick of butter seems like a lot. I only had 2 tbsp in my frig so that's what I used and can't imagine using more! These were a huge hit and would definitely make again.

The dough was so delicious! So fluffy and tender. I resisted the urge to add more flour, even though it was sticky through the end, and I’m glad I did. Only issue I had was the filling pulled away and ran into the bottom a bit when cooled, still delicious.

These are outrageously delicious. I cut back the cardamom and glad I did. Perfect balance w lemon rind. I also cut the recipe in half (baked in 8” pan) and used remaining half of dough for hamburger buns. 
Dough hardly rose at all in fridge overnight- I thought it was a fail but a long proof outside on a summer day proved me wrong. Perfect rise. Next time I’ll let them proof for awhile in the pan before refrigerating overnight. That way we can have them in the morning!

I live in a vegan house riddled with food allergies, I have to modify nearly every recipe.  The Banana Diaries has a great recipe for enriched dough, did not use any nuts, cut the cardamom down, and used maple syrup in place of honey, they were insanely delicious!  The entire pan was eaten in one day.  I didn't get the height I would have liked on the buns, but I also did an overnight rise in the fridge.  These are worth the work.

Sitting down to rest now that I'm at the rise stage—pretty intense recipe. Some notes: Allow plenty of time (2 hours?) to chill the dough log before slicing it; anything less than thoroughly chilled will not achieve the swirly finish. The bread dough recipe doesn't include a temperature to which one should cool the tangzhong, or “water roux” before commencing to mix the bun ingredients. They say cool is essential. This is a yeast recipe, and liquid temperatures matter!

Just unbelievably good. I’d never kneaded dough for so long it the results are well
Worth the effort—and a stand mixer is essential.

Made as directed. My dough looked delicious and rose beautifully. The finished buns were a bit dry on the outermost layer. Any tips from more seasoned bakers? The inner layers were moist. I loved that they were not too sweet.

It needs a lot of preparation but it’s delicious. I was short in pistachios so I adda some hazelnuts and almonds it was more nutty than delicate nutty. Added extra lemon zest and it gave a nice fresh ending when tasting it. Not to do it anytime soon again but for sure a keeper as a special treat.
This recipe was originally published attached to a recipe for bread dough. What happened to it? No longer seems to be attached to that recipe

They were raw at 25 minutes. Is the intended cook time in this recipe 40 minutes? I had to do 25 at 350 then 15 at 275 because they were too browned/dried out, another 10 at 350. They were delicious but I think the heat and cook times are really off. If I make these again, I will try something like 400ish for 10 minutes fo ra big poof and then 300 for 40 minutes or so to cook through. 
(It's not my rise: I did a 1.4 hr bench rise followed by a 12 hour cold rise with a 2 hour rest post shaping)

10 out of 10 would make again. Many steps with a lot of payoff. Requires careful pre-planning and thinking through. True, not very sweet. Perhaps could add more honey as another commenter added .... Made with Demerara sugar. Perhaps could sub with brown sugar, just will be different flavor and texture.
Can anyone tell me please what size loaf pans they use. Love Claire, but her recipes on this site don’t state pan size.
Underwhelmed by this recipe. Not sweet & moist/buttery enough. Will use the dough next time to make classic cinnamon buns.

I thought this recipe was perfect. I followed the recipe exactly and it was 🤌 chef’s kiss. The dough was time consuming but the mixer does all the work. It was so good I plan to find many more uses for it. I loved the salt in the filling and the dough that gave the whole thing a lovely savory quality that really brought the pistachio flavor front and center.

Such a labor of love, but well worth it for all the compliments you'll get after. Also one of the easiest pastry doughs I've ever rolled out (or maybe I'm getting better at it, lol). A couple things:
1. I could only find roasted and salted pistachios at the store. They weren't toooo salty, so I went with them and it wasn't a problem (also it beat shelling pistachios for an hour).
2. Added a tablespoon of honey because we wanted to up the sweetness just a smidge.

stats.Total Time: 1 hour 45 minutes, plus 6 to 26 hours’ resting and proofing

comments.commentBody: Is it ok to use instant yeast?
Yes, I would certainly think it's fine to use instant yeast.  That is what I plan to use.
Not to be a bummer but I used instant yeast and my dough did not rise at all. I don't know if that was the reason but it was a massive fail.
Made it with instant yeast last night (same amount by weight) and it turned out great. Just make sure your yeast is not expired.

I have made this four times, three with Active Dry yeast and once with Instant yeast. 

The three times with the Active Dry yeast, the loaves came our somewhat dense and didn’t rise as I thought it should have. 

The final time with Instant Yeast was much better. I used 4.5 g of Instant Yeast. 

To substitute Active Dry Yeast for Instant Yeast, multiply by 0.75. 
To substitute Instant Yeast for Active Dry Yeast, multiply the by 1.25. 

(All yeast was new and within expiration date)

@Will R. It seems your substitution formula and what you said you did are different… My experience is 1:1 works fine. Also see https://www.kingarthurbaking.com/blog/2022/08/15/active-dry-versus-instant-yeast

Lithuanian buns:
I used 1/2 of bread recipe

After refrigerated rise, roll out dough and cut into 4 inch circles. Place heaping tablespoon of bacon filling in middle then gather dough around filling and shape into bun. Let rise 90 min. Egg wash and bake 350 for 25 min. 

3/4 # bacon chopped, one chopped onion, 1tsp black pepper. Barely cover with water in skillet. Cook until water evaporates. Pulse filling in food processor 3-4 times. Cool prior to filling bun.

For those wondering which size loaf pan to use, I baked one loaf in an 6x10” pan and the other in a 9x5” pan. The 9x5” loaf baked into a more cohesive bread loaf shape, much like the photo in the recipe, with a lovely domed top. The 6x10” loaf was a little squatter and looked more like tear-n-share dinner rolls. I definitely prefer the look of the finished loaf in the 9x5” pan.
There are two standard loaf pan sizes, 9 by 5 and 8 by 4. You should specify which size. It makes a difference to the finished loaf.
Question: After stressing the importance of gluten formation in the main article, I'm wondering why the recipe asks for AP flour instead of bread flour?
My guess is likely access - it's far easier to find APF than BF. I know my grocery store sometimes has BF but always has APF.
@Josh because the bread is light and fluffy, and bread flour tends to make a denser chewier loaf.

@Josh.  I’ve had the same question.  The French often use T45 for brioche, which has less protein than the all purpose T55.  Nonetheless, T45 and all purpose can achieve a good gluten network with slow, proper kneading in the Kitchenaid.  I’ve done it.

Made this bread today. It was delicious. I actually proofed it in the fridge for 2 days as I didn't have the time to bake it. It still turned out great and there wasn't any of that over-fermented yeasty taste. One thing I might change is to use bread flour instead of all purpose. I find the texture of the original recipe leans more toward a cake texture than a bread texture. I personally prefer a bread flour texture.
Is a standard loaf pan 8x4 or 9x5?

I just watched Saffitz making this dough on YouTube and it looks amazing;  I’m sure that bread gives off a heavenly aroma too.  May I be so bold to ask if this recipe can be made non-dairy?  I imagine that leaving out the butter would change things somewhat but is it possible to get a pretty good dough (as opposed to a fantastic one) leaving out the butter.  Looking for helpful responses not snark, please.
@cook in london . Butter is the point.

Made it by hand with 10 minutes kneading after leaving in sponge state for an hour to develop strength, then adding remaining flour and softened butter.   Left to rise 2 hours, formed into loaves, rise again, and cook at 400 from cold oven for 25 min. Great recipe!

I am very disappointed. A few weeks ago I made Claire's croissant recipe and it was perfect. So I followed this one perfectly and my bread is massively underproved and dense. I should have known when I pulled it out of the fridge after its overnight prove - it wasn't doubled or even close. After all that time kneading I am very sad about these results.
bad yeast?
Me too! I did everything like the said but it didn't work. I don't know where we went wrong.
What size is a "standard" bread pan? 9"x 5" or 4" x 8"?

I was excited about this recipe and prepared it as described.   Unfortunately, at the point where the dough is being kneaded by the bread hook and it should no longer stick to the sides -- it did.   I was hoping it would say, "If it continues to stick to the sides, do this..."   Not knowing what to do, I added just a pinch more flour -- and then  it never raised sufficiently.   I am leery to make this again as it is so much work and time, if I don't know how to fix it for the next time around.

Be sure to keep the tangzhong below 65°C/149°F so that you gelatinize the starch without cooking it.  Then (as instructed) fully cool the tangzhong before mixing the dough.

Makes wonderful baked French toast if you let a loaf stale for at least one day, cut it into 1" thick slices, pack into an ovenproof dish, let it soak up the eggs overnight, then bake it covered with foil to set the eggs then take off the foil, add a buttery syrup and bake until it is boiling.  Cool for 10 min before servin!

This turned out amazing and, although it’s a time commitment, is well worth your efforts. Claire’s instructions are very thorough and easy to follow. The recipe really does right by the reader and doesn’t leave you hanging or guessing. This is my new to go brioche/enriched dough recipe. As a professional chef, I can say I didn’t change a thing (and that never happens) and I won’t either, the recipe is absolutely perfect as is. Thank you so much Claire!

I made this exactly as written and it turned out perfectly. I did have to mix for about 20 minutes after adding the butter - very important to follow visual cues. I brought it to Easter dinner and it was the best thing on the table. Claire Saffitz is a national treasure.

Solid four stars.  If this bread wasn’t so fussy to make, it would be 5.

I definitely had some concerns it was going to kill the engine on my stand mixer

I make the Japanese milk bread every week. Family loves it. Just started mixing this dough and excited to try it. Going to make one loaf of bread and with the remaining dough, Lithuanian lasineciai(bacon buns). Former Chicagoan living in NC and have to make my own! I think this dough will be very close to the one at Racine Bakery. Have been trying to replicate them for years. I have the filling down, but the bread has never been light and fluffy enough. This should do it.
Barbara, is there any way you can share your recipe for the Lithuanian buns?  I'm Lithuanian on my dad's side, but I never inherited any Lithuanian recipes.   Thanks.

Used 1/2 of bread recipe. After refrigerated rise, roll dough and cut into 4 inch circles. Add one large TBSP of cooled bacon filling(3/4# chopped bacon, one chopped onion, 1 tsp black pepper barely covered with water and simmered until water evaporates. Pulse a few times in food processor) to circle and enclose filling, shaping into small round bun. Let rise for second time. Apply egg wash and bake for 25 min at 350. Just ate one and it is spot on! Who says you can't go home again? makes 24.

I made this dough to make cinnamon rolls (sorry Claire) for Christmas morning. After letting the dough sit in the fridge for 4/5 hours (step 6), I lined my 13x9 pan with parchment paper. Rolled out the dough, filled with cinnamon roll mixture, rolled it up, cut into 11 rolls. Placed into pan, covered, and refrigerated over night. In the morning I let them sit at room temp for about 1 hour. Then baked as directed in the pistachio morning bun directions. 5 stars

Hi! I made this without a stand mixer. I just replaced the machine with time. 

I made this Thanksgiving week and started on Sunday night. I did make the tangzhong, then the dough. Then, I let it rise in fridge over night and on Monday and Tuesday took it out each day, letting it rise on counter while I cooked other things, punching it down as necessary. By Tuesday dough was nice,soft. On Wednesday I shaped it for Sola El- Wayl's Salted Butterflake Rolls and baked. 
They were delicious!!!
I made this today and the only change I made (I am professional baker) is to add maybe a 1/4 cup of flour as the dough struck me as too wet.  The bread is very tasty.  I use SAF Instant Yeast.

Definitely need to make this using 1/2 bread and 1/2 AP flour (I use King Arthur for my flour). I had to add quite a bit of extra flour to get this to the right development stage and I’m glad I used bread flour. You’ll want to get the gluten developed to a decent degree before adding the butter. You should be an experienced bread maker because you’ll need to use experience, judgment and trouble-shooting skills. Once it was made, it was very easy to manipulate and use for the salted butter flake rolls. Oh, and I use SAF Gold use for all enriched doughs.

I used one batch of this dough to make a. Friday night hamburger buns (slider size) and b. Sunday morning buns (walnut, orange, chocolate). Each rendition using this bread base was great. I used active dry yeast, buttermilk, and AP flour. Claire's instructions are detailed enough to set you up for success if you read carefully and look for her visual cues. It's a great 'intro to bread baking' as it includes tangzhong and brioche techniques that familiarize home bakers for future baking projects.

Used half this dough for morning buns and half for hamburger buns. Mixing directions were very clear. I did not though, get a rise during 24hour fridge proof. 
I thought they were a fail but shaped in pans and placed outside on a summer day. In about3.5 hours I had a perfect rise. Next time I’ll let dough sit on counter for awhile before refrigerating. Baking times as indicated. I used active dry yeast, buttermilk and bread flour. Absolutely delicious.

This bread turned out wonderful, but I would stress a few points. Gluten development should strongly be emphasized in the beginning stage prior to adding the butter. Butter significantly reduces the ability of gluten to form. In addition, I agree with other comments that bread flour would aid in the development of the structure this bread needs. Can’t wait to try the dough in cinnamon rolls!

The batter is so heavy that my mixer got over heated and flipped the electric breaker off so I interrupted the process and put everything in the fridge while the KitchenAid cooled off. Still, it ended up being beautiful and delicious. Will definitely try this one again. Deelish.
I decided I wanted more rolls than 16 so I portioned the dough into 20 (55 grams each). I baked them in a 9x13 pan and it worked out great. I used bread flour as well.

While the bread itself turned out incredible (very soft, plushy, and tasty), unfortunately I wouldn't recommend this recipe -- running my KitchenAid for the 30+ minutes the recipe calls for led to it overheating and ultimately breaking. I had to buy a new mixer. If you make this recipe, very carefully monitor your mixer and give it long breaks to completely cool down every 5-10 minutes.
thanks for the warning! I was starting to get worried about my kitchen aid overheating. trying to be patient during the cool down.

Works perfectly fine by hand! I kneaded the dough for around 15 minutes, smushed each small piece of butter in & kneaded again for around 10. No fuss at all! Judging by the number of comments complaining about their mixer almost breaking down, this seems to be a more economical
Can I use bread flour instead of all purpose? I have some leftover from my quarantine experiments with bread.
Absolutely! Bread flour makes lovely Tangzhong!

The half hour at step six was not enough here. Came out of the fridge the exact same size it went in. Guess it was too cold in my house. Put it in a warm oven to rise because deadlines, and it worked out OK, but not great. Will try to adjust next time.
Is it possible to make a double batch of this recipe? Will it throw anything off?
This looks intriguing! This weekend I was planning to make challah dough to slice and freeze for quick french toast breakfasts for our kids. How would this bread work instead?
Only one way to find out. Please let us know how you fared.
I bet it would work fine.  Challah and brioche are similar.
I assume one could shaped this into 3 or 6 braids for a challah for French toast?
My grandmother's challah looked exactly like the picture.  The braided challah was mainly for holidays although my grandmother never bothered.
Yes instant yeast will do fine
Super excited to see more Saffitz recipes. Her cookbook so far has been fantastic!
Should red or gold SAF yeast be used?  Normally, I would use gold for a bread with this much sugar.

Regarding the dairy free question. I agree with the other responder that oil will not be a successful substitute. Another animal fat would likely work, such as high quality lard. (We use lard in some of our bread in my bakery). Other solid fats would probably also work, such as shortening or coconut fat. I would also avoid things like vegan butter, as they are soft. It's worth noting, though, thst regardless of what you use, you will still probably end up with something edible!

There was essentially no rise during the long period in the refrigerator. After shaping it, it took much longer - by several hours - for the dough to double.  It baked well and tastes ok but it's not replacing regular brioche in my baking rotation.  I do think it would make excellent hamburger rolls.
I generally find these types of breads do better with bread flour. Has anyone tried it?
I only use bread flour (with a smidge of white whole wheat in the, ain dough) for Tangzhong.

I made this dough as written except that I used instant yeast (and followed the shaping instructions for hamburger buns). The dough rose nicely in the fridge and the buns were puffy and tender but still sturdy enough. This isn’t an everyday bread, given the time commitment and ingredient list, but the recipe worked perfectly and I’ll definitely make it again! I’m glad to learn the tangzhong technique.

To Cook in London, fat is fat. Generally butter is used for it's flavor, aroma and mouthfeel. Use one of the better vegan "butters" and your bread should be fine. Understand though, that butter is about 18% water, 80% butterfat and 2% milk solids. If this were a tricky pastry that might make a difference. I doubt it will have much effect on bread.
Don't be afraid to make by hand. I cut eggs to 3, and butter in half. Also mixed in a bit of whole wheat flour, and a touch of sprouted flower.
Goat milk worked well too.

I am an experienced baker, and made this to the exact directions. I think bakers should be warned about the amount of time this recipe takes even with a mixer with a dough hook. My older model KitchenAid was stressed after 43 minutes total time at various stages of the recipe. I could even smell the lubricant in the motor at the end of mixing. I thought I passed the windowpane test, and the dough did rise alter 24 hours in the frig. The end result was disappointing. Will never make again. Sorry.

Follow the Amish rule - first the yeast, with sugar and the warm water, then all the rest of the wet ingredients.  Then, all the dry.  Mix and knead.  Add whatever bit of flour you determine.  Let it rise and double, punch down, separate, rise and double.  Then bake.  You're experienced.  Use the experience.
Use your hands peeps!  Delicious rolls from this. Your hands will tell you when it's ready. I chilled dough for about 6 hours, will bake 2nd half tomorrow. Great flavor.

This is a superb recipe. I used most it for the burger buns and the 230g remainder for a kind of apricot tart based on the one in “Dessert Person.” I will be making this dough again and again.

FYI, I used instant yeast. Given the cold bulk proof in the fridge, I don’t think it made any difference.

Also, I used buttermilk in the tangzhong.

I used this dough (1 less egg) for almond cream cinnamon buns. They are amazing.  Similar to her pistachio buns, but only 12 (20x10), so a bit bigger.  I use Gold instant yeast, which I always use for enriched doughs. And, unflavored dental floss to cut is key. Everything worked out well. I've had hit/miss success with her recent recipes, but I can say that when I use the written recipe and not the video recipe things turn out much better...Thanks, Claire!

Right.  Instead, follow the rules the Amish use.  Take ALL of the wet ingredients first.  Start the yeast - adding sugar and warm water.  Add in all the rest of the wet ingredients.  Then, mix in all the dry ingredients.  Knead, either with a mixer or by hand, adding in whatever extra flour seems relevant.  Throw it into a large bowl, let it rise until double.  Punch down, place into pans, let rise til double, again.  Bake at 350F.

Works beautifully, every single time.  All the wet ingredients

These were amazing and made the best 4th of July burger buns in history- however, while in the fridge for the overnight rest, they essentially exploded and expanded to completely fill the bowl. I wasn't planning to bake them for an additional 9 hours after checking them in the morning, so I punched the air out and re-balled it, returning it to the fridge again for a second slow rise. It totally worked and was a normal doubled size, and after shaping, the buns proofed and baked perfectly.

Delicious! Texture like a lighter weight Challah. I cut the recipe in half so might have used too little salt but delicious nonetheless. I want to immediately make it again but using bread/semolina flour for a bit more texture.

Made this for the first time. The dough never stopped sticking to the bowl and it was harder to manipulate than Claire's, but the end result looked and tasted great! I used one half for a loaf and one half for burger buns. Will make it again!
Agree about needing to add more flour to the dough--it was very wet and sticky at 490 g of flour. I think an extra quarter cup is about right

6 tablespoons of flour is 90 grams, not 50 grams (according to google). A tangzhong often has a 1:5 ratio of flour to liquid (also according to google), so I will go with 50g of flour. Would love to hear if others struggled with this.

I've been toying with this a wee bit. I now freeze my butter so as to not let the heat from the kneading process warm up the dough too much. I also have upped the amount of flour to 520grams from the listed 490.

I made this dough per the recipe, then used the filing & glaze from Joshua Weissmans Cinnabon Copycat recipe: https://www.joshuaweissman.com/post/cinnabon-cinnamon-rolls-but-better with a few small tweaks to the filling, and they came out fanTASTIC! I had been trying to adapt his Cinnabon recipe to include a milk-based tangzhong and I thought I got a pretty good result after several attempts, but of course Claire's recipe outdid my own by miles! Thank you Claire!

Can I use instant yeast and how much my first batch I made my dough never rised
 at all.the recipe calls for çhilled eggs and butter could the cold eggs have killed the yeast that's y it didn't rise

Tried this out with 1/2 the recipe in my bread machine on dough setting. I added the butter 1/2 way thru knead. I followed Claire’s instructions to let it rise 30 min in the bread machine after it was kneaded and then I took it out and shaped it into the bowl for a bulk ferment and followed instructions as normal after. Worked great. I tangzhong-ed in the microwave 1 min, stir, and 30 sec increments. Much faster than stove top and same result.

It is a wet dough, but trust the process.

I don't understand why a temperature isn't given for the cooled tangzhong. [“Cover the bowl and let the tangzhong sit until it’s room temperature, about the better part of an hour. (A cool tangzhong is crucial to ensuring proper dough development; you can speed it along by chilling the bowl and occasionally uncovering it to stir the tangzhong.)”] How can the yeast activate if the liquid is cool? Besides, room temperature and cool are not the same things. Used this for Pistachio Morning Buns.

In case anyone was wondering yes you can make these by hand! Would definitely recommend a bench or bowl scraper though I couldn’t find mine and did without. Do that initial knead just until it smooths out, and then I did put mine in the fridge for maybe ten minutes to rest. Add the butter a few cubes at a time (though you don’t have to incorporate them fully each time), and then it gets a LOT easier. Passed the window pane test after about ten minutes and the dough was well worth the effort!

Since I only had one egg, rather than 4, I replaced the other 3 eggs about a 1/3 cup of milk to allow the bread to bind together. Also had to knead the dough by hand. Despite this, bread still turned out AMAZING-soft, chewy and slightly sweet.
*Note: If kneading by hand, knead for about 10-15 minutes before adding the cold butter, then again for 5-10. Dust flour on hands/work surface to prevent from sticking (it can get really messy), adding a bit more flour to the dough if necessary.

I made this using the new stand mixer for my birthday and it was so fun and turned out great! I have a 9x5 and an 8x4, so I divided the dough roughly 60/40 and that worked well. I did not use parchment and had no problem with sticking. I also proofed my yeast in the slightly cooled tangzhang; I know she said that proofing was not necessary but since I use active yeast that has the coding, I prefer to air on the safe side with yeast proof.
Could you freeze half the dough after the first rise to bake later?
Ran out of milk right before I decided to make this and used water instead and it turned out delightfully light and rich at the same time

Fabulous, delicious, and versatile. Great job, Claire; your instructions are always spot-on and directed to the most crucial recipe steps. I made one whole batch yelding: one loaf, four hamburger buns, and 9 Jagodzianki (Polish blueberry buns because I wanted to bake something different from the old blueberry muffins). This recipe will become a staple in my kitchen. Thank you for being a great baker and teacher.

The recipe is an excellent AP dough and I made it first as written.  The second round gave me the chance to alter the flour by adding a little whole wheat and three eggs.  The second dough was sturdier than the original obviously and the pull was lessened but it was still excellent and more substantial than feathery.

I have made this recipe numerous times. It is miraculous.  EVERY time, the dough has been perfect. The resulting bread keeps beautifully for days and freezes well. I've used the dough for hamburger buns, dinner rolls, and cinnamon buns. All were as great.I can say with confidence that if you aren't having success, it's because your yeast isn't in good shape, you mismeasured (I HIGHLY encourage using a scale instead of measuring cups), or you failed to follow the instructions precisely.

Amazing recipe! Made this 5-7n times in the past few weeks, and perfect and delicious every time. I play with the salt and sweetness for fun. This bread is like a warm hug, for real.  Thank you, Claire. I will make this for the rest of my life.
I made through the weekend and the result was wonderful. I would recommend to watch de video to understand clearly what you need to achieve with the constenticy of the dough.

I think it’s a great recipe.  My dough was very fluffy and I got a good gluten structure.  A lot of kneading with the KitchenAid. 

I try to keep my dough at around 74-78F (no higher) during kneading.  I find that if I pause, after adding and incorporating butter, and chill the dough covered in the fridge for about 15 mins it helps the final kneading.

Ok THE BEST HAMBURGER BUNS I’VE EVER HAD. I was worried because I’m a pretty bad baker and I was also short on time, had expired yeast, did not have a stand or hand mixer, and no milk. It still came out incredible. Basically I used Oatly creamer instead of milk, had to knead everything by hand - I used the slap method instead of the hand kneading -, did not fridge proof them and instead proofed on the counter for 90 minutes (it was pretty warm), and used 50% more yeast to give it a boost. I also made less cause I only needed 6 buns so thank you for having the grams listed in the ingredients! Made it a lot easier to convert just multiplying everything by 0.6. I made the dough and buns in just over 4 hours altogether so anyone short on time be assured that you’ll still get great buns!

Claire Saffitz is one of my Sheroes! This recipe is as yummy and rich as it is simple and straightforward, and creates a ridiculously delicious, tender, and buttery-soft dough. Highly recommend!

My KitchenAid stand mixer definitely got a workout but on medium-low speed (2-3), it was just fine. Made recipe exactly as written; perfection doesn’t need modification. Also made Claire’s Pistachio Morning Buns.

I just made this early this afternoon anticipating using it tomorrow for butter flake rolls.  Since there was sugar in the recipe, I used an osmotolerant yeast: Saf-Instant Gold which I generally use for cinnamon rolls and danish and really everything else.  I keep it in the freezer and the current batch of yeast is two years old but it works just fine.  I put the dough out on my back porch (I'm in Maine where it is currently 31 degrees) and it has fully risen since about 2 pm.

Regarding the dairy-free question: I think subbing olive oil for the butter would work fine, slight change in flavor but not a big deal. The bigger issue is the milk - most plant milks have way too little protein, I wouldn't replace it with anything other than soy. If you can still have eggs (assuming dairy-free and not vegan) I think this is worth a try!  It will have a different flavor than the original dairy version but should have a similar density and richness.
The butter stiffens the dough to make it easier to roll. Oil would not.

Re making this non-dairy, for my standard bread recipe I started out using butter but eventually changed to canola oil for health reasons. It isn't as good but it works just fine.
Enriched yeast doughs should include at least some solid fat to help stabilize the dough structure. If you melt the shortening you don't get the effect you need.

AFAIK instant and active dry yeast can be used interchangeably but you need to dissolve the active dry kind in some of the liquid first.

For a non-dairy version it should be fine to use any kind of butter substitute that's solid at room temp, e.g. margarine or coconut oil (I'd use less coconut oil since butter is around 80% fat and 20% water but coconut oil is 100% fat). Switching the fat will change the flavor a bit but the texture should be comparable.
Can I make half the recipe and one loaf? or can I keep the other half of the dough in the fridge or freezer for a few days?
Might try this for indulgent cinnamon rolls.  Anyone had success with this?

Absolutely yes. The filling (butter, brown sugar, cinnamon, and a touch of cardamom) leaked out, but I just turned them out of the pan upside down and it all ended up inside the rolls where it belonged.
Would it be OK to use half the sugar?  I do not like bread with a sweet taste unless it's for dessert!
You can leave the sugar out completely at a small penalty in bulk fermentation time, though one teaspoon will help boost the yeast activity.
Can I use a gluten free flour for the bread? I want to make a bread with pistachios.

You will need to find a recipe for a gluten free bread and use the pistachio filling. If you just substitute gluten free flour, you won’t get the same results. King Arthur Flour has a recipe for a gluten free cinnamon bun, and at the end has an option for using tangzhong: https://www.kingarthurbaking.com/recipes/gluten-free-cinnamon-rolls-recipe

Use real butter!  And when you get it out of the oven, use some more!

@ cook in london: Your best bet would be to replace the butter with a fat that acts similar (i.e., solid at room temp), like coconut oil, a vegan butter substitute, or margarine (which many kosher bakers use). The flavor would be different, but I suspect the dough would act pretty similar. Replacing the milk in the tangzhong with a non-dairy milk would be no problem. If you like the flavor of coconut, I’d use coconut oil and coconut milk.

Please specify the actual speed number 1-10 that you used on your Kitchenaid mixer, in addition to saying vague things like "medium-low".
34-40 minutes in the mixer? That voids your warranty.  Make this in someone else's mixer.
so I was going to bake today but something came up!  can I push the 24 hour fridge time to tomorrow afternoon.  24 hours would be 4am tomorrow morning so im looking at adding another 12-14 hours.

stats.Total Time: 1 hour 45 minutes, plus 6 to 26 hours’ resting and proofing

comments.commentBody: These turned out perfectly; good flavour, tender crumb but sturdy enough for a juicy burger and plenty of toppings. I used instant yeast but otherwise followed the dough recipe as written.

Hello
Can i ask you that how many grams of instant yeast you use?

thank you

Excellent and easy recipe. The buns are buttery and sturdy. 
I don’t buy store bought anymore.
The absolute best burger buns I’ve had in bombay and I can’t believe they came from my own home! Love, love, love this recipe.

I’ve tried a number of other sandwich bun recipes only to find them not worth the bother. Not this one!  My buns were drop-dead gorgeous, moist, not too sweet, and held up to a juicy hamburger. My whole family was commenting on how great they were and were surprised that they weren’t from a bakery. I think part of it might’ve been that I carefully portioned the buns and formed them in a ring, which made them all quite uniform. The dough looked like a wide biscuit after shaping, but after baking, was exactly what a hamburger bun should look like. 

We had plenty of extras, so I froze the leftovers which a couple months later were still perfect after defrosting. Even better, those defrosted buns stayed relatively fresh for days. It’s now the Tuesday after Thanksgiving and I defrosted the buns on Friday. Still pretty darned good, but beginning to dry out a bit. I attribute their outstanding keeping qualities to the tangzhong-based dough.

Claire, For a baker and pastry chef - YOU ROCK!  This bread was 'da bomb'. I made hamburger buns and put dried onions as a topping (an attempt to simulate Arby's onion buns) but they were of course 1000 times better.  My kids marveled as Dad became the new Roast Beef sandwich champion.  :)

Your instructions were perfect.   My wife - who came from the Sichuan province sealed her stamp of approval as well! Bravo..
-Matt
These are DELICIOUS and not difficult at all to make.  My buns were the talk of the July 4th weekend festivities!  :)  I’ll definitely be making these again!
Most excellent buns for barbecued pulled pork

Marvelous! Followed recipe except used bread flour and added 90 g more during kneading to attain reasonable dough consistency. Although very good, I prefer less sweetness, more substance, and a crust that didn’t shatter into a shower of crumbs on taking a bite. A 2d attempt without added sugar, one less egg, half the butter, and no additional flour achieved that but only baked to a golden brown rather than Claire’s spectacular mahogany. Maybe a little malt next time for color.
cooking time seems a bit long?

Do you think I can do some in an oblong for more of a hot dog or sausage bun?
 I am a new baker, so any input is appreciated!

CindyG:  Absolutely!  There is no reason not to.  This weekend, I will use this dough to make buns/hoagie rolls to hold grilled Italian sausages with grilled onions and peppers.   

I'll form 10 rolls.  I won't sprinkle them with any sort of seed or seasoning.  The baking time will probably not need to be adjusted, but I'll keep my eye on the rolls during the last 5 minutes just in case, as I'd hate to lose that pretzel-colored look of the hamburger buns in the photo.  That color is beautiful!

Superb. My mixer is a little older and needs babysitting when kneading bread, so we bonded for ~half an hour yesterday while mixing this dough. I made 10 x 90g buns (baked on one half sheet pan); with the 230g of dough left over, I made into an apricot brioche “tart” based on the one in “Dessert Person,” baked in a loaf pan. 

These buns were, in a word, sensational.

The steps go 1. Grease the pan 2. Portion dough.
Can I get a step 1.5 where they tell us how to make the dough?
They snuck step 1.5 into the ingredients list -- the second item is the link to the All-Purpose Enriched Bread dough.

Yes, one could just walk across the street to the bodega to get $1.50 hamburger buns - but this baking project was so much fun! As long as you thoroughly read the instructions and follow them correctly you will have perfect fluffy but sturdy buttery buns.
Didn’t bake until deeply browned, which is on me, but they still look fantastic.

I thought the flavor of the buns exceptional and the instructions generally precise (I just had a difficulty following how to shape the buns).  Like most buns, however, it doesn’t hold up all that well against a juicy MR burger—in my experience.
Good for any kind of sandwich, but they don’t hold up well against hamburgers.  Wonderful taste.

If youre making on the day you want to eat it: Let it double in size first  (1-2 hours) before you put it in the fridge to cool, I only let it have 30mnts as the recipe said before putting it in the fridge, after 6 hours it hasnt rised at all. Other than that, great recipe! <3
So wonderful! Thank you for the recipe!

These taste fantastic, but I find that the buns that start on the bottom rack already have a cracked crust before the switch (top ones look perfect). Anyone else run into this? Should I switch sooner or try a thicker egg wash?
Best hamburger buns I have ever eaten. Beautiful and delicious. I baked them for close to 30 min to get them brown though.

I’ve used another similar recipe to dress up Costco croissants, but browning the almond flour and using brown rather than white sugar produces superior results. You can play with the syrup—instead of alcohol, use rum flavoring or almond extract, 2 T raspberry or apricot jam—whatever you like. Another idea is to add some cocoa powder to the almond filling and some a chopped semisweet chocolate inside each croissant. Thanks NYT for improving on a already great idea.
I mean, this is amazing. I’ve been making croissants during the pandemic but always have too many to eat before they go stale. THIS is the solution I’ve been waiting for. So easy.
We’ve used Costco croissants with this recipe, they taste amazing!

made these today to eat tomorrow- perfect for the croissants I made and froze last week- I divided the recipe in two so I could make one sugar-free and one as written. Also used Grand Marnier in place of rum. Both the sugar-free and sugar versions are delicious! Thanks Claire!
I loved the part of the video 15:25 where she talks about turning right triangles into isosceles triangles and shared it with the geometry teachers I coach as an authentic application. Thank you!!!!
Who says this can’t be used on simple bread to make almond toast in the absence of croissants?
I used melted butter in step 2 and skipped all the beating a whisking. I just waited for the mixture to cool. They came out divine!

A decent and delicious recipe. I used almond liqueur (and a bit of vanilla) instead of rum for the syrup. I would suggest different proportions for filling the croissants:  use 2/3 of the total for the insides and only 1/3 for the tops (instead of 50:50). Also pop into the oven about 5 minutes just until the frangipane begins to melt, then sprinkle on sliced almonds and continue baking. This prevents the almonds from sliding off the tops of the croissants when the frangipane first melts.
I added a dusting of powered sugar after they cooled.

I buy croissants in quantity from Costco and end up with too many for my husband an I alone, to eat. We don't particularly like rum or any alcoholic beverages. Would it be possible to replace the rum with something else or choose not to use the simple syrup at all?

The simple syrup helps soften a stale croissant and glue the whole pastry together so that it feels more coherent and less sandwich-like, so I wouldn't skip it. But, the rum is just there for flavor. You can easily omit it from the simple syrup and use a bit of vanilla or maple extract or rosewater  or whatever else appeals to you for flavor, or just leave it plain.
I freeze in bags of 2; slice in half while frozen and toast. Delicious!
Can you do this with fresh croissants; or day-old croissants?
The video shows how to make croissants, not how to revive stale or store-bought ones. In any case, I was wondering if the frangipane or syrup can be made ahead of time.
@Janet -- if you watch to the end, she does show how to revive stale croissants.  It starts at about 25 minutes/15 seconds.  She shows the entire process, and I'm still drooling.
Why use artificial almond extract?  It really throws off the good almond flavor.
My daughter said “ Mom, these taste like Christmas!”the first time I made these; so for the last 3 years I make sure to have Almond Croissants on Christmas morning. They’re sensational!
Can you freeze the final product?
These are truly amazing. However, Step 2 is much easier if you use a stand mixer with a paddle attachment! And I substitute bourbon for the rum! Yum!

Because I didn't feel like setting off the dorm fire alarm, I did not toast the almond flour. I also substituted a liberal amount of almond extract (measuring with the heart) for rum. There was just the right amount of frangipane for seven Costco croissants, and I followed someone's suggestion to put most of it inside rather than splitting it between the inside and the topping. Delicious!!
It's still amazing   Never too late to try it.  So glad I did!

Absolutely fabulous say all of my 'victims'.  Better than any bakery said one.  Clearly all the work is worth the wait for these pastries.  Will willingly do it again just to provide a wee bit of joy during these troubling times.

Turned out great the first try! I did even longer cold rises in the refrigerator (because of work schedule) and didn’t seem to make a difference. Do not underestimate how much they’ll proof and expand in the oven; I only had one full baking sheet so I tried doing 7 on the sheet and 3 in a cake pan. Ended up having sides touching; all worked out though!

I just made these and they are delicious. I used dark chocolate chips instead of batons, they were rather large so I put 8/10 in each croissant. I baked on cookie sheets, don’t be like me, use rimmed sheets as there will be butter overflow. Overall,  a great set of instructions and a fun weekend project!

I am still intimidated by the croissant dough process, but I used these instructions to make pain au chocolat with quick puff pastry. There is a bulk food store in my town that sells the batons, so that is what I use for the chocolate. The pastry is not quite as delicate because it doesn’t rise as much, but they are relatively quick and still delicious!

One heck of a Memorial Day weekend baking project right here! Claire's video and written instructions were enough to make my very first at-home croissants a success. Never imagined I would be able to make these at home with such a tender interior crumb and exterior crunch-shatter. Of course, I'll still get the masterful versions from, say, Konbi and Tartine, this just makes me appreciate the pros even more. Thanks for helping me take these beauties over the finish line.

Just made these, they were phenomenal. I folded the croissant dough one extra turn before rolling out after reading the comments on the other recipe. I just used bar chocolate broken into pieces and it worked great.

Looks like I'm the first person to have baked these! The directions were very good for both this recipe and the dough. I started the proof in the humid oven but then took them out after an hour because I was nervous the butter was beginning to ooze out (so be careful of that). Either than that the croissants were perfect! Very flaky and they looked beautiful. Just like what you can get at the bakery.

Well done.  I lived a few doors down from a Patisserie on the Rue St. Honore in Paris in 1992 and would walk by the door each morning on my way to class.  St. Honore being the patron Saint of Bakers must have had an eye on this Patisserie because they turned out consistently fabulous Pain au Chocolat morning after morning.  I've just used this recipe for a guide, but substituted Salted Vegan Butter in my Pate Feullitee for Dairy Butter and the Pain au Chocolat turned out very nicely.

I may have rushed it, but cutting a bar of chocolate into batons turned into a crumbly mess, even when using a sharp knife and the scoring method described in the video. Easy enough to nestle the shards into the dough when forming, but next time will definitely be ordering batons online to avoid the hassle.

I have made both the basic croissant recipe and this one now and in both cases, the most amazing results. Tear-inducing deliciousness. I used Trader Joe’s hot chocolate sticks in mine. They are a bit big on their own, but cut in half they are perfect - both in flavor, length, and price!
Had a bit of trouble with the dough. Perhaps overproofed them. Butter leaked. Do you know…they were still amazing?

For my first time, I’m very happy with how they came out. Probably will cut a little more narrow than 3” in the future because they are enormous.
A reminder to be patient; I set mine in the oven with the skillet too soon and a lot of butter melted out. Trust the process, they look and taste great, and use good chocolate. I sprang for Belgian sticks (Amazon) and they taste like the real deal.
I have made this recipe 4 times and only once did I have anything less than ideal! I recommend weighing your ingredients and being careful about the heat of the oven for the proof.

I wish I could reconcile a recipe like Claire's, which uses a strong bread flour, with the recipe by Cedric Grolet which uses T45 pastry flour. There is a huge difference between these flours.  Grolet also has access to special dry butter ('beurre patissier' 'beurre de tourage') which is somewhat hard to find in the U.S. (still).  Too much moisture in the butter makes the pastry rise too high, too fast and then the layers collapse.
Froze half after shaping before the last proof, they didn’t rise quite as much but still came out as good as the first half that I followed the exact recipe!!
Thank you Claire. I’m on my 6 try at perfecting the croissant. My 5th batch turned out beautifully. Each attempt improves measurably.

This turned out so well!! For the final proof I froze for 20 mins, put in fridge for an hour and a half, and then froze again for 10 minutes and it came out perfectly! I didn’t have time to proof in the refrigerator for 8-16 hours so I subbed it for this.
First time attempting a puff pastry and it turned  out great! Video was esp. helpful.

These are delicious! They have come out perfectly every time I made them - even when the butter melted a bit! The next time I was more careful to let the skillet of water cool more and placed it on the very bottom of oven. 
Only change I’d recommend is to cut them into 15, still using two batons of chocolate. If you make 10 as the recipe calls for - they are really big and have too much dough:chocolate.
